commit_message:
Validate `proto.dtype()` before calling `set_dtype()`.

This prevents a `DCHECK`-fail when the proto contains an invalid dtype for a tensor shape with 0 elements or for an incomplete tensor shape.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408369083
Change-Id: Ia21a3e3d62a90d642a4561f08f3b543e5ad00c46
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. When decoding a tensor from protobuf, a TensorFlow process can encounter cases where a `CHECK` assertion is invalidated based on user controlled arguments, if the tensors have an invalid `dtype` and 0 elements or an invalid shape. This allows attackers to cause denial of services in TensorFlow processes. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
[lite] Add check for bias_size is zero to avoid division by zero. This shouldn't happen for properly converted models. Just safety check

PiperOrigin-RevId: 416383645
Change-Id: If8e508bf696ae8ecfb927e69c139a8ccf7fe60cb
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. An attacker can craft a TFLite model that would trigger a division by zero in `BiasAndClamp` implementation. There is no check that the `bias_size` is non zero. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
KVM: nSVM: always intercept VMLOAD/VMSAVE when nested (CVE-2021-3656)

If L1 disables VMLOAD/VMSAVE intercepts, and doesn't enable
Virtual VMLOAD/VMSAVE (currently not supported for the nested hypervisor),
then VMLOAD/VMSAVE must operate on the L1 physical memory, which is only
possible by making L0 intercept these instructions.

Failure to do so allowed the nested guest to run VMLOAD/VMSAVE unintercepted,
and thus read/write portions of the host physical memory.

Fixes: 89c8a4984fc9 ("KVM: SVM: Enable Virtual VMLOAD VMSAVE feature")

Suggested-by: Paolo Bonzini <pbonzini@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Maxim Levitsky <mlevitsk@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Paolo Bonzini <pbonzini@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the KVM's AMD code for supporting SVM nested virtualization. The flaw occurs when processing the VMCB (virtual machine control block) provided by the L1 guest to spawn/handle a nested guest (L2). Due to improper validation of the "virt_ext" field, this issue could allow a malicious L1 to disable both VMLOAD/VMSAVE intercepts and VLS (Virtual VMLOAD/VMSAVE) for the L2 guest. As a result, the L2 guest would be allowed to read/write physical pages of the host, resulting in a crash of the entire system, leak of sensitive data or potential guest-to-host escape.

commit_message:
Prevent null dereference read in `GetInitOp`.

We have a map of maps. We test that the key exists in the first map but then we don't have any validation that this also means the second map has the needed key. In the scenarios where this is not the case, we'll dereference a nullptr, if we don't have this check

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408739325
Change-Id: If9bb7ed759aba1f3b56a34913f209508dbaf65ce
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `GetInitOp` is vulnerable to a crash caused by dereferencing a null pointer. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent a null-pointer dereference / `CHECK`-fail in grappler.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409187354
Change-Id: I369c249cca32e6c56ec193f0ebbf2f2768fc7d43
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The Grappler optimizer in TensorFlow can be used to cause a denial of service by altering a `SavedModel` such that `SafeToRemoveIdentity` would trigger `CHECK` failures. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: Fix update session when auth fail
cve_desc: Glewlwyd 2.0.0, fixed in 2.6.1 is affected by an incorrect access control vulnerability. One user can attempt to log in as another user without its password.

commit_message:
Fix memory leak when a graph node is invalid.

If a graph node is invalid but a kernel is created then we set the kernel back to `nullptr` but we forget to delete it. Hence, we get a memory leak.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408968108
Change-Id: I1d8a9d0d8988ed5e08be8b9f2004ce1b4cd11b7c
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. If a graph node is invalid, TensorFlow can leak memory in the implementation of `ImmutableExecutorState::Initialize`. Here, we set `item->kernel` to `nullptr` but it is a simple `OpKernel*` pointer so the memory that was previously allocated to it would leak. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent a null-pointer dereference / `CHECK`-fail in grappler.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409187354
Change-Id: I369c249cca32e6c56ec193f0ebbf2f2768fc7d43
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The Grappler optimizer in TensorFlow can be used to cause a denial of service by altering a `SavedModel` such that `SafeToRemoveIdentity` would trigger `CHECK` failures. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Revert "NFSv4: Handle the special Linux file open access mode"

This reverts commit 44942b4e457beda00981f616402a1a791e8c616e.

After secondly opening a file with O_ACCMODE|O_DIRECT flags,
nfs4_valid_open_stateid() will dereference NULL nfs4_state when lseek().

Reproducer:
  1. mount -t nfs -o vers=4.2 $server_ip:/ /mnt/
  2. fd = open("/mnt/file", O_ACCMODE|O_DIRECT|O_CREAT)
  3. close(fd)
  4. fd = open("/mnt/file", O_ACCMODE|O_DIRECT)
  5. lseek(fd)

Reported-by: Lyu Tao <tao.lyu@epfl.ch>
Signed-off-by: ChenXiaoSong <chenxiaosong2@huawei.com>
Signed-off-by: Trond Myklebust <trond.myklebust@hammerspace.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in fs/nfs/dir.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.5. If an application sets the O_DIRECTORY flag, and tries to open a regular file, nfs_atomic_open() performs a regular lookup. If a regular file is found, ENOTDIR should occur, but the server instead returns uninitialized data in the file descriptor.

commit_message:
Handle invalid inputs instead of crashing.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409549744
Change-Id: I7f5935b34b53f7e426a5462fcc027bdbf5dcda24
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. TensorFlow's type inference can cause a heap out of bounds read as the bounds checking is done in a `DCHECK` (which is a no-op during production). An attacker can control the `input_idx` variable such that `ix` would be larger than the number of values in `node_t.args`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. This is the only affected version.

commit_message:
Prevent `CHECK`-fail when building reference tensor.

The tensor constructor does not allow reference dtypes, as these should not show up explicitly. However, when passed these invalid types instead of building an invalid object the constructor crashes via a `CHECK`-fail. We have a static builder that properly handles this case but is not applicable given current usage.

Instead, before calling the constructor, we can check that the dtype is not a reference type and return an error otherwise, given that the dtype is user controlled so malicious users can trigger denial of service.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409662503
Change-Id: I5892f831fde7f276cd7ab34519cf6b8061c71a59
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. A malicious user can cause a denial of service by altering a `SavedModel` such that Grappler optimizer would attempt to build a tensor using a reference `dtype`. This would result in a crash due to a `CHECK`-fail in the `Tensor` constructor as reference types are not allowed. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fix memory leak when a graph node is invalid.

If a graph node is invalid but a kernel is created then we set the kernel back to `nullptr` but we forget to delete it. Hence, we get a memory leak.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408968108
Change-Id: I1d8a9d0d8988ed5e08be8b9f2004ce1b4cd11b7c
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. If a graph node is invalid, TensorFlow can leak memory in the implementation of `ImmutableExecutorState::Initialize`. Here, we set `item->kernel` to `nullptr` but it is a simple `OpKernel*` pointer so the memory that was previously allocated to it would leak. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
array.c: fix `mrb_ary_shift_m` initialization bug.

The `ARY_PTR` and `ARY_LEN` may be modified in `mrb_get_args`.
cve_desc: mruby is vulnerable to NULL Pointer Dereference

commit_message:
Fix Null-pointer dereference in BuildXlaCompilationCache

If ConfigProto is not used, then use the default settings which is to allow all devices.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 420391800
Change-Id: I88161ad7042990aef678e77b597a2fb2c8f815be
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. When building an XLA compilation cache, if default settings are used, TensorFlow triggers a null pointer dereference. In the default scenario, all devices are allowed, so `flr->config_proto` is `nullptr`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Replace faulty overflow check with a builder for `TensorShape`.

Prevents an integer overflow that was not caught before.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 415381595
Change-Id: I76585ddedc912bd9f4a390aeafa8e2ced1a28863
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `AddManySparseToTensorsMap` is vulnerable to an integer overflow which results in a `CHECK`-fail when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, an assert failure based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Avoid Segfault for scalar shapes.

Calling tensor::FromElementsOp with an empty vector of elements and no type
causes a segfault. We need to let the FromElementsOp know which scalar type it
should have.
Also add back the DynamicBroadcastInDimOp canonicalization patterns, which
previously prevented this bug from happening.
Add a regression test that demonstrates the bug.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 417561444
Change-Id: I6d1d6cfb71aabbad6102422625a00bbe253ac95a
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The `simplifyBroadcast` function in the MLIR-TFRT infrastructure in TensorFlow is vulnerable to a segfault (hence, denial of service), if called with scalar shapes. If all shapes are scalar, then `maxRank` is 0, so we build an empty `SmallVector`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. This is the only affected version.

commit_message:
Handle typeof applied to empty in InstSimplify

Summary:
Do not simplify `typeof` if it is applied to an invalid type. This
handles a case like the one in the added test, where `typeof` is called
on a literal empty in unreachable code.

Reviewed By: kodafb

Differential Revision: D31000173

fbshipit-source-id: 2d7f69cbcc9c1bb0a916585c07171089444c85dc
cve_desc: A type confusion vulnerability could be triggered when resolving the "typeof" unary operator in Facebook Hermes prior to v0.10.0. Note that this is only exploitable if the application using Hermes permits evaluation of untrusted JavaScript. Hence, most React Native applications are not affected.

Prevent a crash due to heap OOB write in grappler.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408318417
Change-Id: If095feb8c001e3a8ac4a85b7387b81e8309df47d
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. TensorFlow is vulnerable to a heap OOB write in `Grappler`. The `set_output` function writes to an array at the specified index. Hence, this gives a malicious user a write primitive. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent null pointer dereference in constant folding.

Under certain conditions, an invalid protobuf saved model with invalid nodes would be loaded. During optimization phase, Grappler optimizer will then dereference a null pointer.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409683530
Change-Id: I1f10340a7ec384bc9bc587300390f1078cf5caa0
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. Under certain scenarios, Grappler component of TensorFlow can trigger a null pointer dereference. There are 2 places where this can occur, for the same malicious alteration of a `SavedModel` file (fixing the first one would trigger the same dereference in the second place). First, during constant folding, the `GraphDef` might not have the required nodes for the binary operation. If a node is missing, the correposning `mul_*child` would be null, and the dereference in the subsequent line would be incorrect. We have a similar issue during `IsIdentityConsumingSwitch`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: Fix incorrect value reading in JIT.
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the compile_xclass_matchingpath() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This involves a unicode property matching issue in JIT-compiled regular expressions. The issue occurs because the character was not fully read in case-less matching within JIT.

commit_message:
Prevent copying uninitialized data in `AssignOp`.

This prevents harder to debug undefined behaviors that cannot be traced back to the original tensor after assignments occur earlier in the graph execution. Several of these undefined behaviors are just reference bindings to null pointers, which are caught when running under ubsan/asan.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408654780
Change-Id: Iad2ec40d43f5fd7ea016c20283356c12d5ddeab1
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `AssignOp` can result in copying uninitialized data to a new tensor. This later results in undefined behavior. The implementation has a check that the left hand side of the assignment is initialized (to minimize number of allocations), but does not check that the right hand side is also initialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

If a node is missing, the correposning `mul_*child` would be null, and the dereference in the subsequent line would be incorrect. We have a similar issue during `IsIdentityConsumingSwitch`. commit_message:
Add a check for Key being scalar tensor for MapStage and OrderedMapStage ops.

According to documentation[1][2], key must be int64 value, but this wasn't enforced and the ops would fail with check failure for non-scalar key value.

[1]https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/cc/class/tensorflow/ops/ordered-map-stage
[2]https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/cc/class/tensorflow/ops/map-stage

commit_message:
run: Don't allow chroot()

If we don't allow pivot_root() then there seems no reason why we should
allow chroot().

Partially fixes GHSA-67h7-w3jq-vh4q.

Signed-off-by: Simon McVittie <smcv@collabora.com>
cve_desc: Flatpak is a system for building, distributing, and running sandboxed desktop applications on Linux. In versions prior to 1.10.4 and 1.12.0, Flatpak apps with direct access to AF_UNIX sockets such as those used by Wayland, Pipewire or pipewire-pulse can trick portals and other host-OS services into treating the Flatpak app as though it was an ordinary, non-sandboxed host-OS process. They can do this by manipulating the VFS using recent mount-related syscalls that are not blocked by Flatpak's denylist seccomp filter, in order to substitute a crafted `/.flatpak-info` or make that file disappear entirely. Flatpak apps that act as clients for AF_UNIX sockets such as those used by Wayland, Pipewire or pipewire-pulse can escalate the privileges that the corresponding services will believe the Flatpak app has. Note that protocols that operate entirely over the D-Bus session bus (user bus), system bus or accessibility bus are not affected by this. This is due to the use of a proxy process `xdg-dbus-proxy`, whose VFS cannot be manipulated by the Flatpak app, when interacting with these buses. Patches exist for versions 1.10.4 and 1.12.0, and as of time of publication, a patch for version 1.8.2 is being planned. There are no workarounds aside from upgrading to a patched version.

commit_message:
Fix Segfault in Concat V2 shape function.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 412120654
Change-Id: I3ff915faea694f9ad8b00024e9af2de9909011be
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of shape inference for `ConcatV2` can be used to trigger a denial of service attack via a segfault caused by a type confusion. The `axis` argument is translated into `concat_dim` in the `ConcatShapeHelper` helper function. Then, a value for `min_rank` is computed based on `concat_dim`. This is then used to validate that the `values` tensor has at least the required rank. However, `WithRankAtLeast` receives the lower bound as a 64-bits value and then compares it against the maximum 32-bits integer value that could be represented. Due to the fact that `min_rank` is a 32-bits value and the value of `axis`, the `rank` argument is a negative value, so the error check is bypassed. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

This means the function proceeds to use the platform and create clients even though the compiler lookup failed, potentially leading to unexpected behavior or crashes downstream.

Platform lookup succeeds.
Compiler::GetForPlatform fails with a status code that is NOT error::NOT_FOUND (e.g., INTERNAL, UNAVAILABLE, etc.).
The inner if condition (status.code() == error::NOT_FOUND) is false, so the return statement is not executed. Execution falls through to continue the function despite the compiler error.

state.value: continues past the error block without returning

step_by_step_execution.description: The function continues to create client_options and eventually creates an XlaCompilationCache, ignoring the compiler error.

commit_message:
Ensure that bundles have metadata on install

If we have a bundle without metadata we wouldn't properly present
the permissions in the transaction.
cve_desc: Flatpak is a Linux application sandboxing and distribution framework. Prior to versions 1.12.3 and 1.10.6, Flatpak doesn't properly validate that the permissions displayed to the user for an app at install time match the actual permissions granted to the app at runtime, in the case that there's a null byte in the metadata file of an app. Therefore apps can grant themselves permissions without the consent of the user. Flatpak shows permissions to the user during install by reading them from the "xa.metadata" key in the commit metadata. This cannot contain a null terminator, because it is an untrusted GVariant. Flatpak compares these permissions to the *actual* metadata, from the "metadata" file to ensure it wasn't lied to. However, the actual metadata contents are loaded in several places where they are read as simple C-style strings. That means that, if the metadata file includes a null terminator, only the content of the file from *before* the terminator gets compared to xa.metadata. Thus, any permissions that appear in the metadata file after a null terminator are applied at runtime but not shown to the user. So maliciously crafted apps can give themselves hidden permissions. Users who have Flatpaks installed from untrusted sources are at risk in case the Flatpak has a maliciously crafted metadata file, either initially or in an update. This issue is patched in versions 1.12.3 and 1.10.6. As a workaround, users can manually check the permissions of installed apps by checking the metadata file or the xa.metadata key on the commit metadata.

In standalone mode (i.e., acting as a proxy or a transparent proxy), with SSL MITM enabled, e2guardian, if built with OpenSSL v1.1.x, did not validate hostnames in certificates of the web servers that it connected to, and thus was itself vulnerable to MITM attacks.

vulnerabilities.description: If SSL_new(ctx) returns NULL (e.g., due to memory allocation failure), the code proceeds to dereference the NULL pointer in subsequent SSL_set_options, SSL_set_mode, SSL_set_connect_state, SSL_set_fd, and SSL_set_tlsext_host_name calls without any NULL check.

step_by_step_execution.description: SSL context (ctx) is successfully created and configured with certificates and verification parameters.
SSL_new(ctx) is called but returns NULL due to an internal error (e.g., memory allocation failure).
SSL_set_options(ssl, SSL_OP_ALL) is called with ssl=NULL, dereferencing a NULL pointer, causing undefined behavior / crash.

vulnerabilities.description: When the outer find on signature_def() succeeds, the code performs an inner find on the outputs() map using kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey but never checks if the result equals outputs().end(). If the key is not present in the outputs map, the iterator is dereferenced at end(), causing undefined behavior (null/invalid pointer dereference).

state.value: valid iterator pointing to an entry in signature_def map

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `init_op_sig_it != sig_def_map.end()` passes, so we enter the if-block.
The code calls init_op_sig_it->second.outputs().find(kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey). If the outputs map does not contain kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey, find() returns outputs().end().
The result of find() is immediately dereferenced with ->second.name(), which dereferences an end() iterator, causing undefined behavior (out-of-bounds read or crash).

commit_message:
Remove a `DCHECK`-fail, log an error instead.

`DCHECK` in debug mode results in crashes. TensorFlow has had multiple vulnerabilities due to this.

Outside of debug mode, `DCHECK` is a no-op.

A better alternative is to report an error to the log buffer and continue. This should happen both in debug mode and in prod mode.

We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
libpq: reject extraneous data after SSL or GSS encryption handshake.

libpq collects up to a bufferload of data whenever it reads data from
the socket.  When SSL or GSS encryption is requested during startup,
any additional data received with the server's yes-or-no reply
remained in the buffer, and would be treated as already-decrypted data
once the encryption handshake completed.  Thus, a man-in-the-middle
with the ability to inject data into the TCP connection could stuff
some cleartext data into the start of a supposedly encryption-protected
database session.

This could probably be abused to inject faked responses to the
client's first few queries, although other details of libpq's behavior
make that harder than it sounds.  A different line of attack is to
exfiltrate the client's password, or other sensitive data that might
be sent early in the session.  That has been shown to be possible with
a server vulnerable to CVE-2021-23214.

To fix, throw a protocol-violation error if the internal buffer
is not empty after the encryption handshake.

Our thanks to Jacob Champion for reporting this problem.

Security: CVE-2021-23222
cve_desc: A man-in-the-middle attacker can inject false responses to the client's first few queries, despite the use of SSL certificate verification and encryption.

commit_message:
Handle invalid inputs instead of crashing.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409549744
Change-Id: I7f5935b34b53f7e426a5462fcc027bdbf5dcda24
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. TensorFlow's type inference can cause a heap out of bounds read as the bounds checking is done in a `DCHECK` (which is a no-op during production). An attacker can control the `input_idx` variable such that `ix` would be larger than the number of values in `node_t.args`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. This is the only affected version.

vulnerabilities.description: The bounds check on `ix` against `node_t.args_size()` is performed using `DCHECK`, which is a no-op in release/optimized builds. If `ix >= node_t.args_size()`, the subsequent call to `node_t.args(ix)` performs an out-of-bounds read on the protobuf repeated field. An attacker who can craft a graph where an edge's `src_output()` exceeds the number of type arguments in the source node's `experimental_type` can trigger this.

step_by_step_execution.description: Vectors `input_nodes` and `input_idx` are initialized based on `props_->input_types.size()`.

state.value: vector of nullptrs, size = number of input types
vector of 0s, size = number of input types

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop over `in_edges_` populates `input_nodes` and `input_idx`. For each non-control edge, `input_idx[i]` is set to `edge->src_output()`.
After verifying no input_nodes entry is nullptr, the code enters the loop over input_nodes. For a node that has `experimental_type` with `type_id != TFT_UNSET`, it reads `ix = input_idx[i]`.
The DCHECK that `ix < node_t.args_size()` is compiled out in release builds, so no bounds check is enforced.
node_t.args(ix) is called with ix out of bounds, causing an out-of-bounds read on the protobuf repeated field.

Prevent null pointer dereference in `mutable_graph_view`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409684472
Change-Id: I577eb9d9ac470fcec0501423171e739a4ec0cb5c
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. Under certain scenarios, Grappler component of TensorFlow can trigger a null pointer dereference. There are 2 places where this can occur, for the same malicious alteration of a `SavedModel` file (fixing the first one would trigger the same dereference in the second place). First, during constant folding, the `GraphDef` might not have the required nodes for the binary operation. If a node is missing, the correposning `mul_*child` would be null, and the dereference in the subsequent line would be incorrect. We have a similar issue during `IsIdentityConsumingSwitch`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fix segfault on OOM in Conv2D.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 404655317
Change-Id: I33588dbd3f5d0fef980e3c908bf5515a9ee09ce7
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions several TensorFlow operations are missing validation for the shapes of the tensor arguments involved in the call. Depending on the API, this can result in undefined behavior and segfault or `CHECK`-fail related crashes but in some scenarios writes and reads from heap populated arrays are also possible. We have discovered these issues internally via tooling while working on improving/testing GPU op determinism. As such, we don't have reproducers and there will be multiple fixes for these issues. These fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If `patch_depth` (filter.dim_size(2)) is zero, a division-by-zero occurs. Even if non-zero, the subsequent `tensor.dim_size(3) / num_groups` expressions will also divide by zero if `num_groups` evaluates to zero (e.g. when `in_depth < patch_depth` in integer division).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads patch_depth from filter.dim_size(2).
Division by zero occurs computing num_groups = in_depth / patch_depth.

vulnerabilities.description: When padding == EXPLICIT, the code accesses explicit_paddings at indices 2 through 5 without verifying the vector has at least 6 elements, leading to an out-of-bounds read if the vector is smaller.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with padding == EXPLICIT and an explicit_paddings vector that has fewer than 6 elements.
The loop body enters the EXPLICIT branch and accesses explicit_paddings[2] through explicit_paddings[5].

commit_message: fixed #1900
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability exists in Gpac through 1.0.1 via a malformed MP4 file in the svc_parse_slice function in av_parsers.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

vulnerabilities.description: The index `si->pps->sps_id + GF_SVC_SSPS_ID_SHIFT` used to access `avc->sps[]` is not validated against the bounds of the `sps` array. The `sps_id` value originates from a previously parsed PPS structure and is attacker-controlled via the bitstream. If `sps_id + GF_SVC_SSPS_ID_SHIFT` exceeds the size of the `avc->sps` array, this results in an out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: A pps_id value is read from the bitstream and validated to be in [0, 255].
si->pps is set to &avc->pps[pps_id]. The pps entry was previously populated by parsing a PPS NAL unit from the (attacker-controlled) bitstream, which set si->pps->sps_id to an arbitrary value.

state.value: attacker-controlled value from previously parsed PPS

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `!si->pps->slice_group_count` passes (non-zero slice_group_count was set during PPS parsing).
avc->sps is indexed with si->pps->sps_id + GF_SVC_SSPS_ID_SHIFT. There is no bounds check on this computed index. If si->pps->sps_id is large enough, this accesses memory outside the sps array.

state.value: potentially out of bounds of avc->sps array

step_by_step_execution.description: The out-of-bounds pointer is then dereferenced: si->sps->log2_max_frame_num is read, and further fields are read/written through si->sps, leading to out-of-bounds read and potentially write.

commit_message: fixed #1895
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the ilst_box_read function in box_code_apple.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

vulnerabilities.description: When the 'size' parameter (u32) is between 1 and 3, the subtraction 'size -= 4' causes an unsigned integer underflow, wrapping 'size' to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF for size=3). This causes the while loop to iterate far beyond the intended data boundary, leading to out-of-bounds reads from the bitstream and excessive memory allocations.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a valid bitstream 'bs' and a small 'size' value, e.g. size=3.
The check 'if (!size)' is false since size is 3, so size remains 3.
cfg is allocated and header fields are read from the bitstream (4 bytes total: 1+7+3+5+1+1+1+1+1+1+2+3+1+4 = 32 bits = 4 bytes). This already reads beyond the declared size of 3 bytes.
The subtraction 'size -= 4' is performed. Since size is u32 with value 3, this underflows to 0xFFFFFFFF.
The while loop condition 'while (size)' is true since size is 0xFFFFFFFF. The loop will attempt to parse OBUs and allocate memory for each, reading far beyond the legitimate data in the bitstream.

In each iteration, gf_av1_parse_obu reads from the bitstream, and memory is allocated via gf_malloc for each OBU. The check 'if (size < obu_size)' may eventually trigger, but not before significant out-of-bounds reading and excessive memory allocation has occurred.

vulnerabilities.description: After checking that `avc->s_info.sps` is not NULL, the code dereferences `n_state.sps->poc_type` without verifying that `n_state.sps` is also non-NULL. The `n_state` struct is populated by `avc_parse_slice()`, which could leave `n_state.sps` as NULL (e.g., if the slice header references an SPS index that hasn't been loaded). This leads to a NULL pointer dereference.

ret is set to 0. The code checks conditions to determine if a new access unit has started. It passes the frame_num and field_pic_flag checks without breaking.
The code checks if (!avc->s_info.sps) and returns -1 if true. In this scenario, avc->s_info.sps is non-NULL (from a previously valid slice), so execution continues.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. Under certain scenarios, Grappler component of TensorFlow can trigger a null pointer dereference. There are 2 places where this can occur, for the same malicious alteration of a `SavedModel` file (fixing the first one would trigger the same dereference in the second place). First, during constant folding, the `GraphDef` might not have the required nodes for the binary operation. If a node is missing, the correposning `mul_*child` would be null, and the dereference in the subsequent line would be incorrect. We have a similar issue during `IsIdentityConsumingSwitch`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Replace faulty overflow check with a builder for `TensorShape`.

Prevents an integer overflow that was not caught before.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 415381595
Change-Id: I76585ddedc912bd9f4a390aeafa8e2ced1a28863
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `AddManySparseToTensorsMap` is vulnerable to an integer overflow which results in a `CHECK`-fail when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, an assert failure based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable `new_num_elements` is declared as `int` (32-bit), but `MultiplyWithoutOverflow` returns `int64_t`. The result is implicitly truncated from 64-bit to 32-bit. A product that is large but fits in a positive `int64_t` can truncate to a positive 32-bit `int`, bypassing the `< 0` overflow check. This allows constructing a TensorShape with an extremely large number of elements, which can lead to downstream issues such as out-of-bounds memory access or resource exhaustion.

step_by_step_execution.description: The input_shape tensor is read. Suppose the sparse tensor shape is something like [2, 2147483649, 2] (product = 8589934596 in int64).
new_num_elements is initialized as a 32-bit int with value 1.
First iteration (i=0): MultiplyWithoutOverflow(1, 2) returns int64_t(2). Truncated to int: 2. 2 >= 0 so overflow check passes.

Second iteration (i=1): MultiplyWithoutOverflow(2, 2147483649) returns int64_t(4294967298) which is 0x100000002. Truncating to 32-bit int yields 2. The check `new_num_elements < 0` is false (2 >= 0), so the overflow is not detected.
Third iteration (i=2): MultiplyWithoutOverflow(2, 2) returns int64_t(4). Truncated to int: 4. Check passes.

The overflow check `!overflow_ocurred` passes. A TensorShape is constructed with the original (very large) dimensions. Downstream operations like tensor allocation or indexing may now operate on memory regions far larger or smaller than expected, enabling out-of-bounds access or denial-of-service.

commit_message: fixed #1895
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the ilst_box_read function in box_code_apple.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

vulnerabilities.description: When the input `size` parameter is between 1 and 3 (inclusive), the subtraction `size -= 4` causes an unsigned integer underflow on the u32 variable, wrapping it to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF). This causes the subsequent `while (size)` loop to iterate far beyond the intended data boundaries, leading to out-of-bounds reads from the bitstream.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a small but non-zero `size`, e.g., size=3.
size is non-zero so the NULL check `if (!size) size = (u32) gf_bs_available(bs);` does not reassign it, and the second `if (!size) return NULL;` does not return.

The function reads 4 bytes (32 bits) of AV1 config header fields from the bitstream (marker, version, seq_profile, etc.). Assuming the bitstream has enough data and the marker/version/reserved checks pass.
The subtraction `size -= 4` is performed on a u32 variable with value 3, causing unsigned integer underflow.

The `while (size)` loop begins with size = 0xFFFFFFFF, which is truthy. The loop will attempt to parse and allocate OBUs for up to ~4GB worth of data, reading far past the legitimate data in the bitstream.

Inside the loop, `gf_av1_parse_obu` reads from the bitstream beyond intended bounds. Data is allocated and copied via `gf_bs_read_data`. The check `if (size < obu_size)` may eventually break the loop, but only after significant out-of-bounds reads and memory allocations have occurred.

state.value: 4294967295 (decremented each iteration but started far too large)

commit_message: fixed #1902
cve_desc: A Segmentation fault caused by a null pointer dereference vulnerability exists in Gpac through 1.0.1 via the gf_avc_parse_nalu function in av_parsers.c when using mp4box, which causes a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: In release/non-debug builds, `assert()` is compiled out as a no-op. If `avc->s_info.sps` is NULL (e.g., no SPS was parsed before receiving a slice NAL unit), the code proceeds to dereference `avc->s_info.sps->poc_type`, causing a NULL pointer dereference. Additionally, `n_state.sps` is never validated and is also dereferenced on the same line.

step_by_step_execution.description: A NAL unit byte is read from the bitstream. Suppose it decodes to `GF_AVC_NALU_IDR_SLICE` or `GF_AVC_NALU_NON_IDR_SLICE`.
n_state is initialized by copying avc->s_info. If no SPS has been parsed yet, avc->s_info.sps is NULL. The copy propagates this.
Code enters the IDR/non-IDR slice case, sets slice=1, and calls avc_parse_slice(). Suppose it returns 0 (success).

The condition checking avc->s_info.nal_unit_type range is evaluated. If avc->s_info.nal_unit_type was previously set to a slice type (e.g., from a prior call), the condition is false and execution continues past the break.
frame_num and field_pic_flag comparisons may match, so no early break. nal_ref_idc check may also not trigger a break.
assert(avc->s_info.sps) is reached. In a release build (NDEBUG defined), this is a no-op and execution continues.
The code dereferences avc->s_info.sps->poc_type and n_state.sps->poc_type, both of which are NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference (crash / undefined behavior).

commit_message: Fix overflow of triggered topics
cve_desc: uWebSockets 18.11.0 and 18.12.0 has a stack-based buffer overflow in uWS::TopicTree::trimTree (called from uWS::TopicTree::unsubscribeAll). NOTE: the vendor's position is that this is "a minor issue or not even an issue at all" because the developer of an application (that uses uWebSockets) should not be allowing the large number of triggered topics to accumulate

vulnerabilities.description: The `triggeredTopics` array (apparently sized 64) is only bounds-checked once at the very beginning of the function. During the loop and at the end, `numTriggeredTopics` is incremented without any further bounds checks. A single call to `publish` can add multiple entries (one per loop iteration for terminatingWildcardChild triggers, plus one at the end for the exact match), and the recursive call for `wildcardChild` can add even more. This can cause `numTriggeredTopics` to exceed 64, resulting in an out-of-bounds write to the `triggeredTopics` array.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `numTriggeredTopics` at some value below 64 (e.g., 63). The initial check `numTriggeredTopics == 64` is false, so drain() is NOT called.
The loop begins iterating over topic segments. On the first iteration, `iterator->terminatingWildcardChild` is non-null and not yet triggered, so `triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++]` is executed.

The loop continues. On the second iteration, another `terminatingWildcardChild` is non-null and not yet triggered. `triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++]` writes at index 64, which is out-of-bounds for a 64-element array.

state.value: 65 (after increment from 64, write was at index 64 — out of bounds)

step_by_step_execution.description: Additionally, the recursive call `publish(iterator->wildcardChild, ...)` can itself add more entries without any bounds check, compounding the overflow. The final exact-match write at the end of the function adds yet another entry.

commit_message:
run: Don't allow chroot()

If we don't allow pivot_root() then there seems no reason why we should
allow chroot().

Partially fixes GHSA-67h7-w3jq-vh4q.

Signed-off-by: Simon McVittie <smcv@collabora.com>
cve_desc: Flatpak is a system for building, distributing, and running sandboxed desktop applications on Linux. In versions prior to 1.10.4 and 1.12.0, Flatpak apps with direct access to AF_UNIX sockets such as those used by Wayland, Pipewire or pipewire-pulse can trick portals and other host-OS services into treating the Flatpak app as though it was an ordinary, non-sandboxed host-OS process. They can do this by manipulating the VFS using recent mount-related syscalls that are not blocked by Flatpak's denylist seccomp filter, in order to substitute a crafted `/.flatpak-info` or make that file disappear entirely. Flatpak apps that act as clients for AF_UNIX sockets such as those used by Wayland, Pipewire or pipewire-pulse can escalate the privileges that the corresponding services will believe the Flatpak app has. Note that protocols that operate entirely over the D-Bus session bus (user bus), system bus or accessibility bus are not affected by this. This is due to the use of a proxy process `xdg-dbus-proxy`, whose VFS cannot be manipulated by the Flatpak app, when interacting with these buses. Patches exist for versions 1.10.4 and 1.12.0, and as of time of publication, a patch for version 1.8.2 is being planned. There are no workarounds aside from upgrading to a patched version.

code_snippets:
if (r < 0 && r == -EFAULT /* unknown syscall */)
        return flatpak_fail_error (error, FLATPAK_ERROR_SETUP_FAILED, _("Failed to block syscall %d"), scall);
vulnerabilities.description: The error check after seccomp_rule_add only treats -EFAULT as a failure, silently ignoring all other error codes (e.g., -EINVAL, -ENOMEM, -EDOM). If seccomp_rule_add fails for any reason other than -EFAULT, the syscall rule is not added to the filter, but execution continues as if it succeeded. This means security-critical syscalls in the blocklist (e.g., mount, unshare, clone with CLONE_NEWUSER) could remain unblocked, allowing sandbox escape.

code_snippets:
if (r < 0 && r == -EFAULT /* unknown syscall */)
        return flatpak_fail_error (error, FLATPAK_ERROR_SETUP_FAILED, _("Failed to block syscall %d"), scall);

if (r < 0 && r == -EFAULT /* unknown syscall */)
            return flatpak_fail_error (error, FLATPAK_ERROR_SETUP_FAILED, _("Failed to block syscall %d"), scall);

step_by_step_execution.description: seccomp_rule_add is called for a blocklisted syscall (e.g., SCMP_SYS(mount)) with an argument filter or without one.

state.value: SCMP_SYS(mount) or any other blocklisted syscall

step_by_step_execution.description: seccomp_rule_add returns a negative error code other than -EFAULT, for example -ENOMEM (out of memory) or -EINVAL (invalid argument).
The error check evaluates: r < 0 is true, but r == -EFAULT is false. The condition short-circuits and the error is NOT returned. Execution continues to the next iteration of the loop.

The seccomp filter is exported and applied without the rule for the blocked syscall. The sandboxed process can now invoke the syscall that should have been blocked (e.g., mount, unshare, or clone with CLONE_NEWUSER), potentially escaping the sandbox.

state.value: Missing rule for the failed syscall_blocklist entry

commit_message:
[lite] Update TfLiteIntArrayCreate to return size_t

PiperOrigin-RevId: 416439896
Change-Id: I847f69b68d1ddaff4b1e925a09b8b69c1756653b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. An attacker can craft a TFLite model that would cause an integer overflow in `TfLiteIntArrayCreate`. The `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` returns an `int` instead of a `size_t. An attacker can control model inputs such that `computed_size` overflows the size of `int` datatype. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow when computing allocation size. The `size` parameter is a signed int and is not validated. A large positive value or a negative value for `size` can cause the multiplication `sizeof(dummy.data[0]) * size` to overflow, or the subsequent addition to overflow, resulting in a small or negative `computed_size`. When used by callers to allocate memory, this leads to an undersized allocation and subsequent out-of-bounds writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a large `size` value, e.g. size = 0x3FFFFFFF on a system where sizeof(dummy.data[0]) == 4 and sizeof(dummy) == 8.

The multiplication sizeof(dummy.data[0]) * size is computed: 4 * 0x3FFFFFFF = 0xFFFFFFFC. Then adding sizeof(dummy) (8) causes: 0xFFFFFFFC + 8 = 0x100000004, which overflows a 32-bit int, wrapping to a small positive value (4).

state.value: 0xFFFFFFFC (as unsigned), or may wrap as signed int
small value due to integer overflow (e.g., 4)

step_by_step_execution.description: The function returns the small `computed_size`. A caller using this value to allocate a buffer (e.g., malloc) will allocate a much smaller buffer than needed, leading to out-of-bounds writes when the array of `size` elements is populated.

state.value: small overflowed value returned to caller

commit_message:
Fix empty resource handle vulnerability.

Some ops that attempt to extract a resource handle from user input
can lead to nullptr dereferences.  This returns an error in such
a case.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445571938
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, multiple TensorFlow operations misbehave in eager mode when the resource handle provided to them is invalid. In graph mode, it would have been impossible to perform these API calls, but migration to TF 2.x eager mode opened up this vulnerability. If the resource handle is empty, then a reference is bound to a null pointer inside TensorFlow codebase (various codepaths). This is undefined behavior. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
[lite] Update TfLiteIntArrayCreate to return size_t

PiperOrigin-RevId: 416439896
Change-Id: I847f69b68d1ddaff4b1e925a09b8b69c1756653b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. An attacker can craft a TFLite model that would cause an integer overflow in `TfLiteIntArrayCreate`. The `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` returns an `int` instead of a `size_t. An attacker can control model inputs such that `computed_size` overflows the size of `int` datatype. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: When `size` is a negative integer, the multiplication `sizeof(dummy.data[0]) * size` interprets the negative int as a large value after implicit conversion to `size_t` (unsigned), leading to an integer wraparound. This can produce a small `computed_size` that, when used to allocate memory, results in a heap buffer overflow when the caller writes `size` elements into the undersized buffer. Similarly, a very large positive `size` can cause the addition to wrap around.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a user-controlled or unchecked `size` parameter, e.g. `size = -1`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The expression `sizeof(dummy.data[0]) * size` is evaluated. `size` (int) is implicitly converted to `size_t` (unsigned). For `size = -1`, this becomes `4 * 0xFFFFFFFF` (on 32-bit size_t) or `4 * 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF` (on 64-bit), wrapping around.

state.value: wraps around to a large or small value due to unsigned overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: The addition `sizeof(dummy) + <wrapped_value>` is computed, potentially yielding a small `computed_size` (e.g., a value much smaller than what would be needed for the intended number of elements).

state.value: small value due to integer wraparound, e.g. a few bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: The caller uses the returned small `computed_size` to allocate a buffer, then writes data assuming `size` elements fit, causing a heap buffer overflow.

Change-Id: Ia21a3e3d62a90d642a4561f08f3b543e5ad00c46
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. When decoding a tensor from protobuf, a TensorFlow process can encounter cases where a `CHECK` assertion is invalidated based on user controlled arguments, if the tensors have an invalid `dtype` and 0 elements or an invalid shape. This allows attackers to cause denial of services in TensorFlow processes. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If TensorShape::IsValid does not fully prevent integer overflow in the product of dimensions, shape.num_elements() can overflow to a small positive value or zero. When N overflows to a small positive value, the decode/allocation functions allocate a buffer sized for the small N, while shape_ is set to the original large shape. Subsequent accesses based on shape_ dimensions will read/write beyond the allocated buffer. When N overflows to zero or negative, the allocation block is skipped entirely (p remains nullptr), but shape_ is still set to the attacker-controlled large shape, creating a mismatch between the recorded shape and the null buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: proto.tensor_shape() is validated by TensorShape::IsValid, which checks individual dimension sizes and possibly total size, but may not catch all overflow scenarios in the product of many dimensions.

state.value: A shape with multiple large dimensions whose product overflows int64_t, e.g., dims = [2^30, 2^30, 2^30]

step_by_step_execution.description: proto.dtype() is checked against DT_INVALID (0) and passes since it is a valid type like DT_FLOAT.
TensorShape is constructed from the proto shape and num_elements() computes the product of dimensions, which overflows int64_t.

state.value: A small positive value due to signed integer overflow, e.g., 0 or a small number

step_by_step_execution.description: If N overflows to 0 or negative, the condition (N > 0 && proto.dtype()) is false, so p stays nullptr. If N overflows to a small positive, a small buffer is allocated.

state.value: nullptr (if N <= 0) or a small buffer (if N is small positive)

step_by_step_execution.description: shape_ is set to the attacker-controlled shape with large dimensions, and buf_ is set to p. This creates a mismatch: shape_ implies a huge tensor while the buffer is null or too small.
Any later code that accesses tensor data based on shape_ dimensions will perform an out-of-bounds heap read/write or null pointer dereference.

commit_message: Improve handling of Gopher responses (#1022)
cve_desc: In Squid 3.x through 3.5.28, 4.x through 4.17, and 5.x before 5.6, due to improper buffer management, a Denial of Service can occur when processing long Gopher server responses.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one buffer overflow when writing the null terminator to the 'line' buffer. The code writes at index `llen + 1` instead of `llen`. When the overflow protection caps `llen`, the total length in the buffer reaches `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1`, causing `line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE]` to be written, which is one byte past the end of the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The 'line' buffer is allocated with size TEMP_BUF_SIZE via LOCAL_ARRAY. Valid indices are 0 to TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1.
The while loop processes input. Assume gopherState->len is 0 and there is a complete line (lpos is found). llen is the length of the line found in the input.
The overflow check triggers because llen >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE. llen is capped to TEMP_BUF_SIZE - gopherState->len - 1 = TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1.
Since gopherState->len == 0, the else branch executes: memcpy(line, pos, llen) copies TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1 bytes. llen remains TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1.
The null terminator is written at line[llen + 1] = line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE]. This index is out of bounds since the buffer only has indices 0 through TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1.

state.value: TEMP_BUF_SIZE (one past the end of the buffer)

commit_message:
Prevent use after free in `DecodePng` kernel.

We are cleaning up the memory in `decode` and then we are using an `OP_REQUIRES` to check an invariant on the `decode` data.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409299145
Change-Id: I4eb93aaca52483eb202e89b78df07fbb2f6cb254
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. A malicious user can cause a use after free behavior when decoding PNG images. After `png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode)` gets called, the values of `decode.width` and `decode.height` are in an unspecified state. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
std::unique_ptr<uint16[]> buffer(
          new uint16[height * width * decode.channels]);
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the buffer size calculation for the DT_FLOAT path. `height`, `width`, and `decode.channels` are all `int` types. While `height * width` (total_size) is checked to be less than 2^29, multiplying by `decode.channels` (up to 4) can yield a value up to ~2^31, which overflows a signed 32-bit int. This undefined behavior can result in allocating a much smaller buffer than intended, while `png::CommonFinishDecode` then writes the full image data into it, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry. A valid PNG image is provided with large dimensions and 4 channels (RGBA). `data_type_` is `DT_FLOAT`, so `channel_bits` is set to 16.
`png::CommonInitDecode` succeeds. `decode.width` and `decode.height` are set to large values, e.g., both close to 2^14 (16384), and `decode.channels` is 4.
Width and height are cast to int and validated. width=16384, height=16384, total_size=16384*16384=268435456 (2^28), which is less than 2^29. Both dimensions are less than 2^27. All checks pass.
Output tensor is allocated. Code reaches the DT_FLOAT branch.

Buffer allocation: `height * width * decode.channels` is computed as `int` multiplication: 16384 * 16384 * 4 = 1073741824 (2^30). This specific value fits, but with slightly larger dimensions (e.g., width=23170, height=23170, total_size=536848900 < 2^29), total_size*4 = 2147395600 which overflows signed int32 (max 2147483647). Or more precisely, width=23171, height=23171, total_size=536896241 < 2^29, and total_size*channels = 2147584964 which overflows int32, wrapping to a negative or small value.

state.value: overflows signed int32, resulting in undefined behavior / small or negative value

step_by_step_execution.description: The `new uint16[overflowed_value]` allocates a much smaller buffer than needed (or the behavior is undefined). Then `png::CommonFinishDecode` writes `height` rows of `decode.channels * width * sizeof(uint16)` bytes each into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: height * width * decode.channels * sizeof(uint16) bytes, much larger than allocated

commit_message:
Prevent overflow in sparse dense cwise ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 415543171
Change-Id: I22dab7c41be2121ab5efe5403ca0e2f9b7cb24b8
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementations of `Sparse*Cwise*` ops are vulnerable to integer overflows. These can be used to trigger large allocations (so, OOM based denial of service) or `CHECK`-fails when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, assert failures based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent memory leak in decoding PNG images.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409300653
Change-Id: I6182124c545989cef80cefd439b659095920763b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. When decoding PNG images TensorFlow can produce a memory leak if the image is invalid. After calling `png::CommonInitDecode(..., &decode)`, the `decode` value contains allocated buffers which can only be freed by calling `png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode)`. However, several error case in the function implementation invoke the `OP_REQUIRES` macro which immediately terminates the execution of the function, without allowing for the memory free to occur. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
std::unique_ptr<uint16[]> buffer(
          new uint16[height * width * decode.channels]);
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the buffer allocation for the DT_FLOAT path. `height`, `width`, and `decode.channels` are all `int` types. The checks ensure `width < 2^27`, `height < 2^27`, and `width*height < 2^29`, but `height * width * decode.channels` (with channels up to 4) can reach ~2^31, overflowing a signed 32-bit int. This can result in a much smaller buffer being allocated than expected, and then `CommonFinishDecode` writes the full image data into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

code_snippets:
std::unique_ptr<uint16[]> buffer(
          new uint16[height * width * decode.channels]);

step_by_step_execution.description: A PNG image is provided with dimensions and channel count that pass all individual checks but overflow when multiplied together. For example, width=16384 (2^14), height=32768 (2^15), channels=4.

Size checks pass: width (16384) < 2^27, height (32768) < 2^27, total_size = 16384*32768 = 536870912 < 2^29 (536870912 == 2^29, so it must be strictly less). Let's adjust: width=16383, height=32767, total_size=536821761 < 2^29. Channels=4.

In the DT_FLOAT branch, the allocation computes `height * width * decode.channels` using int arithmetic: 32767 * 16383 * 4 = 2,147,287,044. This is close to INT_MAX (2,147,483,647). With slightly different values near the boundary, e.g., width=16384, height=32768 (if checks are adjusted), the product 32768*16384*4 = 2,147,483,648 which overflows signed 32-bit int, resulting in undefined behavior (likely wrapping to a negative number or small positive value).

state.value: overflows signed 32-bit int, undefined behavior, potentially wrapping to a small or negative value

step_by_step_execution.description: The `new uint16[overflowed_value]` allocates a buffer much smaller than needed (or the behavior is undefined). If the overflowed value is small and positive, a tiny buffer is allocated.

state.value: much smaller than the actual image data size

step_by_step_execution.description: `CommonFinishDecode` writes the full decoded PNG data (height * width * channels * sizeof(uint16) bytes) into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: ~1 GB of image data written into undersized heap buffer

commit_message:
Prevent memory leak in decoding PNG images.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409300653
Change-Id: I6182124c545989cef80cefd439b659095920763b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. When decoding PNG images TensorFlow can produce a memory leak if the image is invalid. After calling `png::CommonInitDecode(..., &decode)`, the `decode` value contains allocated buffers which can only be freed by calling `png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode)`. However, several error case in the function implementation invoke the `OP_REQUIRES` macro which immediately terminates the execution of the function, without allowing for the memory free to occur. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
std::unique_ptr<uint16[]> buffer(
          new uint16[height * width * decode.channels]);
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in buffer size calculation for the DT_FLOAT code path. `height`, `width`, and `decode.channels` are all `int` types. The existing checks ensure `width < 2^27`, `height < 2^27`, and `width * height < 2^29`, but `decode.channels` (up to 4) is not factored in. The product `height * width * decode.channels` can reach approximately `(2^29 - 1) * 4 ≈ 2^31`, which overflows a 32-bit signed `int`. This can result in allocating a much smaller buffer than intended, and then `CommonFinishDecode` writes the full decoded image into it, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A PNG image is provided with large but individually valid dimensions, e.g. width=16384 (2^14), height=32768 (2^15), channels=4 (RGBA). These pass all existing size checks.
Width and height are cast to int and size checks are performed. width=16384 < 2^27, height=32768 < 2^27, total_size=16384*32768=536870912 < 2^29. All checks pass.

data_type_ is DT_FLOAT, so the float code path is taken. The buffer allocation computes `height * width * decode.channels` as int multiplication: 32768 * 16384 * 4 = 2147483648, which overflows signed 32-bit int (max 2147483647). This is undefined behavior and can wrap to a negative value or small positive value.

state.value: overflow / undefined behavior, e.g., wraps to -2147483648 or 0

step_by_step_execution.description: The overflowed value is implicitly converted to size_t for `new uint16[]`. A negative int converts to a very large size_t (likely failing allocation) or a small positive value results in a tiny allocation. If the overflow wraps to a small positive value, a small buffer is allocated.

state.value: small or incorrect value due to overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: CommonFinishDecode writes the full decoded image (height * width * channels * sizeof(uint16) bytes of actual image data) into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: much smaller due to integer overflow

commit_message: fixed #1897
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the gf_text_get_utf8_line function in load_text.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

vulnerabilities.description: Stack buffer overflow in szLineConv. The buffer szLineConv is fixed at 1024 bytes, but szLine can be up to lineSize bytes (which can exceed 1024). The conversion loop iterates over the full length of szLine writing into szLineConv without any bounds check on j. Additionally, in the non-UTF8 expansion path, a single input byte produces two output bytes (j increments twice for one i increment), further accelerating the overflow even if lineSize equals 1024.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with lineSize > 1024 (e.g., 2048) and unicode_type == 0.
gf_fgets reads up to lineSize bytes into szLine. szLine is filled with data, potentially up to 2047 characters.
unicode_type <= 1 is true, so we enter the conversion loop. j is initialized to 0.

The loop iterates over all len characters of szLine. For each character where (szLine[i] & 0x80) is true and (szLine[i+1] & 0xc0) != 0x80, j is incremented twice for a single i increment. Even without expansion, when i reaches 1024, j has already reached or exceeded 1024.
Writes to szLineConv[j] with j >= 1024 overflow the 1024-byte stack buffer, corrupting adjacent stack memory (return address, saved registers, etc.).

vulnerabilities.description: When unicode_type >= 2, gf_utf8_wcstombs can write up to 1024 bytes to szLineConv. The subsequent strcpy(szLine, szLineConv) copies this into szLine. If lineSize < 1024, this overflows szLine. The gf_fgets initially limits the read to lineSize, but the UTF-16 to UTF-8 conversion can produce output up to 1024 bytes regardless of lineSize.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a small lineSize (e.g., 256) and unicode_type == 2.
gf_fgets reads up to 256 bytes (128 UTF-16 characters) into szLine.

state.value: up to 255 bytes of UTF-16 data

step_by_step_execution.description: unicode_type <= 1 is false, so we skip to the UTF-16 processing. gf_utf8_wcstombs converts UTF-16 to UTF-8 into szLineConv. Multi-byte UTF-8 sequences can expand the output significantly, but even without expansion, the result could be up to ~255 bytes.
However, if lineSize is very small (e.g., 64 bytes) and the conversion produces more bytes than lineSize, strcpy overflows szLine.

commit_message:
virtio-serial: fix ANY_LAYOUT

Don't assume a specific layout for control messages.
Required by virtio 1.

Signed-off-by: Michael S. Tsirkin <mst@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Amit Shah <amit.shah@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: Buffer overflow in the send_control_msg function in hw/char/virtio-serial-bus.c in QEMU before 2.4.0 allows guest users to cause a denial of service (QEMU process crash) via a crafted virtio control message.

vulnerabilities.description: The function copies `len` bytes into `elem.in_sg[0].iov_base` without verifying that the destination buffer (whose size is `elem.in_sg[0].iov_len`) is large enough to hold `len` bytes. A malicious or misconfigured guest could provide a scatter-gather element with a small buffer, leading to a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives `buf` and `len` as parameters, where `len` is the size of the control message to send.

state.value: caller-provided, e.g. sizeof(struct virtio_console_control) which is typically 8+ bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: The virtqueue is checked for readiness and an element is popped via virtqueue_pop, filling `elem` with guest-provided scatter-gather descriptors.

state.value: guest-controlled, could be any value including values smaller than len (e.g., 1)
points to a guest-mapped buffer of size elem.in_sg[0].iov_len

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy is called copying `len` bytes into elem.in_sg[0].iov_base without checking that elem.in_sg[0].iov_len >= len.
The memcpy writes `len` bytes beyond the allocated size of the destination buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
irc: fix crash when receiving a malformed message 352 (who)

Thanks to Stuart Nevans Locke for reporting the issue.
cve_desc: A Vulnerability of LG Electronic web OS TV Emulator could allow an attacker to escalate privileges and overwrite certain files. This vulnerability is due to wrong environment setting. An attacker could exploit this vulnerability through crafted configuration files and executable files.

vulnerabilities.description: When argc == 9 and argv[8] equals "*", arg_start is set to 9. Since argc is 9, valid argv indices are 0 through 8. The subsequent access argv[arg_start][0] (i.e., argv[9][0]) reads out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function checks argc >= 5 (IRC_PROTOCOL_MIN_ARGS(5)) and then checks argc < 8, returning early if so. Execution continues when argc >= 8.
The condition argc > 8 is checked. When argc == 9, this is true, so execution enters the block.
argv[8] is compared against "*". If it equals "*", arg_start is set to 9.
argv[arg_start][0] is accessed, i.e. argv[9][0]. With argc == 9, valid indices are 0..8, so argv[9] is an out-of-bounds access.
