commit_message:
Fix Segfault in Concat V2 shape function.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 412120654
Change-Id: I3ff915faea694f9ad8b00024e9af2de9909011be
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of shape inference for `ConcatV2` can be used to trigger a denial of service attack via a segfault caused by a type confusion. The `axis` argument is translated into `concat_dim` in the `ConcatShapeHelper` helper function. Then, a value for `min_rank` is computed based on `concat_dim`. This is then used to validate that the `values` tensor has at least the required rank. However, `WithRankAtLeast` receives the lower bound as a 64-bits value and then compares it against the maximum 32-bits integer value that could be represented. Due to the fact that `min_rank` is a 32-bits value and the value of `axis`, the `rank` argument is a negative value, so the error check is bypassed. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: When `concat_dim` is read from user-controlled tensor data and equals `INT64_MIN` (-2^63), computing `-concat_dim` causes signed integer overflow (undefined behavior). In practice on two's complement systems, `min_rank` remains `INT64_MIN` (a negative value). This negative `min_rank` passes the `WithRankAtLeast` check trivially, allowing subsequent calls like `c->Dim(input, concat_dim)` and `c->Subshape(input, 0, concat_dim, ...)` to operate with a massively out-of-range index, potentially causing out-of-bounds memory access or other undefined behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: The concat dimension tensor is read from user-controlled input. Its dtype is DT_INT64.
concat_dim is assigned the value from the tensor.
min_rank is calculated. Since concat_dim < 0, the expression is -concat_dim. Negating INT64_MIN overflows signed int64 (undefined behavior). On two's complement, the result is INT64_MIN.

state.value: INT64_MIN (-9223372036854775808) due to signed integer overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: WithRankAtLeast(input, min_rank, &input) is called. Since min_rank is a large negative number, any input shape rank satisfies this check, so the check passes without error.

state.value: INT64_MIN (negative, so rank check is trivially satisfied)

step_by_step_execution.description: c->Subshape(input, 0, concat_dim, &output_before) is called with concat_dim=INT64_MIN. This passes a massively out-of-range end index to Subshape, potentially causing out-of-bounds memory access.
c->Dim(input, concat_dim) is called with concat_dim=INT64_MIN, an extremely large negative index, leading to out-of-bounds dimension access.

commit_message:
Ensure num_streams >= 0 in tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateQuantileStreamResource

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387452765
Change-Id: I9990c760e177fabca6a3b9b4612ceeaeeba51495
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can cause a denial of service in `boosted_trees_create_quantile_stream_resource` by using negative arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantile_ops.cc#L96) does not validate that `num_streams` only contains non-negative numbers. In turn, [this results in using this value to allocate memory](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantiles/quantile_stream_resource.h#L31-L40). However, `reserve` receives an unsigned integer so there is an implicit conversion from a negative value to a large positive unsigned. This results in a crash from the standard library. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 8a84f7a2b5a2b27ecf88d25bad9ac777cd2f7992. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent nullptr deref in SparseTensorSliceDataset

The arguments must determine a valid sparse tensor. This means that when indices are empty then the values must be empty too (and the reverse).

Also added test, by modifying existing test with empty sparse tensor to now run with an invalid sparse tensor input.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388562757
Change-Id: Id8b54cd7c2316025b4f9a77292c8fb5344d17609
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When a user does not supply arguments that determine a valid sparse tensor, `tf.raw_ops.SparseTensorSliceDataset` implementation can be made to dereference a null pointer. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L240-L251) has some argument validation but fails to consider the case when either `indices` or `values` are provided for an empty sparse tensor when the other is not. If `indices` is empty, then [code that performs validation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L260-L261) (i.e., checking that the indices are monotonically increasing) results in a null pointer dereference. If `indices` as provided by the user is empty, then `indices` in the C++ code above is backed by an empty `std::vector`, hence calling `indices->dim_size(0)` results in null pointer dereferencing (same as calling `std::vector::at()` on an empty vector). We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 02cc160e29d20631de3859c6653184e3f876b9d7. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Ensure num_streams >= 0 in tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateQuantileStreamResource

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387452765
Change-Id: I9990c760e177fabca6a3b9b4612ceeaeeba51495
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can cause a denial of service in `boosted_trees_create_quantile_stream_resource` by using negative arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantile_ops.cc#L96) does not validate that `num_streams` only contains non-negative numbers. In turn, [this results in using this value to allocate memory](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantiles/quantile_stream_resource.h#L31-L40). However, `reserve` receives an unsigned integer so there is an implicit conversion from a negative value to a large positive unsigned. This results in a crash from the standard library. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 8a84f7a2b5a2b27ecf88d25bad9ac777cd2f7992. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
[lite] Update TfLiteIntArrayCreate to return size_t

PiperOrigin-RevId: 416439896
Change-Id: I847f69b68d1ddaff4b1e925a09b8b69c1756653b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. An attacker can craft a TFLite model that would cause an integer overflow in `TfLiteIntArrayCreate`. The `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` returns an `int` instead of a `size_t. An attacker can control model inputs such that `computed_size` overflows the size of `int` datatype. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If `size` is a very large value, the computation inside `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` (typically something like `sizeof(int) + sizeof(int) * size`) can experience integer overflow on 32-bit integers, wrapping around to a small positive value. The check `alloc_size <= 0` would pass, causing `malloc` to allocate a tiny buffer. The returned struct then has `ret->size` set to the original large `size`, leading callers to believe the array has far more capacity than actually allocated, resulting in a heap buffer overflow when they write to it.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a very large `size`, e.g., 0x40000001 on a system with 32-bit int.

`TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes(size)` computes the byte size, typically `sizeof(int) + sizeof(int) * size`. With 32-bit arithmetic: 4 + 4 * 0x40000001 = 4 + 0x100000004. The multiplication overflows 32-bit int, wrapping to 0x4, so the result is 4 + 4 = 8.
The check `alloc_size <= 0` is evaluated. Since alloc_size is 8, the check passes (no early return).
`malloc(8)` is called, allocating only 8 bytes on the heap.
`ret->size` is set to the original large `size` value. Callers that iterate up to `ret->size` entries will read/write far beyond the 8-byte allocation, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Prevent overflow in sparse dense cwise ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 415543171
Change-Id: I22dab7c41be2121ab5efe5403ca0e2f9b7cb24b8
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementations of `Sparse*Cwise*` ops are vulnerable to integer overflows. These can be used to trigger large allocations (so, OOM based denial of service) or `CHECK`-fails when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, assert failures based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Cleanup and remove duplicate validation in `SparseCount`.

We have valdiation that is duplicated, checking different conditions, in different formats and failing to capture all cases. This should fix all the previous bugs.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 414886981
Change-Id: Ibf0bba0beb057b76d505324bb9487565daf95f01
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseCountSparseOutput` is vulnerable to a heap overflow. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable `batch` is declared as `int` but is assigned from `indices_values(idx, 0)` which is `int64_t`. A large int64_t value (e.g., 0x80000000LL) truncated to int can become negative. A negative `batch` passes the `batch >= num_batches` check (since num_batches >= 1), and then `per_batch_counts[batch]` performs an out-of-bounds access on the underlying vector.

step_by_step_execution.description: The shape tensor is read and num_batches is computed. Assume shape is [2], so is_1d=false, num_batches=2.
per_batch_counts is created as a BatchedMap with 2 entries (indices 0 and 1 are valid).
In the loop, indices_values(idx, 0) returns a crafted int64_t value, e.g. 0x100000000 + 0 = 4294967296. When truncated to int, this becomes 0.
Alternatively, indices_values(idx, 0) = 0x80000000LL (2147483648). Truncating to int yields -2147483648 (INT_MIN).
The guard check `batch >= num_batches` evaluates to `-2147483648 >= 2` which is false, so the OP_REQUIRES is skipped.
per_batch_counts[batch] is accessed with batch = -2147483648, causing an out-of-bounds read/write on the vector's underlying storage.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable `num_batches` is declared as `int` but assigned from `shape_vector(0)` which is `int64_t`. A large shape value can truncate to a small, zero, or negative int, leading to an incorrectly sized BatchedMap and subsequent out-of-bounds accesses.

step_by_step_execution.description: The shape tensor has 2 elements, so is_1d is false. shape_vector(0) is a large int64_t, e.g. 0x100000001 (4294967297).
num_batches is assigned by truncating int64_t to int: 4294967297 truncated to 32-bit int is 1.
The shape element check passes since shape_vector(0) >= 0.

per_batch_counts is created with size 1, but actual batch indices in the sparse tensor may reference batches 0 through 4294967296. A batch index of, say, 2 (a valid int that fits in int) passes the truncated num_batches check incorrectly if it should be valid for the original shape, or, an index like 0 passes but references an under-allocated structure.

commit_message:
Fixes #4985: 4e+26 is outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned long' at coders/pcl.c:299 (#4986)

* fix Division by zero in XMenuWidget() of MagickCore/widget.c

* Fix memory leak in AnimateImageCommand() of MagickWand/animate.c and DisplayImageCommand() of MagickWand/display.c

* fix Division by zero in ReadEnhMetaFile() of coders/emf.c

* Resolve conflicts

* fix issue: outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned char' at coders/psd.c:1025

* fix error: 4e+26 is outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned long' at coders/pcl.c:299

Co-authored-by: zhailiangliang <zhailiangliang@loongson.cn>
cve_desc: A vulnerability was found in ImageMagick, causing an outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned long' at coders/pcl.c, when crafted or untrusted input is processed. This leads to a negative impact to application availability or other problems related to undefined behavior.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one stack buffer overflow: the byte is written to `command` via `*p++=(char) c` BEFORE the bounds check `(size_t)(p-command) < (MagickPathExtent-1)` is evaluated. When the buffer is nearly full, the write to `command[MagickPathExtent-1]` advances `p` to `command+MagickPathExtent`, and the subsequent `*p='\0'` writes one byte past the end of the stack-allocated `command` buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `command` buffer is declared on the stack as `char command[MagickPathExtent]`, with valid indices 0 through MagickPathExtent-1. The pointer `p` is initialized to point to `command[0]`.
The loop reads bytes from the blob. For each byte, `*p++=(char) c` writes the byte into the buffer and advances `p` unconditionally BEFORE any bounds check.

state.value: incremented by 1 after each byte written

step_by_step_execution.description: When a long line (no '/' or '\n' characters) is read, the continue condition checks `(size_t)(p-command) < (MagickPathExtent-1)`. As long as this is true and c is not '/' or '\n', the loop continues reading bytes.

After MagickPathExtent-1 bytes without '/' or '\n', p points to command[MagickPathExtent-1]. The next byte is written: `*p++=(char) c` writes to command[MagickPathExtent-1] (valid last index), and p advances to command+MagickPathExtent.

The bounds check evaluates: `MagickPathExtent < MagickPathExtent-1` which is false, so the `continue` is not taken. Execution falls through to `*p='\0'`, which writes a null byte to command[MagickPathExtent], one byte past the end of the stack buffer.

state.value: writing '\0' at command[MagickPathExtent] — out of bounds

commit_message: Improve handling of Gopher responses (#1022)
cve_desc: In Squid 3.x through 3.5.28, 4.x through 4.17, and 5.x before 5.6, due to improper buffer management, a Denial of Service can occur when processing long Gopher server responses.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one out-of-bounds write. When the overflow check triggers and clamps `llen`, the subsequent `llen += gopherState->len` can bring `llen` to `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1`. Then `line[llen + 1]` writes at index `TEMP_BUF_SIZE`, which is one past the end of the `line` buffer of size `TEMP_BUF_SIZE`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `line` buffer is allocated on the stack with LOCAL_ARRAY of size TEMP_BUF_SIZE.
In the while loop, `llen` is computed. Assume `gopherState->len` is non-zero (leftover data from previous call) and the new data plus leftover exceeds TEMP_BUF_SIZE.

state.value: value such that N + llen >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE

step_by_step_execution.description: The overflow check triggers and clamps llen: `llen = TEMP_BUF_SIZE - gopherState->len - 1`.
Since `gopherState->len != 0`, leftover data and new data are copied into `line`, then `llen += gopherState->len`.
The code writes `line[llen + 1] = '\0'`, which is `line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE]`, one byte past the end of the buffer.

vulnerabilities.description: Format string argument mismatch. The format string has 5 specifiers (%s, %s, %c, %s, %s) but only 3 arguments are passed (icon_url, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector), html_quote(name)). This causes reads of uninitialized stack values, potentially leaking sensitive data or causing a crash.

code_snippets: outbuf.appendf(" %s\n", icon_url, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector), html_quote(name));

step_by_step_execution.description: The conversion type is HTML_DIR or HTML_INDEX_RESULT, and the gopher line is parsed with gtype set to GOPHER_WWW, and selector does not start with 'GET /'.
The code enters the `else if (gtype == GOPHER_WWW)` branch and calls appendf with a format string containing 5 format specifiers but only 3 variadic arguments.
The printf-like function reads two extra values from the stack as if they were valid arguments, resulting in undefined behavior — potentially reading sensitive stack data into the output or crashing.

state.value: 2 arguments missing, stack garbage read instead

commit_message:
btrfs: fix NULL pointer dereference when deleting device by invalid id

[BUG]
It's easy to trigger NULL pointer dereference, just by removing a
non-existing device id:

 # mkfs.btrfs -f -m single -d single /dev/test/scratch1 \
				     /dev/test/scratch2
 # mount /dev/test/scratch1 /mnt/btrfs
 # btrfs device remove 3 /mnt/btrfs

Then we have the following kernel NULL pointer dereference:

 BUG: kernel NULL pointer dereference, address: 0000000000000000
 #PF: supervisor read access in kernel mode
 #PF: error_code(0x0000) - not-present page
 PGD 0 P4D 0
 Oops: 0000 [#1] PREEMPT SMP NOPTI
 CPU: 9 PID: 649 Comm: btrfs Not tainted 5.14.0-rc3-custom+ #35
 Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (Q35 + ICH9, 2009), BIOS 0.0.0 02/06/2015
 RIP: 0010:btrfs_rm_device+0x4de/0x6b0 [btrfs]
  btrfs_ioctl+0x18bb/0x3190 [btrfs]
  ? lock_is_held_type+0xa5/0x120
  ? find_held_lock.constprop.0+0x2b/0x80
  ? do_user_addr_fault+0x201/0x6a0
  ? lock_release+0xd2/0x2d0
  ? __x64_sys_ioctl+0x83/0xb0
  __x64_sys_ioctl+0x83/0xb0
  do_syscall_64+0x3b/0x90
  entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x44/0xae

[CAUSE]
Commit a27a94c2b0c7 ("btrfs: Make btrfs_find_device_by_devspec return
btrfs_device directly") moves the "missing" device path check into
btrfs_rm_device().

But btrfs_rm_device() itself can have case where it only receives
@devid, with NULL as @device_path.

In that case, calling strcmp() on NULL will trigger the NULL pointer
dereference.

Before that commit, we handle the "missing" case inside
btrfs_find_device_by_devspec(), which will not check @device_path at all
if @devid is provided, thus no way to trigger the bug.

[FIX]
Before calling strcmp(), also make sure @device_path is not NULL.

Fixes: a27a94c2b0c7 ("btrfs: Make btrfs_find_device_by_devspec return btrfs_device directly")
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org # 5.4+
Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Anand Jain <anand.jain@oracle.com>
Signed-off-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>
Reviewed-by: David Sterba <dsterba@suse.com>
Signed-off-by: David Sterba <dsterba@suse.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the btrfs_rm_device function in fs/btrfs/volumes.c in the Linux Kernel, where triggering the bug requires ‘CAP_SYS_ADMIN’. This flaw allows a local attacker to crash the system or leak kernel internal information. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

commit_message: Fix update session when auth fail
cve_desc: Glewlwyd 2.0.0, fixed in 2.6.1 is affected by an incorrect access control vulnerability. One user can attempt to log in as another user without its password.

vulnerabilities.description: On failed password authentication (G_ERROR_UNAUTHORIZED or other non-OK result), the code calls user_session_update with the attacker-supplied username and the last argument set to 1 (same as the successful authentication path). This updates the server-side session to associate the attacker-provided username as if authentication succeeded. Although the HTTP response returns 401, the session state on the server is modified. A subsequent request with the same session cookie could be treated as authenticated for that username, resulting in an authentication bypass.

code_snippets: if ((session_uid = get_session_id(config, request)) != NULL && user_session_update(config, session_uid, u_map_get_case(request->map_header, "user-agent"), issued_for, json_string_value(json_object_get(j_param, "username")), NULL, 1) != G_OK) {

step_by_step_execution.description: The function parses the JSON body from the request. The attacker provides a JSON body with a valid 'username' field, 'scheme_type' set to 'password' (or omitted), and a wrong 'password' value.
The code checks json_string_length of 'username' which is non-zero, so enters the username branch.
scheme_type is NULL so the code enters the password authentication branch.
json_string_length of 'password' is non-zero, so auth_check_user_credentials is called with the wrong password. It returns G_ERROR_UNAUTHORIZED.
check_result_value(j_result, G_OK) is false, so the else branch is taken.
The attacker already has a valid session cookie from a previous visit (e.g., browsing the login page). get_session_id returns a non-NULL session_uid.

state.value: valid session ID from existing cookie

step_by_step_execution.description: user_session_update is called with the attacker-supplied username 'admin', NULL scheme, and the last argument 1 (authenticated flag), identical to the successful auth path. This updates the server-side session to include 'admin' as an authenticated user, even though authentication failed.
The response status is set to 401, but the server-side session has already been updated to mark 'admin' as authenticated in this session.

state.value: session now contains 'admin' as authenticated user

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-p6g5-v97c-w5q4

* Prevent heap buffer overflow when parsing DNS packets

* Make sure packet parsing doesn't advance beyond max/end

* Update checks

* Remove  check

Co-authored-by: sauwming <ming@teluu.com>
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. A buffer overflow vulnerability in versions 2.12 and prior affects applications that use PJSIP DNS resolution. It doesn't affect PJSIP users who utilize an external resolver. This vulnerability is related to CVE-2023-27585. The difference is that this issue is in parsing the query record `parse_rr()`, while the issue in CVE-2023-27585 is in `parse_query()`. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. A workaround is to disable DNS resolution in PJSIP config (by setting `nameserver_count` to zero) or use an external resolver instead.

commit_message:
[lite] Update TfLiteIntArrayCreate to return size_t

PiperOrigin-RevId: 416439896
Change-Id: I847f69b68d1ddaff4b1e925a09b8b69c1756653b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. An attacker can craft a TFLite model that would cause an integer overflow in `TfLiteIntArrayCreate`. The `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` returns an `int` instead of a `size_t. An attacker can control model inputs such that `computed_size` overflows the size of `int` datatype. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If 'size' is a large positive integer, the computation inside TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes (typically sizeof(int) * (size + 1) or similar) can experience integer overflow on platforms where size_t is 32 bits. The resulting alloc_size wraps to a small positive value, bypassing the <= 0 check. malloc then allocates a tiny buffer, but ret->size is set to the original large 'size', leading to a heap buffer overflow when subsequent code accesses elements of the array based on ret->size.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a large 'size' value, e.g., size = 0x40000000 on a 32-bit platform.
TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes computes the allocation size. A typical implementation computes sizeof(int) * (size + 1) = 4 * 0x40000001 = 0x100000004, which wraps to 4 on a 32-bit size_t.
The check 'alloc_size <= 0' is evaluated. Since alloc_size is 4 (positive), the check passes and execution continues.
malloc(4) is called, allocating only 4 bytes on the heap. This succeeds and returns a valid pointer.

ret->size is set to the original large size value. The returned object now claims to hold 0x40000000 int elements but only has 4 bytes allocated, enabling massive heap buffer overflow when consumers index into the array.

commit_message:
Add negative bound check for row and column pooling_sequence in FractionalAvgPoolGrad op to avoid out of bound heap access

PiperOrigin-RevId: 413837346
Change-Id: I2b86034101df31bee161abcb781755e236c7bccd
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalAvgPoolGrad` does not consider cases where the input tensors are invalid allowing an attacker to read from outside of bounds of heap. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: There is no validation that out_batch == in_batch or out_depth == in_depth. The in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat has dimensions (in_depth, in_cols*in_rows*in_batch), but the loop iterates d up to out_depth and b up to out_batch. An attacker can supply an out_backprop tensor with a larger depth or batch dimension than specified in orig_input_tensor_shape, causing out-of-bounds reads/writes on the in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat and out_backprop_mat matrices.

code_snippets:
const double out_backprop_element = static_cast<double>(
                    out_backprop_mat.coeffRef(d, out_index));

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads orig_input_tensor_shape and extracts in_batch, in_rows, in_cols, in_depth from user-controlled input.
The function reads out_backprop dimensions. These are independent of orig_input_tensor_shape and are not validated against in_* dimensions.
in_backprop_tensor_temp is allocated with shape from orig_input_tensor_shape (in_shape), so it has in_depth*in_rows*in_cols*in_batch = 1*2*2*1 = 4 doubles.
The inner loop iterates d from 0 to out_depth-1 = 99. It accesses in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat.coeffRef(d, in_index) where d can be up to 99, but the matrix only has 1 row (in_depth=1).
When d >= 1 (i.e., d=1..99), coeffRef(d, in_index) accesses memory beyond the allocated buffer, causing out-of-bounds read and write.

state.value: 1 to 99 (out of bounds, only row index 0 is valid)
points to memory outside allocated tensor buffer

commit_message:
crypto: digest: use crypto_memneq()

When verifying a digest it is important not to leak timing information
through memcmp(). Use crypto_memneq() instead.

Signed-off-by: Sascha Hauer <s.hauer@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: crypto/digest.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because memcmp is used during digest verification.

Found by F. Çelik.
cve_desc: In Deark before v1.5.8, a specially crafted input file can cause a division by zero in (src/fmtutil.c) because of the value of pixelsize.

commit_message:
pict,macrsrc: Fixed a bug that could cause division by 0

Found by F. Çelik.
cve_desc: In Deark before v1.5.8, a specially crafted input file can cause a division by zero in (src/fmtutil.c) because of the value of pixelsize.

commit_message: add assert for addition overflow on queue creation (#225)
cve_desc: The kernel in Amazon Web Services FreeRTOS before 10.4.3 has an integer overflow in queue.c for queue creation.

code_snippets: pxNewQueue = ( Queue_t * ) pvPortMalloc( sizeof( Queue_t ) + xQueueSizeInBytes );
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the addition `sizeof(Queue_t) + xQueueSizeInBytes` passed to pvPortMalloc. While the multiplication overflow for `uxQueueLength * uxItemSize` is checked via configASSERT, the subsequent addition of `sizeof(Queue_t)` to `xQueueSizeInBytes` is not checked. If `xQueueSizeInBytes` is large enough (close to SIZE_MAX), the addition wraps around, causing a much smaller allocation. The queue then operates as if it has space for `uxQueueLength * uxItemSize` bytes, leading to heap buffer overflows when items are enqueued.

code_snippets: pxNewQueue = ( Queue_t * ) pvPortMalloc( sizeof( Queue_t ) + xQueueSizeInBytes );

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with uxQueueLength and uxItemSize whose product is valid (passes the multiplication overflow check) but is close to SIZE_MAX.
xQueueSizeInBytes is computed as uxQueueLength * uxItemSize. The multiplication overflow assert passes because uxItemSize is 1.
pvPortMalloc is called with sizeof(Queue_t) + xQueueSizeInBytes. Since xQueueSizeInBytes is SIZE_MAX-1, adding sizeof(Queue_t) (e.g. 80 bytes) wraps around to a small value like 78.

state.value: wraps to approximately sizeof(Queue_t) - 2, a very small value

step_by_step_execution.description: pvPortMalloc allocates a tiny buffer. pxNewQueue is not NULL. prvInitialiseNewQueue is called with the original uxQueueLength and uxItemSize, configuring the queue to believe it has space for SIZE_MAX-1 bytes of item storage in a buffer that is only a few bytes.

state.value: pointer to a small heap allocation

step_by_step_execution.description: Subsequent enqueue operations write item data past the end of the allocated heap buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message: fixed #1897
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the gf_text_get_utf8_line function in load_text.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

vulnerabilities.description: Stack buffer overflow in szLineConv. When unicode_type is 0, each non-ASCII input byte whose next byte is not a valid UTF-8 continuation byte produces TWO bytes in szLineConv (expansion from 1 to 2 bytes). The szLineConv buffer is fixed at 2048 bytes, but szLine (and thus the input) can be up to lineSize bytes. If lineSize > 2048 or even if the input has more than ~1024 such non-ASCII bytes, the index j will exceed 2048, causing an out-of-bounds write on the stack.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with lineSize >= 4096 and unicode_type == 0. szLine is zeroed and filled by gf_fgets.
gf_fgets reads a long line of ~3000 bytes, many of which have the high bit set (0x80-0xBF range), where the following byte does NOT have bits 0xc0 == 0x80 (i.e., not valid UTF-8 continuation).

state.value: ~2000+ bytes with high bit set, each followed by an ASCII byte

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop enters the branch for non-UTF8 (win-CP) conversion. For each such byte, two bytes are written to szLineConv: first the 0xc0|... byte, then the modified original byte.

state.value: incrementing roughly 2x as fast as i for non-ASCII bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: After processing ~1024+ such non-ASCII bytes, j exceeds 2048, and writes to szLineConv[j] go past the end of the 2048-byte stack buffer.
Stack buffer overflow occurs, potentially overwriting the return address or other stack variables.

vulnerabilities.description: After the conversion loop, strcpy copies szLineConv back into szLine without checking that the expanded content fits. If the conversion expanded the data beyond lineSize (the allocated size of szLine), this is a second overflow opportunity. Additionally, in the unicode (UTF-16) path, gf_utf8_wcstombs can write up to 2048 bytes into szLineConv which is then strcpy'd into szLine — if lineSize < 2048, this overflows szLine.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a small lineSize (e.g., 256) and unicode_type == 2 or 3 (UTF-16 path).
gf_fgets reads up to 256 bytes of UTF-16 data into szLine.

gf_utf8_wcstombs converts UTF-16 to UTF-8 into szLineConv (up to 2048 bytes). UTF-8 encoding of certain characters can use 3 bytes per UTF-16 code unit, so 128 UTF-16 chars (256 bytes) could produce up to 384 UTF-8 bytes.
strcpy(szLine, szLineConv) copies up to 384 bytes into szLine which is only 256 bytes, causing a heap/stack buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Revert "Refactor: main: substitute is_auth_req macro"

This reverts commit da79b8ba28ad4837a0fee13e5f8fb6f89fe0e24c.

authfile != authkey

Signed-off-by: Jan Friesse <jfriesse@redhat.com>
cve_desc: The authfile directive in the booth config file is ignored, preventing use of authentication in communications from node to node. As a result, nodes that do not have the correct authentication key are not prevented from communicating with other nodes in the cluster.

commit_message: class.c: clear method cache after `remove_method`.
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in mrb_vm_exec in mruby/mruby in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

commit_message: add assert for addition overflow on queue creation (#225)
cve_desc: The kernel in Amazon Web Services FreeRTOS before 10.4.3 has an integer overflow in queue.c for queue creation.

code_snippets: pxNewQueue = ( Queue_t * ) pvPortMalloc( sizeof( Queue_t ) + xQueueSizeInBytes );
vulnerabilities.description: The multiplication `uxQueueLength * uxItemSize` can overflow, and the subsequent addition `sizeof(Queue_t) + xQueueSizeInBytes` can also overflow. The overflow checks rely on `configASSERT`, which is commonly compiled out (defined as a no-op) in release/production builds. When assertions are disabled, an attacker-controlled or large uxQueueLength and uxItemSize can cause an integer overflow, resulting in a small heap allocation via pvPortMalloc. The queue is then initialized as if it has capacity for the full (non-overflowed) number of items, leading to heap buffer overflows when items are enqueued.

code_snippets: pxNewQueue = ( Queue_t * ) pvPortMalloc( sizeof( Queue_t ) + xQueueSizeInBytes );
prvInitialiseNewQueue( uxQueueLength, uxItemSize, pucQueueStorage, ucQueueType, pxNewQueue );

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with large uxQueueLength and uxItemSize values whose product exceeds the size_t range (e.g., on a 32-bit system, uxQueueLength=0x10000, uxItemSize=0x10000).
The multiplication `uxQueueLength * uxItemSize` overflows, wrapping around to a small value (e.g., 0).
configASSERT checks for multiplication and addition overflow are no-ops in release builds (configASSERT is typically defined as ((void)0)). Execution continues.
pvPortMalloc is called with `sizeof(Queue_t) + 0`, allocating only enough space for the Queue_t structure itself, with no storage space for queue items.

state.value: pointer to a heap allocation of sizeof(Queue_t) bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: pucQueueStorage is set to point just past the Queue_t structure, which is at the boundary of (or beyond) the allocated buffer.

state.value: pxNewQueue + sizeof(Queue_t), pointing past the allocated buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: prvInitialiseNewQueue is called with the original (large) uxQueueLength and uxItemSize, initializing the queue metadata to believe it can hold 0x10000 items of size 0x10000 each. Subsequent enqueue operations will write far past the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Fixes #4985: 4e+26 is outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned long' at coders/pcl.c:299 (#4986)

* fix Division by zero in XMenuWidget() of MagickCore/widget.c

* Fix memory leak in AnimateImageCommand() of MagickWand/animate.c and DisplayImageCommand() of MagickWand/display.c

* fix Division by zero in ReadEnhMetaFile() of coders/emf.c

* Resolve conflicts

* fix issue: outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned char' at coders/psd.c:1025

* fix error: 4e+26 is outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned long' at coders/pcl.c:299

Co-authored-by: zhailiangliang <zhailiangliang@loongson.cn>
cve_desc: A vulnerability was found in ImageMagick, causing an outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned long' at coders/pcl.c, when crafted or untrusted input is processed. This leads to a negative impact to application availability or other problems related to undefined behavior.

commit_message:
Fix crash in MatrixSolve when inputs have different batch dimensions.

Before, the process would crash or certain elements would be silently ignored. Now an InvalidArgument is raised.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 384844020
Change-Id: Iba44417e383bdd0e1abc4012bfca83b2377dd335
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions several TensorFlow operations are missing validation for the shapes of the tensor arguments involved in the call. Depending on the API, this can result in undefined behavior and segfault or `CHECK`-fail related crashes but in some scenarios writes and reads from heap populated arrays are also possible. We have discovered these issues internally via tooling while working on improving/testing GPU op determinism. As such, we don't have reproducers and there will be multiple fixes for these issues. These fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: fixed #2175
cve_desc: MP4Box is a component of GPAC-2.0.0, which is a widely-used third-party package on RPM Fusion. When MP4Box tries to parse a MP4 file, it calls the function `diST_box_read()` to read from video. In this function, it allocates a buffer `str` with fixed length. However, content read from `bs` is controllable by user, so is the length, which causes a buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: The loop reads bytes from the bitstream into a stack buffer `str[1024]` without checking that `i` stays within bounds. If the bitstream contains more than 1023 non-null bytes before a null terminator, `i` will exceed 1023 and cause a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A stack buffer `str` of size 1024 is declared and index `i` is initialized to 0.
The while(1) loop begins reading bytes from the bitstream into str[i]. Each non-null byte increments i.
If the bitstream contains 1024 or more non-null bytes before a null byte, i reaches 1024 and str[1024] is written, which is out of bounds of the 1024-element array (valid indices 0..1023).
Further iterations continue writing past the buffer, corrupting the stack (return address, saved registers, other local variables).

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.StagePeek vulnerability with invalid `index`.

Check that input is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445524908
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.StagePeek` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `index` is a scalar but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-5x45-qp78-g4p4

* Prevent infinite loop in scanning xml content

* Simplify scanning method

* Optimization
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in the C language. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a denial-of-service vulnerability that affects PJSIP users that consume PJSIP's XML parsing in their apps. Users are advised to update. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The attribute parsing loop dereferences scanner->curptr without checking for EOF. If a malformed XML input ends without a '>' or '/' character after a tag name, the loop condition reads past the end of the scanner buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a scanner whose buffer contains a malformed XML fragment like "<tag attr" (no closing '>' or '/')
The code passes the initial '<' check and consumes it via pj_scan_get_char, then parses the node name 'tag'.

The while loop condition checks *scanner->curptr != '>' && *scanner->curptr != '/'. Since curptr points to ' ', the loop body is entered. pj_scan_get_until_chr parses 'attr' into attr->name, advancing curptr to the end of the buffer.

state.value: at or past end of buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop condition is evaluated again, dereferencing scanner->curptr which now points past the end of the valid buffer data, causing an out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.description: Multiple locations dereference *(scanner->curptr+1) without verifying that curptr+1 is within the scanner buffer. If the scanner buffer ends exactly at a '<' character, these accesses read one byte past the buffer boundary.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a scanner whose buffer ends with '<' as the very last character (e.g., content "<tag></tag><").
The parser processes the <tag></tag> element normally, then recurses or continues to the sub-nodes while loop.

state.value: points to the final '<' which is the last byte in the buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop condition evaluates *scanner->curptr == '<' (true), then evaluates *(scanner->curptr+1) which is one byte past the end of the buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: points to last byte '<' in buffer
one byte past end of buffer (out of bounds)

commit_message: fixed #1876
cve_desc: GPAC version before commit 71460d72ec07df766dab0a4d52687529f3efcf0a (version v1.0.1 onwards) contains loop with unreachable exit condition ('infinite loop') vulnerability in ISOBMFF reader filter, isoffin_read.c. Function isoffin_process() can result in DoS by infinite loop. To exploit, the victim must open a specially crafted mp4 file.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-r374-qrwv-86hh
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library. In version 2.11.1 and prior, if incoming RTCP XR message contain block, the data field is not checked against the received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP XR. A malicious actor can send a RTCP XR message with an invalid packet size.

vulnerabilities.description: The parsing loop saves pointers to report blocks (rb_stats, rb_voip_mtc, rb_rr_time, rb_dlrr) based only on the block type field without validating that the block's declared length (rb_len) is large enough to cover the full expected structure. After the loop, these pointers are dereferenced to read many fields at offsets far beyond the report block header. An attacker can craft a packet with a valid block type but a small rb_len, causing out-of-bounds reads when the structure fields are accessed beyond the actual packet data.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function receives a crafted RTCP XR packet. The packet type check passes (pt == RTCP_XR).

state.value: e.g. 16 bytes (minimum valid XR packet with one small block)

step_by_step_execution.description: pkt_len is read from the packet header length field. The bounds check `(pkt_len + 1) > (size / 4)` passes, e.g. pkt_len=3, size=16, so (3+1) <= (16/4)=4.
rb_hdr is initialized to point to rtcp_xr->buf (first report block). The while loop condition `rb_hdr < pkt + pkt_len` is true since rb_hdr points within the packet.

state.value: points to offset 4 bytes into pkt (start of first report block in buf)

step_by_step_execution.description: rb_len is read from the attacker-controlled rb_hdr->length field. Attacker sets rb_len=1 (very small) but sets rb_hdr->bt = BT_STATS.

state.value: 1 (only 1 word of content, far less than sizeof(pjmedia_rtcp_xr_rb_stats))

step_by_step_execution.description: Since rb_len != 0, the switch is entered. BT_STATS case sets rb_stats = rb_hdr. No validation that rb_len covers the full pjmedia_rtcp_xr_rb_stats structure.

state.value: points to rb_hdr, which is near end of the valid 16-byte packet

step_by_step_execution.description: rb_hdr advances by rb_len+1=2 words (8 bytes). Now rb_hdr >= pkt + pkt_len, so the while loop exits.

state.value: past end of packet, loop terminates

step_by_step_execution.description: After the loop, since rb_stats is non-NULL, the code dereferences rb_stats to read fields like begin_seq, end_seq, lost, dup, jitter_min, jitter_max, etc. These fields are at offsets well beyond the 1 word of actual report block content, reading past the end of the packet buffer.

state.value: out-of-bounds read beyond the allocated packet buffer
out-of-bounds read beyond the allocated packet buffer

commit_message: fixed #1905
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a null pointer dereference vulnerability in the mpgviddmx_process function in reframe_mpgvid.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service. This vulnerability is possibly due to an incomplete fix for CVE-2021-40566.

vulnerabilities.description: When ctx->bytes_in_header is small (e.g., 1 or 2), the code copies `8 - ctx->bytes_in_header` bytes from `start`, which can be 6 or 7 bytes. However, `remain` (the valid bytes available at `start`) is only guaranteed to be >= 5 (due to the `if (remain<5)` break check). This leads to a heap buffer over-read when `remain` is between 5 and `8 - ctx->bytes_in_header - 1`.

step_by_step_execution.description: In a previous call to mpgviddmx_process, the while loop ends with remain < 5 (e.g., remain = 1), storing bytes and setting bytes_in_header.

state.value: 0 (not set, since this path uses bytes_in_header not hdr_store_size)

step_by_step_execution.description: A new call to mpgviddmx_process occurs. A new packet is obtained with a small pck_size (e.g., 5 bytes). Since ctx->hdr_store_size is 0, the hdr_store aggregation block is skipped.
The while loop is entered. remain (5) >= 5, so the early break for insufficient bytes is not taken.
ctx->bytes_in_header is non-zero (1), so we enter the bytes_in_header block. The code copies 8 - ctx->bytes_in_header = 7 bytes from start into hdr_store.
memcpy reads 7 bytes from start, but start only has 5 valid bytes. This reads 2 bytes beyond the allocated packet data buffer.

commit_message: Fix overflow of triggered topics
cve_desc: uWebSockets 18.11.0 and 18.12.0 has a stack-based buffer overflow in uWS::TopicTree::trimTree (called from uWS::TopicTree::unsubscribeAll). NOTE: the vendor's position is that this is "a minor issue or not even an issue at all" because the developer of an application (that uses uWebSockets) should not be allowing the large number of triggered topics to accumulate

commit_message:
Add negative bound check for row and column pooling_sequence in FractionalAvgPoolGrad op to avoid out of bound heap access

PiperOrigin-RevId: 413837346
Change-Id: I2b86034101df31bee161abcb781755e236c7bccd
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalAvgPoolGrad` does not consider cases where the input tensors are invalid allowing an attacker to read from outside of bounds of heap. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vulnerabilities.description: The values read from row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor (in_row_start, in_col_start) are attacker-controlled and never validated to be non-negative or within the bounds of [0, in_rows) and [0, in_cols). While in_row_end/in_col_end are clamped to the max index, the start values are not. A negative in_row_start or in_col_start leads to a negative in_index, causing an out-of-bounds read/write on in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat. Similarly, a very large start value could also produce an out-of-bounds in_index.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads orig_input_tensor_shape, out_backprop, row_seq_tensor, and col_seq_tensor from context inputs.
The code validates row_seq_tensor.NumElements() > out_rows and col_seq_tensor.NumElements() > out_cols, but does NOT validate individual element values.
in_backprop_tensor_temp is allocated with shape in_shape derived from orig_input_tensor_shape. The matrix map covers in_depth x (in_cols * in_rows * in_batch) elements.

state.value: in_depth x (in_cols * in_rows * in_batch), e.g. valid indices 0..99

step_by_step_execution.description: In the nested loop, for r=0, in_row_start = row_seq_tensor_flat(0) which is -5 (attacker-controlled).
The inner loop iterates in_r from -5 to 1. For in_r=-5, in_index = (0*10 + (-5))*10 + in_c, which is a large negative number.
coeffRef(d, in_index) accesses memory at a negative column offset, causing an out-of-bounds write on the backing buffer of in_backprop_tensor_temp.

vulnerabilities.description: If the attacker provides sequence tensor values such that in_row_start equals in_row_end+1 (e.g., in_row_start=in_max_row_index+1 and in_row_end is clamped to in_max_row_index, but via overlapping_=true where in_row_end=row_seq_tensor_flat(r+1) which equals in_row_start), num_elements_in_pooling_cell could be zero, causing a floating-point division by zero. More critically, if in_row_start > in_row_end but in_col_start <= in_col_end (or vice-versa), the product can be zero or negative while one of the loop pairs still executes under certain integer overflow conditions.

step_by_step_execution.description: Attacker crafts row_seq_tensor so that row_seq_tensor_flat(r) > in_max_row_index while col_seq values are valid.

num_elements_in_pooling_cell is computed as (9-15+1)*(in_col_end-in_col_start+1) = -5 * positive = negative. But the row loop (in_r from 15 to 9) doesn't execute, so no actual division occurs in this specific case.

However, if overlapping_=true and row_seq_tensor_flat(r)=row_seq_tensor_flat(r+1)=V where V <= in_max_row_index, then in_row_start=V, in_row_end=V, giving (V-V+1)=1 row. But if both start and end are equal and in_col has same scenario, we get 1*1=1. For the zero case: if in_row_start=in_max_row_index+1 and overlapping, in_row_end=row_seq_tensor_flat(r+1) clamped to in_max_row_index, num_elements=(in_max_row_index - (in_max_row_index+1) + 1)*col_factor = 0, and the loop from in_max_row_index+1 to in_max_row_index doesn't execute.

state.value: 0 (but inner loop doesn't execute, so division not reached)

commit_message: fixed #1876
cve_desc: GPAC version before commit 71460d72ec07df766dab0a4d52687529f3efcf0a (version v1.0.1 onwards) contains loop with unreachable exit condition ('infinite loop') vulnerability in ISOBMFF reader filter, isoffin_read.c. Function isoffin_process() can result in DoS by infinite loop. To exploit, the victim must open a specially crafted mp4 file.

commit_message: fixed #2175
cve_desc: MP4Box is a component of GPAC-2.0.0, which is a widely-used third-party package on RPM Fusion. When MP4Box tries to parse a MP4 file, it calls the function `diST_box_read()` to read from video. In this function, it allocates a buffer `str` with fixed length. However, content read from `bs` is controllable by user, so is the length, which causes a buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: If s->size is the maximum value for its type (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF for u64), the expression (s->size+1) wraps around to 0, causing gf_malloc to allocate a very small buffer (or zero bytes). The subsequent gf_bs_read_data call then writes s->size bytes into this undersized buffer, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a GF_Box whose size field is attacker-controlled (parsed from the input file).

state.value: attacker-controlled, can be very large, e.g. 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF

step_by_step_execution.description: The allocation size is computed as (s->size + 1). When s->size is the max value, this overflows to 0.
gf_malloc(0) is called, which returns either NULL or a tiny allocation.

state.value: pointer to a 0-byte or very small heap buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: The NULL check passes (some allocators return a valid pointer for size 0).
gf_bs_read_data writes s->size bytes (a huge number) into the undersized buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

state.value: 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF bytes written to a ~0 byte buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: Additionally, p->content_script_types[s->size] = 0 writes a null byte far out of bounds.

commit_message:
Make SparseFillEmptyRows validate that the length of `values` must be equal to the number of index tuples.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 399969549
Change-Id: I3c2f2ca1c1d2cc88bb5951c6958b38c16e9436c8
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `SparseFillEmptyRows` can be made to trigger a heap OOB access. This occurs whenever the size of `indices` does not match the size of `values`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Properly validate sparse tensor in `SparseTensorSliceDataset`

Existing validation was incomplete.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 415375048
Change-Id: I14cd18f29ede73286f3ffac35171bd15828997e9
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseTensorSliceDataset` has an undefined behavior: under certain condition it can be made to dereference a `nullptr` value. The 3 input arguments to `SparseTensorSliceDataset` represent a sparse tensor. However, there are some preconditions that these arguments must satisfy but these are not validated in the implementation. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when indices tensor has shape [N, 0] (N > 0) and values has shape [0]. The emptiness check uses NumElements() (which is 0 for both), allowing it to pass, but the loop iterates dim_size(0) = N times, accessing column 0 of a matrix that has 0 columns.

step_by_step_execution.description: The 'indices' input tensor is obtained. An attacker provides a tensor with shape [N, 0] where N > 0 (e.g., [5, 0]).
The 'values' input tensor is obtained with shape [0].
The 'dense_shape' input is obtained as a valid vector.
TensorShapeUtils::IsMatrix(indices->shape()) check passes because [N, 0] is a 2D tensor (a matrix).
num_indices and num_values are computed. Since indices is [N,0], NumElements() = N*0 = 0. Since values is [0], NumElements() = 0.
The emptiness check (num_indices == 0 || num_values == 0) is true. The inner check num_indices == num_values (0 == 0) passes.
The values vector shape check and dense_shape vector shape check both pass.
The loop iterates from i=0 to i < indices->dim_size(0). dim_size(0) = N > 0, so the loop body executes.
indices->matrix<int64_t>()(i, 0) is accessed. The matrix has 0 columns, so accessing column 0 is an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-p6g5-v97c-w5q4

* Prevent heap buffer overflow when parsing DNS packets

* Make sure packet parsing doesn't advance beyond max/end

* Update checks

* Remove  check

Co-authored-by: sauwming <ming@teluu.com>
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. A buffer overflow vulnerability in versions 2.12 and prior affects applications that use PJSIP DNS resolution. It doesn't affect PJSIP users who utilize an external resolver. This vulnerability is related to CVE-2023-27585. The difference is that this issue is in parsing the query record `parse_rr()`, while the issue in CVE-2023-27585 is in `parse_query()`. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. A workaround is to disable DNS resolution in PJSIP config (by setting `nameserver_count` to zero) or use an external resolver instead.

vulnerabilities.description: After parsing the DNS name, the code reads 4 bytes (2 for type, 2 for class) without verifying that `start + name_part_len + 4 <= max`. If the packet is truncated such that the name is valid but the remaining data is fewer than 4 bytes, the `pj_memcpy` calls will read beyond the `max` boundary, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: get_name_len is called to determine name_part_len and name_len. It validates the name portion fits within (start, max), but does not check for 4 additional bytes after the name.

state.value: length of encoded name portion in the packet (validated to be within bounds)
pointer to end of valid packet data

step_by_step_execution.description: Memory is allocated and get_name extracts the DNS name successfully.
p is set to start + name_part_len, pointing just past the name in the packet.

state.value: start + name_part_len (could be close to or at max)

step_by_step_execution.description: pj_memcpy(&q->type, p, 2) reads 2 bytes from p without checking p + 2 <= max. If the packet is truncated, this reads out of bounds.

state.value: start + name_part_len, potentially at or beyond max - 1

step_by_step_execution.description: p is advanced by 2, then pj_memcpy(&q->dnsclass, p, 2) reads another 2 bytes without a bounds check, potentially reading further out of bounds.

commit_message:
Validate and require subkey binding signatures on PGP public keys

All subkeys must be followed by a binding signature by the primary key
as per the OpenPGP RFC, enforce the presence and validity in the parser.

The implementation is as kludgey as they come to work around our
simple-minded parser structure without touching API, to maximise
backportability. Store all the raw packets internally as we decode them
to be able to access previous elements at will, needed to validate ordering
and access the actual data. Add testcases for manipulated keys whose
import previously would succeed.

Depends on the two previous commits:
7b399fcb8f52566e6f3b4327197a85facd08db91 and
236b802a4aa48711823a191d1b7f753c82a89ec5

Fixes CVE-2021-3521.
cve_desc: There is a flaw in RPM's signature functionality. OpenPGP subkeys are associated with a primary key via a "binding signature." RPM does not check the binding signature of subkeys prior to importing them. If an attacker is able to add or socially engineer another party to add a malicious subkey to a legitimate public key, RPM could wrongly trust a malicious signature. The greatest impact of this flaw is to data integrity. To exploit this flaw, an attacker must either compromise an RPM repository or convince an administrator to install an untrusted RPM or public key. It is strongly recommended to only use RPMs and public keys from trusted sources.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-5x45-qp78-g4p4

* Prevent infinite loop in scanning xml content

* Simplify scanning method

* Optimization
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in the C language. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a denial-of-service vulnerability that affects PJSIP users that consume PJSIP's XML parsing in their apps. Users are advised to update. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: Multiple locations dereference scanner->curptr+1 without first verifying that at least one more byte is available in the scanner buffer. If scanner->curptr points to the very last valid character in the buffer (e.g. a '<' at the end of a truncated input), accessing *(scanner->curptr+1) reads one byte past the end of the valid buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a scanner whose input buffer contains a single '<' character (or the scanner is positioned at the last byte '<' in the buffer).
The first check passes (*scanner->curptr == '<'), so execution continues to the PI check on the same line.
The code accesses *(scanner->curptr+1) to compare against '?'. Since curptr points to the last valid byte in the buffer, curptr+1 is one byte past the end, causing an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: points past the end of the valid scanner buffer

vulnerabilities.description: In the attribute parsing loop, if the scanner reaches EOF without encountering '>' or '/', the while loop condition dereferences scanner->curptr which may point past the end of valid data. Additionally, if pj_scan_get_until_chr finds no separator, the loop could iterate without advancing the scanner position, leading to an infinite loop, or if the attribute name scanning consumes all remaining input, subsequent *scanner->curptr dereferences are out-of-bounds reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input is a malformed XML tag like '<foo bar' (no closing '>' or '/' before end of input). The scanner processes past '<' and the node name 'foo'.
The while loop condition checks *scanner->curptr != '>' && *scanner->curptr != '/'. The current character is a space, so the loop body executes.
pj_scan_get_until_chr scans 'bar' as the attribute name, advancing curptr to end of buffer.

state.value: at or past end of buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop re-evaluates its condition, dereferencing scanner->curptr which now points past the end of the valid buffer data, resulting in an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: points past end of valid buffer

commit_message: vm.c: check if target_class is NULL (when prepended).
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in mrb_obj_is_kind_of in in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. # Impact: Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized. It is then passed to 'prepare_missing()' when a method is not found, and later checked with 'mrb_proc_p(blk)' after a C function method call returns. Reading an uninitialized stack variable leads to undefined behavior and could cause crashes or incorrect control flow if the garbage stack value happens to look like a proc object.

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution enters the OP_SEND or OP_SENDB handler (or arrives via L_SEND_SYM / L_SENDB_SYM goto targets).

state.value: OP_SEND or OP_SENDB or related opcode

step_by_step_execution.description: Local variables 'recv' and 'blk' are declared on the stack. 'blk' is never assigned a value.
If the method is not found (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m) is true), 'blk' is passed as an argument to prepare_missing(), which reads uninitialized memory.

If the method is a C function and returns successfully, 'blk' is checked with mrb_proc_p(blk). Since 'blk' contains garbage, the type check may incorrectly pass, leading to mrb_proc_ptr() being called on an invalid value.

state.value: may return true if garbage has MRB_TT_PROC type tag

vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SUPER handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized before being passed to prepare_missing() when the super method is not found. This reads uninitialized stack memory.

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &b, blk, 1);

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution enters the OP_SUPER handler. Local variables 'recv' and 'blk' are declared.
recv is assigned from regs[0], but blk is never assigned.
If mrb_method_search_vm fails to find the super method (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m) is true), prepare_missing is called with the uninitialized blk value.

commit_message: fixed #1890
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the gf_list_del function in list.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: The 4-bit fields offset_size, length_size, base_offset_size, and index_size are read from the bitstream without validation. Their valid values per the ISO spec are 0, 4, or 8 (bytes), but an attacker can set them to any value from 0 to 15. When multiplied by 8, these produce bit counts up to 120 passed to gf_bs_read_int(). Since gf_bs_read_int typically returns a 64-bit value, requesting 120 bits causes a read beyond the expected bounds—the function will read far more data from the bitstream than the ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE check accounted for if the internal read exceeds 64 bits, or can cause undefined behavior in the bit-reading implementation.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the 4-bit offset_size, length_size, and base_offset_size fields from the bitstream. An attacker crafts a file where these are set to large values (e.g., 15).
item_count is read; attacker sets it to at least 1 so the loop executes.
Inside the loop, ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE(ptr, (2 + ptr->base_offset_size)) checks that the box has at least 2 + 15 = 17 bytes remaining. If the crafted box is large enough, this check passes.

gf_bs_read_int(bs, 8*ptr->base_offset_size) is called with 8*15 = 120 bits. The function attempts to read 120 bits (15 bytes) from the bitstream into a 64-bit return value, causing either truncation, undefined behavior, or over-read depending on implementation.

For extent entries, ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE(ptr, (ptr->offset_size+ptr->length_size)) checks for 15+15 = 30 bytes. If passing, gf_bs_read_int(bs, 120) is called twice—once for extent_offset and once for extent_length—each requesting 120 bits to be read into a 64-bit value.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When a user does not supply arguments that determine a valid sparse tensor, `tf.raw_ops.SparseTensorSliceDataset` implementation can be made to dereference a null pointer. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L240-L251) has some argument validation but fails to consider the case when either `indices` or `values` are provided for an empty sparse tensor when the other is not. If `indices` is empty, then [code that performs validation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L260-L261) (i.e., checking that the indices are monotonically increasing) results in a null pointer dereference. If `indices` as provided by the user is empty, then `indices` in the C++ code above is backed by an empty `std::vector`, hence calling `indices->dim_size(0)` results in null pointer dereferencing (same as calling `std::vector::at()` on an empty vector). We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 02cc160e29d20631de3859c6653184e3f876b9d7. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when `indices` is a matrix with shape [N, 0] (N > 0, 0 columns). The code checks that `indices` is a matrix (rank 2) and that if num_indices or num_values is 0 the other must also be 0. However, a matrix of shape [N, 0] has NumElements() == 0, so the emptiness check passes. The loop then iterates `indices->dim_size(0)` = N times and accesses column 0, which does not exist, causing an out-of-bounds memory read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `indices` tensor is obtained from context input. Attacker provides an indices tensor with shape [N, 0] where N > 0 (e.g., [5, 0]).
The `values` tensor is obtained. Attacker provides a values tensor with shape [0] (empty vector).
IsMatrix check passes because indices has rank 2.
num_indices and num_values are computed. Since indices has shape [5, 0], NumElements is 5*0 = 0. Values has shape [0], so NumElements is 0.
The emptiness check `num_indices == 0 || num_values == 0` is true, and `num_indices == num_values` (both 0) is also true, so the check passes.
IsVector checks for values and dense_shape pass.
The loop condition `i < indices->dim_size(0)` evaluates to `i < 5`, so the loop body executes.
Inside the loop, `indices->matrix<int64>()(i, 0)` accesses column 0 of a matrix with 0 columns. This is an out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.description: php_base64_decode can return NULL on failure (e.g., invalid base64 input). The return value is used directly in ZVAL_STR without a NULL check, resulting in a NULL zend_string pointer being stored. Subsequent operations on ent1->data (e.g., __wakeup call path, hash insertion, zval_ptr_dtor) will dereference this NULL pointer, leading to a crash or potentially exploitable condition.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with an EL_BINARY closing tag name. stack->top > 0 so execution continues.
The name matches EL_BINARY (and other element names in the condition), so the main branch is entered. wddx_stack_top retrieves ent1.

state.value: A zval of type IS_STRING containing invalid base64 data

step_by_step_execution.description: Z_TYPE(ent1->data) is not IS_UNDEF, so the early return is skipped.
The name matches EL_BINARY. php_base64_decode is called with the invalid base64 string data. It returns NULL on decode failure.
zval_ptr_dtor frees the old string in ent1->data. Then ZVAL_STR sets ent1->data to a string zval with a NULL zend_string pointer.

state.value: zval of type IS_STRING with a NULL zend_string pointer

step_by_step_execution.description: Subsequent code (e.g., zval_ptr_dtor, hash insertion, or any access to ent1->data's string value) will dereference the NULL zend_string pointer, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
Fix potential divide by zero error when executing FractionalMaxPool, when pooling ratio is higher than input size for a particular dimension.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 412151722
Change-Id: I06e57cbb8eca43816eff79eac264fa7aae8f7163
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalMaxPool` can be made to crash a TensorFlow process via a division by 0. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
ConstEigenMatrixMap in_mat(tensor_in.flat<T>().data(), input_size[3],
                               input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]);

EigenMatrixMap out_mat(output_tensor->flat<T>().data(), output_size[3],
                           output_size[2] * output_size[1] * output_size[0]);
vulnerabilities.description: The products `input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]` and `output_size[2] * output_size[1] * output_size[0]` are computed as `int * int * int` (since input_size and output_size are `std::vector<int>`). These can overflow for large tensor dimensions, resulting in a truncated/incorrect column count for the Eigen matrix map. Subsequent column accesses using correct int64_t offsets would then read/write out of bounds of the actual tensor buffer.

code_snippets:
ConstEigenMatrixMap in_mat(tensor_in.flat<T>().data(), input_size[3],
                               input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]);

step_by_step_execution.description: A 4D input tensor is provided with large dimensions, e.g. shape [2, 2^16, 2^16, 1]. The tensor has 2 * 65536 * 65536 * 1 = 2^33 elements.
output_size is computed by dividing input_size by pooling_ratio. With pooling_ratio ~1.0 for each dim, output_size is similar to input_size.

The Eigen matrix map for the input is constructed. The column count is computed as `input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]` = `65536 * 65536 * 2`. This is an `int` multiplication: 65536 * 65536 = 2^32, which overflows a 32-bit int to 0, then 0 * 2 = 0.
Similarly, the output Eigen matrix map is constructed with an overflowed column count.

state.value: 0 or small incorrect value due to int overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: In the nested loop, in_offset and out_offset are computed as int64_t values that can be large and correct, but the Eigen maps were created with incorrect (overflowed) dimensions. Accessing `in_mat.col(in_offset)` or `out_mat.col(out_offset)` with an offset larger than the declared number of columns leads to out-of-bounds memory read/write.

state.value: large int64_t value, potentially > overflowed column count
large int64_t value, potentially > overflowed column count

vulnerabilities.description: The check `DCHECK_GT(output_size[i], 0)` is a debug-only assertion that is compiled away in release/optimized builds. If a pooling_ratio is very large relative to the input dimension, output_size[i] becomes 0. This leads to allocating a zero-sized output tensor and constructing Eigen maps with 0 columns, but GeneratePoolingSequence may still return sequences that cause the inner loops to compute offsets and access memory out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input tensor has small dimensions, e.g. shape [1, 1, 1, 1], and pooling_ratio_ values are large enough that floor(1 / ratio) = 0 for height or width.
output_size[1] is computed as floor(1 / 2.0) = 0. DCHECK_GT is a no-op in release builds.
GeneratePoolingSequence is called with input_size=1, output_size=0 which may produce unexpected sequences. The output tensor is allocated with a 0 dimension.

The Eigen output map out_mat is created with 0 columns. If the pooling sequence has entries, the loop computes out_offset values and calls out_mat.col(out_offset) on a zero-column matrix, causing an out-of-bounds write.

commit_message:
Secure tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2

Validate size and shape of min_range and max_range
Ensure axis is within input dims limits

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387232799
Change-Id: I36975281f7b5758e9e31a8dcc73fe610ef456318
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2`, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/quantize_op.cc#L59) has some validation but does not check that `min_range` and `max_range` both have the same non-zero number of elements. If `axis` is provided (i.e., not `-1`), then validation should check that it is a value in range for the rank of `input` tensor and then the lengths of `min_range` and `max_range` inputs match the `axis` dimension of the `input` tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 6da6620efad397c85493b8f8667b821403516708. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
avformat/adtsenc: return value check for init_get_bits in adts_decode_extradata

As the second argument for init_get_bits (buf) can be crafted, a return value check for this function call is necessary.
'buf' is  part of  'AVPacket pkt'.
replace init_get_bits with init_get_bits8.

Signed-off-by: Michael Niedermayer <michael@niedermayer.cc>
cve_desc: adts_decode_extradata in libavformat/adtsenc.c in FFmpeg 4.4 does not check the init_get_bits return value, which is a necessary step because the second argument to init_get_bits can be crafted.
