commit_message: Prevent malformed upload path causing arbitrary write (#1174)
cve_desc: This affects the package drogonframework/drogon before 1.7.5. The unsafe handling of file names during upload using HttpFile::save() method may enable attackers to write files to arbitrary locations outside the designated target folder.

vulnerabilities.description: The member variable `fileName_` (typically derived from user-supplied multipart form data, e.g., the Content-Disposition filename) is used directly in constructing the final file path without sanitization. An attacker can supply a filename like `../../etc/cron.d/malicious` to traverse outside the intended upload directory and write to arbitrary locations on the filesystem.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a valid `path` argument, e.g., "uploads".
fileName_ is checked to be non-empty. Assume it was set from an HTTP multipart upload with a malicious filename.
fsPath is constructed. Since "uploads" is not absolute and has no parent path, it gets prefixed with the upload path, e.g., "/var/www/uploads".
fsFileName is constructed from the unsanitized fileName_ containing path traversal sequences.
The directory /var/www/uploads is created if it doesn't exist.
saveTo is called with fsPath / fsFileName, which resolves to a path outside the intended directory.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.EditDistance vulnerability with negative indices.

Check that indices are non-negative. Fix several identical code sites.
Clean up grammar in error message.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445442017
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.EditDistance` has incomplete validation. Users can pass negative values to cause a segmentation fault based denial of service. In multiple places throughout the code, one may compute an index for a write operation. However, the existing validation only checks against the upper bound of the array. Hence, it is possible to write before the array by massaging the input to generate negative values for `loc`. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: When `truth_st_shape.dims()` is 1, `group_dims` will have size 0, the loop building `output_shape` won't execute, `output_shape.dims()` will be 0, and `output_strides` will be an empty vector. The subsequent access `output_strides[output_shape.dims() - 1]` computes `output_strides[-1]` (which wraps to a huge index as size_t), resulting in an out-of-bounds write on the heap.

step_by_step_execution.description: Attacker provides truth_shape tensor with a single element, representing a 1-dimensional sparse tensor.
truth_st_shape is constructed with 1 dimension.
group_dims is created with size truth_st_shape.dims() - 1 = 0.
The for loop `for (int d = 0; d < group_dims.size(); ++d)` does not execute, so output_shape has 0 dimensions.
output_elements = output_shape.num_elements() returns 1 for a scalar shape, passing the > 0 check.
output_strides is created as an empty vector of size 0.
output_strides[output_shape.dims() - 1] = output_strides[-1] = output_strides[0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF] is an out-of-bounds heap write of value 1.

commit_message:
Fix for bug #72790 and bug #72799

(cherry picked from commit a14fdb9746262549bbbb96abb87338bacd147e1b)

Conflicts:
	ext/wddx/wddx.c
cve_desc: ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid wddxPacket XML document that is mishandled in a wddx_deserialize call, as demonstrated by a stray element inside a boolean element, leading to incorrect pop processing.

vulnerabilities.description: If php_base64_decode returns NULL (e.g., due to invalid base64 input in a <binary> element), the code unconditionally passes NULL to ZVAL_STR, creating a zval with a NULL zend_string pointer. Subsequent operations on this zval (such as the hash insertion further down, or any string access) will dereference a NULL pointer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The XML parser encounters a closing </binary> tag and calls php_wddx_pop_element. stack->top > 0 so we proceed.
The element name matches EL_BINARY, so we enter the main branch and retrieve ent1 from the stack top.

state.value: A zval of type IS_STRING containing invalid base64 data (e.g., non-base64 characters)

step_by_step_execution.description: Z_TYPE(ent1->data) is IS_STRING (not IS_UNDEF), so we do not return early.
The element name matches EL_BINARY, so php_base64_decode is called with the invalid base64 string. It returns NULL because decoding fails.
zval_ptr_dtor(&ent1->data) destroys the old string value, then ZVAL_STR(&ent1->data, new_str) sets ent1->data to a zval with a NULL zend_string pointer.

Execution continues; if stack->top > 1, the code accesses ent2 and may attempt to insert ent1->data into a hash table. Operations on the NULL-string zval (e.g., zend_symtable_str_update or zend_hash_next_index_insert) will dereference the NULL pointer, causing a crash or potentially exploitable behavior.

state.value: zval with NULL zend_string pointer, used in hash insert or other operations

commit_message:
Fix potential divide by zero error when executing FractionalMaxPool, when pooling ratio is higher than input size for a particular dimension.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 412151722
Change-Id: I06e57cbb8eca43816eff79eac264fa7aae8f7163
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalMaxPool` can be made to crash a TensorFlow process via a division by 0. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
ConstEigenMatrixMap in_mat(tensor_in.flat<T>().data(), input_size[3],
                               input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]);

EigenMatrixMap out_mat(output_tensor->flat<T>().data(), output_size[3],
                           output_size[2] * output_size[1] * output_size[0]);
vulnerabilities.description: The number of columns for the Eigen matrix maps is computed as a product of three `int` values (e.g., `input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]`). This multiplication can overflow a 32-bit integer when dimensions are large, resulting in the Eigen map having an incorrect (wrapped-around) column count. Subsequent `col()` accesses using correctly computed `int64_t` offsets can then read/write out-of-bounds memory relative to what the Eigen map believes its dimensions are, or the overflowed dimension itself creates an inconsistency with the actual tensor allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: The input tensor has 4 dimensions. For example, input_size could be {1, 1300, 1300, 1300} with pooling_ratio_ all >= 1.0. The check `input_size[i] >= pooling_ratio_[i]` passes.
output_size is computed as floor(input_size[i] / pooling_ratio_[i]). With ratio 1.0, output_size equals input_size.

The Eigen map column count is computed as `input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]` using int (32-bit) arithmetic. 1300 * 1300 * 1 = 1,690,000 — this fits in int. But consider input_size = {2, 1300, 1300, 1} — then 1300 * 1300 * 2 = 3,380,000 which is fine. For larger values like {1, 46341, 46341, 1}, 46341*46341*1 = ~2.15 billion which overflows int32 max (2,147,483,647).

state.value: overflows 32-bit int, wraps to a small or negative number

step_by_step_execution.description: The ConstEigenMatrixMap in_mat is constructed with the overflowed column count. The Eigen map now believes it has a much smaller (or negative) number of columns than the actual tensor data.

state.value: overflowed int value, e.g. negative or small positive

step_by_step_execution.description: Similarly, EigenMatrixMap out_mat is constructed with overflowed column count for output dimensions.

In the nested loops, in_offset and out_offset are computed as int64_t and can be large valid values (up to ~2.15 billion). When used as `in_mat.col(in_offset)` or `out_mat.col(out_offset)`, these offsets exceed the Eigen map's believed dimensions, causing out-of-bounds reads and writes on the underlying memory buffer.

commit_message: fixed #2163
cve_desc: GPAC 2.1-DEV-rev87-g053aae8-master. has a Null Pointer Dereference vulnerability in gf_isom_parse_movie_boxes_internal due to improper return value handling of GF_SKIP_BOX, which causes a Denial of Service. This vulnerability was fixed in commit 37592ad.

vulnerabilities.description: When processing a MOOV box in non-debug mode, the code iterates over tracks and dereferences trak->Media->information->sampleTable without any NULL checks. A malformed ISO file could contain a track box missing its Media, MediaInformation, or SampleTable sub-boxes, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the main parsing loop and calls gf_isom_parse_root_box to parse a top-level box from the bitstream.
The parsed box has type GF_ISOM_BOX_TYPE_MOOV, so the MOOV case is entered. mov->moov is set to the parsed MOOV box.
The code checks mov->FragmentsFlags & GF_ISOM_FRAG_READ_DEBUG is false, entering the else branch which iterates over mov->moov->trackList.
A GF_TrackBox is retrieved from the track list. In a malformed file, trak->Media or trak->Media->information or trak->Media->information->sampleTable could be NULL.

state.value: pointer to a GF_TrackBox from malformed input

step_by_step_execution.description: The code dereferences trak->Media->information->sampleTable->sampleGroups without any NULL checks, causing a NULL pointer dereference if any intermediate pointer is NULL.

vulnerabilities.description: In the MOOF cleanup code, when mov->root_ssix is non-NULL, the code mistakenly frees mov->seg_ssix instead of mov->root_ssix, then sets mov->root_ssix to NULL. This causes root_ssix to be leaked, and seg_ssix to be freed while its pointer remains dangling, leading to a potential use-after-free when seg_ssix is later accessed or freed again.

step_by_step_execution.description: A SIDX/SSIX box is parsed. When type is GF_ISOM_BOX_TYPE_SSIX in the signal_frag_bounds path, mov->seg_ssix is set to the parsed box.
At some other point, mov->root_ssix is set to a non-NULL value.
A MOOF box is parsed successfully. In the cleanup code after processing the MOOF, the condition mov->root_ssix is checked and is true.
gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_ssix) is called, freeing seg_ssix. But mov->seg_ssix pointer is NOT set to NULL. mov->root_ssix is set to NULL, but was never freed - it is leaked.

state.value: NULL (but the object it pointed to was never freed)

step_by_step_execution.description: On a subsequent iteration, if another SSIX box is encountered, the code checks 'if (mov->seg_ssix) gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_ssix)' which will attempt to free already-freed memory (use-after-free).

commit_message: fixed #2163
cve_desc: GPAC 2.1-DEV-rev87-g053aae8-master. has a Null Pointer Dereference vulnerability in gf_isom_parse_movie_boxes_internal due to improper return value handling of GF_SKIP_BOX, which causes a Denial of Service. This vulnerability was fixed in commit 37592ad.

vulnerabilities.description: When processing a MOOV box in non-debug mode, the code iterates through tracks and dereferences trak->Media->information->sampleTable without null checks. A malformed ISO file could contain a track box where Media, information, or sampleTable is NULL, leading to a null pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: A malformed ISO file is opened for reading. The parser enters the while loop and parses a top-level box.
gf_isom_parse_root_box returns a GF_ISOM_BOX_TYPE_MOOV box successfully (e >= 0).
The MOOV box is assigned to mov->moov. mov->moov->mvhd is not NULL so the early check passes.
FragmentsFlags does not have GF_ISOM_FRAG_READ_DEBUG set, so the else branch is taken to iterate tracks.
The code iterates trackList. A malformed file has a track where trak->Media is NULL or trak->Media->information is NULL or trak->Media->information->sampleTable is NULL.
The code dereferences trak->Media->information->sampleTable->sampleGroups without any null check, causing a null pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.description: After processing a MOOF box, the code checks mov->root_ssix but frees mov->seg_ssix, then sets mov->root_ssix to NULL without setting mov->seg_ssix to NULL. This leaves mov->seg_ssix as a dangling pointer (use-after-free if accessed later). Additionally, mov->root_ssix itself is never freed, resulting in a memory leak. This appears to be a copy-paste/typo bug where root_ssix should be seg_ssix or vice versa.

step_by_step_execution.description: During fragment parsing, a SIDX/SSIX box is parsed. mov->seg_ssix is set to a valid box pointer.
A MOOF box is subsequently parsed and processed.
After MOOF processing, the code checks if mov->root_ssix is non-NULL. It is, so it enters the block.
gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_ssix) frees the seg_ssix box. Then mov->root_ssix is set to NULL, but mov->seg_ssix still points to the freed memory.

state.value: NULL (but was never freed, memory leaked)

step_by_step_execution.description: In a subsequent loop iteration, if a new SSIX box is processed, the code checks 'if (mov->seg_ssix) gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_ssix)' which would call gf_isom_box_del on the dangling pointer, resulting in use-after-free.

commit_message:
Fix a null pointer exception caused by branching on uninitialized data.

This is due to not checking that the params for the quantization exists. If there is no quantization, we should not access the `.params` field.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385163909
Change-Id: I2beb8d50649b6542db224c163033fbcbaa49314f
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions all TFLite operations that use quantization can be made to use unitialized values. [For example](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/depthwise_conv.cc#L198-L200). The issue stems from the fact that `quantization.params` is only valid if `quantization.type` is different that `kTfLiteNoQuantization`. However, these checks are missing in large parts of the code. We have patched the issue in GitHub commits 537bc7c723439b9194a358f64d871dd326c18887, 4a91f2069f7145aab6ba2d8cfe41be8a110c18a5 and 8933b8a21280696ab119b63263babdb54c298538. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/pull/4986
cve_desc: A vulnerability was found in ImageMagick, causing an outside the range of representable values of type 'unsigned long' at coders/pcl.c, when crafted or untrusted input is processed. This leads to a negative impact to application availability or other problems related to undefined behavior.

commit_message: vm.c: check if target_class is NULL (when prepended).
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in mrb_obj_is_kind_of in in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. # Impact: Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized before being used. It is passed to 'prepare_missing()' and later checked with 'mrb_proc_p(blk)'. If the uninitialized stack memory happens to match the MRB_TT_PROC type tag, 'mrb_proc_ptr(blk)' will dereference a garbage pointer, leading to a potential read/write of arbitrary memory.

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution reaches OP_SEND or OP_SENDB opcode handler. Local variables 'recv' and 'blk' are declared on the stack but not initialized.
ARGUMENT_NORMALIZE is called, then recv is set from regs[a]. The variable 'blk' remains uninitialized throughout.
mrb_method_search_vm is called. If the method is not found (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m) is true), prepare_missing is called with the uninitialized 'blk' value as an argument.

After a C function method call completes successfully (MRB_METHOD_CFUNC_P(m) path), the code checks 'mrb_proc_p(blk)'. Since blk is uninitialized, if its garbage value has a type tag matching MRB_TT_PROC, the code proceeds to dereference it via mrb_proc_ptr(blk), treating arbitrary memory as a struct RProc pointer.

state.value: uninitialized; if type bits match MRB_TT_PROC, mrb_proc_ptr dereferences garbage pointer
garbage pointer derived from uninitialized blk

step_by_step_execution.description: The garbage pointer 'p' is dereferenced to access p->flags and potentially MRB_PROC_ENV(p), leading to reads and writes to arbitrary memory locations.

vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SUPER handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized before being passed to 'prepare_missing()' when method_missing is triggered. This is the same class of uninitialized variable bug as in the SEND handler.

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &b, blk, 1);

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution reaches the OP_SUPER handler. Local variables 'recv' and 'blk' are declared but blk is never assigned a value.
After validation checks pass, mrb_method_search_vm is called to look up the super method. If the method is not found (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m)), prepare_missing is invoked with the uninitialized 'blk'.
prepare_missing uses the uninitialized blk value, which could lead to treating arbitrary stack data as a valid mrb_value, potentially causing memory corruption or information disclosure.

commit_message:
Fix a null pointer exception caused by branching on uninitialized data.

This is due to not checking that the params for the quantization exists. If there is no quantization, we should not access the `.params` field.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385163909
Change-Id: I2beb8d50649b6542db224c163033fbcbaa49314f
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions all TFLite operations that use quantization can be made to use unitialized values. [For example](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/depthwise_conv.cc#L198-L200). The issue stems from the fact that `quantization.params` is only valid if `quantization.type` is different that `kTfLiteNoQuantization`. However, these checks are missing in large parts of the code. We have patched the issue in GitHub commits 537bc7c723439b9194a358f64d871dd326c18887, 4a91f2069f7145aab6ba2d8cfe41be8a110c18a5 and 8933b8a21280696ab119b63263babdb54c298538. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fix segmentation fault in shape inference logic.

When running shape functions, some functions (such as `MutableHashTableShape`)
produce extra output information in the form of a `ShapeAndType` struct.  The
shapes embedded in this struct are owned by an inference context that is
cleaned up almost immediately; if the upstream code attempts to access this
shape information, it can trigger a segfault.

`ShapeRefiner` is mitigating this for normal output shapes by cloning them
(and thus putting the newly created shape under ownership of an inference
context that will not die), but we were not doing the same for shapes and
types.  This commit fixes that by doing similar logic on output shapes and
types.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 384761124
Change-Id: I07c0c42d29dfbb55bfa13ec1f09ef825fb0a1a1d
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions when running shape functions, some functions (such as `MutableHashTableShape`) produce extra output information in the form of a `ShapeAndType` struct. The shapes embedded in this struct are owned by an inference context that is cleaned up almost immediately; if the upstream code attempts to access this shape information, it can trigger a segfault. `ShapeRefiner` is mitigating this for normal output shapes by cloning them (and thus putting the newly created shape under ownership of an inference context that will not die), but we were not doing the same for shapes and types. This commit fixes that by doing similar logic on output shapes and types. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit ee119d4a498979525046fba1c3dd3f13a039fbb1. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: Skip MNG CLIP chunk with out-of-range object IDs
cve_desc: In ImageMagick before 6.9.9-0 and 7.x before 7.0.6-1, the ReadOneMNGImage function in coders/png.c has an out-of-bounds read with the MNG CLIP chunk.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_LOOP chunk handler, `loop_level` is read directly from user-controlled data (`chunk[0]`, a byte value 0-255) and used as an index into `mng_info->loop_active[]`, `mng_info->loop_jump[]`, `mng_info->loop_count[]`, and `mng_info->loop_iteration[]` arrays without any bounds checking. If these arrays are smaller than 256 elements, an attacker can craft a MNG file to write out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads an MNG file and enters the main do-while loop to process chunks.
A chunk of type mng_LOOP is read from the file. `length` and `chunk` data are read from the blob.
The code enters the mng_LOOP handler. `loop_level` is assigned from `chunk[0]`, which is a single byte from user-controlled input (range 0-255).
Without any bounds check on `loop_level`, the code writes to `mng_info->loop_active[loop_level]`. If loop_level exceeds the array bounds, this is an out-of-bounds write.
Similarly, `mng_info->loop_jump[loop_level]`, `mng_info->loop_count[loop_level]`, and `mng_info->loop_iteration[loop_level]` are written without bounds checks, causing further out-of-bounds writes.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_ENDL chunk handler, `loop_level` is read from `chunk[0]` (user-controlled, range 0-255) and used as an index into `mng_info->loop_active[]`, `mng_info->loop_count[]`, and `mng_info->loop_iteration[]` without bounds validation, leading to out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function processes MNG chunks in a loop. A chunk of type mng_ENDL is read.
`loop_level` is assigned directly from `chunk[0]`, an attacker-controlled byte value (0-255).
The code accesses `mng_info->loop_active[loop_level]` for reading and writing without checking if `loop_level` is within the valid array bounds, causing out-of-bounds access.

Fix tf.raw_ops.DeleteSessionTensor vulnerability with invalid `handle`.

Check that `handle` input is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445228994
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.DeleteSessionTensor` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message: fixed #1659
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in GPAC version 0.8.0 and 1.0.1. There is an invalid pointer dereference in the function SetupWriters() in isomedia/isom_store.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When the allocation of writer->stsc or writer->stco fails, the function returns GF_OUT_OF_MEM directly instead of going to the 'exit' label. This leaks the current 'writer' object (allocated via GF_SAFEALLOC but not yet added to the 'writers' list), any successfully allocated sub-objects (e.g., writer->stsc when writer->stco fails), and all previously added TrackWriter objects in the 'writers' list (since CleanWriters is never called).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function iterates over tracks. For some iteration i, a TrackWriter 'writer' is allocated via GF_SAFEALLOC.
writer->stsc is allocated via gf_isom_box_new and succeeds.

state.value: valid pointer to newly allocated SampleToChunkBox

step_by_step_execution.description: writer->stco allocation via gf_isom_box_new fails, returning NULL.

The NULL check triggers and the function returns GF_OUT_OF_MEM immediately, bypassing the 'exit' label which would call CleanWriters. The current 'writer' (not yet in the list) and its writer->stsc are leaked. All previously added TrackWriter objects in 'writers' are also leaked since CleanWriters is not called.

state.value: leaked heap memory (not freed, not in writers list)
contains previously added TrackWriters that are never cleaned up

commit_message:
Fix NPE in restoring code.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388303253
Change-Id: Ia8c68568cb854bca538909a182b31a618d68ce55
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When restoring tensors via raw APIs, if the tensor name is not provided, TensorFlow can be tricked into dereferencing a null pointer. Alternatively, attackers can read memory outside the bounds of heap allocated data by providing some tensor names but not enough for a successful restoration. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/47a06f40411a69c99f381495f490536972152ac0/tensorflow/core/kernels/save_restore_tensor.cc#L158-L159) retrieves the tensor list corresponding to the `tensor_name` user controlled input and immediately retrieves the tensor at the restoration index (controlled via `preferred_shard` argument). This occurs without validating that the provided list has enough values. If the list is empty this results in dereferencing a null pointer (undefined behavior). If, however, the list has some elements, if the restoration index is outside the bounds this results in heap OOB read. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e82dce6e6bd1f36a57e08fa85af213e2b2f2622. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository gpac/gpac prior to 2.1-DEV.

commit_message:
crypto: ccp - fix resource leaks in ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd()

There are three bugs in this code:

1) If we ccp_init_data() fails for &src then we need to free aad.
   Use goto e_aad instead of goto e_ctx.
2) The label to free the &final_wa was named incorrectly as "e_tag" but
   it should have been "e_final_wa".  One error path leaked &final_wa.
3) The &tag was leaked on one error path.  In that case, I added a free
   before the goto because the resource was local to that block.

Fixes: 36cf515b9bbe ("crypto: ccp - Enable support for AES GCM on v5 CCPs")
Reported-by: "minihanshen(沈明航)" <minihanshen@tencent.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: John Allen <john.allen@amd.com>
Tested-by: John Allen <john.allen@amd.com>
Signed-off-by: Herbert Xu <herbert@gondor.apana.org.au>
cve_desc: A memory leak flaw was found in the Linux kernel in the ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd() function in drivers/crypto/ccp/ccp-ops.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption). This vulnerability is similar with the older CVE-2019-18808.

vulnerabilities.description: When decrypting (action != CCP_AES_ACTION_ENCRYPT), if aes->src_len is smaller than authsize, the subtraction of two unsigned integers wraps around to a very large value. This huge ilen is then used in scatterwalk_ffwd, ccp_init_data, and other operations, leading to out-of-bounds reads/writes on scatterlist data.

code_snippets: ret = ccp_init_data(&src, cmd_q, p_inp, ilen, AES_BLOCK_SIZE, in_place ? DMA_BIDIRECTIONAL : DMA_TO_DEVICE);

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with aes->action != CCP_AES_ACTION_ENCRYPT (decryption mode) and aes->src_len = 0, aes->authsize = 0 (defaulting authsize to 16).
authsize is computed. Since aes->authsize is 0, it defaults to AES_BLOCK_SIZE (16). The switch statement passes for value 16.
Since action is decrypt, ilen = aes->src_len - authsize = 0 - 16. Both are unsigned, so this wraps around to UINT_MAX - 15 (0xFFFFFFF0).
scatterwalk_ffwd is called with this massive ilen to compute p_tag, advancing far past the actual scatterlist buffer.

state.value: points to invalid memory past scatterlist

step_by_step_execution.description: Since ilen > 0, ccp_init_data is called with the massive ilen value, potentially causing out-of-bounds DMA mapping and memory access on the input scatterlist.

state.value: initialized with massive length, causing OOB access

commit_message:
Fix NPE in restoring code.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388303253
Change-Id: Ia8c68568cb854bca538909a182b31a618d68ce55
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When restoring tensors via raw APIs, if the tensor name is not provided, TensorFlow can be tricked into dereferencing a null pointer. Alternatively, attackers can read memory outside the bounds of heap allocated data by providing some tensor names but not enough for a successful restoration. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/47a06f40411a69c99f381495f490536972152ac0/tensorflow/core/kernels/save_restore_tensor.cc#L158-L159) retrieves the tensor list corresponding to the `tensor_name` user controlled input and immediately retrieves the tensor at the restoration index (controlled via `preferred_shard` argument). This occurs without validating that the provided list has enough values. If the list is empty this results in dereferencing a null pointer (undefined behavior). If, however, the list has some elements, if the restoration index is outside the bounds this results in heap OOB read. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e82dce6e6bd1f36a57e08fa85af213e2b2f2622. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. commit_message:
crypto: ccp - fix resource leaks in ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd()

There are three bugs in this code:

1) If we ccp_init_data() fails for &src then we need to free aad.
   Use goto e_aad instead of goto e_ctx.
2) The label to free the &final_wa was named incorrectly as "e_tag" but
   it should have been "e_final_wa".  One error path leaked &final_wa.
3) The &tag was leaked on one error path.  In that case, I added a free
   before the goto because the resource was local to that block.

Fixes: 36cf515b9bbe ("crypto: ccp - Enable support for AES GCM on v5 CCPs")
Reported-by: "minihanshen(沈明航)" <minihanshen@tencent.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: John Allen <john.allen@amd.com>
Tested-by: John Allen <john.allen@amd.com>
Signed-off-by: Herbert Xu <herbert@gondor.apana.org.au>
cve_desc: A memory leak flaw was found in the Linux kernel in the ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd() function in drivers/crypto/ccp/ccp-ops.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption). This vulnerability is similar with the older CVE-2019-18808.

vulnerabilities.description: When the AES action is decrypt, `ilen` is computed as `aes->src_len - authsize` where both are unsigned integers. There is no check that `aes->src_len >= authsize`. If `aes->src_len` is smaller than `authsize`, this subtraction wraps around to a very large unsigned value, which is then used in `scatterwalk_ffwd`, `ccp_init_data`, and other operations, leading to out-of-bounds memory access.

code_snippets: ret = ccp_init_data(&src, cmd_q, p_inp, ilen, AES_BLOCK_SIZE, in_place ? DMA_BIDIRECTIONAL : DMA_TO_DEVICE);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function validates IV, key_len, and key, all of which pass.
authsize is determined. Suppose aes->authsize is 16 (or defaults to 16).
aes->action is CCP_AES_ACTION_DECRYPT, so we enter the else branch. Suppose aes->src_len is 0 (or any value less than authsize, e.g., 4).
ilen = aes->src_len - authsize undergoes unsigned integer underflow.
scatterwalk_ffwd is called with the wrapped ilen value, attempting to walk far past the actual scatter-gather buffer boundaries.
Later, if ilen > 0 check passes (it does, since ilen is now ~4 billion), ccp_init_data is called with the huge ilen, causing out-of-bounds access on the source scatterlist.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4219: reading before the start of the line

Problem:    Reading before the start of the line.
Solution:   Check boundary before trying to read the character.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: The while loop condition checks VIM_ISWHITE before checking s > 0. When s reaches 0, the expression *(bd->textstart + s - 1) dereferences bd->textstart - 1, which is an out-of-bounds read. If that byte happens to be whitespace, s becomes negative and pnew is decremented further, causing the subsequent *pnew = NUL to write out-of-bounds (before the allocated buffer).

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with exclude_trailing_space=1 and bd->textlen > 0, where all bytes in bd->textstart[0..textlen-1] are whitespace (e.g., spaces).
Allocation of bd->startspaces + bd->endspaces + bd->textlen + 1 = 0 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 3 bytes. Buffer is filled: 0 startspaces, then 2 bytes of text copied, then 0 endspaces. pnew points to buffer + 2.

Enter trailing space removal: s = bd->textlen + bd->endspaces = 2. Loop iteration 1: VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + 1)) is true (whitespace), s > 0 is true. s decremented (assuming single-byte) to 1, pnew decremented to buffer + 1.
Loop iteration 2: VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + 0)) is true (whitespace), s > 0 is true. s decremented to 0, pnew decremented to buffer + 0.

Loop iteration 3: The condition evaluates VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + 0 - 1)) BEFORE checking s > 0. This reads bd->textstart[-1], an out-of-bounds read. If this byte happens to be whitespace, the condition is true, and s > 0 (which is false) would stop the loop due to short-circuit. However, in C, && is left-to-right, so VIM_ISWHITE is evaluated first — this is the out-of-bounds read.

If the out-of-bounds byte is whitespace (not unlikely on the stack/heap), the full condition is false because s > 0 is false, so the loop exits. *pnew = NUL writes to buffer[0], which is within bounds in this case. However, if the check order were reversed or if s could become negative through the mb_head_off arithmetic (e.g., multibyte: s = 0 - mb_head_off_result - 1 becomes negative), pnew would be decremented past the buffer start, and *pnew = NUL would be a heap buffer underflow write.

state.value: buffer + 0 or before buffer with multibyte

commit_message:
Reorganize and add more validation to MKL requantization

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387901341
Change-Id: I2515b9034c64e113db0bcec8337d30643ab0a0f1
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Division by zero occurs when both `input_requested_min_float` and `input_requested_max_float` are 0.0f. The variable `requested_min_max` is computed as `std::max(std::abs(0.0f), std::abs(0.0f))` which equals 0.0f, and is then used as a divisor in the scales computation loop.

step_by_step_execution.description: The input_requested_min and input_requested_max tensors are read from the kernel context.
requested_min_max is computed as std::max(std::abs(0.0f), std::abs(0.0f)).
The OP_REQUIRES checks pass (input is 4D, min/max vec sizes match depth). The DCHECK on input_requested_min_float < 0.0f is only a debug-mode assertion and does not protect in release builds.
In the loop computing scales, division by requested_min_max (0.0f) is performed, producing Inf or NaN, leading to undefined/unexpected behavior in subsequent MKL-DNN reorder operations.

commit_message:
Secure tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2

Validate size and shape of min_range and max_range
Ensure axis is within input dims limits

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387232799
Change-Id: I36975281f7b5758e9e31a8dcc73fe610ef456318
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2`, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/quantize_op.cc#L59) has some validation but does not check that `min_range` and `max_range` both have the same non-zero number of elements. If `axis` is provided (i.e., not `-1`), then validation should check that it is a value in range for the rank of `input` tensor and then the lengths of `min_range` and `max_range` inputs match the `axis` dimension of the `input` tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 6da6620efad397c85493b8f8667b821403516708. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The code iterates up to `num_slices` (derived from `input.dim_size(axis_)`) and indexes into `min_ranges`, `max_ranges`, `output_min_tensor`, and `output_max_tensor` without validating that these tensors have at least `num_slices` elements. An attacker can craft inputs where `input_min_range` / `input_max_range` have fewer elements than `num_slices`, causing out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: axis_ is set to a valid dimension index (e.g., axis_=1) and num_slices is computed as input.dim_size(axis_), say num_slices=10.
input_min_range and input_max_range are provided with shape e.g. {1}, containing only 1 element each. No check enforces their size matches num_slices.
minmax_shape is {1}, so output_min_tensor and output_max_tensor are allocated with shape {1} (1 element each).

The for loop iterates i from 0 to 9. On i >= 1, min_ranges(i) and max_ranges(i) read beyond the 1-element tensors (out-of-bounds read). Similarly, writes to output_min_tensor->flat<float>()(i) and output_max_tensor->flat<float>()(i) go out-of-bounds (out-of-bounds write).

vulnerabilities.description: There is no validation that axis_ is less than input.dims(). If axis_ is greater than or equal to the number of dimensions of the input tensor, input.dim_size(axis_) performs an out-of-bounds access on the tensor's shape array.

step_by_step_execution.description: axis_ is set to a value >= input.dims(), e.g. axis_=5 for a 2-D input tensor.
The condition axis_ > -1 is true, so input.dim_size(axis_) is called with axis_=5, which is out of bounds for the shape array of a 2-D tensor.

commit_message:
Fixed frame allocation from an awaited frame.

njs_function_frame_save() is used to save the awaited frame when "await"
instruction is encountered. The saving was done as a memcpy() of
existing runtime frame.

njs_function_frame_alloc() is used to alloc a new function frame, this
function tries to use a spare preallocated memory from the previous
frame first.  Previously, this function might result in "use-after-free"
when invoked from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().
Because njs_function_frame_save() left pointers to the spare memory of
the original frame which may be already free when saved frame is
restored.

The fix is to erase fields for the spare memory from the saved frame.

This closes #469 issue on Github.
cve_desc: nginx njs 0.7.2 is affected suffers from Use-after-free in njs_function_frame_alloc() when it try to invoke from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().

commit_message:
Fixed typo while calculating module path length.

The issue was introduced in 77c398f26d7e (not released yet).
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.3 was discovered to contain a stack overflow in the function njs_default_module_loader at /src/njs/src/njs_module.c. NOTE: multiple third parties dispute this report, e.g., the behavior is only found in unreleased development code that was not part of the 0.7.2, 0.7.3, or 0.7.4 release

vulnerabilities.description: When `dir` is not NULL, the `length` variable is overwritten with `dir->length` (plus an optional trailing slash). The subsequent bounds check (`length > NJS_MAX_PATH`) therefore only validates the directory portion, not the combined `dir->length + trail + info->name.length`. The function then copies both `dir` and `info->name` into the stack buffer `src[NJS_MAX_PATH + 1]`, resulting in a stack buffer overflow when their combined length exceeds NJS_MAX_PATH.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a non-NULL `dir` (e.g., length 10) and `info->name` with a large length (e.g., NJS_MAX_PATH).
`dir != NULL` branch: `length` is reassigned to `dir->length`. Assuming the last char of dir is '/', `trail` remains 0.
Bounds check: `length > NJS_MAX_PATH` evaluates to `10 > 4096`, which is false. The check passes.
First cpymem copies 10 bytes of dir into `src`. `p` now points to `src[10]`.

Second cpymem copies `info->name.length` (4096) bytes starting at `src[10]`, writing up to `src[4106]`. The buffer `src` is only `NJS_MAX_PATH + 1` (4097) bytes, so this writes 4106 - 4097 = 9 bytes past the end of the stack buffer.

commit_message:
Fixed frame allocation from an awaited frame.

njs_function_frame_save() is used to save the awaited frame when "await"
instruction is encountered. The saving was done as a memcpy() of
existing runtime frame.

njs_function_frame_alloc() is used to alloc a new function frame, this
function tries to use a spare preallocated memory from the previous
frame first.  Previously, this function might result in "use-after-free"
when invoked from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().
Because njs_function_frame_save() left pointers to the spare memory of
the original frame which may be already free when saved frame is
restored.

The fix is to erase fields for the spare memory from the saved frame.

This closes #469 issue on Github.
cve_desc: nginx njs 0.7.2 is affected suffers from Use-after-free in njs_function_frame_alloc() when it try to invoke from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.slice() with slow "this" argument.

Previously, when "this" argument was not a fast array, but the "deleted" array
was a fast array, the "deleted" array may be left in uninitialized state if
"this" argument had gaps.

This fix is to ensure that "deleted" is properly initialized.

This fixes #485 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_value_own_enumerate at src/njs_value.c.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.EditDistance vulnerability with negative indices.

Check that indices are non-negative. Fix several identical code sites.
Clean up grammar in error message.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445442017
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.EditDistance` has incomplete validation. Users can pass negative values to cause a segmentation fault based denial of service. In multiple places throughout the code, one may compute an index for a write operation. However, the existing validation only checks against the upper bound of the array. Hence, it is possible to write before the array by massaging the input to generate negative values for `loc`. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

code_snippets:
OP_REQUIRES(
            ctx, loc < output_elements,
            errors::Internal("Got an inner product ", loc,
                             " which would require in writing to outside of "
                             "the buffer for the output tensor (max elements ",
                             output_elements, ")"));
        output_t(loc) =
            gtl::LevenshteinDistance<T>(truth_seq, hypothesis_seq, cmp);
vulnerabilities.description: The bounds check on `loc` only verifies `loc < output_elements` but does not verify `loc >= 0`. Since `loc` is computed via `std::inner_product` over group indices and output strides (all `int64_t`), a malicious sparse tensor with negative indices can produce a negative `loc`. A negative `loc` passes the `loc < output_elements` check but results in an out-of-bounds write when used as an index into `output_t`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Inputs are read: hypothesis and truth sparse tensors with their indices, values, and shapes.

state.value: Contains a row with a negative index, e.g., [[-1, 0], [0, 1]]

step_by_step_execution.description: ValidateShapes is called, which checks tensor rank/shape consistency but may not reject negative index values within the index tensors.
TensorShape objects are created from the shape tensors. SparseTensor objects are created from indices, values, and shapes.
group_dims is computed as {0} (all dims except the last), output_shape is built as [2] (max of dim 0 sizes), and output_strides is computed.
During iteration, a group with g_truth = {-1} is encountered (from the negative index in truth_indices). loc is computed as inner_product({-1}, {1}, 0) = -1.
The bounds check `loc < output_elements` evaluates to `-1 < 2` which is true, so the check passes.
output_t(loc) is written with loc=-1, which is an out-of-bounds write before the beginning of the output buffer.

vulnerabilities.description: If the truth sparse tensor has a shape with only 1 dimension (dims()==1), `group_dims` will be an empty vector, `output_shape` will have 0 dimensions, and `output_strides` will be an empty vector. The subsequent access `output_strides[output_shape.dims() - 1]` becomes `output_strides[-1]`, which is an out-of-bounds memory write. If dims()==0, the `group_dims` constructor receives `(size_t)-1` causing a massive allocation attempt.

step_by_step_execution.description: Inputs are read. truth_shape is a 1-element tensor, e.g., [5], representing a 1-dimensional sparse tensor.
group_dims is constructed with size truth_st_shape.dims() - 1 = 0.
The loop building output_shape does not execute (group_dims is empty), so output_shape has 0 dimensions.
output_elements = output_shape.num_elements() returns 1 for a scalar shape, passing the >0 check.

output_strides is constructed with size 0 (empty vector). The line `output_strides[output_shape.dims() - 1] = 1` evaluates to `output_strides[-1] = 1`, which is an out-of-bounds write on an empty vector.

commit_message:
Add more validation to `RequantizationRangePerChannel`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387693946
Change-Id: Ife8dcbdb021bec4787eef6a4361dd08f17c14bd6
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable 'depth' is derived from input_max.NumElements() but is never validated against the actual inner dimension of the 'input' tensor. After transposing, chip<0>(i) indexes into the former inner dimension. If an attacker supplies an input_max tensor with more elements than the input tensor's inner dimension, the loop iterates beyond the bounds of the transposed input, causing out-of-bounds reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: depth is set to input_max.NumElements(). An attacker can craft input_max with, say, 100 elements.
input_min is checked to also have dim_size(0) == 100, which passes if the attacker also provides a matching input_min.
input.flat_inner_dims<qint32>() creates a 2D matrix view of input. If input is, e.g., shape [5, 10], the inner dimension is 10.
The loop iterates i from 0 to depth-1 (0 to 99). When i >= 10, transposed_input.chip<0>(i) accesses beyond dimension 0 of the transposed matrix (size 10), resulting in out-of-bounds memory read.

state.value: 10..99 (out of bounds for dimension of size 10)

vulnerabilities.description: If min_per_channel equals INT32_MIN (-2147483648), std::abs(INT32_MIN) is undefined behavior for signed 32-bit integers because the positive value cannot be represented. This can lead to unexpected results or program instability.

step_by_step_execution.description: The transposed_input.chip<0>(i).minimum() computes the minimum qint32 value in a channel.

state.value: -2147483648 (INT32_MIN) if any element in the channel has this value

step_by_step_execution.description: std::abs(min_per_channel) is called with INT32_MIN. Since abs(INT32_MIN) cannot be represented as int32_t (max positive is 2147483647), this is signed integer overflow, which is undefined behavior.

commit_message:
Ensure non-empty rt_nested_splits in tf.raw_ops.RaggedTensorToVariant

Ensure non-empty rt_nested_splits in tf.raw_ops.RaggedTensorToVariant

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/ragged_tensor_to_variant_op.cc#L129) has an incomplete validation of the splits values, missing the case when the argument would be empty. commit_message: fixed #1659
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in GPAC version 0.8.0 and 1.0.1. There is an invalid pointer dereference in the function SetupWriters() in isomedia/isom_store.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When `gf_isom_box_new` fails for `writer->stsc` or `writer->stco`, the function returns `GF_OUT_OF_MEM` directly instead of going to the `exit` label. This leaks the already-allocated `writer` object (and `writer->stsc` in the `stco` failure case) since `writer` has not yet been added to the `writers` list and thus `CleanWriters` cannot free it. Additionally, all previously added TrackWriter objects in the `writers` list are also leaked because `CleanWriters` is never called on the early return paths.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the for loop and iterates. On a given iteration, a valid track is found and the stbl checks pass.
GF_SAFEALLOC allocates a new TrackWriter object for `writer` successfully.
Fields of `writer` are populated (mdia, stbl, timeScale, etc.). Writer has NOT been added to `writers` list yet.

state.value: populated TrackWriter, not in writers list

step_by_step_execution.description: `gf_isom_box_new(GF_ISOM_BOX_TYPE_STSC)` is called and succeeds, assigning to `writer->stsc`.
`gf_isom_box_new` for stco/co64 is called and fails, returning NULL.

The code hits `if (!writer->stco) return GF_OUT_OF_MEM;` and returns immediately. `writer` (and `writer->stsc`) are never freed. Any TrackWriter objects previously added to `writers` in earlier loop iterations are also leaked because `CleanWriters(writers)` is not called.

state.value: list containing previously added TrackWriters that are also leaked

commit_message:
Reorganize and add more validation to MKL requantization

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387901341
Change-Id: I2515b9034c64e113db0bcec8337d30643ab0a0f1
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The validation for `input_requested_max` incorrectly checks `input_requested_min.NumElements() == 1` instead of `input_requested_max.NumElements() == 1`. This means `input_requested_max` is never validated to be a scalar. If an attacker provides an empty tensor for `input_requested_max`, the subsequent access `input_requested_max.flat<float>()(0)` performs an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The input tensor is validated to be 4D.
input_min_vec and input_max_vec are read and validated for matching depth.

state.value: some positive integer matching channel depth

step_by_step_execution.description: input_requested_min is read and correctly validated to have exactly 1 element.

input_requested_max is read from context. The OP_REQUIRES check erroneously validates input_requested_min.NumElements() == 1 again, instead of input_requested_max.NumElements() == 1. An attacker supplies an empty tensor for input_requested_max.
The code accesses input_requested_max.flat<float>()(0), which is an out-of-bounds read on the empty tensor's underlying buffer.

The code now checks for consistency of the MCU sizes across

commit_message:
Make Transpose's shape inference function validate that negative `perm` values are within the tensor's rank.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 403252853
Change-Id: Ia6b31b45b237312668bb31c2c3b3c7bbce2d2610
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the shape inference function for `Transpose` is vulnerable to a heap buffer overflow. This occurs whenever `perm` contains negative elements. The shape inference function does not validate that the indices in `perm` are all valid. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB read in TFLite's `gather_nd.cc`.

Passing negative indices is illegal but there was a missing check so that resulted in OOB accesses.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387208551
Change-Id: I6b7a8a62d3e7c13a16d81619e5bc23ae2cdbc7fd
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TFLite's [`GatherNd` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather_nd.cc#L124) does not support negative indices but there are no checks for this situation. Hence, an attacker can read arbitrary data from the heap by carefully crafting a model with negative values in `indices`. Similar issue exists in [`Gather` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather.cc). We have patched the issue in GitHub commits bb6a0383ed553c286f87ca88c207f6774d5c4a8f and eb921122119a6b6e470ee98b89e65d721663179d. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: Skip MNG CLIP chunk with out-of-range object IDs
cve_desc: In ImageMagick before 6.9.9-0 and 7.x before 7.0.6-1, the ReadOneMNGImage function in coders/png.c has an out-of-bounds read with the MNG CLIP chunk.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_CLIP chunk handler, `first_object` and `last_object` are read as 16-bit values from user-controlled data (range 0-65535). These values are used as indices into `mng_info->exists[]`, `mng_info->frozen[]`, and `mng_info->object_clip[]` arrays without any bounds checking against MNG_MAX_OBJECTS. This leads to an out-of-bounds read/write when the object indices exceed the array sizes.

step_by_step_execution.description: The image format is MNG, and the main loop reads a new chunk.
The chunk length is read and validated to be <= PNG_UINT_31_MAX, then chunk data is read into the `chunk` buffer and `p` points to it.
first_object and last_object are parsed from the chunk data as 16-bit big-endian values with no bounds check.
The loop iterates from first_object to last_object, using i as an index into mng_info arrays (exists, frozen, object_clip) which are sized MNG_MAX_OBJECTS+1 (typically 257).
mng_info->exists[i] is read out-of-bounds. If it happens to be non-zero, mng_info->object_clip[i] is written out-of-bounds via mng_read_box.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_DISC chunk handler, object indices are read as 16-bit values from user-controlled data and passed directly to MngInfoDiscardObject without bounds validation. The index can be 0-65535, exceeding MNG_MAX_OBJECTS array bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The main loop reads a mng_DISC chunk with non-zero length.
The else branch processes pairs of bytes as 16-bit object IDs.
MngInfoDiscardObject is called with an index that can exceed MNG_MAX_OBJECTS, leading to out-of-bounds array access inside that function.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_LOOP chunk handler, loop_level is read from a single byte of user-controlled data (range 0-255) and used to index into mng_info->loop_active, loop_jump, loop_count, and loop_iteration arrays without bounds checking. If these arrays are smaller than 256 elements, this is an out-of-bounds write. The same issue occurs in the ENDL handler.

step_by_step_execution.description: The main loop reads a mng_LOOP chunk with length > 4.
loop_level is set from chunk[0], an attacker-controlled byte.
loop_level is used directly as index into mng_info arrays (loop_active, loop_jump, loop_count, loop_iteration). If these arrays are smaller than 256, this writes out of bounds.

commit_message:
Avoid buffer overflow when loading tensors with insufficient data from checkpoints.

`CopyDataFromTensorSliceToTensorSlice` does not (and cannot conveniently)
provide any bounds checking on its own, so the size is instead checked prior
to passing unvalidated data to that function.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 392971286
Change-Id: If2073b36d4d5eedd386329f56729395fd7effee1
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can trigger undefined behavior, integer overflows, segfaults and `CHECK`-fail crashes if they can change saved checkpoints from outside of TensorFlow. This is because the checkpoints loading infrastructure is missing validation for invalid file formats. The fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Validate and require subkey binding signatures on PGP public keys

All subkeys must be followed by a binding signature by the primary key
as per the OpenPGP RFC, enforce the presence and validity in the parser.

The implementation is as kludgey as they come to work around our
simple-minded parser structure without touching API, to maximise
backportability. Store all the raw packets internally as we decode them
to be able to access previous elements at will, needed to validate ordering
and access the actual data. Add testcases for manipulated keys whose
import previously would succeed.

Depends on the two previous commits:
7b399fcb8f52566e6f3b4327197a85facd08db91 and
236b802a4aa48711823a191d1b7f753c82a89ec5

Fixes CVE-2021-3521.
cve_desc: There is a flaw in RPM's signature functionality. OpenPGP subkeys are associated with a primary key via a "binding signature." RPM does not check the binding signature of subkeys prior to importing them. If an attacker is able to add or socially engineer another party to add a malicious subkey to a legitimate public key, RPM could wrongly trust a malicious signature. The greatest impact of this flaw is to data integrity. To exploit this flaw, an attacker must either compromise an RPM repository or convince an administrator to install an untrusted RPM or public key. It is strongly recommended to only use RPMs and public keys from trusted sources.

vulnerabilities.description: Memory leak of `selfsig`. When `selfsig` is allocated (because `expect` is non-zero and the tag matches), and then `pgpPrtPkt` returns an error causing a `break`, or the subkey binding signature type check fails causing a `break`, the allocated `selfsig` is never freed. The cleanup code at the end of the function only frees `digp` and `all`, but not `selfsig`. An attacker can trigger this repeatedly with crafted packets to cause a denial of service through memory exhaustion.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins parsing packets. A PGPTAG_PUBLIC_SUBKEY packet is encountered and successfully processed, setting `expect = PGPTAG_SIGNATURE`.
The loop iterates again. The next packet's tag matches `expect` (PGPTAG_SIGNATURE), so `selfsig` is allocated via `pgpDigParamsNew(pkt->tag)`.

`pgpPrtPkt(pkt, selfsig)` is called to process the signature packet. If this function returns a non-zero error value (e.g., due to a malformed signature packet), the code executes `break` to exit the while loop.
Execution reaches the cleanup code after the loop. `free(all)` is called and `digp` is freed via `pgpDigParamsFree(digp)`, but `selfsig` is never freed, causing a memory leak.

state.value: leaked — allocated memory with no remaining reference

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB read in TFLite's `gather_nd.cc`.

Passing negative indices is illegal but there was a missing check so that resulted in OOB accesses.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387208551
Change-Id: I6b7a8a62d3e7c13a16d81619e5bc23ae2cdbc7fd
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TFLite's [`GatherNd` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather_nd.cc#L124) does not support negative indices but there are no checks for this situation. Hence, an attacker can read arbitrary data from the heap by carefully crafting a model with negative values in `indices`. Similar issue exists in [`Gather` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather.cc). We have patched the issue in GitHub commits bb6a0383ed553c286f87ca88c207f6774d5c4a8f and eb921122119a6b6e470ee98b89e65d721663179d. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fixed typo while calculating module path length.

The issue was introduced in 77c398f26d7e (not released yet).
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.3 was discovered to contain a stack overflow in the function njs_default_module_loader at /src/njs/src/njs_module.c. NOTE: multiple third parties dispute this report, e.g., the behavior is only found in unreleased development code that was not part of the 0.7.2, 0.7.3, or 0.7.4 release

vulnerabilities.description: When `dir` is not NULL and `dir->length` is 0, but `info->name.length` is non-zero, the combined `length` will be non-zero, so the `length == 0` early return is not taken. The code then evaluates `dir->start[dir->length - 1]`. Since `dir->length` is of an unsigned type (`size_t`), `dir->length - 1` wraps around to a very large value (e.g., `SIZE_MAX`), causing an out-of-bounds read from `dir->start`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `dir` pointing to a valid `njs_str_t` where `dir->length == 0`, and `info->name.length > 0`.
`length` is initialized to `info->name.length`.
`dir != NULL` is true, so we enter the branch. `length += dir->length` doesn't change length since `dir->length` is 0.
Check `length == 0` fails because `length` is 5, so we do NOT return early.
`dir->length - 1` is evaluated. Since `dir->length` is an unsigned `size_t` with value 0, `0 - 1` wraps to `SIZE_MAX`. The code reads `dir->start[SIZE_MAX]`, which is a massive out-of-bounds read.

state.value: dir->start + SIZE_MAX — far out of bounds

commit_message:
Avoid buffer overflow when loading tensors with insufficient data from checkpoints.

`CopyDataFromTensorSliceToTensorSlice` does not (and cannot conveniently)
provide any bounds checking on its own, so the size is instead checked prior
to passing unvalidated data to that function.
