This is because the checkpoints loading infrastructure is missing validation for invalid file formats. The fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-r374-qrwv-86hh
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library. In version 2.11.1 and prior, if incoming RTCP XR message contain block, the data field is not checked against the received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP XR. A malicious actor can send a RTCP XR message with an invalid packet size.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.LoadAndRemapMatrix vulnerability with invalid `row_remapping`.

Check that `row_remapping` has the correct dims().

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445522800
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.LoadAndRemapMatrix does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `initializing_values` is a vector but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message: Fix handling of APNG with 0 delay_den (#313)
cve_desc: libjxl v0.3.7 is affected by a Divide By Zero in issue in lib/extras/codec_apng.cc jxl::DecodeImageAPNG(). When encoding a malicous APNG file using cjxl, an attacker can trigger a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in computing `imagesize = h * rowbytes` (where `rowbytes = w * 4`). Both `w` and `h` are `unsigned int` values that are only checked against `cMaxPNGSize`. If `cMaxPNGSize` is large enough (or even moderate values like w=65536, h=65536), the multiplication overflows a 32-bit unsigned int, resulting in a much smaller allocation than expected. Subsequent writes via `frameRaw.rows[j] = frameRaw.p + j * rowbytes` and the pixel processing loop write far beyond the allocated buffer.

code_snippets: for (j = 0; j < h; j++) frameRaw.rows[j] = frameRaw.p + j * rowbytes;

step_by_step_execution.description: The PNG signature and IHDR chunk are read and validated.
Width and height are read from IHDR chunk and checked against cMaxPNGSize individually.
rowbytes is computed as w * 4. For w=65536, this is 262144 which fits in unsigned int.
imagesize is computed as h * rowbytes = 65536 * 262144 = 17179869184, which overflows unsigned int (max 4294967295), wrapping to a small value.
A heap buffer of the truncated `imagesize` bytes is allocated (e.g., 0 bytes or a very small amount).
The loop sets up row pointers that point well beyond the end of the allocated buffer, and subsequent libpng decoding writes pixel data through these pointers, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

state.value: frameRaw.p + j * 262144, far beyond allocated size

vulnerabilities.description: Division by zero when `delay_den` is read from the fcTL chunk as 0 via `png_get_uint_16`. There is no check that `delay_den` is non-zero before computing `delay_num * 1000 / delay_den`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The code processes an fcTL chunk, reading frame control parameters from chunk data.
After decoding the next frame's pixel data, the code calls processing_finish and enters the frame finalization block.
The duration is computed as `delay_num * 1000 / delay_den`, which is a division by zero if delay_den == 0.

commit_message:
Prevent unitialized variable use in grappler.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 399702928
Change-Id: Id7e75451fbff297692dfb687f60ea04b25c96b24
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer has a use of unitialized variable. If the `train_nodes` vector (obtained from the saved model that gets optimized) does not contain a `Dequeue` node, then `dequeue_node` is left unitialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The local pointer `dequeue_node` is declared without initialization. If the loop over `train_nodes` never finds a dequeue op (i.e., `IsDequeueOp` is never true), `dequeue_node` remains uninitialized. The subsequent `if (dequeue_node)` check reads this uninitialized pointer, which is undefined behavior. Depending on the garbage value, the branch may be taken, leading to dereferencing an invalid pointer (`dequeue_node->name()`).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins execution; `train_nodes` is populated by `ComputeTransitiveFanin`.

state.value: vector of NodeDef pointers from the graph

step_by_step_execution.description: `dequeue_node` is declared as `const NodeDef*` without initialization.
The loop iterates over `train_nodes`. If none of the nodes satisfy `IsDequeueOp`, the loop completes without assigning `dequeue_node`.
The code evaluates `if (dequeue_node)`. Reading an uninitialized pointer is undefined behavior. If the indeterminate value is non-null, the branch is entered.
Inside the branch, `dequeue_node->name()` dereferences the garbage pointer, causing a read from an arbitrary memory address.

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB in sparse reduction ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387934524
Change-Id: I894aa30f1e454f09b471d565b4a325da49322c1a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a1bc56203f21a5a4995311825ffaba7a670d7747/tensorflow/core/kernels/sparse_reduce_op.cc#L217-L228) fails to validate that each reduction group does not overflow and that each corresponding index does not point to outside the bounds of the input tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 87158f43f05f2720a374f3e6d22a7aaa3a33f750. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Disallow division by zero FPE in `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv`

Had to update a test that was broken.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388516976
Change-Id: Ic358e6bf0559e011539974d453fc7aa18b427e9c
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv` is vulnerable to a division by 0 error. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/resource_variable_ops.cc#L865) uses a common class for all binary operations but fails to treat the division by 0 case separately. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4aacb30888638da75023e6601149415b39763d76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The code checks that updates rank equals indices.dims() + params.dims() - 1, and that updates.shape starts with indices.shape, but never validates that the trailing dimensions of updates match params.shape[1:]. This allows a mismatch between the inner dimension of updates_flat ({N, num_updates/N}) and params_flat (flat_outer_dims). For example, if params has shape [3, 10], indices has shape [2], and updates has shape [2, 1], then updates_flat has shape [2, 1] and params_flat has shape [3, 10]. The scatter functor would read out of bounds from updates when attempting to scatter a row of size 1 into a param row of size 10. Conversely, if updates' trailing dimensions are larger than params', the functor writes out of bounds into params.

code_snippets:
const Index bad_i = functor(c, c->template eigen_device<Device>(),
                                    params_flat, updates_flat, indices_flat);

step_by_step_execution.description: A resource variable 'params' with shape [3, 10] is looked up. Indices tensor has shape [2] with valid indices (e.g., [0, 1]). Updates tensor has shape [2, 1].
Rank check: updates.dims() (2) == indices.dims() (1) + params->dims() (2) - 1 = 2. Check passes.
N_big = indices.NumElements() = 2, which fits in Index. N = 2. Since N > 0, we enter the branch.
indices_flat has 2 elements. params_flat = params->flat_outer_dims<T>() has shape [3, 10].
updates is not scalar, so we enter the else branch. num_updates = updates.NumElements() = 2.
StartsWith check: updates.shape() [2, 1] starts with indices.shape() [2]. First dimension matches (2 == 2). Check passes.
updates_flat = updates.shaped<T, 2>({N, num_updates / N}) = shaped({2, 1}). This has inner dimension 1, but params_flat has inner dimension 10.

The ScatterFunctor is invoked with mismatched inner dimensions. It scatters rows from updates_flat (width 1) into params_flat (width 10), causing out-of-bounds reads from the updates tensor buffer as it reads 10 elements from a row that only has 1 element.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad vulnerability with invalid input_min or input_max.

Check that argument is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445198280
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: When axis_ is 0, the else branch is taken (since axis_ != -1), and `axis_ - 1` evaluates to -1. This negative value is passed to `flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>()`, which expects a non-negative dimension index. This leads to an out-of-bounds read/write or undefined behavior within the Eigen reshape logic. The validation allows axis_ == 0 (it checks axis_ >= -1 and axis_ < input.shape().dims()), so this path is reachable.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads axis_ which is a member variable. axis_ can be 0 based on the validation checks.
Validation checks pass: axis_ >= -1 is true (0 >= -1), and axis_ < input.shape().dims() is true for any tensor with dims > 0.
Since axis_ != -1, the else branch is taken.
flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>(axis_ - 1) is called with axis_ - 1 = -1. This negative index causes out-of-bounds memory access within Eigen's reshape/dimension computation logic.

commit_message:
string_number_format: Correctly handles return value of snprintf

Summary: `snprintf` can return a value greater than the number of bytes copied. In case the first byte of the string is not a digit (could be '-'), size of `tmpstr` was being updated without checking `tmplen`. This resulted in either an assertion error or a heap overflow depending on whether the assertion is compiled or not.

Reviewed By: mofarrell, qianxuweiren

Differential Revision: D17327899

fbshipit-source-id: ee53875d21e02608c6d870388eecf1464de24ff1
cve_desc: Insufficient boundary checks when formatting numbers in number_format allows read/write access to out-of-bounds memory, potentially leading to remote code execution. This issue affects HHVM versions prior to 3.30.10, all versions between 4.0.0 and 4.8.5, all versions between 4.9.0 and 4.18.2, and versions 4.19.0, 4.19.1, 4.20.0, 4.20.1, 4.20.2, 4.21.0, 4.22.0, 4.23.0.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the result buffer size calculation. The overflow check operates in size_t (64-bit unsigned) arithmetic, which won't wrap for realistic inputs. However, the result is stored into `integral` (an `int`). When `thousand_sep` is very large (user-controlled), the computed value `((integral-1) / 3) * thousand_sep.size()` can exceed INT_MAX. Storing this back into `int integral` causes signed integer overflow (undefined behavior in C++), typically resulting in a small or negative value. This leads to an undersized `reslen`, a too-small buffer allocation, and subsequent out-of-bounds writes via memcpy and pointer dereferences in the copy loops.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a number having many integral digits (e.g. d=9999999999.0 giving 10 integral digits), dec=0, and a thousand_sep string with a very large size (e.g. 800,000,000 bytes).
After formatting with snprintf, tmpbuf contains '9999999999' and integral = tmplen = 10.

The overflow check computes `integral + thousand_sep.size() * ((integral-1) / 3)` in size_t arithmetic: 10 + 800000000 * 3 = 2400000010. This is a valid size_t and is > 10, so the overflow check passes (no error raised).

`integral += ((integral-1) / 3) * thousand_sep.size()` computes RHS as size_t = 2400000000, then adds to integral (int). The result 2400000010 exceeds INT_MAX (2147483647), causing signed integer overflow. The value stored in `integral` is implementation-defined but typically wraps to a small or negative number (e.g. 252516362 or -1894967286).

state.value: overflowed int value, e.g. wraps or is negative

step_by_step_execution.description: `String resstr(reslen, ReserveString)` allocates a buffer much smaller than needed (or potentially misinterprets negative size).

state.value: much smaller than the actual data to be written

step_by_step_execution.description: The copy loops write digits and thousand_sep data via `*t-- = *s--` and `memcpy(t + (1 - thousand_sep.size()), thousand_sep.data(), thousand_sep.size())`, writing far beyond the allocated buffer bounds.

state.value: pointer moving out of bounds of resbuf

commit_message:
Validate that matrix dimension sizes in SparseMatMul are positive.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 401149683
Change-Id: Ib33eafc561a39c8741ece80b2edce6d4aae9a57d
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the code for sparse matrix multiplication is vulnerable to undefined behavior via binding a reference to `nullptr`. This occurs whenever the dimensions of `a` or `b` are 0 or less. In the case on one of these is 0, an empty output tensor should be allocated (to conserve the invariant that output tensors are always allocated when the operation is successful) but nothing should be written to it (that is, we should return early from the kernel implementation). Otherwise, attempts to write to this empty tensor would result in heap OOB access. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int m = transpose_a_ ? a.dim_size(1) : a.dim_size(0);
const int k = transpose_a_ ? a.dim_size(0) : a.dim_size(1);
const int n = transpose_b_ ? b.dim_size(0) : b.dim_size(1);
const int k2 = transpose_b_ ? b.dim_size(1) : b.dim_size(0);
vulnerabilities.description: The dimension sizes from dim_size() (which returns int64) are truncated when stored into int variables (m, k, n, k2). If a tensor has a dimension exceeding INT_MAX, the truncation can produce a small or negative value. This causes the output tensor to be allocated with an incorrect (too small) shape, while the subsequent matrix multiplication operates on the original large tensor dimensions, leading to a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Tensor `a` is read with a shape like [0x100000002, 1] and tensor `b` with shape [1, 1]. Both pass the IsMatrix check.
dim_size(0) returns int64 value 4294967298 (0x100000002), which is truncated to int, yielding 2.
The check k == k2 passes since both are 1.
Output tensor is allocated with shape {m, n} = {2, 1}, a very small buffer.

The matrix multiplication (contract or DoMatMul) is invoked on the original tensors which have actual dimensions much larger than what the output buffer was allocated for, resulting in an out-of-bounds write to the output buffer.

commit_message:
password: Use crypto_memneq() to compare hashes

Cryptographic verifications should be time-constant so that an attacker
cannot get information about the secrets used by observing the system,
so use crypto_memneq() rather than memcmp() to compare password hashes.

Signed-off-by: Sascha Hauer <s.hauer@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: common/password.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because strncmp is used during hash comparison.

commit_message:
Fix a null pointer exception in SVDF

This is due to not checking that `GetVariableInput` returns non-null tensor.

Also fix a potential null pointer exception in `GetVariableInput`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385160147
Change-Id: Iadf3f0705b036a9014d27caa5a8bbd91f4c4c401
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of SVDF in TFLite is [vulnerable to a null pointer error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/svdf.cc#L300-L313). The [`GetVariableInput` function](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L115-L119) can return a null pointer but `GetTensorData` assumes that the argument is always a valid tensor. Furthermore, because `GetVariableInput` calls [`GetMutableInput`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L82-L90) which might return `nullptr`, the `tensor->is_variable` expression can also trigger a null pointer exception. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5b048e87e4e55990dae6b547add4dae59f4e1c76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fix EinsumHelper::ParseEquation to avoid uninitialized accesses.

EinsumHelper::ParseEquation is supposed to return true or false in
input_has_ellipsis and output_has_ellipsis to indicate whether there is
ellipsis in the inputs and output. Previously, when there is no ellipsis in the
inputs or output, the routine doesn't assign false to the variables. This
change initializes the two variables with false to fix the problem.
PiperOrigin-RevId: 391772004
Change-Id: I17b6c88aadef4131470378e48cced054bf252e86
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affeced versions during execution, `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` is supposed to set the flags in `input_has_ellipsis` vector and `*output_has_ellipsis` boolean to indicate whether there is ellipsis in the corresponding inputs and output. However, the code only changes these flags to `true` and never assigns `false`. This results in unitialized variable access if callers assume that `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` always sets these flags. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If `num_inputs` is 0 (i.e., `ParseEinsumEquation` returns an empty input list), the short-circuit evaluation of `num_inputs == 1` yields false, causing an out-of-bounds read on the empty `input_label_counts` vector at index 0 (and potentially index 1). The code never validates that `num_inputs >= 2` before accessing indices 0 and 1.

step_by_step_execution.description: ParseEinsumEquation parses the equation and populates input_str. If the equation has no inputs (e.g., an arrow with only an output like "->ab"), input_str is empty.
num_inputs is set to input_str.size(), which is 0. input_labels, input_label_counts, and input_has_ellipsis are all resized to 0.
The input loop (for i=0; i<num_inputs) does not execute. MapToLabels is called for output_str, which may add labels to label_mapping, making num_labels > 0.
label_types is resized to num_labels, and the loop iterates over labels 0..num_labels-1.

The condition `num_inputs == 1` evaluates to false (num_inputs is 0). Due to short-circuit evaluation, the next operand `(*input_label_counts)[0][label]` is evaluated, which is an out-of-bounds access on the empty vector.

commit_message:
Fix heap OOB in `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388355464
Change-Id: If14d96231d1cd7aad7c4d1c22c1bab1576b75717
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions if the arguments to `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather` don't determine a valid ragged tensor code can trigger a read from outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/ragged_gather_op.cc#L70) directly reads the first dimension of a tensor shape before checking that said tensor has rank of at least 1 (i.e., it is not a scalar). Furthermore, the implementation does not check that the list given by `params_nested_splits` is not an empty list of tensors. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit a2b743f6017d7b97af1fe49087ae15f0ac634373. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent unitialized variable use in grappler.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 399702928
Change-Id: Id7e75451fbff297692dfb687f60ea04b25c96b24
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer has a use of unitialized variable. If the `train_nodes` vector (obtained from the saved model that gets optimized) does not contain a `Dequeue` node, then `dequeue_node` is left unitialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Pointers obtained from graph_.mutable_node(i) are stored in all_nodes_. Subsequently, AddNodeDivConst() and AddNodeDiv() add new nodes to graph_, which is a protobuf GraphDef. Adding elements to a protobuf RepeatedPtrField can trigger reallocation of the underlying storage, invalidating all previously stored NodeDef* pointers. These dangling pointers are then dereferenced when accessing all_nodes_[apply_gradient_node_name], leading to a use-after-free condition.

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop iterates over all existing graph nodes, storing mutable pointers into the all_nodes_ map.

state.value: map of node name -> NodeDef* pointers into graph_'s internal repeated field storage

step_by_step_execution.description: AddNodeDivConst() is called, which adds a new node to graph_. This may cause the protobuf RepeatedPtrField to reallocate its internal storage, invalidating all NodeDef* pointers previously obtained from graph_.mutable_node(i).

state.value: potentially dangling pointers if reallocation occurred

step_by_step_execution.description: The code iterates over apply_gradients_nodes_ and looks up the node pointer from all_nodes_, which may now be a dangling pointer.

state.value: potentially dangling pointer obtained from all_nodes_[apply_gradient_node_name]

step_by_step_execution.description: The dangling pointer is dereferenced to read its op() and input(), and then to modify its input via mutable_input(). This constitutes a use-after-free.

state.value: dangling pointer being dereferenced for read and write operations

commit_message:
Fix heap OOB in `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388355464
Change-Id: If14d96231d1cd7aad7c4d1c22c1bab1576b75717
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions if the arguments to `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather` don't determine a valid ragged tensor code can trigger a read from outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/ragged_gather_op.cc#L70) directly reads the first dimension of a tensor shape before checking that said tensor has rank of at least 1 (i.e., it is not a scalar). Furthermore, the implementation does not check that the list given by `params_nested_splits` is not an empty list of tensors. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit a2b743f6017d7b97af1fe49087ae15f0ac634373. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Make Transpose's shape inference function validate that negative `perm` values are within the tensor's rank.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 403252853
Change-Id: Ia6b31b45b237312668bb31c2c3b3c7bbce2d2610
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the shape inference function for `Transpose` is vulnerable to a heap buffer overflow. This occurs whenever `perm` contains negative elements. The shape inference function does not validate that the indices in `perm` are all valid. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The bounds check on `in_idx` only verifies that it is less than `rank`, but does not check for negative values. A negative `in_idx` from the permutation tensor would bypass the check and be passed to `c->Dim(input, in_idx)`, resulting in an out-of-bounds read (negative index access).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the input shape and the permutation tensor.
Rank is determined, e.g. from input rank.
The permutation tensor data is extracted via AsInt64. An attacker provides a negative value, e.g. data = [-1, 0, 1].
In the loop iteration i=0, in_idx = data[0] = -1. The check `in_idx >= rank` evaluates to `-1 >= 3` which is false, so the check is bypassed.
c->Dim(input, in_idx) is called with in_idx = -1, causing an out-of-bounds/negative index access on the shape dimensions.

commit_message: Fix fido2 signature validation bug
cve_desc: scheme/webauthn.c in Glewlwyd SSO server through 2.5.3 has a buffer overflow during FIDO2 signature validation in webauthn registration.

code_snippets: unsigned char data_signed[200], client_data_hash[32], cert_export[32], cert_export_b64[64];
vulnerabilities.description: Stack buffer overflow in `data_signed`. The fixed-size 200-byte stack buffer receives multiple variable-length inputs (rpid_hash, client_data_hash, credential_id, cert_x, cert_y) via memcpy without any bounds checking. The total size is 1 + rpid_hash_len + 32 + credential_id_len + 1 + cert_x_len + cert_y_len. Since credential_id can be up to 1023 bytes per the WebAuthn specification, and these lengths are caller-controlled, the buffer can be easily overflowed.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a credential_id of, say, 256 bytes (valid per WebAuthn spec), rpid_hash_len=32, cert_x_len=32, cert_y_len=32.
data_signed is a stack buffer of 200 bytes.
First byte set to 0x0, offset becomes 1.
rpid_hash (32 bytes) is copied at offset 1. Offset becomes 33.
client_data_hash (32 bytes) is copied at offset 33. Offset becomes 65.

credential_id (256 bytes) is copied at offset 65 into a 200-byte buffer. This writes 256 bytes starting at data_signed[65], overflowing past the end of the buffer (needs up to index 320, but buffer ends at index 199).

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad vulnerability with invalid input_min or input_max.

Check that argument is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445198280
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: When axis_ is 0, the else branch is taken (since axis_ != -1), and `flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>(axis_ - 1)` is called with argument -1. This negative index is passed as the split dimension to the reshape operation, which can cause undefined behavior, an out-of-bounds access, or a crash. The validation checks allow axis_ == 0 (the condition `axis_ >= -1` and `axis_ < input.shape().dims()` both pass for axis_=0 when input has at least 1 dimension), but the code does not guard against axis_=0 before computing axis_-1.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads inputs and allocates the output tensor for input_backprop.
The check `axis_ >= -1` passes since 0 >= -1.
The check `axis_ == -1 || axis_ < input.shape().dims()` passes since axis_ (0) < dims for any tensor with at least 1 dimension.
depth is computed as `input.dim_size(axis_)` = `input.dim_size(0)`, which is valid.
min/max tensor validation and output allocation proceed normally.
Since axis_ != -1, the else branch is taken. `flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>(axis_ - 1)` is called with axis_ - 1 = -1.
The negative value -1 is used as the split dimension in flat_inner_outer_dims, causing an out-of-bounds memory access or crash due to invalid reshape parameters.

commit_message:
string_number_format: Correctly handles return value of snprintf

Summary: `snprintf` can return a value greater than the number of bytes copied. In case the first byte of the string is not a digit (could be '-'), size of `tmpstr` was being updated without checking `tmplen`. This resulted in either an assertion error or a heap overflow depending on whether the assertion is compiled or not.

Reviewed By: mofarrell, qianxuweiren

Differential Revision: D17327899

fbshipit-source-id: ee53875d21e02608c6d870388eecf1464de24ff1
cve_desc: Insufficient boundary checks when formatting numbers in number_format allows read/write access to out-of-bounds memory, potentially leading to remote code execution. This issue affects HHVM versions prior to 3.30.10, all versions between 4.0.0 and 4.8.5, all versions between 4.9.0 and 4.18.2, and versions 4.19.0, 4.19.1, 4.20.0, 4.20.1, 4.20.2, 4.21.0, 4.22.0, 4.23.0.

vulnerabilities.description: The overflow checks compute in size_t (unsigned 64-bit) due to thousand_sep.size() and dec_point.size() returning size_t, but the results are stored in int variables (integral, reslen). On a 64-bit platform, the size_t addition will not wrap around, so the overflow check passes, but the subsequent assignment to the int variable can overflow, resulting in a small or negative value. This leads to a too-small buffer allocation and subsequent out-of-bounds writes when the actual data is copied.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a value like d=1000.0, dec=0, and a very large thousand_sep string (e.g., size ~65 million bytes or more).
d is positive, so is_negative=0. After snprintf, tmpbuf contains "1000" and tmplen=4.
dec is 0, so dp is set to nullptr. integral is set to tmplen.

thousand_sep is not empty. The overflow check computes in size_t: integral (promoted to size_t: 4) + thousand_sep.size() * ((integral-1)/3) = 4 + 65000000 * 1 = 65000004. This is not < 4 (size_t comparison), so the check passes.

integral (int) += ((4-1)/3) * 65000000 = 1 * 65000000 = 65000000. integral becomes 65000004. With larger thousand_sep.size() values (e.g., ~2 billion / ((integral-1)/3)), this overflows a 32-bit int, wrapping to a small or negative value.

state.value: overflowed int value, e.g., negative or small positive due to signed int overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: reslen is set to the overflowed integral value. A buffer of this (small/negative) size is allocated via String(reslen, ReserveString), resulting in a very small buffer.

state.value: small or negative value from overflowed integral

step_by_step_execution.description: The writing loop copies digits and thousand_sep data via memcpy into resbuf, writing far beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: much larger than allocated buffer size

commit_message:
Fix a null pointer exception in SVDF

This is due to not checking that `GetVariableInput` returns non-null tensor.

Also fix a potential null pointer exception in `GetVariableInput`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385160147
Change-Id: Iadf3f0705b036a9014d27caa5a8bbd91f4c4c401
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of SVDF in TFLite is [vulnerable to a null pointer error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/svdf.cc#L300-L313). The [`GetVariableInput` function](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L115-L119) can return a null pointer but `GetTensorData` assumes that the argument is always a valid tensor. Furthermore, because `GetVariableInput` calls [`GetMutableInput`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L82-L90) which might return `nullptr`, the `tensor->is_variable` expression can also trigger a null pointer exception. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5b048e87e4e55990dae6b547add4dae59f4e1c76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
TfLiteTensor* tensor = GetMutableInput(context, node, index);
  return tensor->is_variable ? tensor : nullptr;
vulnerabilities.description: If GetMutableInput returns nullptr (e.g., invalid index or context), the subsequent dereference tensor->is_variable will cause a null pointer dereference, leading to a crash or potentially exploitable behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: GetMutableInput is called with the provided context, node, and index.
GetMutableInput returns nullptr because the index is invalid or the input is unavailable.
The code dereferences tensor via tensor->is_variable without a null check, causing a null pointer dereference.

commit_message:
Improve to cover scale value greater than one

PiperOrigin-RevId: 433050921
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, certain TFLite models that were created using TFLite model converter would crash when loaded in the TFLite interpreter. The culprit is that during quantization the scale of values could be greater than 1 but code was always assuming sub-unit scaling. Thus, since code was calling `QuantizeMultiplierSmallerThanOneExp`, the `TFLITE_CHECK_LT` assertion would trigger and abort the process. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

Thus, since code was calling `QuantizeMultiplierSmallerThanOneExp`, the `TFLITE_CHECK_LT` assertion would trigger and abort the process. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
Validate that matrix dimension sizes in SparseMatMul are positive.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 401149683
Change-Id: Ib33eafc561a39c8741ece80b2edce6d4aae9a57d
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the code for sparse matrix multiplication is vulnerable to undefined behavior via binding a reference to `nullptr`. This occurs whenever the dimensions of `a` or `b` are 0 or less. In the case on one of these is 0, an empty output tensor should be allocated (to conserve the invariant that output tensors are always allocated when the operation is successful) but nothing should be written to it (that is, we should return early from the kernel implementation). Otherwise, attempts to write to this empty tensor would result in heap OOB access. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int m = transpose_a_ ? a.dim_size(1) : a.dim_size(0);
const int k = transpose_a_ ? a.dim_size(0) : a.dim_size(1);
const int n = transpose_b_ ? b.dim_size(0) : b.dim_size(1);
const int k2 = transpose_b_ ? b.dim_size(1) : b.dim_size(0);
vulnerabilities.description: Tensor dimension sizes (int64) are truncated to int (int32). A dimension value exceeding INT_MAX (e.g. 0x100000001 = 4294967297) would wrap around when cast to int, yielding a small positive value (e.g. 1). The validation checks on m, n, k would pass with these small wrapped values, and the output tensor would be allocated with incorrect (too small) dimensions. Subsequent matrix operations (contract, DoMatMul) operate on the actual tensor data with its real dimensions, causing out-of-bounds memory writes into the undersized output buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Tensor 'a' is read with shape e.g. [1, 0x100000001] and tensor 'b' with shape [0x100000001, 1]. Both pass the IsMatrix check.

Dimension sizes are narrowed from int64 to int32. With transpose_a_=false, transpose_b_=false: m = (int)a.dim_size(0), k = (int)a.dim_size(1), n = (int)b.dim_size(1), k2 = (int)b.dim_size(0). The value 0x100000001 truncated to int32 gives 1.
The check k == k2 passes (1 == 1). The check m >= 0 && n >= 0 && k >= 0 passes (all are 1).
Output tensor is allocated with shape {1, 1}, a single float element.

The matrix contraction (or DoMatMul) operates using the actual tensor matrix views which have the real int64 dimensions. The result of multiplying a [1, 4294967297] by [4294967297, 1] matrix writes to a buffer sized for [1,1], causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Fix EinsumHelper::ParseEquation to avoid uninitialized accesses.

EinsumHelper::ParseEquation is supposed to return true or false in
input_has_ellipsis and output_has_ellipsis to indicate whether there is
ellipsis in the inputs and output. Previously, when there is no ellipsis in the
inputs or output, the routine doesn't assign false to the variables. This
change initializes the two variables with false to fix the problem.
PiperOrigin-RevId: 391772004
Change-Id: I17b6c88aadef4131470378e48cced054bf252e86
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affeced versions during execution, `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` is supposed to set the flags in `input_has_ellipsis` vector and `*output_has_ellipsis` boolean to indicate whether there is ellipsis in the corresponding inputs and output. However, the code only changes these flags to `true` and never assigns `false`. This results in unitialized variable access if callers assume that `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` always sets these flags. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If num_inputs is 0 (i.e., ParseEinsumEquation returns an empty input list), the short-circuit evaluation of the 'unique' boolean first checks 'num_inputs == 1' (which is false), then accesses (*input_label_counts)[0], which is an out-of-bounds read on a zero-sized vector. Similarly, if num_inputs is exactly 1, the short-circuit stops, but if num_inputs >= 2 but input_label_counts was resized to num_inputs (e.g. 2), index 1 is valid. The critical case is num_inputs == 0.

step_by_step_execution.description: ParseEinsumEquation is called and returns successfully with an empty input_str vector (e.g., equation is '->a').
num_inputs is set to input_str.size(), which is 0. input_labels is resized to 0.
The first for-loop (MapToLabels for inputs) does not execute since num_inputs is 0.
MapToLabels is called for output_str 'a', adding a mapping for 'a' -> 0 in label_mapping.
input_label_counts is resized to 0 (num_inputs == 0). input_has_ellipsis is resized to 0.
The second for-loop (computing input label counts) does not execute since num_inputs is 0.
output_label_counts is resized to 1 (num_labels). The output label loop counts label 0 once.
label_types is resized to 1 (num_labels). The loop iterates with label=0.
For label=0, 'removed' is computed. Then 'unique' is evaluated: num_inputs == 1 is false (0 != 1), so (*input_label_counts)[0] is accessed, which is an out-of-bounds read on a vector of size 0.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-vhxv-phmx-g52q

* Prevent OOB read/write when parsing RTCP FB RPSI

* Add log information

* Modification based on comments.
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. PJSIP versions 2.12 and prior do not parse incoming RTCP feedback RPSI (Reference Picture Selection Indication) packet, but any app that directly uses pjmedia_rtcp_fb_parse_rpsi() will be affected. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The `padlen` value is read directly from attacker-controlled packet data without validation. If `padlen` is greater than `rpsi_len*8 - 16`, the unsigned subtraction `rpsi_len*8 - 16 - padlen` wraps around to a very large value. This corrupted `rpsi_bit_len` is then used to compute the string length `(rpsi->rpsi_bit_len + 7)/8`, resulting in `pj_strset` creating a string descriptor with an enormous length, leading to potential out-of-bounds reads when the string is later accessed.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function receives a crafted RTCP packet buffer. Assertions check buf, rpsi are non-null and length >= sizeof(pjmedia_rtcp_common).
Header fields are checked: pt==RTCP_PSFB and count==3. The crafted packet passes these checks.
rpsi_len is computed from hdr->length. Attacker sets hdr->length (network byte order) such that after ntohs it equals 3, giving rpsi_len=(3-2)*4=4.
Length check: length < rpsi_len + 12 => length < 16. If length >= 16 (e.g. 20), this passes.
Pointer p is set past the common header. padlen is read from *p. Attacker sets padlen to a large value, e.g. 255.
rpsi->rpsi_bit_len = rpsi_len*8 - 16 - padlen = 32 - 16 - 255 = -239. As unsigned, this wraps to a very large value (e.g. 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFF11 on 64-bit or 0xFFFFFF11 on 32-bit).

pj_strset is called with length (rpsi->rpsi_bit_len + 7)/8, which is an enormous value, creating a string descriptor that points far beyond the actual buffer. Any subsequent access to this string results in out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
Fixed recursive async function calls.

Previously, PromiseCapability record was stored (function->context)
directly in function object during a function invocation.  This is
not correct, because PromiseCapability record should be linked to
current execution context.  As a result, function->context is
overwritten with consecutive recursive calls which results in
use-after-free.

This closes #451 issue on Github.
cve_desc: njs through 0.7.0, used in NGINX, was discovered to contain a heap use-after-free in njs_await_fulfilled.

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.lastIndexOf() with unicode string as "this".

Previously, when lastIndexOf() was called with unicode string as "this"
argument and a negative "fromIndex" argument null-pointer dererence
might occur because njs_string_offset() was called with invalid index
value whereas njs_string_offset() should always be called with valid
index argument.

The fix is to verify that from index is valid.

This closes #482 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_string_offset at src/njs_string.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When the value is a byte/ASCII string, the pointer `p` is computed as `string_prop.start + from` without checking that `from` is less than the string's length (`string_prop.size`). If `args->from` is greater than or equal to the string length, `p` will point past the string buffer, and the subsequent `njs_string_new` call reads 1 byte from out-of-bounds memory. Similarly, if `to` is negative, the `p--` decrement in the loop can cause `p` to go before `string_prop.start`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads `from` and `to` from `args`, which originate from caller-supplied values.
The value is determined to be a string (njs_is_string(value) is true), so execution enters the string handling branch.
njs_string_prop extracts the string properties. Suppose the string is a short ASCII string.
Since length == string_prop.size, the code enters the byte/ASCII branch. Pointer `p` is set to `string_prop.start + from`.

state.value: string_prop.start + 100 (95 bytes past end of 5-byte buffer)

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop condition `i-- > to` is satisfied (i starts at 101, decrements). njs_string_new is called with `p` pointing 95 bytes past the buffer, reading 1 byte out of bounds.

cve_desc: The ECDSA operation of the micro-ecc library 1.0 is vulnerable to simple power analysis attacks which allows an adversary to extract the private ECC key.

This closes #451 issue on Github.

commit_message:
Prevent use after free.

A very old version of the code used `result` as a simple pointer to a resource. Two years later, the pointer got changed to a `unique_ptr` but author forgot to remove the call to `Unref`. Three years after that, we finally uncover the UAF.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387924872
Change-Id: I70fb6f199164de49fac20c168132a07b84903f9b
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateEnsemble` can result in a use after free error if an attacker supplies specially crafted arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/resource_ops.cc#L55) uses a reference counted resource and decrements the refcount if the initialization fails, as it should. However, when the code was written, the resource was represented as a naked pointer but later refactoring has changed it to be a smart pointer. Thus, when the pointer leaves the scope, a subsequent `free`-ing of the resource occurs, but this fails to take into account that the refcount has already reached 0, thus the resource has been already freed. During this double-free process, members of the resource object are accessed for cleanup but they are invalid as the entire resource has been freed. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5ecec9c6fbdbc6be03295685190a45e7eee726ab. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Disallow division by zero FPE in `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv`

Had to update a test that was broken.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388516976
Change-Id: Ic358e6bf0559e011539974d453fc7aa18b427e9c
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv` is vulnerable to a division by 0 error. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/resource_variable_ops.cc#L865) uses a common class for all binary operations but fails to treat the division by 0 case separately. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4aacb30888638da75023e6601149415b39763d76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The code validates that the rank of updates equals rank(indices) + rank(params) - 1 and that updates.shape starts with indices.shape, but never validates that the trailing dimensions of updates match params.shape[1:]. This allows an attacker to supply an updates tensor whose inner dimensions are larger than the corresponding params dimensions, causing the scatter functor to write beyond the bounds of the params buffer. For example, params=[5,3], indices=[2], updates=[2,10] passes all checks but writes 10 elements per row into a buffer with only 3 columns.

code_snippets:
const Index bad_i = functor(c, c->template eigen_device<Device>(),
                                    params_flat, updates_flat, indices_flat);

step_by_step_execution.description: params tensor is looked up from a resource variable. Suppose params has shape [5, 3], so params_flat via flat_outer_dims<T> has shape {5, 3}.
indices tensor is read from input 1. Suppose indices has shape [2] with valid values e.g. {0, 1}.
updates tensor is read from input 2. Suppose updates has shape [2, 10].
Rank check: updates.dims() == indices.dims() + params->dims() - 1 evaluates to 2 == 1 + 2 - 1 == 2, which is true. Check passes.
N_big (2) is within Index max, and params->dim_size(0) (5) is within Index max. Both checks pass. N is set to 2, entering the if (N > 0) block.
updates is not scalar, so we enter the else branch. num_updates = 20. StartsWith check: updates.shape()=[2,10] starts with indices.shape()=[2] → true. Check passes.

updates_flat is reshaped to {N, num_updates/N} = {2, 10}. The scatter functor is called with params_flat of shape {5, 3} and updates_flat of shape {2, 10}. For each index i, the functor copies/applies 10 elements from updates row into a params row that only has 3 elements, writing 7 elements out-of-bounds.

commit_message:
i2c: fix stack buffer overflow vulnerability in i2c md command

When running "i2c md 0 0 80000100", the function do_i2c_md parses the
length into an unsigned int variable named length. The value is then
moved to a signed variable:

    int nbytes = length;
    #define DISP_LINE_LEN 16
    int linebytes = (nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN) ? DISP_LINE_LEN : nbytes;
    ret = dm_i2c_read(dev, addr, linebuf, linebytes);

On systems where integers are 32 bits wide, 0x80000100 is a negative
value to "nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN" is false and linebytes gets assigned
0x80000100 instead of 16.

The consequence is that the function which reads from the i2c device
(dm_i2c_read or i2c_read) is called with a 16-byte stack buffer to fill
but with a size parameter which is too large. In some cases, this could
trigger a crash. But with some i2c drivers, such as drivers/i2c/nx_i2c.c
(used with "nexell,s5pxx18-i2c" bus), the size is actually truncated to
a 16-bit integer. This is because function i2c_transfer expects an
unsigned short length. In such a case, an attacker who can control the
response of an i2c device can overwrite the return address of a function
and execute arbitrary code through Return-Oriented Programming.

Fix this issue by using unsigned integers types in do_i2c_md. While at
it, make also alen unsigned, as signed sizes can cause vulnerabilities
when people forgot to check that they can be negative.

Signed-off-by: Nicolas Iooss <nicolas.iooss+uboot@ledger.fr>
Reviewed-by: Heiko Schocher <hs@denx.de>
cve_desc: In Das U-Boot through 2022.07-rc5, an integer signedness error and resultant stack-based buffer overflow in the "i2c md" command enables the corruption of the return address pointer of the do_i2c_md function.

commit_message:
i2c: fix stack buffer overflow vulnerability in i2c md command

When running "i2c md 0 0 80000100", the function do_i2c_md parses the
length into an unsigned int variable named length. The value is then
moved to a signed variable:

    int nbytes = length;
    #define DISP_LINE_LEN 16
    int linebytes = (nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN) ? DISP_LINE_LEN : nbytes;
    ret = dm_i2c_read(dev, addr, linebuf, linebytes);

On systems where integers are 32 bits wide, 0x80000100 is a negative
value to "nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN" is false and linebytes gets assigned
0x80000100 instead of 16.

The consequence is that the function which reads from the i2c device
(dm_i2c_read or i2c_read) is called with a 16-byte stack buffer to fill
but with a size parameter which is too large. In some cases, this could
trigger a crash. But with some i2c drivers, such as drivers/i2c/nx_i2c.c
(used with "nexell,s5pxx18-i2c" bus), the size is actually truncated to
a 16-bit integer. This is because function i2c_transfer expects an
unsigned short length. In such a case, an attacker who can control the
response of an i2c device can overwrite the return address of a function
and execute arbitrary code through Return-Oriented Programming.

Fix this issue by using unsigned integers types in do_i2c_md. While at
it, make also alen unsigned, as signed sizes can cause vulnerabilities
when people forgot to check that they can be negative.

Signed-off-by: Nicolas Iooss <nicolas.iooss+uboot@ledger.fr>
Reviewed-by: Heiko Schocher <hs@denx.de>
cve_desc: In Das U-Boot through 2022.07-rc5, an integer signedness error and resultant stack-based buffer overflow in the "i2c md" command enables the corruption of the return address pointer of the do_i2c_md function.

commit_message: fixed #1885
cve_desc: Segmentation fault vulnerability exists in Gpac through 1.0.1 via the gf_odf_size_descriptor function in desc_private.c when using mp4box, which causes a denial of service.

code_snippets:
if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;
		else if (esd->OCRESID == esd->ESID) esd->OCRESID = 0;
vulnerabilities.description: If gf_isom_get_esd() returns NULL for the scene track (sceneT), the code dereferences the NULL pointer when accessing esd->decoderConfig inside the sample-processing block, and later unconditionally accesses esd->OCRESID. The same issue exists for the OD track (odT) esd if gf_isom_get_esd returns NULL there.

code_snippets:
if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;
else if (esd->OCRESID == esd->ESID) esd->OCRESID = 0;

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with IOD_Profile == GF_SDP_IOD_ISMA and a file that has a scene track (sceneT != 0).
The code enters the IOD_Profile == GF_SDP_IOD_ISMA branch and calls gf_isom_get_esd(file, sceneT, 1), which may return NULL if the track has no valid ESD.

If gf_isom_get_sample_count(file, sceneT)==1, the code enters the block and calls gf_isom_get_sample. If samp is valid and gf_hinter_can_embbed_data returns true, the code dereferences esd->decoderConfig, causing a NULL pointer dereference.
Even if the sample count is not 1, execution reaches 'if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;' which also dereferences the NULL esd pointer unconditionally.

commit_message:
[misc] Fix use-after-free in net_bind()

Thanks to Ba Jinsheng for reporting this bug
cve_desc: OwnTone (aka owntone-server) through 28.1 has a use-after-free in net_bind() in misc.c.

vulnerabilities.description: After freeaddrinfo(servinfo) is called, the pointer 'ptr' (which points into the freed servinfo linked list) is dereferenced to access ptr->ai_addr and ptr->ai_addrlen. This is a use-after-free bug.

step_by_step_execution.description: getaddrinfo is called, allocating the servinfo linked list.
The for loop iterates over the servinfo list. When bind() succeeds, the loop breaks with ptr pointing to the successful addrinfo node.

state.value: points to a node within the servinfo linked list

step_by_step_execution.description: freeaddrinfo(servinfo) is called, freeing the entire addrinfo linked list including the node ptr points to.
The check '!ptr' is false (ptr is non-null but dangling), so execution continues past the error check.
getsockname(fd, ptr->ai_addr, &ptr->ai_addrlen) dereferences the dangling pointer ptr, reading ptr->ai_addr and ptr->ai_addrlen from freed memory. This is a use-after-free.

commit_message: fixed #2194
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository gpac/gpac prior to v2.1.0-DEV.

vulnerabilities.description: The code calls gf_node_unregister twice on the node, which decrements the reference count twice. The comment says 'register TWICE' but the code actually unregisters twice. If the node's reference count after gf_bifs_dec_node is 1 (or 2), the first (or second) unregister call frees the node. The node pointer is then stored in inf->new_node, codec->ActiveQP, and codec->scenegraph->global_qp — all of which become dangling pointers to freed memory, leading to use-after-free when any of these pointers are later dereferenced.

step_by_step_execution.description: gf_bifs_dec_node decodes a node from the bitstream. The returned node has an initial reference count (e.g., 1).

state.value: valid pointer to a decoded GF_Node

step_by_step_execution.description: The tag check passes (TAG_MPEG4_QuantizationParameter), so execution continues to the registration block.
codec->ActiveQP is set to node and codec->scenegraph->global_qp is set to node without incrementing reference count.
First gf_node_unregister(node, NULL) is called, decrementing the reference count to 0, which frees the node.
Second gf_node_unregister(node, NULL) is called on the already-freed node — this is a double-free / use-after-free.
The freed node pointer is stored into inf->new_node, codec->ActiveQP, and codec->scenegraph->global_qp, creating persistent dangling references that will be dereferenced later.

commit_message: fixed #1885
cve_desc: Segmentation fault vulnerability exists in Gpac through 1.0.1 via the gf_odf_size_descriptor function in desc_private.c when using mp4box, which causes a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: If gf_isom_get_esd(file, sceneT, 1) returns NULL, the code unconditionally dereferences esd when accessing esd->OCRESID and esd->ESID outside the sample-count if-block, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with IOD_Profile == GF_SDP_IOD_ISMA and a valid file that has a scene track.
The code enters the ISMA IOD rewriting block. After handling the OD track (if any), it calls gf_isom_get_esd for the scene track.

state.value: return value of gf_isom_get_esd(file, sceneT, 1), which may be NULL

step_by_step_execution.description: If the scene track's sample count is not exactly 1, the inner if-block is skipped entirely. esd is still whatever gf_isom_get_esd returned (possibly NULL).

state.value: !=1, so the if block is skipped

step_by_step_execution.description: The code reaches 'if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;' which dereferences esd without a NULL check, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.description: The base64 encoding output buffer buf64 is 5000 bytes but the limit parameter passed to gf_base64_encode is 2000. If gf_base64_encode does not properly enforce the output limit and the input data is large enough, the base64 output could exceed buf64's 5000-byte capacity. Furthermore, the encoded IOD (buffer, size) is not bounded — the IOD can grow arbitrarily with embedded ESD data, and if the base64 output exceeds buf64 or sdpLine capacity, a stack buffer overflow occurs.

step_by_step_execution.description: The IOD descriptor is written to a buffer via gf_odf_desc_write. The IOD may contain embedded ES descriptors with URLStrings containing base64-encoded sample data.

state.value: potentially large (depends on IOD content and embedded data)

step_by_step_execution.description: gf_base64_encode is called with buffer of 'size' bytes, output to buf64 (5000 bytes), with output_size parameter 2000. If gf_base64_encode doesn't enforce the output limit and 'size' > ~1500 bytes, the base64 output (4/3 * size) can exceed 2000 and potentially exceed 5000 bytes, overflowing buf64.

state.value: could exceed 5000 if size is large enough and limit is not enforced
stack buffer of 5000 bytes, potentially overflowed

step_by_step_execution.description: buf64[size64] = 0 writes a null terminator potentially out of bounds of the 5000-byte buf64 array, causing a stack buffer overflow.

patch 8.2.4215: illegal memory access when copying lines in Visual mode

Problem:    Illegal memory access when copying lines in Visual mode.
Solution:   Adjust the Visual position after copying lines.

commit_message:
FTP: Fix buffer overflow in PORT parser, reported by Aaron Esau

Signed-off-by: Joachim Nilsson <troglobit@gmail.com>
cve_desc: An unauthenticated stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in common.c's handle_PORT in uftpd FTP server versions 2.10 and earlier can be abused to cause a crash and could potentially lead to remote code execution.

vulnerabilities.description: The values a, b, c, d are parsed from user input as arbitrary integers (not bounded to 0-255). sprintf then formats them into a fixed-size buffer `addr[INET_ADDRSTRLEN]` (16 bytes). A malicious client can supply large integer values (e.g., very large negative numbers like -2147483648) that produce a string far exceeding 15 characters, causing a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: User-controlled string `str` is passed to sscanf, which parses six integers without range validation.
sprintf formats a, b, c, d into addr buffer. Each value can be up to 11 characters (e.g. -2147483648), plus 3 dots and a null terminator = up to 47 bytes.
The formatted string overflows the 16-byte addr buffer, corrupting adjacent stack memory (return address, other locals).

commit_message: Make line_no with too large value(2**20) invalid. Fixes #124
cve_desc: tinyexr 0.9.5 has a integer overflow over-write in tinyexr::DecodePixelData in tinyexr.h, related to OpenEXR code.

vulnerabilities.description: On 32-bit platforms (sizeof(void*) == 4), size_t is 32 bits. The multiplication of data_width * data_height * num_channels can overflow a 32-bit size_t, wrapping around to a small value. The overflow check `total_data_len_overflown` is explicitly set to `false` on 32-bit systems, so no overflow detection occurs. This leads to AllocateImage allocating a buffer far too small for the actual data, and subsequent DecodePixelData calls write out of bounds.

code_snippets:
if (!tinyexr::DecodePixelData(
                      exr_image->images, exr_header->requested_pixel_types,
                      data_ptr, static_cast<size_t>(data_len),
                      exr_header->compression_type, exr_header->line_order,
                      data_width, data_height, data_width, y, line_no,
                      num_lines, static_cast<size_t>(pixel_data_size),
                      ...))

step_by_step_execution.description: data_width and data_height are computed from data_window and pass the negative and threshold checks. For example, on a 32-bit platform, data_width = 65536 and data_height = 65537, num_channels = 1.

total_data_len is computed as size_t(65536) * size_t(65537) * size_t(1). On a 32-bit platform, size_t is 32 bits. 65536 * 65537 = 4,295,032,832 which exceeds UINT32_MAX (4,294,967,295), so it wraps around to 65536.
The overflow check is skipped on 32-bit: total_data_len_overflown is set to false because sizeof(void*) != 8. total_data_len (65536) is non-zero, so both checks pass.
AllocateImage is called with data_width=65536 and data_height=65537. Internally, it likely computes allocation sizes that also overflow on 32-bit, resulting in a small buffer allocation.
DecodePixelData is called in the loop with the true data_width=65536 and data_height=65537 dimensions, writing decoded pixel data into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: The return value of calloc for exr_image->tiles is never checked for NULL. If the allocation fails (e.g., num_tiles is very large or memory is exhausted), the code dereferences a NULL pointer on the very next line inside the loop when accessing exr_image->tiles[tile_idx].images.

step_by_step_execution.description: The code enters the tiled branch (exr_header->tiled is true). num_tiles is derived from offsets.size().
calloc is called with sizeof(EXRTile) * num_tiles. If num_tiles is large enough, calloc fails and returns NULL.
The loop begins with tile_idx = 0 and dereferences exr_image->tiles[0].images, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
xfrm: policy: check policy direction value

The 'dir' parameter in xfrm_migrate() is a user-controlled byte which is used
as an array index. This can lead to an out-of-bound access, kernel lockup and
DoS. Add a check for the 'dir' value.

This fixes CVE-2017-11600.

References: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1474928
Fixes: 80c9abaabf42 ("[XFRM]: Extension for dynamic update of endpoint address(es)")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # v2.6.21-rc1
Reported-by: "bo Zhang" <zhangbo5891001@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Vladis Dronov <vdronov@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c in the Linux kernel through 4.12.3, when CONFIG_XFRM_MIGRATE is enabled, does not ensure that the dir value of xfrm_userpolicy_id is XFRM_POLICY_MAX or less, which allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds access) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an XFRM_MSG_MIGRATE xfrm Netlink message.

commit_message:
Add a check for pad width to be a positive value.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 413275853
Change-Id: I261a8db9dabf5ce48a806a9e58129080c9fac619
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `StringNGrams` can be used to trigger a denial of service attack by causing an out of memory condition after an integer overflow. We are missing a validation on `pad_witdh` and that result in computing a negative value for `ngram_width` which is later used to allocate parts of the output. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow when casting 64-bit tensor sizes to 32-bit int. If the splits tensor has more than INT_MAX elements (e.g. 2^31+2), splits_vec_size overflows to a negative value, causing the validation block to be skipped entirely (splits_vec_size > 0 is false). Similarly, num_batch_items overflows to a negative value, causing the ngrams_splits population loop to not execute. Subsequently, ngrams_splits_data[num_batch_items] performs an out-of-bounds read with a large negative index on the allocated tensor.

step_by_step_execution.description: The splits tensor is provided with a size exceeding INT_MAX, e.g., 2^31 + 2 elements. The data tensor has at least 1 element.
input_data_size and splits_vec_size are computed by truncating 64-bit sizes to 32-bit int, causing overflow.

state.value: truncated to int, value depends on actual data size

step_by_step_execution.description: The validation block is guarded by 'if (splits_vec_size > 0)'. Since splits_vec_size is negative, the entire validation of split values is skipped.
num_batch_items is computed from the 64-bit size minus 1, then truncated to int.
The ngrams_splits output is allocated with splits->shape (2^31+2 elements). The early return check 'splits_vec.size() == 0' is false, so execution continues.
ngrams_splits_data[0] is set to 0. The loop 'for (int i = 1; i <= num_batch_items; ++i)' does not execute because 1 <= -2147483647 is false.

ngrams_splits_data[num_batch_items] is accessed as an index for output allocation. Since num_batch_items is -2147483647, this reads memory before the start of the allocated tensor buffer, constituting an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: out-of-bounds read at large negative offset

commit_message:
exec: --cap do not set inheritable capabilities

Closes: CVE-2022-27650

Signed-off-by: Giuseppe Scrivano <gscrivan@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in crun where containers were incorrectly started with non-empty default permissions. A vulnerability was found in Moby (Docker Engine) where containers were started incorrectly with non-empty inheritable Linux process capabilities. This flaw allows an attacker with access to programs with inheritable file capabilities to elevate those capabilities to the permitted set when execve(2) runs.

vulnerabilities.description: The loop iterates `argc - first_arg` times, accessing `argv[first_arg + i + 1]`. On the last iteration (i = argc - first_arg - 1), the index becomes `first_arg + (argc - first_arg - 1) + 1 = argc`. By the C standard, `argv[argc]` is NULL, so `xstrdup(NULL)` is called, causing a NULL pointer dereference. Additionally, `process->args_len` is set to `argc` instead of the actual number of exec arguments (`argc - first_arg - 1`), which is an incorrect length that could cause out-of-bounds access by callers.

step_by_step_execution.description: argp_parse processes options and sets first_arg to the index of the first non-option argument.
crun_assert_n_args checks argc - first_arg >= 2 (since exec_options.process is NULL in the else branch). This passes, e.g. 4 - 1 = 3 >= 2.
The else branch is entered. process->args is allocated with (argc + 1) entries, and process->args_len is set to argc.
Loop runs with i from 0 to argc - first_arg - 1 = 2. On last iteration i=2, accesses argv[first_arg + 2 + 1] = argv[1 + 2 + 1] = argv[4] = argv[argc].
xstrdup(NULL) is called, resulting in a NULL pointer dereference (passing NULL to strdup/strlen).

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB in sparse reduction ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387934524
Change-Id: I894aa30f1e454f09b471d565b4a325da49322c1a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a1bc56203f21a5a4995311825ffaba7a670d7747/tensorflow/core/kernels/sparse_reduce_op.cc#L217-L228) fails to validate that each reduction group does not overflow and that each corresponding index does not point to outside the bounds of the input tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 87158f43f05f2720a374f3e6d22a7aaa3a33f750. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
const int64_t idx = CoordinatesToFlatIndex(g.group(), output_strides);
      out_flat(idx) = reduced_val();
vulnerabilities.description: The index `idx` computed by `CoordinatesToFlatIndex` is derived from sparse tensor coordinates (which originate from user-supplied `input_indices`) and user-supplied `input_shape`. There is no bounds check ensuring `idx` falls within [0, out_flat.size()). A malformed sparse tensor with out-of-range indices (negative or exceeding declared shape) can produce an `idx` that is out of bounds for the output tensor, leading to an out-of-bounds memory write.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides `input_indices`, `input_values`, `input_shape`, and `reduction_axes` tensors. The indices tensor can contain arbitrary coordinate values that are not validated against the shape.

state.value: user-controlled, e.g. contains coordinates like [999, 999] for a 2x2 shape

step_by_step_execution.description: A SparseTensor `sp` is created from deep copies of the user-supplied indices, values, and shape. No validation is done to ensure indices are within bounds of the declared shape.
Output tensor `out_values` is allocated with `reduction.reduced_shape`, which is derived from `input_shape` and `reduction_axes`. For a 2x2 input reduced on one axis, output might have size 2.

state.value: e.g. 2 (matching reduced shape from declared input_shape)

step_by_step_execution.description: Output strides are computed from `shape_vec` (the declared shape), so they reflect the expected output layout.

state.value: computed from declared shape, e.g. [1] for single remaining dim of size 2

step_by_step_execution.description: During iteration over groups, `g.group()` returns coordinates derived from the actual sparse tensor indices (which can be out of range). `CoordinatesToFlatIndex` computes idx = coords[0]*strides[0] + ... which can yield a large or negative value.
The out-of-bounds index is used to write to the output tensor with `out_flat(idx) = reduced_val()`, causing an out-of-bounds memory write.
