commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-26j7-ww69-c4qj
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions prior to and including 2.12.1 a stack buffer overflow vulnerability affects PJSIP users that use STUN in their applications, either by: setting a STUN server in their account/media config in PJSUA/PJSUA2 level, or directly using `pjlib-util/stun_simple` API. A patch is available in commit 450baca which should be included in the next release. There are no known workarounds for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The while loop parses attributes and increments msg->attr_count without ever checking if it exceeds the bounds of the msg->attr array. A crafted STUN message with many small attributes can cause writes beyond the end of the fixed-size msg->attr array, leading to a stack or heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a buffer and parses the STUN header, extracting msg_len from the header.

state.value: value from header, e.g. large enough to contain many attributes

step_by_step_execution.description: msg->attr_count is initialized to 0 and p_attr points past the header.
The while loop iterates over attributes. Each attribute has a minimum size of sizeof(pjstun_attr_hdr) (4 bytes typically). A crafted message with many minimal attributes will iterate many times.

state.value: sizeof(pjstun_attr_hdr) rounded up to 4-byte boundary (e.g., 4 bytes)

step_by_step_execution.description: On each iteration, msg->attr[msg->attr_count] is written to. If msg->attr is a fixed-size array (e.g., PJSTUN_MAX_ATTR elements), and the number of attributes exceeds this limit, the write goes out of bounds.

state.value: incremented each iteration with no upper bound check, can exceed array size

step_by_step_execution.description: The out-of-bounds write at &msg->attr[msg->attr_count] overwrites adjacent memory, causing a buffer overflow.

state.value: greater than the allocated size of msg->attr array

commit_message: fixed #1905
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a null pointer dereference vulnerability in the mpgviddmx_process function in reframe_mpgvid.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service. This vulnerability is possibly due to an incomplete fix for CVE-2021-40566.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in `ctx->hdr_store_size + pck_size` (both u32) can wrap around to a small value, causing the reallocation check to be skipped. The subsequent memcpy then writes `pck_size` bytes at offset `ctx->hdr_store_size` in a buffer that is too small, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters with ctx->resume_from == 0 and ctx->hdr_store_size > 0 from a previous iteration that stored partial header data.
A new packet is received and its size is obtained. pck_size is a u32 value.

The overflow check is evaluated: ctx->hdr_store_alloc < ctx->hdr_store_size + pck_size. The addition ctx->hdr_store_size + pck_size overflows u32: 0xFFFF0000 + 0x00020000 = 0x00010000 (wraps around). Since ctx->hdr_store_alloc (0xFFFF0000) is NOT less than the wrapped value (0x00010000), the reallocation is skipped.

memcpy writes pck_size (0x00020000) bytes to ctx->hdr_store at offset ctx->hdr_store_size (0xFFFF0000). The buffer is only ctx->hdr_store_alloc (0xFFFF0000) bytes large, so writing 0x00020000 bytes at offset 0xFFFF0000 writes 0x00020000 bytes past the end of the heap buffer.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow of u64 variable `size` when gf_m4v_parse_frame returns GF_EOS with size < 3. The assert(size >= 3) is compiled out in release builds, so size -= 3 underflows to a very large u64 value. This value is then cast to u32 for allocation and used as size_t for memcpy, causing a mismatch and potential heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: gf_m4v_parse_frame is called and returns GF_EOS with a small size value (e.g., 0, 1, or 2), indicating not enough data to parse a complete frame.
The code enters the GF_EOS handling block since ctx->input_is_au_end is false. It reads the last 3 bytes of the remaining data.

state.value: last 3 bytes of start buffer, at least one is 0

step_by_step_execution.description: Since at least one of b1/b2/b3 is zero, copy_last_bytes is set. The assert(size >= 3) is a no-op in release builds. size -= 3 underflows the u64 variable.

Allocation is attempted with (u32) size which truncates to ~0xFFFFFFFD. If allocation succeeds on a 32-bit system or with a smaller wrap value, the subsequent memcpy with (size_t) size reads far beyond the source buffer.

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.slice() with slow "this" argument.

Previously, when "this" argument was not a fast array, but the "deleted" array
was a fast array, the "deleted" array may be left in uninitialized state if
"this" argument had gaps.

This fix is to ensure that "deleted" is properly initialized.

This fixes #485 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_value_own_enumerate at src/njs_value.c.

vulnerabilities.description: TOCTOU (Time-of-check Time-of-use) vulnerability: The array length is fetched once via njs_object_length(), then njs_value_to_integer() is called on user-supplied arguments. These calls can trigger valueOf()/toString() callbacks which may modify the underlying array (e.g., shrink it). The cached 'length' is then used to compute 'start' and 'delete', which can exceed the actual array bounds. In the fast array path, this leads to out-of-bounds reads when copying deleted elements and out-of-bounds writes via memcpy when inserting new items.

code_snippets:
ret = njs_array_copy_within(vm, this, start + items, start + delete,
                                        array->length - (start + delete), 0);

step_by_step_execution.description: njs_object_length() retrieves the length property of the array. Assume 'this' is a fast array with length 10.

njs_value_to_integer() is called on args[1] (the start argument). If args[1] is an object with a valueOf() method that shrinks the array (e.g., sets this.length = 0 or splices elements), the callback executes and reduces the array's actual allocated/valid size.
start is computed from the stale length: start = njs_min(start_arg, length). For example, if start_arg is 0, start = 0.
With nargs == 2, delete is computed as length - start = 10 - 0 = 10, based on the stale length.
njs_array_alloc allocates 'deleted' array with size 10.

njs_is_fast_array(this) is checked and may still return true (the object type hasn't changed, only internal length/data). The code enters the fast path and iterates: for n = 0..9, reads array->start[n]. But array->length is now 0 (or the backing buffer was reallocated to a smaller size), so indices 0..9 are out of bounds.

In the second fast path block, array->length - (start + delete) = 0 - (0 + 10) = -10. This negative value is passed as a count to njs_array_copy_within, potentially causing further undefined behavior. Then array->length += delta sets array->length to -10 (or a very large unsigned value).

commit_message:
Fix security vulnerability with LSTMBlockCellOp

PiperOrigin-RevId: 446028341
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.LSTMBlockCell` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code does not validate the ranks of any of the arguments to this API call. This results in `CHECK`-failures when the elements of the tensor are accessed. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The rank checks (e.g., x_tensor->dims() == 2, cs_prev_tensor->dims() == 2) are performed far too late — after dim_size(0) and dim_size(1) have already been called on those tensors. If a malicious or malformed input provides a scalar (rank-0) or rank-1 tensor for 'x' or 'cs_prev', calling dim_size(1) on it leads to an out-of-bounds read from the tensor's shape metadata array, potentially causing a crash or information disclosure.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function retrieves the input tensor 'x'. An attacker provides a rank-0 or rank-1 tensor for 'x'.
The function retrieves 'cs_prev' tensor. An attacker could also provide a rank-0 or rank-1 tensor.

The code calls x_tensor->dim_size(0) and x_tensor->dim_size(1) without first verifying x_tensor has rank >= 2. For a rank-0 tensor, dim_size(0) and dim_size(1) access out-of-bounds indices in the shape array.
Similarly, cs_prev_tensor->dim_size(1) is called without verifying rank >= 2, causing another potential out-of-bounds read.

The rank-2 checks (x_tensor->dims() == 2, cs_prev_tensor->dims() == 2) that would have caught this are located much later in the function, after allocations and other dim_size calls, so they never get a chance to prevent the earlier out-of-bounds accesses.

commit_message:
RDMA/rtrs-clt: Fix possible double free in error case

Callback function rtrs_clt_dev_release() for put_device() calls kfree(clt)
to free memory. We shouldn't call kfree(clt) again, and we can't use the
clt after kfree too.

Replace device_register() with device_initialize() and device_add() so that
dev_set_name can() be used appropriately.

Move mutex_destroy() to the release function so it can be called in
the alloc_clt err path.

Fixes: eab098246625 ("RDMA/rtrs-clt: Refactor the failure cases in alloc_clt")
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220217030929.323849-1-haris.iqbal@ionos.com
Reported-by: Miaoqian Lin <linmq006@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Md Haris Iqbal <haris.iqbal@ionos.com>
Reviewed-by: Jack Wang <jinpu.wang@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Jason Gunthorpe <jgg@nvidia.com>
cve_desc: drivers/infiniband/ulp/rtrs/rtrs-clt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.12 has a double free related to rtrs_clt_dev_release.

commit_message:

Callback function rtrs_clt_dev_release() for put_device() calls kfree(clt)

commit_message:
password: Use crypto_memneq() to compare hashes

Cryptographic verifications should be time-constant so that an attacker
cannot get information about the secrets used by observing the system,
so use crypto_memneq() rather than memcmp() to compare password hashes.

Signed-off-by: Sascha Hauer <s.hauer@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: common/password.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because strncmp is used during hash comparison.

vulnerabilities.description: strncmp is used to compare cryptographic hash/key outputs (unsigned char arrays that may contain null bytes). strncmp stops comparing at the first null byte ('\0'). If both the computed hash and stored hash happen to share a null byte at the same position early in the buffer, strncmp will return 0 (equal) having only compared a small prefix, effectively bypassing password verification. The correct function to use is memcmp, which compares the full length regardless of null bytes.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with an attacker-controlled passwd and length.
hash_len is determined (either PBKDF2_LENGTH or digest_length(d)). passwd1_sum is allocated with calloc(hash_len * 2, ...), so it's zero-initialized. passwd2_sum points to passwd1_sum + hash_len.
The stored password hash is read into passwd2_sum via read_env_passwd or read_default_passwd. This hash is a binary value that may contain 0x00 bytes at arbitrary positions.

state.value: stored password hash, may contain embedded null bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: The attacker-supplied password is hashed into passwd1_sum (via PBKDF2 or digest_digest). The attacker can try many passwords; some will produce hashes with a 0x00 in the first byte.

state.value: computed hash of attacker input, may have 0x00 as first byte

step_by_step_execution.description: strncmp(passwd1_sum, passwd2_sum, hash_len) is called. If passwd1_sum[0] == 0x00 and passwd2_sum[0] == 0x00, strncmp immediately returns 0 after comparing only the first byte, treating the buffers as equal. The probability of the stored hash having a null first byte is ~1/256, and the attacker can brute-force inputs until their hash also starts with 0x00.

state.value: 0 (equal) — only 1 byte compared instead of hash_len bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: ret is set to 1, indicating successful authentication despite the password being incorrect.

commit_message:
Don't do any work when reshaping 0 elements sparse tensor.

If reshaping to 0 elements tensor, check that input has no elements.
If reshaping no elements input, check that output has no elements.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388296986
Change-Id: Iadc9fe7252e14313ca987e69bf0d7042fd10232a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.SparseReshape` can be made to trigger an integral division by 0 exception. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L176-L181) calls the reshaping functor whenever there is at least an index in the input but does not check that shape of the input or the target shape have both a non-zero number of elements. The [reshape functor](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L40-L78) blindly divides by the dimensions of the target shape. Hence, if this is not checked, code will result in a division by 0. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4923de56ec94fff7770df259ab7f2288a74feb41. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1 as this is the other affected version.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `product`. The variable `product` (int64_t) is multiplied by each specified dimension size in a loop without overflow checking. An attacker can provide large dimension values in `target_shape_in` that cause `product` to overflow, potentially wrapping to a small positive value. This corrupted `product` then leads to an incorrect `missing` dimension calculation (`dense_size / product`), resulting in a shape that does not truly match `dense_size`. This can cause out-of-bounds memory access in the subsequent `ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor` call.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives `target_shape_in` with multiple large dimension values (e.g., two dimensions each close to 2^32) and one dimension of -1.
Loop iteration d=0: size is -1, so unknown_index is set to 0, output_shape gets dim 1.
Loop iteration d=1: size is 2^32 (passes size >= 0 check), product is multiplied by 2^32.
Loop iteration d=2: size is 2^32 (passes size >= 0 check), product is multiplied by 2^32 again, overflowing int64_t (2^64 wraps around). The result wraps to 0 or another incorrect value.

state.value: 0 or wrapped value due to signed integer overflow (undefined behavior)

step_by_step_execution.description: If product wraps to a positive value, the check `product > 0` passes. Then `missing = dense_size / product` computes an incorrect dimension size. The subsequent check `product * missing == dense_size` may also pass due to overflow arithmetic, allowing an inconsistent output_shape to be constructed.

state.value: incorrect value derived from overflowed product
dimensions that do not truly correspond to dense_size

step_by_step_execution.description: The ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor is invoked with the inconsistent output_shape, potentially causing out-of-bounds reads/writes when converting sparse indices.

state.value: allocated with dimensions based on corrupted output_shape

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5050: using freed memory when searching for pattern in path

Problem:    Using freed memory when searching for pattern in path.
Solution:   Make a copy of the line.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: When the file stack needs to grow because `depth + 1 == old_files`, if the `ALLOC_MULT` allocation for `bigger` fails (returns NULL), `files` is not resized but execution falls through to `files[depth + 1].fp = mch_fopen(...)`. Since `depth + 1 == old_files == max_path_depth` (the total number of elements allocated), this writes one element past the end of the `files` heap buffer, causing an out-of-bounds heap write. Subsequent accesses to `files[depth]` after `++depth` (e.g., setting `.name`, `.lnum`, `.matched`) further corrupt heap memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `files` array is allocated with `max_path_depth` (50) entries. `old_files` is set to 50, `depth` starts at -1.
Through processing 50 levels of nested #include files, depth reaches 49. At each level, depth is incremented and files[depth] is populated.
A 51st nested #include is encountered. The code checks `depth + 1 == old_files` (50 == 50), which is true, and attempts to reallocate.

ALLOC_MULT for `bigger` fails and returns NULL (e.g., due to memory pressure). The `if (bigger != NULL)` block is skipped entirely. `files` remains the original 50-element array. `max_path_depth` and `old_files` are NOT updated.

Execution falls through to `files[depth + 1].fp = mch_fopen(...)`. This accesses `files[50]`, which is one past the end of the 50-element heap buffer. If fopen succeeds, an fp value is written out-of-bounds.

After fopen succeeds, `++depth` makes depth=50, which equals old_files. The recovery code runs but itself accesses `files[old_files]` = `files[50]` (out-of-bounds) for vim_free and then writes `.name`, `.lnum`, `.matched` to `files[50]`.

commit_message:
rsi: release skb if rsi_prepare_beacon fails

In rsi_send_beacon, if rsi_prepare_beacon fails the allocated skb should
be released.

Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the rsi_send_beacon() function in drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_mgmt.c in the Linux kernel through 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering rsi_prepare_beacon() failures, aka CID-d563131ef23c.

vulnerabilities.description: When rsi_prepare_beacon() fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the allocated skb, causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: An skb is allocated via dev_alloc_skb(MAX_MGMT_PKT_SIZE).
Alignment adjustment is performed on skb->data.
rsi_prepare_beacon() is called and returns a non-zero (failure) value.
The function returns -EINVAL without calling dev_kfree_skb(skb), leaking the allocated skb.

state.value: allocated but never freed — memory leak

commit_message:
Fix security vulnerability with LSTMBlockCellOp

PiperOrigin-RevId: 446028341
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.LSTMBlockCell` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code does not validate the ranks of any of the arguments to this API call. This results in `CHECK`-failures when the elements of the tensor are accessed. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The shapes of wci_tensor, wcf_tensor, and wco_tensor are never validated against cell_size before being passed as vec<T>() to the LSTM functor. The functor will index these vectors assuming they have cell_size elements, but an attacker can supply tensors of arbitrary size, leading to out-of-bounds memory reads/writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: Inputs are read; x_tensor, cs_prev_tensor, h_prev_tensor, w_tensor, wci_tensor, wcf_tensor, wco_tensor, b_tensor are loaded from the op context.
Shape checks are performed for cs_prev, h_prev, w, and b tensors. However, no shape checks are performed for wci_tensor, wcf_tensor, or wco_tensor.

state.value: unchecked, could be any shape, e.g. a vector of size 1
unchecked, could be any shape, e.g. a vector of size 1
unchecked, could be any shape, e.g. a vector of size 1

step_by_step_execution.description: Output and temp tensors are allocated based on batch_size and cell_size.

LSTMBlockCellFprop is called with wci_tensor->vec<T>(), wcf_tensor->vec<T>(), wco_tensor->vec<T>(). The functor assumes these vectors have cell_size elements and indexes them up to index cell_size-1 (e.g. 127). If the actual vector has fewer elements (e.g. 1), this results in out-of-bounds memory access.

commit_message:
exec: --cap do not set inheritable capabilities

Closes: CVE-2022-27650

Signed-off-by: Giuseppe Scrivano <gscrivan@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in crun where containers were incorrectly started with non-empty default permissions. A vulnerability was found in Moby (Docker Engine) where containers were started incorrectly with non-empty inheritable Linux process capabilities. This flaw allows an attacker with access to programs with inheritable file capabilities to elevate those capabilities to the permitted set when execve(2) runs.

vulnerabilities.description: The loop iterates `argc - first_arg` times while accessing `argv[first_arg + i + 1]`. On the last iteration (i = argc - first_arg - 1), the index becomes `first_arg + (argc - first_arg - 1) + 1 = argc`. `argv[argc]` is NULL by the C standard, so `xstrdup(NULL)` is called, causing a NULL pointer dereference. Additionally, `process->args_len` is set to `argc` (the total argument count) instead of the actual number of elements copied (`argc - first_arg - 1`), which causes downstream consumers of the `process` struct to read uninitialized/NULL entries as valid arguments, leading to out-of-bounds access.

step_by_step_execution.description: argp_parse processes options and sets first_arg to the index of the first non-option argument. crun_assert_n_args ensures argc - first_arg >= 2 (container name + at least one command arg).
The else branch is taken (exec_options.process is NULL). process is allocated and process->args_len is set to argc (the total argument count, not the number of exec arguments).

state.value: 4 (should be 2, the actual command args count)

step_by_step_execution.description: process->args is allocated with (argc + 1) entries. The loop runs for i = 0 to i < argc - first_arg (i.e., i < 3, so i = 0, 1, 2).

On the last iteration (i=2), argv[first_arg + i + 1] = argv[1 + 2 + 1] = argv[4] = argv[argc]. By the C standard argv[argc] is NULL, so xstrdup(NULL) is called, resulting in a NULL pointer dereference.

Even if the NULL dereference doesn't crash (e.g., xstrdup handles NULL), process->args_len is argc (4), but only argc - first_arg entries (3) are populated. Downstream code iterating args_len entries will read NULL/uninitialized pointers from the array.

state.value: 3 (argc - first_arg), with last one being NULL from xstrdup(NULL)

commit_message: Clean up handling CAP LS
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in the client in HexChat 2.11.0 allows remote IRC servers to read or modify arbitrary files via a .. (dot dot) in the server name.

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.lastIndexOf() with unicode string as "this".

Previously, when lastIndexOf() was called with unicode string as "this"
argument and a negative "fromIndex" argument null-pointer dererence
might occur because njs_string_offset() was called with invalid index
value whereas njs_string_offset() should always be called with valid
index argument.

The fix is to verify that from index is valid.

This closes #482 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_string_offset at src/njs_string.c.

vulnerabilities.description: In the ASCII/byte string branch, the pointer `p` is computed as `string_prop.start + from` without validating that `from` is within the bounds of the string (i.e., `from < string_prop.size`). If `args->from` is greater than or equal to `string_prop.size`, the pointer `p` will point outside the string buffer, and the subsequent read via `njs_string_new` will perform an out-of-bounds read. Similarly, if `to` is negative or less than 0, the loop will decrement `p` past the beginning of the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads `from` and `to` from `args` without bounds validation against the string length.
The value is determined to be a string type, entering the string processing branch.
String properties are extracted. For a short ASCII string, size and length are equal.
The condition `(size_t) length == string_prop.size` is true, entering the ASCII branch. Pointer `p` is computed as `string_prop.start + from`, which is past the end of the buffer.

state.value: string_prop.start + 100 (95 bytes past end of 5-byte buffer)

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop begins with `i = from + 1 = 101`. The condition `i-- > to` (100 > 0) is true. `njs_string_new` is called with `p` pointing out of bounds, reading 1 byte from invalid memory.

vulnerabilities.description: In the UTF-8 string branch, `njs_string_offset` is called with `from` that may exceed the string's character length, causing the returned pointer to go past `end`. Subsequently, `njs_utf8_next` and `njs_utf8_prev` operate on out-of-bounds pointers, and `njs_string_new` reads from an out-of-bounds memory location.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads `from` and `to` from `args` without bounds validation.
The value is a UTF-8 string with multi-byte characters, so `length != string_prop.size`, entering the UTF-8 branch.
`njs_string_offset` is called to find the byte offset of character index `from` (100), which exceeds the string's character length (10). The returned pointer goes past `end`.
`njs_utf8_next(p, end)` is called on the out-of-bounds pointer, and subsequently `njs_utf8_prev(p)` and `njs_string_new` read from out-of-bounds memory.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-7fw8-54cv-r7pm
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions 2.11.1 and prior, parsing an incoming SIP message that contains a malformed multipart can potentially cause out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all PJSIP users that accept SIP multipart. The patch is available as commit in the `master` branch. There are no known workarounds.

commit_message:
fix possible pattern crash

issue #325
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in tildearrow Furnace. It has been classified as problematic. This is due to an incomplete fix of CVE-2022-1211. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely but it requires user interaction. The issue got fixed with the patch 0eb02422d5161767e9983bdaa5c429762d3477ce.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on the `extFxColors` array. The effect value `pat->data[i][index]` is checked to be >= 0xe0 (by falling through all prior conditions), but there is no upper bound check. If the value is significantly larger than 0xFF (pattern data is stored as int), the index `pat->data[i][index] - 0xe0` can be arbitrarily large, reading beyond the `extFxColors` array bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function iterates over channels and their effect rows in the inner loop `for (int k=0; k<e->song.pat[j].effectRows; k++)`.
For a given effect column, `pat->data[i][index]` is read. If it is not -1, the code enters the else branch to determine the color.

The chain of `if/else if` checks tests ranges < 0x10, < 0x20, < 0x30, < 0x48, < 0x90, < 0xa0, < 0xc0, < 0xd0, < 0xe0. A value >= 0xe0 (such as 0x200) falls through all of these to the final `else` branch.

The else branch computes the array index as `pat->data[i][index] - 0xe0` and uses it to index `extFxColors`. With a value of 0x200, the index is 0x200 - 0xe0 = 0x120 (288), which is far beyond the expected bounds of `extFxColors` (typically 32 entries for values 0xe0-0xff).

state.value: 288 (out of bounds for an array expected to have ~32 entries)

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on the `fxColors` array with a negative index. If `pat->data[i][index]` is a negative value other than -1 (e.g., -2), the condition `< 0x10` is true (since negative ints are less than 0x10), leading to `fxColors[negative_value]`, which reads before the start of the array.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function iterates over effect rows. For each effect, `pat->data[i][index]` is checked against -1.
Since the value is not -1, the code enters the else branch to determine the effect color. The first condition checks `pat->data[i][index] < 0x10`.

state.value: -2 (which is < 0x10 since -2 < 16 for signed int comparison)

step_by_step_execution.description: The condition is true, so `fxColors[pat->data[i][index]]` is accessed with index -2, which is an out-of-bounds read before the start of the array.

commit_message:
Don't do any work when reshaping 0 elements sparse tensor.

If reshaping to 0 elements tensor, check that input has no elements.
If reshaping no elements input, check that output has no elements.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388296986
Change-Id: Iadc9fe7252e14313ca987e69bf0d7042fd10232a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.SparseReshape` can be made to trigger an integral division by 0 exception. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L176-L181) calls the reshaping functor whenever there is at least an index in the input but does not check that shape of the input or the target shape have both a non-zero number of elements. The [reshape functor](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L40-L78) blindly divides by the dimensions of the target shape. Hence, if this is not checked, code will result in a division by 0. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4923de56ec94fff7770df259ab7f2288a74feb41. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1 as this is the other affected version.

vulnerabilities.description: The accumulation of `product` via multiplication of user-controlled dimension sizes can cause signed integer overflow (undefined behavior in C++). An attacker can supply multiple large positive dimension values in `target_shape_in` such that their product overflows a 64-bit signed integer. This can wrap `product` to zero (causing division by zero at `dense_size / product`) or to a small/negative value, bypassing subsequent validation checks and leading to incorrect memory allocation sizes or other unexpected behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads `target_shape_in` which contains user-controlled dimension values. `output_rank` is set to the number of elements in `target_shape_in`.

state.value: e.g. 3 (attacker controls the number of dimensions)

step_by_step_execution.description: `product` is initialized to 1 and the loop iterates over each dimension in `target_shape`.

The attacker supplies one dimension as -1 (to trigger the unknown_index path) and two other dimensions with very large values, e.g. size = 2^40 for two dimensions. The check `size >= 0` passes for these values.
On the second iteration, `product *= 1099511627776` (2^40). Product is now 2^40.

On the third iteration, `product *= 1099511627776` (2^40). This results in 2^80, which overflows int64_t (max ~2^63-1). This is signed integer overflow, which is undefined behavior in C++. In practice, it may wrap to 0 or a small value.

state.value: undefined (signed overflow) — may wrap to 0 or negative

step_by_step_execution.description: If `product` wraps to 0, the check `product > 0` in the unknown_index branch fails, but if it wraps to a small positive value, the division `dense_size / product` proceeds with a corrupted divisor, computing an incorrect `missing` value. This leads to an `output_shape` that does not truly match `dense_size`, potentially bypassing the `output_shape.num_elements() == dense_size` check (which itself uses overflowed arithmetic). Subsequent memory allocation and index computation use these incorrect dimensions.

state.value: incorrectly computed value based on overflowed product

commit_message: Fix handling of APNG with 0 delay_den (#313)
cve_desc: libjxl v0.3.7 is affected by a Divide By Zero in issue in lib/extras/codec_apng.cc jxl::DecodeImageAPNG(). When encoding a malicous APNG file using cjxl, an attacker can trigger a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `rowbytes = w * 4` and `imagesize = h * rowbytes`. Both `w`, `h`, `rowbytes`, and `imagesize` are `unsigned int` (32-bit). If `w` is large enough (e.g., >= 2^30), `w * 4` wraps around to a small value. This causes a tiny heap allocation for `frameRaw.p`, but the PNG decoder will write based on the actual image dimensions, leading to a heap buffer overflow.

code_snippets: for (j = 0; j < h; j++) frameRaw.rows[j] = frameRaw.p + j * rowbytes;

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads and validates the PNG signature and IHDR chunk.
Width and height are read from the IHDR chunk as 32-bit unsigned integers.
The check `w > cMaxPNGSize || h > cMaxPNGSize` passes if cMaxPNGSize is large enough to accommodate such values.
rowbytes is computed as w * 4. Since w = 0x40000001, w * 4 = 0x100000004 which truncates to 4 in a 32-bit unsigned int.
imagesize is computed as h * rowbytes = 2 * 4 = 8.
A heap buffer of only 8 bytes is allocated for frameRaw.p.
Row pointers are set up: rows[0] = p + 0, rows[1] = p + 4. These point within the tiny 8-byte buffer.

The PNG decoder (processing_start/processing_data/processing_finish) decodes the image into frameRaw via the row pointers. It believes the image is 0x40000001 pixels wide and writes w*4 = ~4GB bytes per row into a buffer that is only 8 bytes, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

state.variable: bytes written per row by decoder

vulnerabilities.description: When processing an fcTL chunk, the code reads data at offsets up to chunk.p[33] without verifying that the chunk is large enough. A malformed fcTL chunk with insufficient size leads to out-of-bounds heap reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the main chunk reading loop after successfully processing IHDR.
A chunk with id kId_fcTL is read via read_chunk. The chunk may have a small size (e.g., 16 bytes including header).
The code enters the branch for kId_fcTL. Since hasInfo may be false and isAnimated may be true, it proceeds to read fcTL fields.

state.value: true or handling first fcTL after IEND path

step_by_step_execution.description: The code reads from chunk.p + 12 through chunk.p + 33 without checking that chunk.size >= 34. With a chunk of only 16 bytes, accesses at offsets 16-33 read beyond the allocated buffer.

commit_message:
xfrm: policy: check policy direction value

The 'dir' parameter in xfrm_migrate() is a user-controlled byte which is used
as an array index. This can lead to an out-of-bound access, kernel lockup and
DoS. Add a check for the 'dir' value.

This fixes CVE-2017-11600.

References: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1474928
Fixes: 80c9abaabf42 ("[XFRM]: Extension for dynamic update of endpoint address(es)")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # v2.6.21-rc1
Reported-by: "bo Zhang" <zhangbo5891001@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Vladis Dronov <vdronov@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c in the Linux kernel through 4.12.3, when CONFIG_XFRM_MIGRATE is enabled, does not ensure that the dir value of xfrm_userpolicy_id is XFRM_POLICY_MAX or less, which allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds access) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an XFRM_MSG_MIGRATE xfrm Netlink message.

commit_message:
Fix null deref in xnu.kernelcache ##crash

* Reported by @xshad3 via huntr.dev
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.0.

vulnerabilities.description: In the `beach` error-handling label, `obj->cache_buf = NULL` is executed unconditionally, but `obj` can be NULL at that point. Multiple `goto beach` paths occur before `obj` is allocated (e.g., when `prelink_range` is NULL) or after `obj` has been explicitly freed with `R_FREE(obj)`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry: `obj` is declared and initialized to NULL.
`main_mach0` is created successfully, execution continues.
`prelink_range` is obtained from `get_prelink_info_range_from_mach0`. Suppose it returns NULL.
The check `if (!prelink_range)` is true, so execution jumps to `goto beach`.
At the `beach` label, `r_buf_free(fbuf)` is called, then `obj->cache_buf = NULL` dereferences the NULL pointer `obj`, causing a crash.

commit_message:
swtpm: Check header size indicator against expected size (CID 375869)

This fix addresses Coverity issue CID 375869.

Check the header size indicated in the header of the state against the
expected size and return an error code in case the header size indicator
is different. There was only one header size so far since blobheader was
introduced, so we don't need to deal with different sizes.

Without this fix a specially craft header could have cause out-of-bounds
accesses on the byte array containing the swtpm's state.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: swtpm is a libtpms-based TPM emulator with socket, character device, and Linux CUSE interface. Versions prior to 0.5.3, 0.6.2, and 0.7.1 are vulnerable to out-of-bounds read. A specially crafted header of swtpm's state, where the blobheader's hdrsize indicator has an invalid value, may cause an out-of-bounds access when the byte array representing the state of the TPM is accessed. This will likely crash swtpm or prevent it from starting since the state cannot be understood. Users should upgrade to swtpm v0.5.3, v0.6.2, or v0.7.1 to receive a patch. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The length check uses `sizeof(bh)` where `bh` is a `blobheader *` (pointer). This checks against the size of a pointer (4 or 8 bytes depending on architecture) instead of `sizeof(blobheader)` (the size of the struct). If the actual `blobheader` struct is larger than a pointer, the check passes with insufficient data, leading to out-of-bounds reads when accessing struct members like `bh->totlen`, `bh->min_version`, `bh->version`, `bh->hdrsize`, and `bh->flags`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `data` pointing to a buffer and `length` indicating its size. For example, `length` could be 8 on a 64-bit system (or 5 on a 32-bit system), which is smaller than `sizeof(blobheader)` but >= `sizeof(bh)` (pointer size).

state.value: 8 (example on 64-bit; any value >= sizeof(pointer) but < sizeof(blobheader))

step_by_step_execution.description: The guard check `length < sizeof(bh)` is evaluated. Since `bh` is a pointer, `sizeof(bh)` is 4 (32-bit) or 8 (64-bit), not `sizeof(blobheader)`. With `length >= sizeof(pointer)`, the check passes even though the buffer is too small for a full `blobheader` struct.

state.value: size of a pointer: 4 or 8 bytes
larger than sizeof(pointer) (the actual struct size)

step_by_step_execution.description: Code proceeds to access `bh->totlen`, `bh->min_version`, `bh->version`, `bh->hdrsize`, `bh->flags` — all of which may read beyond the bounds of the provided `data` buffer since the buffer is smaller than `sizeof(blobheader)`.

state.value: out-of-bounds read if field offset >= length
out-of-bounds read if field offset >= length

commit_message:
swtpm: Check header size indicator against expected size (CID 375869)

This fix addresses Coverity issue CID 375869.

Check the header size indicated in the header of the state against the
expected size and return an error code in case the header size indicator
is different. There was only one header size so far since blobheader was
introduced, so we don't need to deal with different sizes.

Without this fix a specially craft header could have cause out-of-bounds
accesses on the byte array containing the swtpm's state.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: swtpm is a libtpms-based TPM emulator with socket, character device, and Linux CUSE interface. Versions prior to 0.5.3, 0.6.2, and 0.7.1 are vulnerable to out-of-bounds read. A specially crafted header of swtpm's state, where the blobheader's hdrsize indicator has an invalid value, may cause an out-of-bounds access when the byte array representing the state of the TPM is accessed. This will likely crash swtpm or prevent it from starting since the state cannot be understood. Users should upgrade to swtpm v0.5.3, v0.6.2, or v0.7.1 to receive a patch. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The length check uses `sizeof(bh)` where `bh` is a `blobheader *` (a pointer). This checks against the size of a pointer (4 or 8 bytes depending on architecture), not the size of the `blobheader` structure. If a `blobheader` struct is larger than a pointer, the check passes for buffers too small to hold a full header, leading to out-of-bounds reads when accessing fields of `bh` (e.g., `bh->totlen`, `bh->min_version`, `bh->hdrsize`, `bh->version`, `bh->flags`).

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `data` pointing to a small buffer and `length` set to, e.g., 8 (on a 64-bit system where sizeof(pointer)==8), but the actual `blobheader` struct is larger (e.g., 20+ bytes).
The guard check `length < sizeof(bh)` evaluates `8 < 8` which is false, so execution continues past the check.

The code dereferences `bh->totlen`, which accesses bytes at an offset within the blobheader structure. If `totlen` is at an offset beyond the 8 valid bytes, this is an out-of-bounds read from the `data` buffer.

state.value: read from beyond the valid 8 bytes of data — out-of-bounds read

step_by_step_execution.description: If the totlen check happens to pass (or other fields are accessed), subsequent field accesses like `bh->min_version`, `bh->hdrsize`, `bh->version`, `bh->flags` all read beyond the valid buffer.

commit_message:
ui/vnc: limit client_cut_text msg payload size

currently a malicious client could define a payload
size of 2^32 - 1 bytes and send up to that size of
data to the vnc server. The server would allocated
that amount of memory which could easily create an
out of memory condition.

This patch limits the payload size to 1MB max.

Please note that client_cut_text messages are currently
silently ignored.

Signed-off-by: Peter Lieven <pl@kamp.de>
Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the VNC display driver in QEMU before 2.1.0 allows attachers to cause a denial of service (process crash) via a CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message, which triggers an infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT handling. The expression `8 + dlen` (where dlen is uint32_t) can overflow, causing the function to return a small value. The caller then re-invokes the function with a buffer much smaller than expected, leading to an out-of-bounds read when `client_cut_text` is called with the original large dlen but a tiny data buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Client sends a VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message. The function is first called with len=1, returns 8 to request more data.
Function is called again with len=8. It reads dlen from data at offset 4.

dlen > 0 is true, so the function computes `8 + dlen`. Since dlen is uint32_t with value 0xFFFFFFFC, the addition overflows: 8 + 0xFFFFFFFC = 0x100000004, which truncates to 4 as a uint32_t, then is returned as int value 4.
The caller re-invokes the function with len=4. In the VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT case, len is not 1 and not 8, so both length checks are skipped entirely.

Execution reaches `client_cut_text(vs, read_u32(data, 4), data + 8)`. read_u32(data, 4) reads 4 bytes starting at offset 4, but the buffer is only 4 bytes, causing an out-of-bounds read. Additionally, `data + 8` points well past the buffer, and client_cut_text will process 0xFFFFFFFC bytes from this invalid pointer.

state.value: out-of-bounds read at offset 4 in a 4-byte buffer

commit_message:
Validate dimensions of input tensor in `FractionalAvgPoolGrad`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388286227
Change-Id: Ieb7566155e92acc8993a2212c76deacadc0edc8a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.FractionalAvgPoolGrad` can be tricked into accessing data outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/fractional_avg_pool_op.cc#L205) does not validate that the input tensor is non-empty. Thus, code constructs an empty `EigenDoubleMatrixMap` and then accesses this buffer with indices that are outside of the empty area. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 0f931751fb20f565c4e94aa6df58d54a003cdb30. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vulnerabilities.description: The values read from row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor are used directly as start indices (in_row_start, in_col_start) for accessing in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat without any bounds checking. If an attacker supplies negative values or values exceeding in_rows/in_cols in the sequence tensors, the computed in_index will be out of bounds, causing out-of-bounds read/write on the backing buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: out_backprop is read and its dimensions extracted. Suppose out_batch=1, out_rows=1, out_cols=1, out_depth=1.
row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor are validated to have at least out_rows+1=2 and out_cols+1=2 elements respectively, but their actual values are not validated.
orig_input_tensor_shape is read. Suppose in_batch=1, in_rows=4, in_cols=4, in_depth=1. in_backprop_tensor_temp is allocated with shape [1,4,4,1] (16 doubles).
Loop begins with b=0, r=0. in_row_start is read from row_seq_tensor_flat(0) which is -5. in_row_end = min(row_seq_tensor_flat(1)-1, 3) = min(0, 3) = 0.
c=0. in_col_start=0, in_col_end=0. num_elements_in_pooling_cell = (0-(-5)+1)*(0-0+1) = 6.
Inner loop iterates in_r from -5 to 0. For in_r=-5, in_c=0: in_index = (0*4 + (-5))*4 + 0 = -20. coeffRef(0, -20) accesses memory 20 columns before the buffer start, which is an out-of-bounds write.

vulnerabilities.description: When in_row_end < in_row_start or in_col_end < in_col_start (e.g., when sequence tensor values cause the start to exceed the clamped end but both are non-negative), num_elements_in_pooling_cell can be zero or negative. A value of zero leads to floating-point division by zero. While for negative values the inner loops won't execute, specific combinations of overlapping_ and sequence values can produce zero.

step_by_step_execution.description: Suppose out_rows=1, out_cols=1 and overlapping_=false.
row_seq_tensor_flat = [3, 3], col_seq_tensor_flat = [0, 1]. in_rows=4, so in_max_row_index=3.
in_row_start = row_seq_tensor_flat(0) = 3. in_row_end = row_seq_tensor_flat(1) - 1 = 2. min(2, 3) = 2. in_col_start=0, in_col_end=0.
num_elements_in_pooling_cell = (2-3+1)*(0-0+1) = 0*1 = 0. Division by zero occurs when computing out_backprop_element / num_elements_in_pooling_cell.

commit_message:
Fix overflow CHECK issue with `tf.raw_ops.UnsortedSegmentJoin`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 370766155
Change-Id: I33e7c6626224e1060a8a4ab51ad5d861c6d4c63e
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. An attacker can cause a denial of service by controlling the values of `num_segments` tensor argument for `UnsortedSegmentJoin`. This is because the implementation(https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a2a607db15c7cd01d754d37e5448d72a13491bdb/tensorflow/core/kernels/unsorted_segment_join_op.cc#L92-L93) assumes that the `num_segments` tensor is a valid scalar. Since the tensor is empty the `CHECK` involved in `.scalar<T>()()` that checks that the number of elements is exactly 1 will be invalidated and this would result in process termination. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.5.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.4.2, TensorFlow 2.3.3, TensorFlow 2.2.3 and TensorFlow 2.1.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
HID: elo: fix memory leak in elo_probe

When hid_parse() in elo_probe() fails, it forgets to call usb_put_dev to
decrease the refcount.

Fix this by adding usb_put_dev() in the error handling code of elo_probe().

Fixes: fbf42729d0e9 ("HID: elo: update the reference count of the usb device structure")

commit_message:
FTP: Fix buffer overflow in PORT parser, reported by Aaron Esau

Signed-off-by: Joachim Nilsson <troglobit@gmail.com>
cve_desc: An unauthenticated stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in common.c's handle_PORT in uftpd FTP server versions 2.10 and earlier can be abused to cause a crash and could potentially lead to remote code execution.

code_snippets: snprintf(addr, sizeof(addr), "%d.%d.%d.%d", a, b, c, d);
vulnerabilities.description: The return value of sscanf is not checked. If 'str' is malformed (e.g., empty string or contains fewer than 6 comma-separated integers), some or all of the local variables a, b, c, d, e, f will remain uninitialized. These uninitialized values are then used in snprintf to construct an address and to compute the data port. The garbage address string may still pass the inet_aton check (or produce unexpected values), leading to data connections being directed to arbitrary/uncontrolled addresses and ports.

code_snippets: snprintf(addr, sizeof(addr), "%d.%d.%d.%d", a, b, c, d);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a malformed 'str', e.g., an empty string or a string like "1,2" that does not contain six comma-separated integers.
sscanf is called but parses fewer than 6 values. Its return value (indicating how many items were assigned) is ignored. Variables that were not assigned remain uninitialized.
snprintf uses potentially uninitialized values a,b,c,d to construct an IP address string. The resulting address string contains arbitrary numbers derived from stack garbage.

state.value: arbitrary string like "1.2.<garbage>.<garbage>" derived from uninitialized stack memory

step_by_step_execution.description: inet_aton checks the constructed address. Depending on the garbage values, the string may form a syntactically valid IP address (e.g., any string of dot-separated numbers passes), allowing execution to continue.

state.value: may return non-zero (success) for garbage-derived but syntactically valid address

step_by_step_execution.description: The uninitialized values of e and f are used to compute ctrl->data_port, and the garbage address is copied into ctrl->data_address. The server will later open a data connection to this arbitrary address and port.

state.value: arbitrary/attacker-uncontrolled IP address from stack garbage

commit_message: fiber.c: should pack 15+ arguments in an array.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2.

code_snippets: mrb_stack_extend(mrb, len+2); /* for receiver and (optional) block */
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in `len+2` when `len` is close to the maximum value of `mrb_int`. If `len` is, e.g., `MRB_INT_MAX` (0x7FFFFFFF for 32-bit), `len+2` overflows to a negative or small value, causing `mrb_stack_extend` to allocate a small (or no) buffer. The subsequent loop then copies `len` elements (a huge number) into the undersized stack buffer, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a very large `len` value (e.g., MRB_INT_MAX for 32-bit mrb_int) and `status` of the fiber context is MRB_FIBER_CREATED.
`mrb_stack_extend(mrb, len+2)` is called. `len+2` overflows: 0x7FFFFFFF + 2 = 0x80000001, which as a signed 32-bit integer is -2147483647. The function allocates a very small stack or does nothing.
`b` is set to `c->stbase+1` and `e` is set to `b + len` (b + 0x7FFFFFFF), pointing far beyond the allocated stack buffer.
The while loop `while (b < e)` iterates, copying values from `a` into the stack. Since `e` is far past the end of the allocated stack, this writes out of bounds on the heap.

state.value: up to 0x7FFFFFFF, writing far past allocated stack buffer

commit_message:
vm.c: create break object before clearing GC arena.

Otherwise it possibly cause use-after-free.
cve_desc: Use-After-Free in str_escape in mruby/mruby in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);
vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized before being used. It is passed to 'prepare_missing()' when the method is not found, and later checked with 'mrb_proc_p(blk)' in the C function return path. Reading uninitialized stack memory can lead to treating garbage data as a valid mrb_value, potentially causing the code to dereference an invalid pointer when it calls 'mrb_proc_ptr(blk)' on random stack data that happens to look like MRB_TT_PROC.

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution enters the L_SENDB_SYM block (via OP_SEND, OP_SENDB, OP_SSEND, or OP_SSENDB opcodes).

state.value: uninitialized (whatever is on the C stack)

step_by_step_execution.description: 'recv' is assigned from regs[a] and method lookup is performed. The method is found (not undefined), so prepare_missing is skipped, but blk remains uninitialized.

state.value: a valid C function method (MRB_METHOD_CFUNC_P is true)

step_by_step_execution.description: The C method function is invoked and returns successfully (mrb->exc is NULL).

state.value: return value of the C method

step_by_step_execution.description: The code checks 'if (mrb_proc_p(blk))' which reads the uninitialized 'blk' variable. If the uninitialized stack memory happens to have type bits matching MRB_TT_PROC, the condition is true.

state.value: uninitialized garbage that may look like MRB_TT_PROC

step_by_step_execution.description: 'mrb_proc_ptr(blk)' extracts a pointer from the uninitialized value and dereferences it (p->flags |= MRB_PROC_ORPHAN), leading to a write to an arbitrary/invalid memory location.

state.value: garbage pointer extracted from uninitialized blk

commit_message: Make line_no with too large value(2**20) invalid. Fixes #124
cve_desc: tinyexr 0.9.5 has a integer overflow over-write in tinyexr::DecodePixelData in tinyexr.h, related to OpenEXR code.

vulnerabilities.description: The return value of calloc for exr_image->tiles is never checked for NULL. If the allocation fails (e.g., due to a very large attacker-controlled num_tiles derived from offsets.size()), the subsequent dereference exr_image->tiles[tile_idx].images causes a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with exr_header->tiled being true, and the offsets vector having a very large number of entries (num_tiles).
calloc is called with a very large num_tiles, which causes the allocation to fail and return NULL.
The loop begins with tile_idx = 0 and dereferences exr_image->tiles[0].images, which is a NULL pointer dereference since exr_image->tiles is NULL.

vulnerabilities.description: In the scanline path, line_no is read from untrusted file data and can be INT_MAX. Adding num_scanline_blocks (up to 32) to it causes signed integer overflow, which is undefined behavior in C/C++. This could lead to unexpected control flow, bypassing the num_lines <= 0 check, and causing out-of-bounds memory access in DecodePixelData.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the scanline (non-tiled) path. Compression type is PIZ, setting num_scanline_blocks to 32.
A block is read from the file. line_no is read from attacker-controlled data via memcpy and swap4.

data_len passes validation (positive and within data_size). The code computes end_line_no = min(line_no + num_scanline_blocks, data_window[3]+1). The expression line_no + num_scanline_blocks = INT_MAX + 32 is signed integer overflow (undefined behavior).

Due to undefined behavior, the compiler may assume the overflow does not happen, potentially making end_line_no and num_lines take unexpected values, bypassing the num_lines <= 0 check and leading to out-of-bounds access in DecodePixelData.

commit_message: Clean up handling CAP LS
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in the client in HexChat 2.11.0 allows remote IRC servers to read or modify arbitrary files via a .. (dot dot) in the server name.

vulnerabilities.description: A stack-based buffer overflow can occur. The buffer is 256 bytes, initialized with "CAP REQ :" (9 bytes). If a malicious or misconfigured server advertises all recognized extensions, every strcat appends to the buffer. The total length of all possible appended strings is: "identify-msg "(13) + "multi-prefix "(13) + "away-notify "(12) + "account-notify "(15) + "extended-join "(14) + "userhost-in-names "(18) + "znc.in/server-time-iso "(22) + "znc.in/server-time "(19) + "server-time "(12) + "sasl "(5) = 143 + 9 prefix = 152. While in this specific set the total fits, a more subtle issue is that extensions_str is externally controlled and the extension list is split by spaces — if the server sends the same recognized extension name repeated many times (e.g., "multi-prefix" repeated 20+ times), each occurrence matches the strcmp and strcat is called each time, easily overflowing the 256-byte stack buffer since there is no length check before any strcat call.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with extensions_str provided by a remote IRC server. An attacker-controlled server sends a CAP LS response with the same capability repeated many times, e.g. 'multi-prefix' repeated 20 times.
buffer is declared as a 256-byte stack array and initialized with "CAP REQ :" (9 bytes + null terminator).
extensions_str is split by spaces into an array of 20 identical strings "multi-prefix".
The loop iterates over each extension. On each iteration, strcmp matches "multi-prefix" and strcat appends "multi-prefix " (13 bytes) to buffer. After iteration 0, buffer length = 9 + 13 = 22.
After 19 iterations (i=0..18), buffer length = 9 + 19*13 = 256, which already fills the buffer completely (with null terminator it's 257, already overflowing by 1).
On the 20th iteration (i=19), strcat appends another 13 bytes, writing well past the end of the 256-byte stack buffer, corrupting the stack.

vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler (L_SENDB_SYM block), the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized before being used. It is passed to 'prepare_missing()' and later read via 'mrb_proc_p(blk)'. Reading an uninitialized stack variable leads to undefined behavior and can leak stack data or cause incorrect control flow decisions (e.g., treating garbage as a valid Proc pointer and dereferencing it).

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);

Local variables 'recv' and 'blk' are declared on the stack. 'blk' is not assigned any value.

step_by_step_execution.description: ARGUMENT_NORMALIZE is called, recv is assigned from regs[a], but blk remains uninitialized.
If the method is undefined (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m) is true), prepare_missing is called with the uninitialized 'blk' value. This passes garbage data to the function.

After the C function call returns (MRB_METHOD_CFUNC_P path), 'mrb_proc_p(blk)' is evaluated. Since blk contains uninitialized stack data, the type check may incorrectly identify it as MRB_TT_PROC, leading to mrb_proc_ptr(blk) dereferencing a garbage pointer.

state.value: potentially garbage pointer derived from uninitialized blk

vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SUPER handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized before being passed to 'prepare_missing()' when the method is not found. This is the same uninitialized variable pattern as in the SEND handler.

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution reaches the OP_SUPER handler. Local variables 'recv' and 'blk' are declared.
recv is assigned from regs[0], various checks are performed, but blk is never assigned.
mrb_method_search_vm fails to find the method (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m) is true), and prepare_missing is called with the uninitialized blk.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/131
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in the CalcMinMax function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick before 6.9.4-0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read and application crash) via a crafted mat file.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `ldblk` when multiplying `MATLAB_HDR.SizeX` (an attacker-controlled unsigned 32-bit value from the file) by a constant (2, 4, or 8). The multiplication is performed in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic before being cast to `ssize_t`, so it can wrap around to a small value. This causes `BImgBuff` to be allocated with a much smaller size than needed, while `image->columns` is set to the original large `SizeX`. `ImportQuantumPixels` then reads beyond the undersized buffer based on `image->columns`, causing a heap buffer over-read/over-write.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted MAT file is opened and the header is read, establishing endian-specific read functions.
The object parser loop reads DataType and ObjectSize, then reads matrix dimensions. The attacker sets MATLAB_HDR.SizeX to a large value, e.g., 0x20000001, and CellType to miINT64.
In the miINT64 case, ldblk is computed as `(ssize_t)(8 * MATLAB_HDR.SizeX)`. The multiplication `8 * 0x20000001` = `0x100000008` overflows the 32-bit unsigned int to `0x8`.
image->columns is set to MATLAB_HDR.SizeX (the original large value).
BImgBuff is allocated via AcquireQuantumMemory((size_t)(ldblk), sizeof(double)) = AcquireQuantumMemory(8, 8) = 64 bytes.

In the scanline reading loop, ReadBlob reads only ldblk=8 bytes into BImgBuff (small read). Then ImportQuantumPixels is called with image->columns=0x20000001, which attempts to read pixel data for 536870913 pixels from the 64-byte BImgBuff, causing a massive heap buffer over-read.

vulnerabilities.description: When image->depth is set to 64 (for miINT64, miUINT64, or miDOUBLE cell types), the expression `one << image->depth` where `one` is `size_t` results in undefined behavior, as shifting by the full width of the type (64 bits for a 64-bit size_t) is undefined in C.

step_by_step_execution.description: A MAT file with CellType miDOUBLE, miINT64, or miUINT64 is read.
image->depth is set to 64 in the switch statement.
`one` is declared as `size_t` and set to 1. Then `image->colors = one << image->depth` shifts a 64-bit value left by 64, which is undefined behavior in C.

state.value: 64 (equals bit width of size_t on 64-bit platform, UB)

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx

* Fix HTTP request smuggling vulnerability

See GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx or CVE-2021-41136 for more info.

* 4.3.9 release note

* 5.5.1 release note

* 5.5.1
cve_desc: Puma is a HTTP 1.1 server for Ruby/Rack applications. Prior to versions 5.5.1 and 4.3.9, using `puma` with a proxy which forwards HTTP header values which contain the LF character could allow HTTP request smugggling. A client could smuggle a request through a proxy, causing the proxy to send a response back to another unknown client. The only proxy which has this behavior, as far as the Puma team is aware of, is Apache Traffic Server. If the proxy uses persistent connections and the client adds another request in via HTTP pipelining, the proxy may mistake it as the first request's body. Puma, however, would see it as two requests, and when processing the second request, send back a response that the proxy does not expect. If the proxy has reused the persistent connection to Puma to send another request for a different client, the second response from the first client will be sent to the second client. This vulnerability was patched in Puma 5.5.1 and 4.3.9. As a workaround, do not use Apache Traffic Server with `puma`.

commit_message:
Prevent use after free.

A very old version of the code used `result` as a simple pointer to a resource. Two years later, the pointer got changed to a `unique_ptr` but author forgot to remove the call to `Unref`. Three years after that, we finally uncover the UAF.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387924872
Change-Id: I70fb6f199164de49fac20c168132a07b84903f9b
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateEnsemble` can result in a use after free error if an attacker supplies specially crafted arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/resource_ops.cc#L55) uses a reference counted resource and decrements the refcount if the initialization fails, as it should. However, when the code was written, the resource was represented as a naked pointer but later refactoring has changed it to be a smart pointer. Thus, when the pointer leaves the scope, a subsequent `free`-ing of the resource occurs, but this fails to take into account that the refcount has already reached 0, thus the resource has been already freed. During this double-free process, members of the resource object are accessed for cleanup but they are invalid as the entire resource has been freed. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5ecec9c6fbdbc6be03295685190a45e7eee726ab. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: When InitFromSerialized fails, result->Unref() is called on the ref-counted resource object (which decrements the refcount to 0 and deletes it), but the std::unique_ptr<BoostedTreesEnsembleResource> still holds the raw pointer. When OP_REQUIRES causes the function to return, the unique_ptr destructor fires and calls delete on the already-freed pointer, resulting in a double-free.

step_by_step_execution.description: A new BoostedTreesEnsembleResource is allocated and stored in a unique_ptr. The resource is ref-counted, starting with a refcount of 1.

state.value: unique_ptr owning a newly allocated BoostedTreesEnsembleResource with refcount=1

step_by_step_execution.description: InitFromSerialized is called and returns false (e.g., due to a malformed protobuf input).
result->Unref() is called, decrementing the refcount to 0, which causes the object to be deleted/freed.

state.value: unique_ptr still holds a dangling raw pointer to the freed object

step_by_step_execution.description: OP_REQUIRES evaluates the condition (false), constructs the error, sets it on context, and causes the function to return.

state.value: unique_ptr still holds the dangling pointer

step_by_step_execution.description: Upon function return, the unique_ptr destructor runs and calls delete on the already-freed pointer, causing a double-free.

state.value: unique_ptr destructor calls delete on dangling pointer

vulnerabilities.description: result.release() transfers ownership of the raw pointer to CreateResource. If CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS, the resource was not stored, but unique_ptr no longer owns it. The raw pointer is leaked, and an attacker can trigger this repeatedly to exhaust memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: A BoostedTreesEnsembleResource is created and managed by unique_ptr, and InitFromSerialized succeeds.
result.release() is called, releasing ownership from the unique_ptr and passing the raw pointer to CreateResource.

state.value: unique_ptr is now null, raw pointer passed to CreateResource

step_by_step_execution.description: CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS because the resource already exists in the resource manager.
The code checks status.code() != ALREADY_EXISTS, which is false, so it skips OP_REQUIRES_OK. The function returns normally, but the raw pointer was never stored and is never freed.

commit_message:
libnsgif: Fix null pointer deref on frameless GIF input

A crafted GIF file with no frame data could cause a null pointer
dereference leading to denial of service (crash). Reported by
@JieyongMa via huntr.dev.
cve_desc: chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file. in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.10.2. chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file.

vulnerabilities.description: The LZW-decoded `colour` byte (0–255) is used as an index into `colour_table` without any bounds check against `colour_table_size`. The colour table can be as small as 2 entries (when `flags & GIF_COLOUR_TABLE_SIZE_MASK` is 0, giving `2 << 0 = 2`), but the decoded colour value can be up to 255, leading to an out-of-bounds read from the colour table array.

step_by_step_execution.description: The image descriptor flags byte is read, and the colour table size is computed.

state.value: gif_data[9], e.g. 0x80 (local colour table flag set, size bits = 0)

step_by_step_execution.description: Because the local colour table flag is set, `colour_table` is set to `gif->local_colour_table` and only 2 entries (indices 0 and 1) are populated from the GIF data.
LZW decoding begins. The LZW stream can produce arbitrary byte values (0–255) as pixel colour indices.
The decoded `colour` byte is used directly as an index into the 2-entry colour_table with no bounds check, causing an out-of-bounds read when colour >= 2.

state.value: out-of-bounds read at index 200 in a 2-element array

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5050: using freed memory when searching for pattern in path

Problem:    Using freed memory when searching for pattern in path.
Solution:   Make a copy of the line.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow when the reallocation of the 'files' array (via 'bigger') fails. If included files are nested deeply enough that depth + 1 == old_files == max_path_depth, a resize is attempted. If the ALLOC_MULT for 'bigger' returns NULL, the code falls through and writes to files[depth + 1], which is files[max_path_depth] — one element past the end of the allocated array. This is an out-of-bounds write on the heap.

step_by_step_execution.description: The 'files' array is allocated with max_path_depth (50) entries, and old_files is set to max_path_depth (50). depth starts at -1.
Through a chain of 50 nested #include directives (none reaching EOF), depth increments from -1 up to 49. old_files remains at 50 because no files have been closed (popped).
A 51st include is encountered. The condition 'depth + 1 == old_files' (50 == 50) is true, so the code tries to resize by allocating a bigger array via ALLOC_MULT(SearchedFile, max_path_depth * 2).
The ALLOC_MULT call returns NULL (e.g., under memory pressure). The 'bigger' pointer is NULL, so the resize block is skipped entirely. The files array, max_path_depth, and old_files remain unchanged.
Execution continues to 'files[depth + 1].fp = mch_fopen(...)' which writes to files[50]. Since the array only has 50 entries (indices 0–49), this is a heap out-of-bounds write.

commit_message:
HID: elo: fix memory leak in elo_probe

When hid_parse() in elo_probe() fails, it forgets to call usb_put_dev to
decrease the refcount.

Fix this by adding usb_put_dev() in the error handling code of elo_probe().

Fixes: fbf42729d0e9 ("HID: elo: update the reference count of the usb device structure")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>

cve_desc: In drivers/hid/hid-elo.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.11, a memory leak exists for a certain hid_parse error condition.

vulnerabilities.description: In the error path (err_free label), the function frees `priv` but does not call `usb_put_dev(priv->usbdev)` to release the USB device reference acquired earlier via `usb_get_dev(udev)`. This leads to a reference count leak on the USB device, preventing it from ever being freed.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function acquires a reference on the USB device via usb_get_dev().

state.value: valid usb_device pointer with incremented refcount

At err_free, kfree(priv) is called, freeing the priv structure, but usb_put_dev(priv->usbdev) is never called to release the reference.

state.value: incremented by 1, never decremented — leaked reference
