commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-26j7-ww69-c4qj
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions prior to and including 2.12.1 a stack buffer overflow vulnerability affects PJSIP users that use STUN in their applications, either by: setting a STUN server in their account/media config in PJSUA/PJSUA2 level, or directly using `pjlib-util/stun_simple` API. A patch is available in commit 450baca which should be included in the next release. There are no known workarounds for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: If buf_len is smaller than sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr), the code dereferences msg->hdr->type and msg->hdr->length before any buffer length validation is performed, causing an out-of-bounds read. The length check `msg_len != buf_len - sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)` comes after the reads, and moreover the subtraction `buf_len - sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)` would itself suffer unsigned integer underflow (wrapping to a huge value) when buf_len < sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr).

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a small buf_len, e.g. buf_len = 2, which is less than sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr) (typically 20 bytes for STUN).
msg->hdr is set to point to buf, which only has 2 valid bytes.
msg->hdr->type is read via pj_ntohs, reading beyond the 2-byte buffer to access the type field within the header struct. This is an out-of-bounds read.
msg->hdr->length is read via pj_ntohs, again reading beyond the valid buffer.

The length validation check computes buf_len - sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr), which is 2 - 20 = underflow to a very large unsigned value since pj_size_t is unsigned. This check occurs too late—the OOB reads have already happened.

state.value: wraps to ~0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFEC (on 64-bit) due to unsigned underflow

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0260: using freed memory when using 'quickfixtextfunc' recursively

Problem:    Using freed memory when using 'quickfixtextfunc' recursively.
Solution:   Do not allow for recursion.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0260.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/131
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in the CalcMinMax function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick before 6.9.4-0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read and application crash) via a crafted mat file.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow when computing ldblk from attacker-controlled MATLAB_HDR.SizeX. The multiplication (e.g., 8 * MATLAB_HDR.SizeX) is performed in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic before being cast to ssize_t, allowing wrap-around to a small value. This leads to a small heap allocation for BImgBuff, while image->columns retains the original large SizeX. ImportQuantumPixels then reads past the end of the undersized buffer based on image->columns.

step_by_step_execution.description: MATLAB_HDR.SizeX is read from the file via ReadBlobXXXLong(image2). An attacker crafts a MAT file with SizeX = 0x20000002.
CellType is miDOUBLE, so ldblk is computed as (ssize_t)(8 * MATLAB_HDR.SizeX). The multiplication 8 * 0x20000002 = 0x100000010, which overflows 32-bit unsigned int to 0x10 (16).
image->columns is set to MATLAB_HDR.SizeX and image->rows to MATLAB_HDR.SizeY.
SetImageExtent is called. Depending on resource limits, it may succeed for rows=1 since it's a thin image.
BImgBuff is allocated via AcquireQuantumMemory((size_t)ldblk, sizeof(double)) = AcquireQuantumMemory(16, 8) = 128 bytes.
ReadBlob reads ldblk=16 bytes into BImgBuff, which fits the buffer.

ImportQuantumPixels is called with image->columns=0x20000002 pixels. It processes the BImgBuff buffer assuming it contains enough data for 536870914 pixels (at 8 bytes each = ~4GB), but the buffer is only 128 bytes. This causes a massive heap buffer over-read.

commit_message:
Validate dimensions of input tensor in `FractionalAvgPoolGrad`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388286227
Change-Id: Ieb7566155e92acc8993a2212c76deacadc0edc8a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.FractionalAvgPoolGrad` can be tricked into accessing data outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/fractional_avg_pool_op.cc#L205) does not validate that the input tensor is non-empty. Thus, code constructs an empty `EigenDoubleMatrixMap` and then accesses this buffer with indices that are outside of the empty area. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 0f931751fb20f565c4e94aa6df58d54a003cdb30. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The code does not validate that out_depth == in_depth or that out_batch == in_batch. The in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat is mapped with in_depth rows, but the inner loop iterates d from 0 to out_depth. If an attacker provides an out_backprop tensor with out_depth > in_depth, this causes an out-of-bounds read/write on in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat. Similarly, iterating b up to out_batch with in_index computed using in_rows can overflow the column dimension if out_batch > in_batch.

step_by_step_execution.description: orig_input_tensor_shape is read with in_depth (e.g., 1) and in_batch (e.g., 1).
out_backprop tensor is provided with different depth/batch, e.g. out_depth=100, out_batch=1.
in_backprop_tensor_temp is allocated with shape from orig_input_tensor_shape (batch=1, rows=4, cols=4, depth=1), total 16 elements.
in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat is mapped with in_depth=1 rows and in_cols*in_rows*in_batch=16 columns.
The inner loop iterates d from 0 to out_depth (100). When d >= 1 (in_depth), coeffRef(d, in_index) accesses beyond the allocated buffer, causing out-of-bounds write.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vulnerabilities.description: The values from row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor are used directly as start indices (in_row_start, in_col_start) without any bounds checking. An attacker can provide negative values or values exceeding in_rows/in_cols, causing out-of-bounds access on in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat. While in_row_end/in_col_end are clamped to in_max_row_index from above, in_row_start and in_col_start are never validated.

step_by_step_execution.description: Attacker provides row_seq_tensor with a negative value, e.g., row_seq_tensor_flat(0) = -10.
In the loop for r=0, in_row_start is set to row_seq_tensor_flat(0) = -10. No bounds check is performed.
in_row_end is computed and clamped to in_max_row_index from above, but could still be >= in_row_start so the loop executes.
Inner loop iterates in_r from -10 to in_row_end. in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c. With in_r = -10, this computes a negative index.
coeffRef(d, in_index) with negative in_index accesses memory before the allocated buffer, causing out-of-bounds read/write.

vulnerabilities.description: If in_row_end < in_row_start or in_col_end < in_col_start (possible when sequence tensor values lead to in_row_end being clamped below in_row_start), num_elements_in_pooling_cell can be zero or negative. While with zero the loop wouldn't execute (the division wouldn't happen), with carefully crafted inputs where the loop does execute once with num_elements_in_pooling_cell = 0, a division by zero occurs.

step_by_step_execution.description: Attacker crafts row_seq_tensor so that in_row_start equals in_rows (beyond max index), and in_row_end is clamped to in_max_row_index (in_rows - 1).
Similarly for columns: in_col_start = 0, in_col_end = some valid value.
num_elements_in_pooling_cell = (3 - 4 + 1) * (3 - 0 + 1) = 0 * 4 = 0

The inner loops won't execute since in_row_start > in_row_end, so division by zero is avoided in this case. However, if in_row_start = in_row_end + 1 and in_col values cause the col loop to not execute either, but with negative num_elements if in_row_start is much larger and in_col range is valid while in_row range iterates (due to unsigned comparison issues), division by negative/zero could occur.

We want to format even the secure information in tests.

Signed-off-by: Peter Krempa <pkrempa@redhat.com>

This flaw allows an attacker to access potentially sensitive information in the domain configuration via the `dumpxml` command.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0260: using freed memory when using 'quickfixtextfunc' recursively

Problem:    Using freed memory when using 'quickfixtextfunc' recursively.
Solution:   Do not allow for recursion.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0260.

commit_message: Fix #184: Heap overflow in S3M loader
cve_desc: XMFile::read in XMFile.cpp in milkyplay in MilkyTracker 1.02.00 has a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: The loop copies order entries from the file into header->ord[] using an incrementing index j, but never checks whether j exceeds the fixed size of the header->ord[] array. Since header->ordnum is read directly from the file (up to 65535), and entries are copied whenever they are not 0xFF, this can overflow the fixed-size ord[] buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: header->ordnum is read from the file as a 16-bit word, which can be up to 65535.
An orders array of size header->ordnum is allocated from the heap.
orders[] is filled by reading header->ordnum bytes from the file.

state.value: 65535 bytes of attacker-controlled data, none equal to 0xFF

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop iterates over all ordnum entries. For each entry not equal to 255, it writes to header->ord[j++]. If no entry is 255, j reaches header->ordnum.

state.value: fixed-size array (typically 256), being written past its bounds

step_by_step_execution.description: When j exceeds the fixed size of header->ord[], subsequent writes corrupt adjacent memory in the TXMHeader structure.

vulnerabilities.description: The sample index 's' is incremented for each valid sample instrument found, but there is no check that 's' stays within the bounds of the pre-allocated smp[] array. An attacker can craft a file with a large insnum value, causing out-of-bounds writes to the smp[] array.

step_by_step_execution.description: header->insnum is read from the file as a 16-bit word, potentially larger than the allocated smp[] array size.
The loop iterates i from 0 to header->insnum - 1. For each instrument with type==1 and valid sample data, s is incremented.

state.value: increments with each valid sample instrument found

step_by_step_execution.description: If the number of valid sample instruments exceeds the allocated size of module->smp[], then accesses like smp[s].flags write out of bounds.

commit_message:
vnc: sanitize bits_per_pixel from the client

bits_per_pixel that are less than 8 could result in accessing
non-initialized buffers later in the code due to the expectation
that bytes_per_pixel value that is used to initialize these buffers is
never zero.

To fix this check that bits_per_pixel from the client is one of the
values that the rfb protocol specification allows.

This is CVE-2014-7815.

Signed-off-by: Petr Matousek <pmatouse@redhat.com>

[ kraxel: apply codestyle fix ]

Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>
cve_desc: The set_pixel_format function in ui/vnc.c in QEMU allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a small bytes_per_pixel value.

vulnerabilities.description: When an invalid audio format is received, vnc_client_error(vs) is called, which typically initiates client disconnection and may free/invalidate the VncState. However, the 'break' statement only exits the inner format switch, not the outer VNC_MSG_CLIENT_QEMU_AUDIO_SET_FORMAT case. Execution then continues to access vs->as.nchannels and vs->as.freq on the potentially freed/invalidated VncState object, leading to a use-after-free.

step_by_step_execution.description: Client sends a QEMU extended message (data[0] == VNC_MSG_CLIENT_QEMU). len >= 10 (the full audio set format message).
The sub-message type is VNC_MSG_CLIENT_QEMU_AUDIO (data[1]).
The audio sub-command is VNC_MSG_CLIENT_QEMU_AUDIO_SET_FORMAT (read_u16(data, 2)).
len is 10, so the 'if (len == 4) return 10' check is skipped. The inner switch on read_u8(data, 4) is entered with an invalid audio format value (e.g., 255).
The default case is hit: vnc_client_error(vs) is called, which disconnects/frees the client state. Then 'break' exits only the inner format switch (switch on read_u8(data, 4)).
Execution continues past the inner switch. The code writes to vs->as.nchannels = read_u8(data, 5), accessing the potentially freed VncState.
The code then writes to vs->as.freq = read_u32(data, 6), another access to potentially freed memory.

commit_message:
selinux,smack: fix subjective/objective credential use mixups

Jann Horn reported a problem with commit eb1231f73c4d ("selinux:
clarify task subjective and objective credentials") where some LSM
hooks were attempting to access the subjective credentials of a task
other than the current task.  Generally speaking, it is not safe to
access another task's subjective credentials and doing so can cause
a number of problems.

Further, while looking into the problem, I realized that Smack was
suffering from a similar problem brought about by a similar commit
1fb057dcde11 ("smack: differentiate between subjective and objective
task credentials").

This patch addresses this problem by restoring the use of the task's
objective credentials in those cases where the task is other than the
current executing task.  Not only does this resolve the problem
reported by Jann, it is arguably the correct thing to do in these
cases.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: eb1231f73c4d ("selinux: clarify task subjective and objective credentials")
Fixes: 1fb057dcde11 ("smack: differentiate between subjective and objective task credentials")
Reported-by: Jann Horn <jannh@google.com>
Acked-by: Eric W. Biederman <ebiederm@xmission.com>
Acked-by: Casey Schaufler <casey@schaufler-ca.com>
Signed-off-by: Paul Moore <paul@paul-moore.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.14.8. A use-after-free in selinux_ptrace_traceme (aka the SELinux handler for PTRACE_TRACEME) could be used by local attackers to cause memory corruption and escalate privileges, aka CID-a3727a8bac0a. This occurs because of an attempt to access the subjective credentials of another task.

ncurses 6.2 - patch 20200531

+ correct configure version-check/warnng for g++ to allow for 10.x

+ add a check for end-of-string in cvtchar to handle a malformed
  string in infotocap (report/testcase by "puppet-meteor").

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4956: reading past end of line with "gf" in Visual block mode

Problem:    Reading past end of line with "gf" in Visual block mode.
Solution:   Do not include the NUL in the length.
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in function grab_file_name in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4956. This vulnerability is capable of crashing the software, memory modification, and possible remote execution.

commit_message:
rsi: fix a double free bug in rsi_91x_deinit()

`dev` (struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) field of adapter
(struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) is allocated  and initialized in
`rsi_init_usb_interface`. If any error is detected in information
read from the device side,  `rsi_init_usb_interface` will be
freed. However, in the higher level error handling code in
`rsi_probe`, if error is detected, `rsi_91x_deinit` is called
again, in which `dev` will be freed again, resulting double free.

This patch fixes the double free by removing the free operation on
`dev` in `rsi_init_usb_interface`, because `rsi_91x_deinit` is also
used in `rsi_disconnect`, in that code path, the `dev` field is not
 (and thus needs to be) freed.

This bug was found in v4.19, but is also present in the latest version
of kernel. Fixes CVE-2019-15504.

Reported-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Mathias Payer <mathias.payer@nebelwelt.net>
Signed-off-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Guenter Roeck <linux@roeck-us.net>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.2.9 has a Double Free via crafted USB device traffic (which may be remote via usbip or usbredir).

virstoragetest: testBackingParse: Use VIR_DOMAIN_DEF_FORMAT_SECURE when formatting xml

We want to format even the secure information in tests.

This flaw allows an attacker to access potentially sensitive information in the domain configuration via the `dumpxml` command.

commit_message: Fix #184: Heap overflow in S3M loader
cve_desc: XMFile::read in XMFile.cpp in milkyplay in MilkyTracker 1.02.00 has a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Buffer overflow writing to header->ord[]. header->ordnum is read from file as a 16-bit word (up to 65535), but header->ord is a fixed-size array (typically 256 elements). The loop copies order entries without bounds-checking j against the size of header->ord.

step_by_step_execution.description: header->ordnum is read from the file as a 16-bit word, which can be any value up to 65535.
An orders array of size header->ordnum is allocated.
orders are read from the file. If no byte equals 255, all ordnum entries are valid.
The loop iterates up to header->ordnum times, writing to header->ord[j++] for each non-255 entry. j can reach up to 65535, far exceeding the fixed size of header->ord (typically 256).

vulnerabilities.description: Memory leak of the 'orders' array. After allocating 'orders', the checks on header->insnum and header->patnum return MP_LOADER_FAILED without freeing 'orders'.

step_by_step_execution.description: orders is allocated with new mp_ubyte[header->ordnum].
header->insnum is read from file. If it exceeds MP_MAXINS, the function returns MP_LOADER_FAILED without delete[] orders.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds access on the smp[] array. The variable 's' increments for each valid sample instrument found, but there is no check that 's' stays within the bounds of the module->smp array (MP_MAXSAMPLES or similar). If many instruments have type==1, s can exceed the array size.

step_by_step_execution.description: header->insnum is read from file, can be up to MP_MAXINS.
For each instrument with type==1, s is incremented after writing to smp[s].

state.value: increments unbounded relative to smp array size

step_by_step_execution.description: If the smp array has fewer slots than the number of type-1 instruments, smp[s] accesses memory out of bounds.

Jann Horn reported a problem with commit eb1231f73c4d ("selinux:

access another task's subjective credentials and doing so can cause

Further, while looking into the problem, I realized that Smack was

1fb057dcde11 ("smack: differentiate between subjective and objective

This patch addresses this problem by restoring the use of the task's

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4397: crash when using many composing characters in error message

Problem:    Crash when using many composing characters in error message.
Solution:   Use mb_cptr2char_adv() instead of mb_ptr2char_adv().
cve_desc: Stack-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message:
libnsgif: Fix null pointer deref on frameless GIF input

A crafted GIF file with no frame data could cause a null pointer
dereference leading to denial of service (crash). Reported by
@JieyongMa via huntr.dev.
cve_desc: chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file. in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.10.2. chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file.

vulnerabilities.description: An LZW-decoded colour byte (0-255) is used as an index into `colour_table` without checking that the index is within `colour_table_size`. The colour table size can be as small as 2 entries (when the low 3 bits of flags are 0, size = 2 << 0 = 2), but the decoded `colour` value from the LZW stream can be any value from 0 to 255. This allows an out-of-bounds read on either `gif->local_colour_table` or `gif->global_colour_table`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the image descriptor flags byte from gif_data[9].

state.value: gif_data[9], e.g. 0x00 (no local colour table, no interlace)

step_by_step_execution.description: colour_table_size is computed as 2 << (flags & GIF_COLOUR_TABLE_SIZE_MASK). With flags & 0x07 == 0, this equals 2.
Since the local colour table flag is not set, colour_table is set to gif->global_colour_table, which may have been populated with only a small number of entries.

state.value: gif->global_colour_table (with as few as 2 valid entries)

step_by_step_execution.description: LZW decoding begins, producing colour bytes from the compressed data stream.

The decoded colour byte is used directly as an index into colour_table without any bounds check against colour_table_size. If colour >= colour_table_size (e.g., colour=200 but table has 2 entries), this reads out of bounds.

vulnerabilities.description: In the clear_image branch, gif->background_index is used to index into colour_table without validating that it is within colour_table_size bounds. A crafted GIF with a background_index larger than the colour table can cause an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with clear_image=true (from the recursive call when previous frame disposal_method is GIF_FRAME_CLEAR).
colour_table is set to either local or global colour table with a potentially small number of entries (e.g., 2).

state.value: pointer to table with 2 valid entries

step_by_step_execution.description: In the else (clear_image) branch, when disposal_method is GIF_FRAME_CLEAR and transparency is false, colour_table is indexed with gif->background_index.

state.value: e.g. 128 (set from GIF header, could exceed colour_table_size)

step_by_step_execution.description: colour_table[gif->background_index] performs an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
misc: fastrpc: prevent memory leak in fastrpc_dma_buf_attach

In fastrpc_dma_buf_attach if dma_get_sgtable fails the allocated memory
for a should be released.

Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20190925152742.16258-1-navid.emamdoost@gmail.com
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the fastrpc_dma_buf_attach() function in drivers/misc/fastrpc.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.9 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering dma_get_sgtable() failures, aka CID-fc739a058d99.

vulnerabilities.description: When dma_get_sgtable fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the memory allocated by kzalloc for 'a', resulting in a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: kzalloc allocates memory for struct fastrpc_dma_buf_attachment and assigns it to 'a'.
dma_get_sgtable is called and returns a negative value indicating failure.
The error path returns -EINVAL without calling kfree(a), leaking the allocated memory.

commit_message: 0.16.1: fix for dcraw ljpeg_start() vulnerability
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the ljpeg_start function in dcraw 7.00 and earlier allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted image, which triggers a buffer overflow, related to the len variable.

vulnerabilities.description: If a crafted JPEG marker has a length field of 0 or 1, `len` becomes -2 or -1. When this negative `int` is passed to `fread` which expects a `size_t` parameter, it is implicitly cast to an extremely large unsigned value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFE on 32-bit or larger on 64-bit). This causes `fread` to attempt to write far more bytes than the 0x10000-byte stack buffer `data` can hold, resulting in a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the first 2 bytes and checks for JPEG SOI marker (0xFFD8). Assume this passes.
In the do-while loop, `fread(data, 2, 2, ifp)` reads 4 bytes: 2 bytes for tag, 2 bytes for len.
Tag is computed as `data[0] << 8 | data[1]`.
len is computed as `(data[2] << 8 | data[3]) - 2`. With data[2]=0x00 and data[3]=0x01, len = 1 - 2 = -1.
The check `if (tag <= 0xff00) return 0;` passes because tag is 0xFFC0 which is > 0xFF00.

`fread(data, 1, len, ifp)` is called. `len` is -1 (int), which is implicitly cast to `size_t`. On a 32-bit system this is 0xFFFFFFFF (~4GB), on 64-bit it is even larger. fread attempts to write up to that many bytes into the stack buffer `data` which is only 0x10000 bytes, causing a stack buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Even with a positive `len`, the length field in the JPEG marker can be up to 0xFFFF, yielding `len` up to 0xFFFF - 2 = 65533. Since `data` is 0x10000 (65536) bytes, this is within bounds. However, there is no check that `len` does not exceed the size of the `data` buffer. A more subtle concern is the `case 0xffda` which accesses `data[1+data[0]*2]` and `data[3+data[0]*2]` without verifying that these indices are within the `len` bytes actually read, potentially using stale/uninitialized stack data.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted JPEG has an SOS marker (0xFFDA) with a very small length, e.g. length field = 3, so len = 1.
fread reads 1 byte into data[0]. Only data[0] is valid from this read.
In case 0xffda, `data[1+data[0]*2]` is accessed. With data[0]=100, this accesses data[201]. This index is within the buffer size but reads stale/uninitialized stack data since only 1 byte was read.

state.value: 201 (within 0x10000 buffer but beyond the 1 byte read)

step_by_step_execution.description: Similarly `data[3+data[0]*2]` accesses data[203], also reading uninitialized data, and modifies jh->bits.

cve_desc: An information leak flaw was found due to uninitialized memory in the Linux kernel's TIPC protocol subsystem, in the way a user sends a TIPC datagram to one or more destinations. This flaw allows a local user to read some kernel memory. This issue is limited to no more than 7 bytes, and the user cannot control what is read. This flaw affects the Linux kernel versions prior to 5.17-rc1.

commit_message:
Never follow symlinks when setting file flags on Linux

When opening a file descriptor to set file flags on linux, ensure
no symbolic links are followed. This fixes the case when an archive
contains a directory entry followed by a symlink entry with the same
path. The fixup code would modify file flags of the symlink target.
cve_desc: An improper link resolution flaw can occur while extracting an archive leading to changing modes, times, access control lists, and flags of a file outside of the archive. An attacker may provide a malicious archive to a victim user, who would trigger this flaw when trying to extract the archive. A local attacker may use this flaw to gain more privileges in a system.

commit_message: Fix wrong boundary checks in inflections parser resulting in stack buffer over-read with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11. This vulnerability is capable of arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.description: In the delete branch (mod == 'd', dir == '<'), `pos` is decremented before any check that it remains non-negative. If `pos` is 0 when entering this branch, it becomes -1. The subsequent bounds check (`l < 0 || d + l > decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX`) does not catch this because `l = *decoded_size - (-1) = *decoded_size + 1` is positive, and `d + l = decoded + *decoded_size` is likely within the buffer limit. This leads to an out-of-bounds read at `decoded[pos]` (i.e., `decoded[-1]`) and an out-of-bounds write via `memmove` to `decoded + pos` (i.e., one byte before the buffer).

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with *decoded_size = 1 (buffer has one character). pos is initialized to *decoded_size.
First rule byte c = 3 (0x03): sets mod to 'd' (since c > 2), dir to '<' (since c & 2 is true). olddir was '<' which equals new dir, so pos is not reset.
Second rule byte c = 'A' (0x41, value > 10): enters else branch with mod == 'd'. Since dir == '<', pos is decremented to 0.

Bounds check: l = *decoded_size - pos = 1 - 0 = 1. d = decoded + 0. d + l = decoded + 1 <= decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX. Check passes. decoded[0] is compared to c, memmove executes, *decoded_size becomes 0.
Third rule byte c = 'B' (0x42, value > 10): enters else branch with mod == 'd'. Since dir == '<', pos is decremented: pos = 0 - 1 = -1.

Bounds check: l = *decoded_size - pos = 0 - (-1) = 1, which is not < 0. d = decoded + (-1) = one byte before buffer. d + l = decoded + 0, which is <= decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX. Bounds check passes.
decoded[pos] accesses decoded[-1], which is an out-of-bounds read. If the check passes, memmove(decoded - 1, decoded, 1) performs an out-of-bounds write one byte before the buffer.

commit_message:
ALSA: pcm: oss: Fix race at SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC

There is a small race window at snd_pcm_oss_sync() that is called from
OSS PCM SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl; namely the function calls
snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() at first, then takes the params_lock mutex
for the rest.  When the stream is set up again by another thread
between them, it leads to inconsistency, and may result in unexpected
results such as NULL dereference of OSS buffer as a fuzzer spotted
recently.

The fix is simply to cover snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() call into the same
params_lock mutex with snd_pcm_oss_make_ready_locked() variant.

Reported-and-tested-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Jaroslav Kysela <perex@perex.cz>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/CAFcO6XN7JDM4xSXGhtusQfS2mSBcx50VJKwQpCq=WeLt57aaZA@mail.gmail.com
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220905060714.22549-1-tiwai@suse.de
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: A race condition flaw was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem due to improper locking. It could lead to a NULL pointer dereference while handling the SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl. A privileged local user (root or member of the audio group) could use this flaw to crash the system, resulting in a denial of service condition

commit_message:
Bug 701819: fixed ordering in if expression to avoid out-of-bounds access.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -dBATCH -dNOPAUSE -r965 -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=pcx16 ../bug-701819.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in pcx_write_rle() in contrib/japanese/gdev10v.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.description: After advancing `from += step`, the code dereferences `*from` in the condition `data != *from` before checking whether `from` has reached or exceeded `end`. Due to C short-circuit evaluation of `||`, the left operand `data != *from` is evaluated first. If `from >= end` after the increment, this is an out-of-bounds read. Additionally, `from += step` can overshoot `end` (making `from > end`), so the `from == end` check would be false even though `from` is already past the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the while loop with `from < end`. Suppose `from` points to the last valid element (i.e., `from + step >= end`).

state.value: points to last valid element before end
one past the last valid byte in buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: `byte data = *from;` reads the last valid byte.
`from += step;` advances the pointer. Now `from >= end`.
The condition `data != *from` is evaluated first (short-circuit). Since `from >= end`, `*from` dereferences memory at or beyond the end of the buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
rsi: fix a double free bug in rsi_91x_deinit()

`dev` (struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) field of adapter
(struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) is allocated  and initialized in
`rsi_init_usb_interface`. If any error is detected in information
read from the device side,  `rsi_init_usb_interface` will be
freed. However, in the higher level error handling code in
`rsi_probe`, if error is detected, `rsi_91x_deinit` is called
again, in which `dev` will be freed again, resulting double free.

This patch fixes the double free by removing the free operation on
`dev` in `rsi_init_usb_interface`, because `rsi_91x_deinit` is also
used in `rsi_disconnect`, in that code path, the `dev` field is not
 (and thus needs to be) freed.

This bug was found in v4.19, but is also present in the latest version
of kernel. Fixes CVE-2019-15504.

Reported-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Mathias Payer <mathias.payer@nebelwelt.net>
Signed-off-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Guenter Roeck <linux@roeck-us.net>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.2.9 has a Double Free via crafted USB device traffic (which may be remote via usbip or usbredir).

vulnerabilities.description: On the `fail_eps` error path, the function returns without freeing `rsi_dev`, which was allocated with `kzalloc`. This causes a memory leak every time an error occurs before or at the `fail_eps` label (e.g., when `rsi_find_bulk_in_and_out_endpoints` fails or `tx_buffer` allocation fails).

step_by_step_execution.description: rsi_dev is allocated via kzalloc
adapter->rsi_dev is set to rsi_dev
rsi_find_bulk_in_and_out_endpoints fails, so we jump to fail_eps
At fail_eps, the function returns status without calling kfree(rsi_dev). The allocated memory is leaked.

(Bug 1044, CVE-2010-4345)
cve_desc: Exim 4.72 and earlier allows local users to gain privileges by leveraging the ability of the exim user account to specify an alternate configuration file with a directive that contains arbitrary commands, as demonstrated by the spool_directory directive.

code_snippets:
uschar *s = expand_string(host_number_string);
  long int n = Ustrtol(s, &end, 0);
vulnerabilities.description: If expand_string(host_number_string) returns NULL (expansion failure), the result is passed directly to Ustrtol without a NULL check, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The code checks if host_number_string is not NULL and enters the block.

state.value: non-NULL string that will fail expansion

step_by_step_execution.description: expand_string is called on host_number_string. If expansion fails, it returns NULL.
Ustrtol(s, &end, 0) is called with s being NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.description: Under CONFIGURE_FILE_USE_NODE or CONFIGURE_FILE_USE_EUID build configurations, suffix points to the end of the filename within big_buffer. The sprintf calls append up to ~270 additional bytes. If the filename already occupies most of big_buffer, the writes go past big_buffer's bounds, causing a buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: string_nextinlist reads a filename into big_buffer, up to big_buffer_size bytes.

state.value: pointer into big_buffer, could be up to big_buffer_size - 1 chars long

step_by_step_execution.description: suffix is set to the end of the filename string within big_buffer.

state.value: filename + Ustrlen(filename), near end of big_buffer if filename is long

step_by_step_execution.description: sprintf appends '.%ld.%.256s' which can write up to ~270+ additional bytes past suffix. If filename already consumed most of big_buffer, this writes past the end of big_buffer.

state.value: up to ~270 bytes written past current position, potentially beyond big_buffer bounds

PiperOrigin-RevId: 370766155
Change-Id: I33e7c6626224e1060a8a4ab51ad5d861c6d4c63e
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. An attacker can cause a denial of service by controlling the values of `num_segments` tensor argument for `UnsortedSegmentJoin`. This is because the implementation(https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a2a607db15c7cd01d754d37e5448d72a13491bdb/tensorflow/core/kernels/unsorted_segment_join_op.cc#L92-L93) assumes that the `num_segments` tensor is a valid scalar. Since the tensor is empty the `CHECK` involved in `.scalar<T>()()` that checks that the number of elements is exactly 1 will be invalidated and this would result in process termination. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.5.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.4.2, TensorFlow 2.3.3, TensorFlow 2.2.3 and TensorFlow 2.1.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The `num_segments` value is read from user input and checked only for non-emptiness, but never validated to be non-negative. If `NUM_SEGMENTS_TYPE` is a signed integer type (e.g., int32 or int64), a user can supply a negative value. This negative value is then passed to `GetOutputShape`, which may construct a `TensorShape` with a negative dimension. Depending on the implementation of `GetOutputShape` and TensorFlow's internal shape handling, this could lead to an undersized or incorrectly-sized output tensor allocation. If the allocation succeeds with an unexpected size, subsequent writes via `output_flat(output_index)` could go out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: num_segments_tensor is read from context->input(2). The only check is that NumElements() != 0.

state.value: user-controlled tensor with at least 1 element

step_by_step_execution.description: num_segments is extracted as a scalar. If the user provides a negative value (e.g., -1) and NUM_SEGMENTS_TYPE is a signed type, num_segments becomes negative.
segment_dims and input_dims pass validation (segment_dims >= 1, segment_dims <= input_dims, matching dimensions).

GetOutputShape is called with the negative num_segments value. This may produce a TensorShape with a negative or zero-sized dimension, potentially leading to a very small or zero-element output tensor allocation.

state.value: shape with negative dimension from num_segments = -1

step_by_step_execution.description: The segment_id validation loop checks flat_segment_id(i) < num_segments (which is -1) AND >= 0. If the segment_id tensor has any elements, all would be rejected. However, if segment_id has zero elements (shape like [0]), the loop does not execute and no error is raised.

big_stride and small_stride are computed from input and segment_id shapes. With a zero-element segment_id, relative_offset_set may also be empty, but if GetOutputShape allowed a malformed shape, the output tensor state is already corrupted for any future use.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.LoadAndRemapMatrix vulnerability with invalid `row_remapping`.

Check that `row_remapping` has the correct dims().

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445522800
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.LoadAndRemapMatrix does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `initializing_values` is a vector but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The final loops use `int` loop variables (`i` and `j`) to iterate up to `num_rows_` and `num_cols_` which are `int64_t`. If `num_rows_` or `num_cols_` exceeds `INT_MAX` (~2.1 billion), the `int` variable overflows, leading to undefined behavior. In practice, the signed overflow wraps the counter negative, causing the comparison `i < num_rows_` to remain perpetually true, and negative indices are used to access `row_id_present[i]`, `col_id_present[j]`, and `output_matrix(i, j)`, resulting in out-of-bounds reads and writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: The op is constructed with `num_rows_` set to a value greater than INT_MAX (e.g., 2147483648).

Input tensors pass validation: `row_remapping` has size `num_rows_`, etc. The output matrix of shape {num_rows_, num_cols_} is allocated successfully (the int64_t-based TensorShape handles large sizes).

The final loop begins: `for (int i = 0; i < num_rows_; ++i)`. The variable `i` is of type `int` (32-bit signed). When `i` reaches INT_MAX (2147483647), the next increment causes signed integer overflow.

After overflow, `i` wraps to a negative value (undefined behavior per the C++ standard, but commonly wraps to -2147483648). The comparison `i < num_rows_` is between a promoted negative value and a large positive int64_t, so it remains true.
The code accesses `row_id_present[i]` with a negative index, causing an out-of-bounds read. Similarly, `output_matrix(i, j)` is accessed with a negative row index, causing an out-of-bounds write.

commit_message:
ALSA: pcm: oss: Fix race at SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC

There is a small race window at snd_pcm_oss_sync() that is called from
OSS PCM SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl; namely the function calls
snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() at first, then takes the params_lock mutex
for the rest.  When the stream is set up again by another thread
between them, it leads to inconsistency, and may result in unexpected
results such as NULL dereference of OSS buffer as a fuzzer spotted
recently.

The fix is simply to cover snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() call into the same
params_lock mutex with snd_pcm_oss_make_ready_locked() variant.

Reported-and-tested-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Jaroslav Kysela <perex@perex.cz>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/CAFcO6XN7JDM4xSXGhtusQfS2mSBcx50VJKwQpCq=WeLt57aaZA@mail.gmail.com
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220905060714.22549-1-tiwai@suse.de
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: A race condition flaw was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem due to improper locking. It could lead to a NULL pointer dereference while handling the SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl. A privileged local user (root or member of the audio group) could use this flaw to crash the system, resulting in a denial of service condition

commit_message:
handshake: check inappropriate fallback against the configured max version

That allows to operate on a server which is explicitly configured to
utilize earlier than TLS 1.2 versions.
cve_desc: The SSL protocol 3.0, as used in OpenSSL through 1.0.1i and other products, uses nondeterministic CBC padding, which makes it easier for man-in-the-middle attackers to obtain cleartext data via a padding-oracle attack, aka the "POODLE" issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The first loop over the input data accesses data[i] and data[i+1] before the datalen % 2 check is performed. If datalen is odd, the last iteration of the loop will access data[datalen-1] (valid) and data[datalen] (one byte out of bounds), resulting in an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with an odd datalen, e.g. datalen = 5, and data points to a 5-byte buffer.
The first for loop starts iterating: i=0, accesses data[0] and data[1] — both valid.
Loop continues: i=2, accesses data[2] and data[3] — both valid.
Loop continues: i=4, which is less than datalen (5). Accesses data[4] (valid) and data[4+1] = data[5], which is one byte past the end of the buffer — out-of-bounds read.
The datalen % 2 != 0 check that would reject odd-length input is only performed after this loop has already completed, so the OOB read has already occurred.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1221
cve_desc: The ReadMATImageV4 function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick 7.0.8-7 uses an uninitialized variable, leading to memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.description: When HDR.imagf==1 and HDR.Type[1] is 2, 3, 4, or default, the code reads depth/8*columns bytes into the pixels buffer (where depth is 8 or 16), but then passes the buffer to InsertComplexFloatRow which interprets it as an array of floats (4 bytes each). This means InsertComplexFloatRow reads 4*columns bytes from a buffer that only contains 1*columns or 2*columns bytes of valid data, causing a heap buffer over-read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The MAT4 header is parsed. HDR.Type[1] is set to 2 (from the type digit decomposition), meaning unsigned 16-bit format.
HDR.imagf is read and equals 1, indicating complex data is present.
image->columns is set to HDR.nRows, e.g. 100.
quantum_info is created and configured with depth=16. The pixel buffer obtained via GetQuantumPixels is sized according to depth=16 and columns=100, roughly 2*100 = 200 bytes (plus any overhead).
The main grayscale pixel loop runs, reading depth/8*columns = 2*100 = 200 bytes per row. This fits the buffer.
Since HDR.imagf==1, the complex pixel loop begins. ReadBlob reads depth/8*columns = 200 bytes into the pixels buffer.

HDR.Type[1] is 2 (not 0), so the else branch is taken and InsertComplexFloatRow is called, casting pixels to (float*). This function interprets each element as a 4-byte float and iterates over columns=100 elements, thus accessing 4*100 = 400 bytes from a buffer that only has ~200 bytes of valid allocation.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-f5qg-pqcg-765m
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a stack buffer overflow vulnerability that affects PJSUA2 users or users that call the API `pjmedia_sdp_print(), pjmedia_sdp_media_print()`. Applications that do not use PJSUA2 and do not directly call `pjmedia_sdp_print()` or `pjmedia_sdp_media_print()` should not be affected. A patch is available on the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The initial buffer length check does not account for the cumulative size of the format (fmt) entries written in the loop. An attacker controlling the SDP media description with many or large fmt entries can cause a heap/stack buffer overflow past the end of 'buf'.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function checks if `len` is large enough for the media name, transport, and a fixed overhead of 12+24=36 bytes. This check does NOT include the total size of all fmt entries.

state.value: e.g. 10, with each fmt[i].slen being significant

step_by_step_execution.description: The function writes the 'm=' prefix, media name, port, transport into the buffer. Pointer p advances but stays within the initially validated region.
The function enters the fmt loop. For each fmt entry, it writes a space and copies fmt[i].slen bytes without checking if p remains within bounds (p < end).

state.value: Advances by 1 + fmt[i].slen per iteration, potentially exceeding 'end'

step_by_step_execution.description: After several iterations, p exceeds 'end', and pj_memcpy writes beyond the allocated buffer, causing an out-of-bounds write.

state.value: buf + len + N (past the end of the buffer)

commit_message:
handshake: check inappropriate fallback against the configured max version

That allows to operate on a server which is explicitly configured to
utilize earlier than TLS 1.2 versions.
cve_desc: The SSL protocol 3.0, as used in OpenSSL through 1.0.1i and other products, uses nondeterministic CBC padding, which makes it easier for man-in-the-middle attackers to obtain cleartext data via a padding-oracle attack, aka the "POODLE" issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The check for `datalen % 2 != 0` occurs AFTER the first loop that iterates over `data` in 2-byte steps. If `datalen` is odd, the last iteration of the first loop accesses `data[datalen-1]` (valid) and `data[datalen]` (one byte past the buffer), resulting in an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with an odd `datalen`, e.g. datalen = 5. The `data` buffer has 5 valid bytes (indices 0-4).
The first for-loop begins: `for (i = 0; i < datalen; i += 2)`. First iteration with i=0 accesses data[0] and data[1] — both valid.
Second iteration with i=2 accesses data[2] and data[3] — both valid.

Third iteration with i=4. Condition `i < datalen` is `4 < 5` which is true. The code accesses data[4] (valid) and data[4+1] = data[5] which is one byte past the end of the buffer — out-of-bounds read.
The `datalen % 2 != 0` check that would have rejected this input is only performed later in the function, after the vulnerable loop has already executed.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4253: using freed memory when substitute with function call

Problem:    Using freed memory when substitute uses a recursive function call.
Solution:   Make a copy of the substitute text.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in `needed_len` calculation can bypass the buffer reallocation check, leading to a heap buffer overflow. `needed_len` is declared as `int`, and when accumulated with `len` (the current length of `new_start`), the sum can overflow to a negative value. Since the comparison `needed_len > (int)new_start_len` would then be false (negative < positive), the buffer is not resized, and subsequent `mch_memmove` and `vim_regsub_multi` write beyond the allocated `new_start` buffer. This can be triggered with repeated global substitutions (`:s/.../long_replacement/g`) on a sufficiently long line.

code_snippets: (void)vim_regsub_multi(&regmatch, sub_firstlnum - regmatch.startpos[0].lnum, sub, new_end, TRUE, magic_isset(), TRUE);

step_by_step_execution.description: The substitute command is invoked with the global flag (e.g., `:%s/./AAAA.../g`) on a long line, causing many matches on one line.
On the first match, `new_start` is allocated with `new_start_len = needed_len + 50`. The substitution text is written into `new_start`.

On subsequent matches in the inner `for(;;)` loop, the code enters the `else` branch to check if the existing buffer is large enough. `len = (unsigned)STRLEN(new_start)` gets the current content length.

state.value: accumulated length of substituted text so far (int, grows with each match)

step_by_step_execution.description: `needed_len += len` is computed. After many iterations with a long replacement string, this sum exceeds INT_MAX and wraps to a negative value due to signed integer overflow.

state.value: positive unsigned value from the last reallocation

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `if (needed_len > (int)new_start_len)` evaluates to false because the negative `needed_len` is less than the positive `(int)new_start_len`. The reallocation is skipped.

state.value: new_start + len (points near end of the too-small buffer)

step_by_step_execution.description: `mch_memmove(new_end, sub_firstline + copycol, (size_t)copy_len)` and `vim_regsub_multi(..., new_end, TRUE, ...)` write substitution data past the end of the `new_start` buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: points beyond the allocated region of new_start

commit_message:
media: vidtv: Check for null return of vzalloc

As the possible failure of the vzalloc(), e->encoder_buf might be NULL.
Therefore, it should be better to check it in order
to guarantee the success of the initialization.
If fails, we need to free not only 'e' but also 'e->name'.
Also, if the allocation for ctx fails, we need to free 'e->encoder_buf'
else.

Fixes: f90cf6079bf6 ("media: vidtv: add a bridge driver")
Signed-off-by: Jiasheng Jiang <jiasheng@iscas.ac.cn>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
Signed-off-by: Mauro Carvalho Chehab <mchehab@kernel.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.16-rc6. There is a lack of check after calling vzalloc() and lack of free after allocation in drivers/media/test-drivers/vidtv/vidtv_s302m.c.

vulnerabilities.description: The return value of vzalloc() is not checked for NULL. If the allocation fails, e->encoder_buf will be NULL, and subsequent code that uses this buffer will dereference a NULL pointer.

step_by_step_execution.description: kzalloc for 'e' succeeds.
vzalloc(VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ) is called but fails, returning NULL. No check is performed.
The function continues to set up the encoder and eventually returns 'e' with a NULL encoder_buf, leading to a NULL pointer dereference when the buffer is later accessed (e.g., by vidtv_s302m_encode).

vulnerabilities.description: When kzalloc for 'ctx' fails, only 'e' is freed with kfree, but e->name (allocated by kstrdup) and e->encoder_buf (allocated by vzalloc) are not freed, causing memory leaks.

step_by_step_execution.description: kzalloc for 'e' succeeds.
args.name is non-NULL, so kstrdup allocates memory for e->name.
vzalloc succeeds, allocating e->encoder_buf.
kzalloc for ctx fails, returning NULL. The error path only calls kfree(e), leaking e->name and e->encoder_buf.

commit_message:
Check configure file permissions even for non-default files if still privileged

(Bug 1044, CVE-2010-4345)
cve_desc: Exim 4.72 and earlier allows local users to gain privileges by leveraging the ability of the exim user account to specify an alternate configuration file with a directive that contains arbitrary commands, as demonstrated by the spool_directory directive.

vulnerabilities.description: Buffer overflow via sprintf into big_buffer. The variable 'filename' points into 'big_buffer' (populated by string_nextinlist with up to big_buffer_size bytes), and 'suffix' points to the end of that string within big_buffer. The sprintf calls append up to ~279 additional bytes (for the .euid.nodename case) past the end of the existing filename string, with no check that there is sufficient remaining space in big_buffer. If the filename is close to big_buffer_size in length, this overflows the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: string_nextinlist reads a filename from config_main_filelist into big_buffer, up to big_buffer_size bytes.

state.value: points to big_buffer, containing a string of length up to big_buffer_size - 1

step_by_step_execution.description: suffix is calculated as filename + Ustrlen(filename), pointing to the null terminator at the end of the filename within big_buffer.

state.value: big_buffer + Ustrlen(filename), e.g. big_buffer + (big_buffer_size - 1) if filename fills the buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: uname() succeeds, providing uts.nodename (up to 256 chars allowed by %.256s format).

state.value: a hostname string up to 256 characters

step_by_step_execution.description: sprintf writes '.%ld.%.256s' starting at suffix, appending up to ~279 bytes beyond the end of the existing filename string. Since suffix may already be near the end of big_buffer, this write goes past the buffer boundary.

state.value: up to ~279 bytes (1 dot + up to 20 digits for long + 1 dot + up to 256 chars + null)
as little as 1 byte (the null terminator)

code_snippets:
uschar *s = expand_string(host_number_string);
  long int n = Ustrtol(s, &end, 0);
vulnerabilities.description: NULL pointer dereference when expand_string(host_number_string) returns NULL. The return value is used directly in Ustrtol without a NULL check, unlike similar expand_string calls elsewhere in the function that check for NULL.

step_by_step_execution.description: host_number_string is non-NULL, so the code enters the block.

state.value: non-NULL string containing an expression that fails to expand

step_by_step_execution.description: expand_string is called and returns NULL due to an expansion failure.
Ustrtol(s, &end, 0) is called with s = NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA: Don't return 4 extra bytes at end

labels_data_count already accounts for the unicode null character at the
end of the array. There is no need in adding space for it again.

Signed-off-by: Christof Schmitt <christof.schmitt@us.ibm.com>
Reviewed-by: Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Reviewed-by: Simo Sorce <idra@samba.org>

Autobuild-User(master): Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Autobuild-Date(master): Tue Aug  6 04:03:17 CEST 2013 on sn-devel-104
cve_desc: Samba 3.6.6 through 3.6.23, 4.0.x before 4.0.18, and 4.1.x before 4.1.8, when a certain vfs shadow copy configuration is enabled, does not properly initialize the SRV_SNAPSHOT_ARRAY response field, which allows remote authenticated users to obtain potentially sensitive information from process memory via a (1) FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA or (2) FSCTL_SRV_ENUMERATE_SNAPSHOTS request.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in labels_data_count calculation for FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA. If shadow_data->num_volumes (returned by SMB_VFS_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA) is large enough, the expression `num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)` can overflow a uint32, resulting in a small labels_data_count and a small buffer allocation. The subsequent loop then writes num_volumes entries into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters the FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA case with max_out_len large enough to pass the initial check (>16), and labels is set to True.
shadow_data is allocated and SMB_VFS_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA is called, returning shadow_data with a very large num_volumes value.

state.value: large value causing overflow, e.g. if sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL) is 25, then num_volumes=85899346 makes num_volumes*2*25 = 4294967300 which overflows uint32 to 4

step_by_step_execution.description: labels_data_count is computed with integer overflow: (num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)) + 2 wraps around uint32.
*out_len is set to 12 + labels_data_count, a small value.
The check `max_out_len < *out_len` passes since max_out_len is large.
talloc_zero_array allocates a buffer of only *out_len (18) bytes for cur_pdata.
The loop iterates num_volumes (85899346) times, each iteration writing 2*sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL) bytes and advancing cur_pdata, writing far beyond the allocated 18-byte buffer.

state.value: advances past allocated buffer on second iteration

commit_message:
media: vidtv: Check for null return of vzalloc

As the possible failure of the vzalloc(), e->encoder_buf might be NULL.
Therefore, it should be better to check it in order
to guarantee the success of the initialization.
If fails, we need to free not only 'e' but also 'e->name'.
Also, if the allocation for ctx fails, we need to free 'e->encoder_buf'
else.

Fixes: f90cf6079bf6 ("media: vidtv: add a bridge driver")
Signed-off-by: Jiasheng Jiang <jiasheng@iscas.ac.cn>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
Signed-off-by: Mauro Carvalho Chehab <mchehab@kernel.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.16-rc6. There is a lack of check after calling vzalloc() and lack of free after allocation in drivers/media/test-drivers/vidtv/vidtv_s302m.c.

vulnerabilities.description: The encoder buffer is allocated with vzalloc() but freed with kfree() in the error path. Mixing vmalloc/vzalloc allocations with kfree causes undefined behavior and can lead to memory corruption, as the kernel memory allocators use different metadata and freeing mechanisms.

step_by_step_execution.description: The encoder structure 'e' is successfully allocated with kzalloc.
args.name may be non-NULL, so e->name may be allocated via kstrdup.

state.value: possibly a valid kstrdup'd string or NULL

step_by_step_execution.description: e->encoder_buf is allocated with vzalloc(VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ) and succeeds.
The allocation of ctx via kzalloc(priv_sz, GFP_KERNEL) fails, returning NULL.

Execution jumps to out_kfree_buf, which calls kfree(e->encoder_buf) on a vzalloc-allocated buffer. This is invalid: vfree() should be used instead. This causes undefined behavior / memory corruption in the kernel.

state.value: vzalloc'd pointer passed to kfree — invalid

Problem:    NULL pointer access when using invalid pattern.
Solution:   Check for failed regexp program.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx

* Fix HTTP request smuggling vulnerability

See GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx or CVE-2021-41136 for more info.

* 4.3.9 release note

* 5.5.1 release note

* 5.5.1
cve_desc: Puma is a HTTP 1.1 server for Ruby/Rack applications. Prior to versions 5.5.1 and 4.3.9, using `puma` with a proxy which forwards HTTP header values which contain the LF character could allow HTTP request smugggling. A client could smuggle a request through a proxy, causing the proxy to send a response back to another unknown client. The only proxy which has this behavior, as far as the Puma team is aware of, is Apache Traffic Server. If the proxy uses persistent connections and the client adds another request in via HTTP pipelining, the proxy may mistake it as the first request's body. Puma, however, would see it as two requests, and when processing the second request, send back a response that the proxy does not expect. If the proxy has reused the persistent connection to Puma to send another request for a different client, the second response from the first client will be sent to the second client. This vulnerability was patched in Puma 5.5.1 and 4.3.9. As a workaround, do not use Apache Traffic Server with `puma`.

vulnerabilities.description: The function declares the input buffer as `const char *buffer`, but the parser casts away const and writes to the buffer in-place when processing HTTP header field names via `snake_upcase_char((char *)p)`. This modifies data the caller expects to be unmodified, and if the caller passes a pointer to genuinely read-only memory (e.g., mmap'd read-only page or a string literal), this results in undefined behavior or a crash.

step_by_step_execution.description:
Function is called with a const char *buffer containing a valid HTTP request with headers, e.g. "GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com

", and off=0.

state.value: pointer to const char data (possibly read-only memory)

step_by_step_execution.description: Parser processes "GET" as method (states 1->2->27->28), encounters space, transitions to state 3 via tr2, calling request_method callback.
Parser processes "/ " as request URI and path, then "HTTP/1.1\r\n" as version, reaching state 15 (header field parsing).

state.value: points to first character of header field name, e.g. 'H' in 'Host'

step_by_step_execution.description: At state 15, the character matches a valid header field start character (e.g. 'H'). Transition tr21 is taken, which executes MARK(field_start, p) and then snake_upcase_char((char *)p), writing to the const buffer.

state.value: points to 'H' in the const buffer
being written to via cast that removes const qualifier

step_by_step_execution.description: For each subsequent character in the header field name, transition tr23 is taken, which again calls snake_upcase_char((char *)p), writing to the const buffer repeatedly.

state.value: advancing through header field name characters
being modified in-place despite const declaration

code_snippets: assert(off <= len && "offset past end of buffer");
vulnerabilities.description: The check that `off <= len` is performed only via an assert(), which is compiled out when NDEBUG is defined (typical in release/production builds). If a caller passes off > len, p will point past pe, the `p == pe` check will be false, and the parser will dereference `*p` which is out of bounds of the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with off > len (e.g., len=10, off=20). In a release build with NDEBUG defined, the assert is a no-op.
p is set to buffer+off and pe is set to buffer+len. Since off > len, p > pe.
The check `if (p == pe)` evaluates to false since p != pe (p > pe), so execution falls through to the switch statement.
The parser dereferences *p in `switch( (*p) )`, reading memory beyond the end of the buffer.

commit_message:
Fix oobread and unaligned casts in the NE entrypoint logic ##crash

* Reported by @hmsec via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: nepocaligns
* BountyID: ec538fa4-06c6-4050-a141-f60153ddeaac
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability may allow attackers to read sensitive information or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.description: In the moveable bundle case (bundle_type == 0xff), `segnum` is read from attacker-controlled entry table data and only checked to be > 0. There is no upper-bound check against the actual number of segment entries. An attacker can craft a NE binary with a segnum value larger than the segment_entries array, leading to an out-of-bounds read on `bin->segment_entries`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a crafted NE binary. bin->entry_table is non-NULL, entries and segments lists are created.
The while loop begins parsing the entry table. bundle_length is read as a non-zero value, and bundle_type is read as 0xff (moveable).
The inner for-loop begins. The bounds check `tableat + off + 4 >= r_buf_size(bin->buf)` passes. off is incremented by 1 (flags byte), then by 2 (skipping two bytes).
segnum is read from the entry table at the current offset. The attacker has placed a large value, e.g. 200, at this position. segnum > 0 check passes.

bin->segment_entries[segnum - 1] is accessed, which is bin->segment_entries[199]. If the actual segment count (e.g. bin->ne_header->SegCount) is much smaller (e.g. 4), this reads far beyond the allocated segment_entries array.

vulnerabilities.description: The bounds check at the start of each inner loop iteration validates only `off + 4` bytes, but the moveable (0xff) code path consumes 6 bytes (1 flag + 2 skip + 1 segnum + 2 segoff). This means the last 2 bytes (the ut16 segoff read) can read beyond what was validated against the buffer size, causing an out-of-bounds read on the entry_table buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop condition `off < bin->ne_header->EntryTableLength` passes. The inner bounds check `tableat + off + 4 >= r_buf_size(bin->buf)` also passes, meaning there are at least 4 bytes remaining from the current offset.

state.value: e.g. r_buf_size(bin->buf) - 5 (exactly 5 bytes remain, check passes since 5 < buf_size)
5 bytes guaranteed by bounds check (off+4 < buf_size means at least 5 bytes)

step_by_step_execution.description: bundle_type is 0xff (moveable). In the inner loop, off is incremented: off++ (flags, 1 byte), off += 2 (2 bytes), segnum read at off then off++ (1 byte). Total consumed so far: 4 bytes.

A ut16 read is performed at the new off position, requiring 2 more bytes. Total needed: 6 bytes from initial off. But only 5 bytes were guaranteed. The ut16 read at off accesses 1 byte beyond what was validated.

commit_message:
Fix oobread in java parser ##crash

* Reported by @bet4it via @huntrdev
* BountyID c8f4c2de-7d96-4ad4-857a-c099effca2d6
* Reproducer: bootstrap.class
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_java_bootstrap_methods_attr_new function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0. The bug causes the program reads data past the end 2f the intented buffer. Typically, this can allow attackers to read sensitive information from other memory locations or cause a crash. More details see [CWE-125: Out-of-bounds read](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/125.html).

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read occurs when `sz` is less than 8. The variable `offset` is set to 6 before reading a 2-byte unsigned short from `buffer + offset` (i.e., buffer[6] and buffer[7]), but there is no check that `sz >= offset + 2` (i.e., `sz >= 8`) before the read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a small buffer, e.g. sz = 7 (enough to pass default_attr_new but not enough for the USHORT read).
r_bin_java_default_attr_new is called and returns a non-NULL attr pointer (it parses the first 6 bytes of the attribute header).
offset is incremented by 6.
The attr is non-NULL, so we enter the if block. R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT(buffer, offset) reads 2 bytes at buffer[6] and buffer[7]. Since sz is 7, buffer[7] is out of bounds.

commit_message: Add test to demonstrate buffer overrun
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds write vulnerability exists in the drill format T-code tool number functionality of Gerbv 2.7.0, dev (commit b5f1eacd), and the forked version of Gerbv (commit 71493260). A specially-crafted drill file can lead to code execution. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.description: The expression `fd++` performs a post-increment on the `fd` pointer (of type `gerb_file_t *`). After this statement, `fd` is advanced by `sizeof(gerb_file_t)` bytes, pointing to invalid memory. The original `fd` value is passed to `get_line`, which is correct for that call, but immediately afterward `fd->filename` is accessed using the now-incremented (invalid) pointer. All subsequent uses of `fd` in the function also operate on this out-of-bounds pointer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads a character after 'T' and stores it in `temp`.
The condition `(temp == 'C') && ((fd->ptr + 2) < fd->datalen)` is true, so we enter the first if-block.
gerb_fgetc(fd) reads the next char which is 'S', so we enter the second if-block.
gerb_fgetc(fd) reads the next char which is 'T', so we enter the third if-block.
`fd->ptr -= 4` adjusts the file pointer backward.
`get_line(fd++)` is called. The post-increment passes the original `fd` to `get_line` (correct), but then increments `fd` to point past the gerb_file_t struct.

state.value: now points to (original_fd + sizeof(gerb_file_t)), which is out-of-bounds/invalid memory

step_by_step_execution.description: `gerbv_stats_printf` is called with `fd->filename`, which dereferences the now-invalid `fd` pointer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message: Fix mask usage in image-compositor
cve_desc: A flaw was found in cairo's image-compositor.c in all versions prior to 1.17.4. This flaw allows an attacker who can provide a crafted input file to cairo's image-compositor (for example, by convincing a user to open a file in an application using cairo, or if an application uses cairo on untrusted input) to cause a stack buffer overflow -> out-of-bounds WRITE. The highest impact from this vulnerability is to confidentiality, integrity, as well as system availability.

vulnerabilities.description: The buffer pointer `m`, initialized to `r->_buf`, is incremented without any bounds checking against the allocated size of `r->_buf`. When spans have partial coverage (not 0xff and not 0x00), data is written into `r->_buf` via `m` for the entire length of each span (`spans[1].x - spans[0].x`). A sequence of spans with partial coverage covering a large total x-range can cause writes past the end of the `_buf` buffer, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with num_spans > 0. Pointer m is initialized to r->_buf.
The first span has coverage that is neither 0xff nor 0x00, so the else branch is taken.
The coverage byte is written at *m and m is incremented. Then memset fills len-1 more bytes and m advances by len-1. Total advance is len bytes, with no check that m + len <= r->_buf + buf_size.
If multiple consecutive spans also have partial coverage, the loop continues writing into r->_buf at increasing offsets without any bounds check, potentially exceeding the allocated size of r->_buf.

commit_message: Added missing allocation checks
cve_desc: Array index error in the (1) dtoa implementation in dtoa.c (aka pdtoa.c) and the (2) gdtoa (aka new dtoa) implementation in gdtoa/misc.c in libc, as used in multiple operating systems and products including in FreeBSD 6.4 and 7.2, NetBSD 5.0, OpenBSD 4.5, Mozilla Firefox 3.0.x before 3.0.15 and 3.5.x before 3.5.4, K-Meleon 1.5.3, SeaMonkey 1.1.8, and other products, allows context-dependent attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via a large precision value in the format argument to a printf function, which triggers incorrect memory allocation and a heap-based buffer overflow during conversion to a floating-point number.

commit_message: Fix possible buffer overflow
cve_desc: None

vulnerabilities.description: The code retrieves the first element of the x5c CBOR array and immediately calls cbor_bytestring_handle() and cbor_bytestring_length() on it without first verifying that the element is actually a CBOR bytestring. If an attacker provides an x5c array whose element is a different CBOR type (e.g., integer, string, map), these functions will interpret the internal cbor_item_t data structure incorrectly (type confusion), leading to reads from invalid memory locations.

step_by_step_execution.description: att_stmt is validated to be a CBOR map of size 2.
The for loop iterates over the map keys and finds a key matching "x5c" (via prefix matching), setting x5c to its associated value.

state.value: attacker-controlled CBOR value from the map

step_by_step_execution.description: x5c is checked to be a CBOR array of size 1. The attacker provides a valid CBOR array of size 1, but the single element is not a bytestring (e.g., it is a CBOR uint or text string).

state.value: CBOR array of size 1, containing a non-bytestring element

step_by_step_execution.description: att_cert = cbor_array_get(x5c, 0) retrieves the first element. No type check on att_cert is performed.

state.value: CBOR item of non-bytestring type (e.g., uint)

step_by_step_execution.description: cbor_bytestring_handle(att_cert) is called on a non-bytestring item, causing type confusion. The function interprets internal data fields as a byte buffer pointer, returning a potentially invalid pointer.
cbor_bytestring_length(att_cert) is called on the same non-bytestring item, returning a potentially large or invalid size.
gnutls_x509_crt_import(cert, &cert_dat, ...) is called with the invalid pointer and size, potentially causing out-of-bounds reads.

vulnerabilities.description: The key comparison uses MIN(expected_key_length, actual_key_length) as the comparison length. When the actual key length is 0, the comparison length is 0, and o_strncmp returns 0 (match) for any input. This means an empty-string key will always match "x5c". Additionally, any key that is a prefix of the expected key (e.g., "x" matches "x5c", "s" matches "sig") or any key that starts with the expected key (e.g., "x5cFOO" matches "x5c") will also match. This allows an attacker to supply crafted key names that bypass intended key validation, potentially assigning incorrect CBOR values to x5c or sig.

step_by_step_execution.description: att_stmt is a valid CBOR map of size 2. The attacker crafts the map with keys that are not exactly "x5c" and "sig" but still match due to the flawed comparison.

state.value: CBOR map with 2 entries, e.g., keys "" (empty) and "sig"

step_by_step_execution.description: For i=0, key is an empty CBOR string (length 0). MIN(3, 0) = 0, so o_strncmp compares 0 bytes and returns 0 (equal). x5c is set to the value associated with the empty-string key.

state.value: set to attacker-controlled value that was not keyed by "x5c"

step_by_step_execution.description: For i=1, key is "sig" and sig is set normally. The code proceeds with an x5c value that was associated with an unrelated key, bypassing proper key validation.

state.value: CBOR value from incorrect map entry

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-f5qg-pqcg-765m
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a stack buffer overflow vulnerability that affects PJSUA2 users or users that call the API `pjmedia_sdp_print(), pjmedia_sdp_media_print()`. Applications that do not use PJSUA2 and do not directly call `pjmedia_sdp_print()` or `pjmedia_sdp_media_print()` should not be affected. A patch is available on the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

commit_message:
esp: Fix possible buffer overflow in ESP transformation

The maximum message size that can be send is bigger than
the  maximum site that skb_page_frag_refill can allocate.
So it is possible to write beyond the allocated buffer.

Fix this by doing a fallback to COW in that case.

v2:

Avoid get get_order() costs as suggested by Linus Torvalds.

Fixes: cac2661c53f3 ("esp4: Avoid skb_cow_data whenever possible")
Fixes: 03e2a30f6a27 ("esp6: Avoid skb_cow_data whenever possible")
Reported-by: valis <sec@valis.email>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow flaw was found in IPsec ESP transformation code in net/ipv4/esp4.c and net/ipv6/esp6.c. This flaw allows a local attacker with a normal user privilege to overwrite kernel heap objects and may cause a local privilege escalation threat.
