commit_message:
FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA: Don't return 4 extra bytes at end

labels_data_count already accounts for the unicode null character at the
end of the array. There is no need in adding space for it again.

Signed-off-by: Christof Schmitt <christof.schmitt@us.ibm.com>
Reviewed-by: Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Reviewed-by: Simo Sorce <idra@samba.org>

Autobuild-User(master): Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Autobuild-Date(master): Tue Aug  6 04:03:17 CEST 2013 on sn-devel-104
cve_desc: Samba 3.6.6 through 3.6.23, 4.0.x before 4.0.18, and 4.1.x before 4.1.8, when a certain vfs shadow copy configuration is enabled, does not properly initialize the SRV_SNAPSHOT_ARRAY response field, which allows remote authenticated users to obtain potentially sensitive information from process memory via a (1) FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA or (2) FSCTL_SRV_ENUMERATE_SNAPSHOTS request.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA when computing labels_data_count. If shadow_data->num_volumes is sufficiently large, the multiplication (num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)) overflows a uint32, resulting in a small labels_data_count and consequently a small buffer allocation. The subsequent loop then writes num_volumes entries into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters the FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA case. max_out_len is a large value provided by the caller (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF). labels is set to True since max_out_len > 16.
SMB_VFS_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA returns successfully with a large num_volumes from the VFS backend (e.g., a malicious or compromised snapshot provider returns num_volumes = 0x02000000).

labels_data_count is computed: num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL). Assuming SHADOW_COPY_LABEL is 25 bytes: 0x02000000 * 2 * 25 = 0x02000000 * 50 = 0x640000000, which overflows uint32 and wraps to a small value.

state.value: Small value due to uint32 overflow (e.g., wraps around)

step_by_step_execution.description: *out_len = 12 + labels_data_count + 4 is computed as a small value due to the overflowed labels_data_count.
The check max_out_len < *out_len passes because max_out_len (0xFFFFFFFF) is much larger than the small *out_len.
cur_pdata is allocated with talloc_zero_array of size *out_len — a small buffer.
The loop iterates num_volumes (33554432) times, writing 2*sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL) = 50 bytes per iteration into cur_pdata. This massively overflows the small heap buffer.

commit_message:
Save stack space while handling errors

Because error handling (luaG_errormsg) uses slots from EXTRA_STACK,
and some errors can recur (e.g., string overflow while creating an
error message in 'luaG_runerror', or a C-stack overflow before calling
the message handler), the code should use stack slots with parsimony.

This commit fixes the bug "Lua-stack overflow when C stack overflows
while handling an error".
cve_desc: An issue in the component luaG_runerror of Lua v5.4.4 and below leads to a heap-buffer overflow when a recursive error occurs.

commit_message: improve fix #72558, while (u>=0) with unsigned int will always be true
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the _gdContributionsAlloc function in gd_interpolation.c in GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) before 2.2.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds memory write or memory consumption) via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable `u` is declared as `unsigned int`. In the error cleanup loop, `while (u >= 0)` is always true for an unsigned integer. After freeing the Weights at index 0, `u--` wraps `u` to UINT_MAX, causing an out-of-bounds read on `res->ContribRow[UINT_MAX]` and passing a garbage pointer to `gdFree`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with valid `line_length` (e.g., 2) and `windows_size` such that `windows_size * sizeof(double)` does not overflow.
Memory for `res` and `res->ContribRow` is successfully allocated. The for-loop begins with u=0.
Iteration u=0: `gdMalloc` for Weights succeeds. u increments to 1.
Iteration u=1: `gdMalloc` for Weights returns NULL (allocation failure). The error path is entered.
`u--` decrements u to 0. The while loop condition `u >= 0` is checked; since u is unsigned, this is always true.
`gdFree(res->ContribRow[0].Weights)` is called, freeing the successfully allocated weights. Then `u--` is executed.

The while loop condition `u >= 0` is checked again — still true because u is unsigned. The code accesses `res->ContribRow[4294967295].Weights`, which is a massive out-of-bounds read, leading to undefined behavior (likely a crash or exploitable memory corruption).

commit_message: Add test to demonstrate buffer overrun
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds write vulnerability exists in the drill format T-code tool number functionality of Gerbv 2.7.0, dev (commit b5f1eacd), and the forked version of Gerbv (commit 71493260). A specially-crafted drill file can lead to code execution. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.description: When tool_num is outside the valid range [TOOL_MIN, TOOL_MAX), the code logs an error but does not return or otherwise prevent execution from continuing. It proceeds to use tool_num as an array index into image->aperture[], resulting in an out-of-bounds array access.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads a character after 'T' and determines it is a digit, so it proceeds to parse the tool number.
gerb_fgetint is called and returns a tool number outside the valid range, e.g., a very large number from crafted input.
The bounds check fires and logs an error message, but execution continues (no return statement).
The out-of-bounds tool_num is used to index into image->aperture[], reading and potentially writing beyond the array.

code_snippets:
gerbv_stats_printf(stats->error_list, GERBV_MESSAGE_NOTE, -1,
			_("Tool change stop switch found \"%s\" "
			    "at line %ld in file \"%s\""),
			tmps, file_line, fd->filename);
vulnerabilities.description: The expression 'fd++' uses post-increment on the pointer parameter 'fd'. While get_line receives the original pointer, after this statement fd points to memory past the original gerb_file_t structure. The subsequent access to fd->filename reads from invalid memory (out-of-bounds read on the struct pointer).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the character after 'T' and finds 'C'.
The next character is read and is 'S', then the following character is 'T', matching the TCS+T pattern.
fd->ptr is decremented by 4, then get_line(fd++) is called. Post-increment returns original fd to get_line but advances the local fd pointer by sizeof(gerb_file_t).

state.value: original_fd + 1 (points past the valid gerb_file_t structure)

step_by_step_execution.description: fd->filename is accessed in gerbv_stats_printf, but fd now points to invalid memory beyond the original structure.

commit_message: Fix mask usage in image-compositor
cve_desc: A flaw was found in cairo's image-compositor.c in all versions prior to 1.17.4. This flaw allows an attacker who can provide a crafted input file to cairo's image-compositor (for example, by convincing a user to open a file in an application using cairo, or if an application uses cairo on untrusted input) to cause a stack buffer overflow -> out-of-bounds WRITE. The highest impact from this vulnerability is to confidentiality, integrity, as well as system availability.

vulnerabilities.description: When processing spans with partial coverage (not 0x00 or 0xFF), the function writes coverage values into the mask buffer `m` without any bounds checking against the actual allocated size of the mask image data. The pointer `m` is advanced by the pixel length of each partial-coverage span. If consecutive partial-coverage spans collectively span a range larger than the mask buffer allocation, this results in a heap buffer overflow via the `*m++` write and the `memset` call.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with spans data. `base` is set to the start of the mask image pixel data, which has a fixed allocated size.

state.value: pointer to mask image data buffer of fixed size

step_by_step_execution.description: num_spans is checked to be > 0, then x0 is set to spans[0].x and the loop begins.
For the current span, len is computed as spans[1].x - spans[0].x. If coverage is neither 0xFF (with sufficient run length) nor 0x00, the else branch is taken.
In the else branch, *m++ writes one byte, then if len > 1, memset writes (len-1) more bytes, advancing m by len total. No check is performed to ensure m stays within the mask buffer bounds.

state.value: base + len (after write, may exceed mask buffer allocation)

step_by_step_execution.description: If multiple consecutive spans have partial coverage, the loop repeats the else branch, further advancing m. The cumulative advancement of m can exceed the mask buffer size, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Bug 701785: fixed sanitizer heap-buffer-overflow in lprn_is_black().

In contrib/lips4/gdevlprn.c:lprn_is_black(), it seems that bpl is not
necessarily a multiple of lprn->nBw, so we need to explicitly avoid straying
into the next line's data.

This also avoids accessing beyond our buffer if we are already on the last
line, and so fixes the sanitizer error.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=lips2p ../bug-701785.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in lprn_is_black() in contrib/lips4/gdevlprn.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.description: If `r + h - bh` is negative, the C `%` operator can yield a negative result, making `y0` negative. This causes the index `(y0 + y) * bpl + bx * lprn->nBw` to be negative, leading to an out-of-bounds read before the start of `lprn->ImageBuf`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with parameters where `r + h < bh`. For example, r=0, h=1, and lprn->nBh (bh) = 8.
maxY is computed as `lprn->BlockLine / lprn->nBh * lprn->nBh`. Assume BlockLine=64, so maxY=64.
y0 is computed as `(r + h - bh) % maxY` = `(0 + 1 - 8) % 64` = `(-7) % 64`. In C, this yields -7.
The loop starts with y=0. The index into ImageBuf is `(y0 + y) * bpl + bx * lprn->nBw` = `(-7 + 0) * bpl + bx * nBw`, which is a negative offset (assuming bx*nBw < 7*bpl).
The pointer `p` points before the start of `lprn->ImageBuf`, and `p[x]` performs an out-of-bounds read from heap memory.

commit_message: improve fix #72558, while (u>=0) with unsigned int will always be true
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the _gdContributionsAlloc function in gd_interpolation.c in GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) before 2.2.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds memory write or memory consumption) via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.description: When the first iteration (u=0) of the allocation loop fails (either due to overflow or malloc returning NULL), `u--` causes an unsigned integer underflow. Since `u` is `unsigned int`, decrementing from 0 wraps to UINT_MAX. The subsequent cleanup loop `for (i=0; i<=u; i++)` then iterates up to UINT_MAX times, calling gdFree on uninitialized `Weights` pointers from out-of-bounds memory in `res->ContribRow`, leading to heap corruption, out-of-bounds reads, and freeing of invalid pointers.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with any valid line_length and a windows_size that causes overflow2(windows_size, sizeof(double)) to return true (e.g., a very large windows_size).
Memory for res and res->ContribRow is allocated successfully.

state.value: valid pointer to array of line_length ContributionType

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop begins with u = 0. overflow2(windows_size, sizeof(double)) returns true, so overflow_error is set to 1. The else branch (gdMalloc) is skipped, so res->ContribRow[0].Weights is never assigned.
The condition `overflow_error == 1` is true, so we enter the error-handling block. `u--` is executed on unsigned int u=0, causing underflow.

The cleanup loop `for (i=0; i<=u; i++)` iterates from 0 to UINT_MAX, reading far beyond the bounds of res->ContribRow and calling gdFree on uninitialized/garbage Weights pointers, causing out-of-bounds memory access and heap corruption.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

commit_message:
Improve fix for avoiding huge number of tiny dashes

Some pathological cases were not caught by the previous fix.

Fixes: QTBUG-95239
Pick-to: 6.2 6.1 5.15
Change-Id: I0337ee3923ff93ccb36c4d7b810a9c0667354cc5
Reviewed-by: Robert Löhning <robert.loehning@qt.io>
cve_desc: Qt 5.x before 5.15.6 and 6.x through 6.1.2 has an out-of-bounds write in QOutlineMapper::convertPath (called from QRasterPaintEngine::fill and QPaintEngineEx::stroke).

vulnerabilities.description: When processing a CurveToElement, the code reads 6 qreal values (points[0]..points[5]) and advances by 6, but the loop guard only checks `points < lastPoint`, which guarantees at most 2 remaining values (one point). A malformed QVectorPath with a CurveToElement type near the end of its elements array (where fewer than 3 coordinate pairs remain) will cause an out-of-bounds read from the points buffer. This pattern appears in both the non-cosmetic and cosmetic pen code paths.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a QVectorPath `path`. Suppose path has elementCount() == 2, with types[0]=MoveToElement and types[1]=CurveToElement. The points buffer has 4 qreal values (2 points).
lastPoint is computed as points + (pointCount << 1) = points + 4.
The loop begins: `while (points < lastPoint)`. Initially points is at index 0, which is less than points+4, so we enter the loop.
First iteration processes MoveToElement: reads points[0], points[1] (valid), advances points by 2 and types by 1.
Second iteration: points (points+2) < lastPoint (points+4) is true, so we enter the loop. *types is CurveToElement.
The CurveToElement case reads points[0]..points[5], i.e., 6 qreal values. Only 2 values remain in the buffer. Accesses to points[2], points[3], points[4], points[5] are out-of-bounds reads.

commit_message:
Bluetooth: virtio_bt: fix memory leak in virtbt_rx_handle()

On the reception of packets with an invalid packet type, the memory of
the allocated socket buffers is never freed. Add a default case that frees
these to avoid a memory leak.

Fixes: afd2daa26c7a ("Bluetooth: Add support for virtio transport driver")
Signed-off-by: Soenke Huster <soenke.huster@eknoes.de>
Signed-off-by: Marcel Holtmann <marcel@holtmann.org>
cve_desc: drivers/bluetooth/virtio_bt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.3 has a memory leak (socket buffers have memory allocated but not freed).

commit_message:
atlantic: Fix OOB read and write in hw_atl_utils_fw_rpc_wait

This bug report shows up when running our research tools. The
reports is SOOB read, but it seems SOOB write is also possible
a few lines below.

In details, fw.len and sw.len are inputs coming from io. A len
over the size of self->rpc triggers SOOB. The patch fixes the
bugs by adding sanity checks.

The bugs are triggerable with compromised/malfunctioning devices.
They are potentially exploitable given they first leak up to
0xffff bytes and able to overwrite the region later.

The patch is tested with QEMU emulater.
This is NOT tested with a real device.

Attached is the log we found by fuzzing.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4253: using freed memory when substitute with function call

Problem:    Using freed memory when substitute uses a recursive function call.
Solution:   Make a copy of the substitute text.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the buffer reallocation logic. `needed_len` and `len` are declared as `int`, but `len` is assigned from `(unsigned)STRLEN(new_start)`. When repeated substitutions on a single line cause `new_start` to grow very large (approaching or exceeding INT_MAX bytes), the cast of the unsigned STRLEN result to `int` produces a negative value. Adding this negative `len` to `needed_len` causes `needed_len` to become smaller than the actual required size (or even negative). The subsequent check `needed_len > (int)new_start_len` then incorrectly evaluates to false, skipping reallocation. The code then writes into the undersized buffer via `mch_memmove` and `vim_regsub_multi`, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A substitute command with the global flag is executed on a line, e.g., `:%s/x/LARGE_REPLACEMENT/g`, where the replacement string is significantly larger than the matched pattern. The loop iterates for each match on the line.
On the first match, `needed_len` is computed and `new_start` is allocated with `new_start_len = needed_len + 50`. The substitution result is appended to `new_start`.
After many iterations of match-and-replace on the same line (with `do_all`), `new_start` accumulates substituted text and grows. Eventually STRLEN(new_start) approaches or exceeds 2^31 bytes.

`len` is computed as `(unsigned)STRLEN(new_start)`. The unsigned value (e.g., 2147483648u) is assigned to `int len`, causing a sign conversion overflow. The `int` value wraps to a large negative number.

`needed_len += len` is computed. Since `len` is negative and `needed_len` (the size of the new content to append) is positive but much smaller in magnitude, the result is a negative or very small positive `needed_len`.

state.value: negative or much smaller than the actual required size

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `needed_len > (int)new_start_len` evaluates to false because `needed_len` is negative/small and `new_start_len` is the existing buffer size. The reallocation is skipped.

`new_end` is set to `new_start + len`, which points near or past the end of the allocated buffer. `mch_memmove(new_end, sub_firstline + copycol, (size_t)copy_len)` and `vim_regsub_multi(... new_end ...)` write beyond the allocated `new_start` buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: new_start + len, pointing at or beyond the buffer boundary
copy_len + sublen bytes written out of bounds

commit_message:
Remove double free() in contrib/shpsrt, issue #39

This fixes issue #39
cve_desc: A double-free condition exists in contrib/shpsort.c of shapelib 1.5.0 and older releases. This issue may allow an attacker to cause a denial of service or have other unspecified impact via control over malloc.

commit_message:
sctp: account stream padding length for reconf chunk

sctp_make_strreset_req() makes repeated calls to sctp_addto_chunk()
which will automatically account for padding on each call. inreq and
outreq are already 4 bytes aligned, but the payload is not and doing
SCTP_PAD4(a + b) (which _sctp_make_chunk() did implicitly here) is
different from SCTP_PAD4(a) + SCTP_PAD4(b) and not enough. It led to
possible attempt to use more buffer than it was allocated and triggered
a BUG_ON.

Cc: Vlad Yasevich <vyasevich@gmail.com>
Cc: Neil Horman <nhorman@tuxdriver.com>
Cc: Greg KH <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Fixes: cc16f00f6529 ("sctp: add support for generating stream reconf ssn reset request chunk")
Reported-by: Eiichi Tsukata <eiichi.tsukata@nutanix.com>
Signed-off-by: Eiichi Tsukata <eiichi.tsukata@nutanix.com>
Signed-off-by: Marcelo Ricardo Leitner <marcelo.leitner@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Marcelo Ricardo Leitner <mleitner@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Xin Long <lucien.xin@gmail.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/b97c1f8b0c7ff79ac4ed206fc2c49d3612e0850c.1634156849.git.mleitner@redhat.com
Signed-off-by: Jakub Kicinski <kuba@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the sctp_make_strreset_req function in net/sctp/sm_make_chunk.c in the SCTP network protocol in the Linux kernel with a local user privilege access. In this flaw, an attempt to use more buffer than is allocated triggers a BUG_ON issue, leading to a denial of service (DOS).

vulnerabilities.description: The computation `stream_num * sizeof(__u16)` is done in __u16 arithmetic, which can overflow/wrap around for large stream_num values (e.g., stream_num > 32767). This would make stream_len much smaller than expected, leading to undersized buffer allocation via sctp_make_reconf, while later sctp_addto_chunk writes the truncated stream_len bytes from stream_list. However, the param_hdr.length field is also computed from the truncated values, so the protocol-level length is inconsistent with actual stream_num. More critically, if the caller relies on stream_num being faithfully represented, the mismatch between allocated size and logical content constitutes a potential issue. That said, the most concrete concern is the integer overflow in __u16 causing outlen/inlen to be small, allocating a small chunk, and then writing stream_len bytes which, while also truncated, could still exceed the allocated buffer if the padding math differs.

step_by_step_execution.description: stream_len is computed as stream_num * sizeof(__u16) in __u16 arithmetic
outlen is computed using the truncated stream_len
sctp_make_reconf allocates a chunk based on the small outlen+inlen

state.value: based on SCTP_PAD4(outlen) + SCTP_PAD4(inlen), which is small

step_by_step_execution.description: sctp_addto_chunk writes stream_len (2) bytes from stream_list, but the param_hdr.length claims only sizeof(outreq)+2 bytes. The data written is inconsistent with the actual stream_num streams the caller intended, causing data truncation and protocol-level inconsistency. With other stream_num values the wrap could cause outlen itself to wrap and the allocated vs written sizes to diverge.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-3qx3-cg72-wrh9
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming RTCP BYE message contains a reason's length, this declared length is not checked against the actual received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP. A malicious actor can send a RTCP BYE message with an invalid reason length. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

commit_message:
parallels: Sanity check for s->tracks (CVE-2014-0142)

This avoids a possible division by zero.

Convert s->tracks to unsigned as well because it feels better than
surviving just because the results of calculations with s->tracks are
converted to unsigned anyway.

Signed-off-by: Kevin Wolf <kwolf@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Max Reitz <mreitz@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Stefan Hajnoczi <stefanha@redhat.com>
cve_desc: QEMU, possibly before 2.0.0, allows local users to cause a denial of service (divide-by-zero error and crash) via a zero value in the (1) tracks field to the seek_to_sector function in block/parallels.c or (2) extent_size field in the bochs function in block/bochs.c.

commit_message: Added missing allocation checks
cve_desc: Array index error in the (1) dtoa implementation in dtoa.c (aka pdtoa.c) and the (2) gdtoa (aka new dtoa) implementation in gdtoa/misc.c in libc, as used in multiple operating systems and products including in FreeBSD 6.4 and 7.2, NetBSD 5.0, OpenBSD 4.5, Mozilla Firefox 3.0.x before 3.0.15 and 3.5.x before 3.5.4, K-Meleon 1.5.3, SeaMonkey 1.1.8, and other products, allows context-dependent attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via a large precision value in the format argument to a printf function, which triggers incorrect memory allocation and a heap-based buffer overflow during conversion to a floating-point number.

vulnerabilities.description: If MALLOC returns NULL (allocation failure), the code proceeds to dereference rv without any NULL check, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with some value k. The freelist[k] entry is NULL, so we enter the else branch.
x is computed as 1 << k, and MALLOC is called to allocate memory. The allocation fails and returns NULL.
rv->k and rv->maxwds are assigned, which is already a NULL dereference. Even if those are somehow skipped, rv->sign = rv->wds = 0 dereferences the NULL pointer.

vulnerabilities.description: The parameter k is used without bounds checking as an index into the freelist array and to compute 1 << k. A large or negative k can cause an out-of-bounds array access on freelist, or undefined behavior / integer overflow via the left shift.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with an attacker-influenced or unchecked value of k that is negative or exceeds the bounds of the freelist array.

state.value: e.g. negative or >= size of freelist array

step_by_step_execution.description: freelist[k] is accessed, which is an out-of-bounds read on the freelist array.
If the freelist path is not taken, 1 << k is computed. For k >= 31 (on 32-bit int), this is undefined behavior due to signed integer overflow.

commit_message:
ip6_gre: fix ip6gre_err() invalid reads

Andrey Konovalov reported out of bound accesses in ip6gre_err()

If GRE flags contains GRE_KEY, the following expression
*(((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1)

accesses data ~40 bytes after the expected point, since
grehlen includes the size of IPv6 headers.

Let's use a "struct gre_base_hdr *greh" pointer to make this
code more readable.

p[1] becomes greh->protocol.
grhlen is the GRE header length.

Fixes: c12b395a4664 ("gre: Support GRE over IPv6")
Signed-off-by: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Reported-by: Andrey Konovalov <andreyknvl@google.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: The ip6gre_err function in net/ipv6/ip6_gre.c in the Linux kernel allows remote attackers to have unspecified impact via vectors involving GRE flags in an IPv6 packet, which trigger an out-of-bounds access.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow in MTU calculation. When `be32_to_cpu(info)` is smaller than `offset`, the subtraction wraps around to a very large unsigned value (since `mtu` is `__u32`). This large value passes the `mtu < IPV6_MIN_MTU` minimum check and is assigned directly to `t->dev->mtu`, allowing a remote attacker to set an excessively large MTU on the tunnel device via a crafted ICMPv6 Packet Too Big message.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with an ICMPv6 error packet. `offset` is the offset to the GRE header within the inner packet, a positive integer (at least sizeof(ipv6hdr) = 40).

state.value: >=40 (e.g., 40 or more depending on extension headers)

step_by_step_execution.description: The GRE header is parsed and validated. `grehlen` grows based on flags. The function proceeds past the pskb_may_pull checks.
A tunnel lookup succeeds (t is non-NULL), and execution enters the switch statement with `type == ICMPV6_PKT_TOOBIG`.
An attacker sends a crafted ICMPv6 Packet Too Big with a small `info` value (e.g., info representing MTU of 0 or a value less than `offset`). `mtu = be32_to_cpu(info) - offset` underflows.
The check `mtu < IPV6_MIN_MTU` (IPV6_MIN_MTU = 1280) evaluates to false because the wrapped-around value is extremely large.
The excessively large MTU value is assigned to the tunnel device, potentially causing incorrect packet handling or memory corruption when the device later attempts to use this MTU.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4206: condition with many "(" causes a crash

Problem:    Condition with many "(" causes a crash.
Solution:   Limit recursion to 1000.
cve_desc: Access of Memory Location Before Start of Buffer in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

Cc: Vlad Yasevich <vyasevich@gmail.com>

Fixes: cc16f00f6529 ("sctp: add support for generating stream reconf ssn reset request chunk")
Reported-by: Eiichi Tsukata <eiichi.tsukata@nutanix.com>
Signed-off-by: Eiichi Tsukata <eiichi.tsukata@nutanix.com>
Signed-off-by: Marcelo Ricardo Leitner <marcelo.leitner@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Marcelo Ricardo Leitner <mleitner@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Xin Long <lucien.xin@gmail.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/b97c1f8b0c7ff79ac4ed206fc2c49d3612e0850c.1634156849.git.mleitner@redhat.com
Signed-off-by: Jakub Kicinski <kuba@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the sctp_make_strreset_req function in net/sctp/sm_make_chunk.c in the SCTP network protocol in the Linux kernel with a local user privilege access. In this flaw, an attempt to use more buffer than is allocated triggers a BUG_ON issue, leading to a denial of service (DOS).

vulnerabilities.description: The computation of stream_len uses __u16 arithmetic, so stream_num * sizeof(__u16) can overflow a 16-bit value when stream_num is large (e.g., stream_num > 32767). This causes stream_len to wrap around to a small value. Subsequently, outlen and inlen (also __u16) are computed from this truncated stream_len, leading to a smaller-than-needed buffer allocation via sctp_make_reconf. The later sctp_addto_chunk calls then write the full stream_list data (based on the original stream_num) using the wrapped stream_len, potentially writing beyond the allocated chunk buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: stream_len is computed as stream_num * sizeof(__u16) in __u16 arithmetic. For example, if stream_num = 0x8000 (32768), then stream_num * 2 = 0x10000, which wraps to 0 in __u16.
outlen is computed as (sizeof(outreq) + stream_len) * out. With out=true and stream_len=0, outlen = sizeof(outreq) (a small value like 16).
sctp_make_reconf allocates a chunk buffer sized for outlen + inlen, which is far too small since stream_len was truncated to 0.

state.value: outlen + inlen (small, does not account for stream data)

step_by_step_execution.description: Inside the if(outlen) block, sctp_addto_chunk writes sizeof(outreq) bytes (fits), then checks if(stream_len) — stream_len is 0 so it skips. In this specific case with stream_len=0 no overflow occurs. However, with a value like stream_num=0xC000, stream_len wraps to 0x8000 instead of 0x18000, causing a partial mismatch. The allocation is based on the truncated stream_len but the actual stream_list pointer may reference more data.

More critically, outlen = (sizeof(outreq) + stream_len) is also computed in __u16 and can itself overflow. E.g., stream_len=0xFFF0 (stream_num=0x7FF8), outlen = sizeof(outreq)+0xFFF0 can wrap in __u16, leading to a tiny allocation but large writes via sctp_addto_chunk.

state.value: wraps around in __u16, e.g. small value

commit_message:
Fix oobread and unaligned casts in the NE entrypoint logic ##crash

* Reported by @hmsec via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: nepocaligns
* BountyID: ec538fa4-06c6-4050-a141-f60153ddeaac
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability may allow attackers to read sensitive information or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.description: In the moveable bundle branch (bundle_type == 0xff), the code reads 6 bytes from off_start (1 flag + 2 skip + 1 segnum + 2 segoff), but the bounds check at the top of the for loop only guarantees 5 bytes are accessible (tableat + off + 4 < buf_size). The r_read_le16 at offset off_start+4 reads a second byte at off_start+5 which is beyond the validated range, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop begins with some value of off. The bounds check at the for-loop's start verifies tableat + off + 4 < r_buf_size(bin->buf), ensuring only 5 bytes from off are in bounds.

state.value: off_start (value at start of for-loop iteration)

step_by_step_execution.description: entry is allocated, then off is incremented by 1 (flags byte).
bundle_type == 0xff so we enter the moveable branch. off is incremented by 2.
segnum is read from entry_table[off_start + 3], which is in bounds. off is incremented by 1.

The check 'off > EntryTableLength' may pass (not break). Then r_read_le16 reads 2 bytes at entry_table[off_start + 4] and entry_table[off_start + 5]. The byte at off_start + 5 was never validated by the bounds check and may be out of bounds of the allocated entry_table buffer.

state.value: reads entry_table[off_start + 4] and entry_table[off_start + 5], but only up to off_start + 4 was validated

vulnerabilities.description: In both the moveable and fixed bundle branches, when an early break occurs due to a bounds check, the previously allocated 'entry' object is not freed, resulting in a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: A new RBinAddr entry is allocated with R_NEW0.

In the moveable branch, after advancing off, the check 'off > bin->ne_header->EntryTableLength' triggers a break. In the fixed branch, the check 'off + 2 >= bin->ne_header->EntryTableLength' triggers a break. In both cases, the allocated entry is never freed or appended to the entries list.

commit_message:
Fix possible stack corruption with XM instrument headers claiming a size of less than 4

Closes #275
cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on the pattern data buffer. The index 'pc' into 'buffer' (allocated with size phead[y].patdata) is never checked against the buffer size. A malformed XM file can craft pattern data such that the compressed/packed data requires reading past the end of the allocated buffer, because the loop iterates phead[y].rows * header->channum times reading a variable number of bytes without ensuring pc remains within bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: Pattern header is read from file. phead[y].rows and phead[y].patdata are read from file data.
Pattern data buffer is allocated with size phead[y].patdata.
The nested loop iterates rows * channum = 256 * 32 = 8192 times, each iteration reading 1-5 bytes from buffer[pc].
After only a few iterations, pc exceeds phead[y].patdata (10), causing reads of buffer[pc] to go out of bounds.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on smpReloc array. The nbu array contains mp_ubyte values (0-255) read from the file, used as indices into smpReloc which has MP_MAXINSSAMPS entries (typically 96). If any nbu[sc] value is >= MP_MAXINSSAMPS, accessing smpReloc[nbu[sc]] results in an out-of-bounds read. This occurs in both the v0x102/v0x103 and v0x104 code paths.

step_by_step_execution.description: Instrument data is read from file. nbu is populated from file data via memcpy(nbu, insDataPtr, MP_MAXINSSAMPS). Each byte is 0-255.
smpReloc is declared as mp_sint32 smpReloc[MP_MAXINSSAMPS]. In v0x104, it is zeroed; in v0x102, it may contain uninitialized data. Only indices 0 to instr[y].samp-1 are explicitly set.
Loop iterates sc from 0 to MP_MAXINSSAMPS-1, accessing smpReloc[nbu[sc]]. If nbu[sc] is e.g. 200 and MP_MAXINSSAMPS is 96, this accesses smpReloc[200] which is out of bounds.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in pattern data allocation. phead[y].rows (up to 65535) multiplied by header->channum (up to 65535) multiplied by 6 can overflow a 32-bit integer, resulting in a small allocation but large memset and subsequent writes, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Pattern rows and channel number are read from file.

The multiplication phead[y].rows * header->channum * 6 = 65535 * 65535 * 6 overflows. As 32-bit: 65535*65535 = 0xFFFE0001, *6 = 0x5FFF40006 truncated to 0xFFF40006 or similar small value depending on exact overflow.

state.value: truncated/wrapped small value due to integer overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: A small buffer is allocated, then memset writes using the same overflowed size, and the pattern parsing loop writes based on rows*channum iterations, causing heap buffer overflow.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1221
cve_desc: The ReadMATImageV4 function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick 7.0.8-7 uses an uninitialized variable, leading to memory corruption.

commit_message:
Remove double free() in contrib/shpsrt, issue #39

This fixes issue #39
cve_desc: A double-free condition exists in contrib/shpsort.c of shapelib 1.5.0 and older releases. This issue may allow an attacker to cause a denial of service or have other unspecified impact via control over malloc.

vulnerabilities.description: When realloc fails and result is NULL (i.e., on the very first iteration when i==0), the condition `!tmp && result` is false because result is NULL. The function continues, setting `result = tmp` (which is NULL), and then dereferences it with `result[i++] = dupstr(cptr)`, causing a NULL pointer dereference / write.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters the for loop with the first token. At this point, result is NULL and i is 0.
realloc(NULL, sizeof(*result) * 1) is called but fails, returning NULL.
The check `!tmp && result` evaluates to `!NULL && NULL` which is `true && false` = false. The error handling block is skipped.
result is assigned tmp (NULL), then result[i++] = dupstr(cptr) dereferences a NULL pointer, writing to address 0.

commit_message:
parallels: Sanity check for s->tracks (CVE-2014-0142)

This avoids a possible division by zero.

Convert s->tracks to unsigned as well because it feels better than
surviving just because the results of calculations with s->tracks are
converted to unsigned anyway.

Signed-off-by: Kevin Wolf <kwolf@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Max Reitz <mreitz@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Stefan Hajnoczi <stefanha@redhat.com>
cve_desc: QEMU, possibly before 2.0.0, allows local users to cause a denial of service (divide-by-zero error and crash) via a zero value in the (1) tracks field to the seek_to_sector function in block/parallels.c or (2) extent_size field in the bochs function in block/bochs.c.

commit_message: check max member count in class
cve_desc: sqclass.cpp in Squirrel through 2.2.5 and 3.x through 3.1 allows an out-of-bounds read (in the core interpreter) that can lead to Code Execution. If a victim executes an attacker-controlled squirrel script, it is possible for the attacker to break out of the squirrel script sandbox even if all dangerous functionality such as File System functions has been disabled. An attacker might abuse this bug to target (for example) Cloud services that allow customization via SquirrelScripts, or distribute malware through video games that embed a Squirrel Engine.

patch 8.2.4206: condition with many "(" causes a crash

Problem:    Condition with many "(" causes a crash.
Solution:   Limit recursion to 1000.
cve_desc: Access of Memory Location Before Start of Buffer in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message:
bpf, ringbuf: Deny reserve of buffers larger than ringbuf

A BPF program might try to reserve a buffer larger than the ringbuf size.
If the consumer pointer is way ahead of the producer, that would be
successfully reserved, allowing the BPF program to read or write out of
the ringbuf allocated area.

Reported-by: Ryota Shiga (Flatt Security)
Fixes: 457f44363a88 ("bpf: Implement BPF ring buffer and verifier support for it")
Signed-off-by: Thadeu Lima de Souza Cascardo <cascardo@canonical.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Acked-by: Andrii Nakryiko <andrii@kernel.org>
Acked-by: Alexei Starovoitov <ast@kernel.org>
cve_desc: The eBPF RINGBUF bpf_ringbuf_reserve() function in the Linux kernel did not check that the allocated size was smaller than the ringbuf size, allowing an attacker to perform out-of-bounds writes within the kernel and therefore, arbitrary code execution. This issue was fixed via commit 4b81ccebaeee ("bpf, ringbuf: Deny reserve of buffers larger than ringbuf") (v5.13-rc4) and backported to the stable kernels in v5.12.4, v5.11.21, and v5.10.37. It was introduced via 457f44363a88 ("bpf: Implement BPF ring buffer and verifier support for it") (v5.8-rc1).

commit_message:
Fix possible stack corruption with XM instrument headers claiming a size of less than 4

Closes #275
cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on the pattern data buffer. The variable `pc` is used to index into `buffer` (allocated with size `phead[y].patdata`) without any bounds checking during the row/channel parsing loop. A crafted XM file with a small `patdata` value but large `rows * channum` will cause reads past the end of the heap-allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A pattern header is read from the file, including `phead[y].rows` and `phead[y].patdata`.
A buffer is allocated with size `phead[y].patdata` and filled from the file.

The nested loop iterates over rows * channum = 256 * 32 = 8192 channel slots. Each slot reads 1-5 bytes from `buffer[pc]` and increments `pc`. No bounds check is performed on `pc` against the buffer size (10).

state.value: increments past 10 within the first few channel slots

step_by_step_execution.description: When `pc` exceeds the buffer size (10), subsequent reads like `buffer[pc]` or `memcpy(slot, buffer+pc, 5)` access heap memory beyond the allocated buffer.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on `smpReloc` array. The `nbu` array is filled from file data and contains byte values (0-255). These values are used directly as indices into the `smpReloc` array (sized MP_MAXINSSAMPS). If `nbu[sc]` is greater than or equal to MP_MAXINSSAMPS (and samp count is less than that), the access `smpReloc[nbu[sc]]` reads beyond the array bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `nbu` array is populated from instrument data read from the file: `memcpy(nbu, insDataPtr, MP_MAXINSSAMPS)`. These are raw byte values from attacker-controlled file data.
The `smpReloc` array is a stack-allocated array of size MP_MAXINSSAMPS. Only entries at indices 0..instr[y].samp-1 are written with valid values.

state.value: stack array of MP_MAXINSSAMPS entries, only first instr[y].samp entries initialized (in v0.102 path, rest are uninitialized)

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop `for (sc = 0; sc < MP_MAXINSSAMPS; sc++)` accesses `smpReloc[nbu[sc]]`. If nbu[sc] >= MP_MAXINSSAMPS (e.g., nbu[sc]=200 when MP_MAXINSSAMPS=96), this is an out-of-bounds read from the stack array.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow when `instr[y].size` is less than 4. The expression `instr[y].size - 4` underflows for unsigned 32-bit values (since `size` is a dword), resulting in a very large read length passed to `f.read()`, causing a heap or stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: In the v0.104 instrument loading path, `instr[y].size` is read from the file via `f.readDwords(&instr[y].size, 1)`.
The condition `instr[y].size < 29` is true, so the code enters the branch with a 29-byte stack buffer.
The expression `instr[y].size - 4` is computed as unsigned arithmetic: 2 - 4 = 0xFFFFFFFE (4294967294).
`f.read(buffer, 1, 0xFFFFFFFE)` attempts to read ~4GB into a 29-byte stack buffer, causing a massive stack buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Improve fix for avoiding huge number of tiny dashes

Some pathological cases were not caught by the previous fix.

Fixes: QTBUG-95239
Pick-to: 6.2 6.1 5.15
Change-Id: I0337ee3923ff93ccb36c4d7b810a9c0667354cc5
Reviewed-by: Robert Löhning <robert.loehning@qt.io>
cve_desc: Qt 5.x before 5.15.6 and 6.x through 6.1.2 has an out-of-bounds write in QOutlineMapper::convertPath (called from QRasterPaintEngine::fill and QPaintEngineEx::stroke).

vulnerabilities.description: When a CurveToElement is encountered, the code reads 6 qreal values from the `points` array (points[0] through points[5]) and advances the pointer by 6. However, the loop condition only checks `points < lastPoint`, which merely ensures at least one point pair remains, not the three pairs (6 qreals) required for a cubic curve. A malformed QVectorPath with a CurveToElement near the end of its data (with fewer than 6 remaining qreals) causes an out-of-bounds read. This pattern appears in both the non-cosmetic and cosmetic pen branches.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function receives a QVectorPath `path` with, e.g., 2 elements: a MoveToElement followed by a CurveToElement. The points array thus has 2*2 = 4 qreal values.
lastPoint is computed as `points + (pointCount << 1)`, pointing 4 qreals past the start of the array.
The first iteration processes the MoveToElement, reading points[0] and points[1], then advancing points by 2 and types by 1.
Loop condition `points < lastPoint` is checked: start+2 < start+4 is true, so the loop continues.

The switch hits CurveToElement and attempts to read points[0] through points[5]. Only points[0] and points[1] are within bounds; points[2] through points[5] are out-of-bounds reads past the allocated buffer.

vulnerabilities.description: In the switch over element types, the `default` case does not advance either the `points` or `types` pointer. If the types array contains an unexpected value (e.g., CurveToDataElement which would appear after a CurveToElement if types is iterated incorrectly, or any corrupted value), the loop `while (points < lastPoint)` will spin forever since no progress is made, causing a denial of service via infinite loop.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function receives a QVectorPath where the types array contains an element that is not MoveToElement, LineToElement, or CurveToElement (e.g., CurveToDataElement or a corrupted value).
The while loop condition `points < lastPoint` is true since there are still points to process.
The switch statement falls through to `default: break;` which does not advance `points` or `types`.
The loop condition is re-evaluated and is still true. The same default case is hit again indefinitely, causing an infinite loop.

commit_message:
Bug 701785: fixed sanitizer heap-buffer-overflow in lprn_is_black().

In contrib/lips4/gdevlprn.c:lprn_is_black(), it seems that bpl is not
necessarily a multiple of lprn->nBw, so we need to explicitly avoid straying
into the next line's data.

This also avoids accessing beyond our buffer if we are already on the last
line, and so fixes the sanitizer error.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=lips2p ../bug-701785.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in lprn_is_black() in contrib/lips4/gdevlprn.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.description: When `r + h - bh` is negative, the C `%` operator preserves the sign of the dividend, so `y0` becomes a negative value. This negative `y0` is then used as part of an array index into `lprn->ImageBuf`, causing an out-of-bounds read before the start of the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is entered with parameters where `r + h` is less than `bh` (e.g., r=0, h=1, bh=8).
`maxY` is computed as `lprn->BlockLine / lprn->nBh * lprn->nBh`. Assume a valid positive value, e.g., 64.
`y0` is computed as `(r + h - bh) % maxY = (0 + 1 - 8) % 64 = (-7) % 64`. In C, this yields -7 (sign of dividend is preserved).
The loop begins with y=0. The index `(y0 + y) * bpl + bx * lprn->nBw` becomes `(-7) * bpl + bx * nBw`, a negative value. Pointer `p` now points before the start of `lprn->ImageBuf`.
`p[x]` is read, accessing memory before the `ImageBuf` allocation, resulting in an out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.description: If `lprn->BlockLine` is less than `lprn->nBh`, integer division truncates `BlockLine / nBh` to 0, making `maxY = 0`. The subsequent modulo operation `% maxY` triggers a division by zero (undefined behavior / crash).

step_by_step_execution.description: `lprn->BlockLine` is smaller than `lprn->nBh`, e.g., BlockLine=4, nBh=8.
`maxY` is computed as `4 / 8 * 8 = 0 * 8 = 0`.
The modulo operation `(r + h - bh) % 0` is executed, which is undefined behavior (division by zero).

commit_message:
io_uring: fix off-by-one in sync cancelation file check

The passed in index should be validated against the number of registered
files we have, it needs to be smaller than the index value to avoid going
one beyond the end.

Fixes: 78a861b94959 ("io_uring: add sync cancelation API through io_uring_register()")
Reported-by: Luo Likang <luolikang@nsfocus.com>
Signed-off-by: Jens Axboe <axboe@kernel.dk>
cve_desc: off-by-one in io_uring module.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/pull/5034
cve_desc: In ImageMagick, there is load of misaligned address for type 'double', which requires 8 byte alignment and for type 'float', which requires 4 byte alignment at MagickCore/property.c. Whenever crafted or untrusted input is processed by ImageMagick, this causes a negative impact to application availability or other problems related to undefined behavior.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

commit_message:
video: fbdev: pxa3xx-gcu: Fix integer overflow in pxa3xx_gcu_write

In pxa3xx_gcu_write, a count parameter of type size_t is passed to words of
type int.  Then, copy_from_user() may cause a heap overflow because it is used
as the third argument of copy_from_user().

Signed-off-by: Hyunwoo Kim <imv4bel@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Helge Deller <deller@gmx.de>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.19. In pxa3xx_gcu_write in drivers/video/fbdev/pxa3xx-gcu.c, the count parameter has a type conflict of size_t versus int, causing an integer overflow and bypassing the size check. After that, because it is used as the third argument to copy_from_user(), a heap overflow may occur. NOTE: the original discoverer disputes that the overflow can actually happen.

vulnerabilities.description: TOCTOU race condition leading to NULL pointer dereference. The check `if (!priv->free)` and the subsequent `pxa3xx_gcu_wait_free()` are performed without holding the spinlock. Between the time `priv->free` is observed to be non-NULL (either because it was already non-NULL, or because `wait_free` returned successfully) and the time the spinlock is acquired, another thread/context could consume all free buffers, setting `priv->free` to NULL. The code then does `buffer = priv->free` (which is NULL) and dereferences it via `buffer->next`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is entered by thread A. `priv->free` is non-NULL (one buffer remaining), so the `if (!priv->free)` block is skipped.
Before thread A acquires the spinlock, thread B (another concurrent call to pxa3xx_gcu_write) also enters the function and sees `priv->free` as non-NULL, also skipping the wait.
Thread B acquires the spinlock first, takes the last free buffer: `buffer = priv->free; priv->free = buffer->next;` which sets `priv->free = NULL`.
Thread B releases the spinlock. Thread A now acquires the spinlock and executes `buffer = priv->free;`, which assigns NULL to `buffer`.
Thread A dereferences the NULL pointer via `priv->free = buffer->next;`, causing a NULL pointer dereference / kernel oops.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4646: using buffer line after it has been freed

Problem:    Using buffer line after it has been freed in old regexp engine.
Solution:   After getting mark get the line again.
cve_desc: Use after free in utf_ptr2char in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4646.

vulnerabilities.description: In the MULTIBYTECODE case, `rex.input += len` is executed unconditionally regardless of whether the match succeeded or failed. When the status is RA_NOMATCH (either from the composing-character path or the byte-comparison path), the input pointer is still advanced by `len` bytes. This can advance `rex.input` past the NUL terminator of the current line buffer, causing subsequent reads from `rex.input` (e.g., `c = *rex.input` at the top of the loop, or character class checks) to access out-of-bounds memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the inner for-loop and reads the current opcode. `op` is determined to be MULTIBYTECODE and `has_mbyte` is true.
The operand of the scan node is fetched and its multibyte length is computed. `len` is >= 2 (a valid multibyte character in the pattern).

The code enters the non-composing else branch. It compares opnd[i] with rex.input[i] byte by byte. If rex.input is at or near the end of the line (e.g., only 1 byte before NUL), the first byte comparison may fail and status is set to RA_NOMATCH, breaking out of the comparison loop.

Execution reaches `rex.input += len` unconditionally. Since status is RA_NOMATCH, this should not advance the pointer, but it does. The input pointer is now advanced past the NUL terminator of the current line.

state.value: advanced by `len` bytes past previous position, potentially past NUL terminator

step_by_step_execution.description: On the next iteration or when subsequent code dereferences rex.input (e.g., `c = *rex.input` or character class operations), it reads from out-of-bounds memory.

state.value: points beyond the allocated/valid line buffer

commit_message:
drivers: hamradio: 6pack: fix UAF bug caused by mod_timer()

When a 6pack device is detaching, the sixpack_close() will act to cleanup
necessary resources. Although del_timer_sync() in sixpack_close()
won't return if there is an active timer, one could use mod_timer() in
sp_xmit_on_air() to wake up timer again by calling userspace syscall such
as ax25_sendmsg(), ax25_connect() and ax25_ioctl().

This unexpected waked handler, sp_xmit_on_air(), realizes nothing about
the undergoing cleanup and may still call pty_write() to use driver layer
resources that have already been released.

One of the possible race conditions is shown below:

      (USE)                      |      (FREE)
ax25_sendmsg()                   |
 ax25_queue_xmit()               |
  ...                            |
  sp_xmit()                      |
   sp_encaps()                   | sixpack_close()
    sp_xmit_on_air()             |  del_timer_sync(&sp->tx_t)
     mod_timer(&sp->tx_t,...)    |  ...
                                 |  unregister_netdev()
                                 |  ...
     (wait a while)              | tty_release()
                                 |  tty_release_struct()
                                 |   release_tty()
    sp_xmit_on_air()             |    tty_kref_put(tty_struct) //FREE
     pty_write(tty_struct) //USE |    ...

The corresponding fail log is shown below:
===============================================================
BUG: KASAN: use-after-free in __run_timers.part.0+0x170/0x470
Write of size 8 at addr ffff88800a652ab8 by task swapper/2/0
...
Call Trace:
  ...
  queue_work_on+0x3f/0x50
  pty_write+0xcd/0xe0pty_write+0xcd/0xe0
  sp_xmit_on_air+0xb2/0x1f0
  call_timer_fn+0x28/0x150
  __run_timers.part.0+0x3c2/0x470
  run_timer_softirq+0x3b/0x80
  __do_softirq+0xf1/0x380
  ...

This patch reorders the del_timer_sync() after the unregister_netdev()
to avoid UAF bugs. Because the unregister_netdev() is well synchronized,
it flushs out any pending queues, waits the refcount of net_device
decreases to zero and removes net_device from kernel. There is not any
running routines after executing unregister_netdev(). Therefore, we could
not arouse timer from userspace again.

Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Reviewed-by: Lin Ma <linma@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A use-after-free vulnerabilitity was discovered in drivers/net/hamradio/6pack.c of linux that allows an attacker to crash linux kernel by simulating ax25 device using 6pack driver from user space.

commit_message:
media: em28xx: initialize refcount before kref_get

The commit 47677e51e2a4("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct
em28xx after finishing all extensions") adds kref_get to many init
functions (e.g., em28xx_audio_init). However, kref_init is called too
late in em28xx_usb_probe, since em28xx_init_dev before will invoke
those init functions and call kref_get function. Then refcount bug
occurs in my local syzkaller instance.

Fix it by moving kref_init before em28xx_init_dev. This issue occurs
not only in dev but also dev->dev_next.

Fixes: 47677e51e2a4 ("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct em28xx after finishing all extensions")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
cve_desc: A flaw use after free in the Linux kernel video4linux driver was found in the way user triggers em28xx_usb_probe() for the Empia 28xx based TV cards. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message:

io_rw_init_file does not initialize kiocb->private, so when iocb_bio_iopoll

commit_message:
ovl: prevent private clone if bind mount is not allowed

Add the following checks from __do_loopback() to clone_private_mount() as
well:

 - verify that the mount is in the current namespace

 - verify that there are no locked children

Reported-by: Alois Wohlschlager <alois1@gmx-topmail.de>
Fixes: c771d683a62e ("vfs: introduce clone_private_mount()")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # v3.18
Signed-off-by: Miklos Szeredi <mszeredi@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the Linux kernel's OverlayFS subsystem in the way the user mounts the TmpFS filesystem with OverlayFS. This flaw allows a local user to gain access to hidden files that should not be accessible.

commit_message:
video: fbdev: pxa3xx-gcu: Fix integer overflow in pxa3xx_gcu_write

In pxa3xx_gcu_write, a count parameter of type size_t is passed to words of
type int.  Then, copy_from_user() may cause a heap overflow because it is used
as the third argument of copy_from_user().

Signed-off-by: Hyunwoo Kim <imv4bel@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Helge Deller <deller@gmx.de>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.19. In pxa3xx_gcu_write in drivers/video/fbdev/pxa3xx-gcu.c, the count parameter has a type conflict of size_t versus int, causing an integer overflow and bypassing the size check. After that, because it is used as the third argument to copy_from_user(), a heap overflow may occur. NOTE: the original discoverer disputes that the overflow can actually happen.

vulnerabilities.description: TOCTOU race condition: priv->free is checked (and waited on) outside the spinlock, but by the time the spinlock is acquired, another concurrent writer could have consumed the last free buffer, making priv->free NULL. The code then dereferences `buffer` (which is NULL) via `buffer->next`, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: Thread A enters pxa3xx_gcu_write. priv->free points to the last available buffer.
Thread B also enters pxa3xx_gcu_write concurrently. Both threads see priv->free as non-NULL, so neither enters the wait_free path.
Thread B acquires the spinlock first, takes the last free buffer, and sets priv->free = buffer->next (which is NULL).
Thread B releases the spinlock and proceeds with copy_from_user.
Thread A now acquires the spinlock. It executes `buffer = priv->free;` which assigns NULL to buffer.
Thread A dereferences the NULL buffer pointer via `priv->free = buffer->next;`, causing a NULL pointer dereference / kernel crash.

commit_message:
fuse: fix live lock in fuse_iget()

Commit 5d069dbe8aaf ("fuse: fix bad inode") replaced make_bad_inode()
in fuse_iget() with a private implementation fuse_make_bad().

The private implementation fails to remove the bad inode from inode
cache, so the retry loop with iget5_locked() finds the same bad inode
and marks it bad forever.

kmsg snip:

[ ] rcu: INFO: rcu_sched self-detected stall on CPU
...
[ ]  ? bit_wait_io+0x50/0x50
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ? find_inode.isra.32+0x60/0xb0
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ilookup5_nowait+0x65/0x90
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ilookup5.part.36+0x2e/0x80
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ? fuse_inode_eq+0x20/0x20
[ ]  iget5_locked+0x21/0x80
[ ]  ? fuse_inode_eq+0x20/0x20
[ ]  fuse_iget+0x96/0x1b0

Fixes: 5d069dbe8aaf ("fuse: fix bad inode")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # 5.10+
Signed-off-by: Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Miklos Szeredi <mszeredi@redhat.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in fs/fuse/fuse_i.h in the Linux kernel before 5.11.8. A "stall on CPU" can occur because a retry loop continually finds the same bad inode, aka CID-775c5033a0d1.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

commit_message:
Fix integer overflow in string search causing oobread ##crash

* Reported by @greatergoodest via huntrdev
* BountyID: 8a3dc5cb-08b3-4807-82b2-77f08c137a04
* Reproducer bfileovf
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0.

code_snippets:
if (needle < to - 4) {
			ut32 n1 = r_read_le32 (buf + (needle - from));
vulnerabilities.description: When `to` is less than 4 (but greater than `from`), the expression `to - 4` causes an unsigned integer wraparound (since `to` is `ut64`), resulting in a very large value. This makes the condition `needle < to - 4` always true, leading to a 4-byte read via `r_read_le32` from a heap buffer that may only be 1-3 bytes long, causing an out-of-bounds heap read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `from = 0` and `to = 3` (a valid range since `from < to`).
`len` is computed as `to - from = 3`. Passes the checks `len < 1` and `len > ST32_MAX`.
`buf` is allocated via `calloc(len, 1)`, allocating 3 bytes.
The while loop starts with `needle = from = 0`. Condition `needle < to` (0 < 3) is true.
The unsigned subtraction `to - 4` is evaluated. Since `to` is `ut64` with value 3, `3 - 4` wraps around to `0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFC` (UINT64_MAX - 3).
The condition `needle < to - 4` becomes `0 < 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFC` which is true, so the optimization block is entered.
`r_read_le32(buf + (needle - from))` reads 4 bytes starting at `buf + 0`, but `buf` is only 3 bytes long. This is a heap out-of-bounds read of 1 byte.

commit_message:
CamelGpgContext: Enclose email addresses in brackets.

The recipient list for encrypting can be specified by either key ID or
email address.  Enclose email addresses in brackets to ensure an exact
match, as per the gpg man page:

HOW TO SPECIFY A USER ID

       ...

       By exact match on an email address.
              This is indicated by enclosing the email address in the
              usual way with left and right angles.

         <heinrichh@uni-duesseldorf.de>

Without the brackets gpg uses a substring match, which risks selecting
the wrong recipient.
cve_desc: The gpg_ctx_add_recipient function in camel/camel-gpg-context.c in GNOME Evolution 3.8.4 and earlier and Evolution Data Server 3.9.5 and earlier does not properly select the GPG key to use for email encryption, which might cause the email to be encrypted with the wrong key and allow remote attackers to obtain sensitive information.

commit_message:
ipf: release unhandled packets from the batch

Since 640d4db788ed ("ipf: Fix a use-after-free error, ...") the ipf
framework unconditionally allocates a new dp_packet to track
individual fragments.  This prevents a use-after-free.  However, an
additional issue was present - even when the packet buffer is cloned,
if the ip fragment handling code keeps it, the original buffer is
leaked during the refill loop.  Even in the original processing code,
the hardcoded dnsteal branches would always leak a packet buffer from
the refill loop.

This can be confirmed with valgrind:

==717566== 16,672 (4,480 direct, 12,192 indirect) bytes in 8 blocks are definitely lost in loss record 390 of 390
==717566==    at 0x484086F: malloc (vg_replace_malloc.c:380)
==717566==    by 0x537BFD: xmalloc__ (util.c:137)
==717566==    by 0x537BFD: xmalloc (util.c:172)
==717566==    by 0x46DDD4: dp_packet_new (dp-packet.c:153)
==717566==    by 0x46DDD4: dp_packet_new_with_headroom (dp-packet.c:163)
==717566==    by 0x550AA6: netdev_linux_batch_rxq_recv_sock.constprop.0 (netdev-linux.c:1262)
==717566==    by 0x5512AF: netdev_linux_rxq_recv (netdev-linux.c:1511)
==717566==    by 0x4AB7E0: netdev_rxq_recv (netdev.c:727)
==717566==    by 0x47F00D: dp_netdev_process_rxq_port (dpif-netdev.c:4699)
==717566==    by 0x47FD13: dpif_netdev_run (dpif-netdev.c:5957)
==717566==    by 0x4331D2: type_run (ofproto-dpif.c:370)
==717566==    by 0x41DFD8: ofproto_type_run (ofproto.c:1768)
==717566==    by 0x40A7FB: bridge_run__ (bridge.c:3245)
==717566==    by 0x411269: bridge_run (bridge.c:3310)
==717566==    by 0x406E6C: main (ovs-vswitchd.c:127)

The fix is to delete the original packet when it isn't able to be
reinserted into the packet batch.  Subsequent valgrind runs show that
the packets are not leaked from the batch any longer.

Fixes: 640d4db788ed ("ipf: Fix a use-after-free error, and remove the 'do_not_steal' flag.")
Fixes: 4ea96698f667 ("Userspace datapath: Add fragmentation handling.")
Reported-by: Wan Junjie <wanjunjie@bytedance.com>
Reported-at: https://github.com/openvswitch/ovs-issues/issues/226
Signed-off-by: Aaron Conole <aconole@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Tested-by: Wan Junjie <wanjunjie@bytedance.com>
Signed-off-by: Alin-Gabriel Serdean <aserdean@ovn.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak was found in Open vSwitch (OVS) during userspace IP fragmentation processing. An attacker could use this flaw to potentially exhaust available memory by keeping sending packet fragments.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-3qx3-cg72-wrh9
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming RTCP BYE message contains a reason's length, this declared length is not checked against the actual received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP. A malicious actor can send a RTCP BYE message with an invalid reason length. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The length of the BYE reason string is read from the packet byte at offset 8 and clamped only against the destination buffer size (peer_sdes_buf_), but is never validated against the actual remaining packet size (`size - 9`). An attacker can craft a packet where the length byte at offset 8 is larger than the remaining data in the packet, causing pj_memcpy to read beyond the bounds of the `pkt` buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a crafted packet where `size` is, for example, 10 (just barely > 8), and the byte at offset 8 is set to a large value, e.g., 200.
The condition `size > 8` is true, so the branch is entered.

reason.slen is calculated as PJ_MIN(sizeof(peer_sdes_buf_), 200). Assuming peer_sdes_buf_ is >= 200 bytes, reason.slen becomes 200. Even if peer_sdes_buf_ is smaller, say 128, reason.slen becomes 128, which is still far more than the 1 byte of payload available (size - 9 = 1).
pj_memcpy copies reason.slen bytes starting from pkt+9, but only (size - 9) = 1 byte of valid data exists in the packet buffer. This reads up to 127 or 199 bytes past the end of the packet buffer.

commit_message:
ipf: release unhandled packets from the batch

Since 640d4db788ed ("ipf: Fix a use-after-free error, ...") the ipf
framework unconditionally allocates a new dp_packet to track
individual fragments.  This prevents a use-after-free.  However, an
additional issue was present - even when the packet buffer is cloned,
if the ip fragment handling code keeps it, the original buffer is
leaked during the refill loop.  Even in the original processing code,
the hardcoded dnsteal branches would always leak a packet buffer from
the refill loop.

This can be confirmed with valgrind:

==717566== 16,672 (4,480 direct, 12,192 indirect) bytes in 8 blocks are definitely lost in loss record 390 of 390
==717566==    at 0x484086F: malloc (vg_replace_malloc.c:380)
==717566==    by 0x537BFD: xmalloc__ (util.c:137)
==717566==    by 0x537BFD: xmalloc (util.c:172)
==717566==    by 0x46DDD4: dp_packet_new (dp-packet.c:153)
==717566==    by 0x46DDD4: dp_packet_new_with_headroom (dp-packet.c:163)
==717566==    by 0x550AA6: netdev_linux_batch_rxq_recv_sock.constprop.0 (netdev-linux.c:1262)
==717566==    by 0x5512AF: netdev_linux_rxq_recv (netdev-linux.c:1511)
==717566==    by 0x4AB7E0: netdev_rxq_recv (netdev.c:727)
==717566==    by 0x47F00D: dp_netdev_process_rxq_port (dpif-netdev.c:4699)
==717566==    by 0x47FD13: dpif_netdev_run (dpif-netdev.c:5957)
==717566==    by 0x4331D2: type_run (ofproto-dpif.c:370)
==717566==    by 0x41DFD8: ofproto_type_run (ofproto.c:1768)
==717566==    by 0x40A7FB: bridge_run__ (bridge.c:3245)
==717566==    by 0x411269: bridge_run (bridge.c:3310)
==717566==    by 0x406E6C: main (ovs-vswitchd.c:127)

The fix is to delete the original packet when it isn't able to be
reinserted into the packet batch.  Subsequent valgrind runs show that
the packets are not leaked from the batch any longer.

Fixes: 640d4db788ed ("ipf: Fix a use-after-free error, and remove the 'do_not_steal' flag.")
Fixes: 4ea96698f667 ("Userspace datapath: Add fragmentation handling.")
Reported-by: Wan Junjie <wanjunjie@bytedance.com>
Reported-at: https://github.com/openvswitch/ovs-issues/issues/226
Signed-off-by: Aaron Conole <aconole@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Tested-by: Wan Junjie <wanjunjie@bytedance.com>
Signed-off-by: Alin-Gabriel Serdean <aserdean@ovn.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak was found in Open vSwitch (OVS) during userspace IP fragmentation processing. An attacker could use this flaw to potentially exhaust available memory by keeping sending packet fragments.

commit_message:
CamelGpgContext: Enclose email addresses in brackets.

The recipient list for encrypting can be specified by either key ID or
email address.  Enclose email addresses in brackets to ensure an exact
match, as per the gpg man page:

HOW TO SPECIFY A USER ID

       ...

       By exact match on an email address.
              This is indicated by enclosing the email address in the
              usual way with left and right angles.

         <heinrichh@uni-duesseldorf.de>

Without the brackets gpg uses a substring match, which risks selecting
the wrong recipient.
cve_desc: The gpg_ctx_add_recipient function in camel/camel-gpg-context.c in GNOME Evolution 3.8.4 and earlier and Evolution Data Server 3.9.5 and earlier does not properly select the GPG key to use for email encryption, which might cause the email to be encrypted with the wrong key and allow remote attackers to obtain sensitive information.
