commit_message:
Fix #73280: Stack Buffer Overflow in GD dynamicGetbuf

We make sure to never pass a negative `rlen` as size to memcpy().

Cf. <https://github.com/libgd/libgd/commit/53110871>.
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the dynamicGetbuf function in gd_io_dp.c in the GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) through 2.2.3, as used in PHP before 5.6.28 and 7.x before 7.0.13, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (stack-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted imagecreatefromstring call.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/pull/5034
cve_desc: In ImageMagick, there is load of misaligned address for type 'double', which requires 8 byte alignment and for type 'float', which requires 4 byte alignment at MagickCore/property.c. Whenever crafted or untrusted input is processed by ImageMagick, this causes a negative impact to application availability or other problems related to undefined behavior.

commit_message:
Initialize the max upload file size when quotas are enabled

Due to an unwanted check, files causing the quota to be exceeded
were deleted after the upload, but not during the upload.

The bug was introduced in 2009 in version 1.0.23

Spotted by @DroidTest, thanks!
cve_desc: In Pure-FTPd before 1.0.50, an incorrect max_filesize quota mechanism in the server allows attackers to upload files of unbounded size, which may lead to denial of service or a server hang. This occurs because a certain greater-than-zero test does not anticipate an initial -1 value. (Versions 1.0.23 through 1.0.49 are affected.)

vulnerabilities.description: When `strlen(name)` equals `sizeof shm_data_cur->filename`, the else branch is taken (since `sl < sizeof shm_data_cur->filename` is false). The expression `sl - sizeof shm_data_cur->filename - 1U` computes `0 - 1U` which, because `size_t` is unsigned, wraps to `SIZE_MAX`. This causes `&name[SIZE_MAX]` to be used as the source for `memcpy`, resulting in a massive out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a `name` whose length exactly equals `sizeof shm_data_cur->filename`. All preceding checks pass, and control reaches the FTPWHO block.

state.value: a string of length equal to sizeof shm_data_cur->filename

step_by_step_execution.description: `sl` is computed as `strlen(name)`.
The condition `sl < sizeof shm_data_cur->filename` is evaluated. Since `sl == sizeof shm_data_cur->filename`, the condition is false and the else branch is taken.
The offset `sl - sizeof shm_data_cur->filename - 1U` is computed. Since all operands are `size_t` (unsigned), this is `0 - 1` which wraps around to `SIZE_MAX`.

`memcpy` is called with source `&name[SIZE_MAX]`, which is a wildly out-of-bounds pointer. This reads `sizeof shm_data_cur->filename` bytes from an invalid memory location into `shm_data_cur->filename`.

vulnerabilities.description: Under `SHOW_REAL_DISK_SPACE`, `FSTATFS` is called on the file descriptor `f` after it has already been closed by `close(f)`. This is a use-after-close of a file descriptor, which at best gives an error but could operate on a reassigned file descriptor in a multi-threaded environment.

step_by_step_execution.description: The upload completes and `close(f)` is called to close the file descriptor.
Execution reaches the SHOW_REAL_DISK_SPACE block, which calls `FSTATFS(f, &statfsbuf)` using the already-closed file descriptor.

commit_message:
Fix #73280: Stack Buffer Overflow in GD dynamicGetbuf

We make sure to never pass a negative `rlen` as size to memcpy().

Cf. <https://github.com/libgd/libgd/commit/53110871>.
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the dynamicGetbuf function in gd_io_dp.c in the GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) through 2.2.3, as used in PHP before 5.6.28 and 7.x before 7.0.13, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (stack-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted imagecreatefromstring call.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

commit_message: 0.16.1: fix for dcraw ljpeg_start() vulnerability
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the ljpeg_start function in dcraw 7.00 and earlier allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted image, which triggers a buffer overflow, related to the len variable.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow in `len` calculation. If the JPEG marker length field is 0 or 1 (i.e., data[2]=0, data[3]=0 or data[3]=1), the subtraction of 2 wraps the unsigned short `len` to 0xFFFE or 0xFFFF. This huge value is then passed to `fread` and used as a loop bound in the 0xffc4 case, causing processing of uninitialized stack data through `make_decoder_ref`, which can lead to heap corruption.

code_snippets:
for (dp = data; dp < data+len && (c = *dp++) < 4; )
	  jh->free[c] = jh->huff[c] = make_decoder_ref (&dp);

step_by_step_execution.description: Function reads the initial 2 bytes and verifies JPEG SOI marker (0xFFD8).
Function reads 4 bytes for tag and length. Attacker crafts a marker with tag 0xFFC4 and a length field of 0x0000.
len is computed as (0 << 8 | 0) - 2. The subtraction underflows since len is ushort.
fread(data, 1, 65534, ifp) is called. Since the file may not have that many bytes, fread returns fewer bytes. The rest of the 65536-byte stack buffer contains uninitialized data.

state.value: partially filled by fread, rest is uninitialized stack data

step_by_step_execution.description: The switch enters case 0xffc4. Since info_only may be 0, the for loop iterates with bound dp < data+65534, processing uninitialized stack memory.

state.value: iterates from data[0] up to data[65533]
uninitialized values from stack, if < 4 the loop body executes

step_by_step_execution.description: make_decoder_ref is called with dp pointing into uninitialized stack data, potentially constructing corrupt Huffman decoder tables on the heap based on garbage values.

state.value: decoder built from uninitialized data, possible heap corruption

vulnerabilities.description: In the 0xFFDA case, array indices are computed from attacker-controlled data[0] without verifying against the actual number of bytes read (`len`). If data[0] is large (up to 255), indices up to 513 are accessed. While within the stack buffer bounds, if `len` is small, these accesses read uninitialized stack memory, leaking potentially sensitive data into jh->psv and jh->bits.

step_by_step_execution.description: A JPEG marker with tag 0xFFDA is encountered with a small length field (e.g., len=4, meaning only 4 bytes are read into data).
fread reads only 4 bytes into data[0..3]. The rest of the stack buffer is uninitialized.
Index 1 + data[0]*2 = 1 + 400 = 401 is computed. data[401] is within the 65536-byte buffer but was never written by fread, so it contains uninitialized stack data.
jh->psv is assigned the uninitialized value. Similarly data[403] is read for jh->bits.

commit_message:
io_uring: fix off-by-one in sync cancelation file check

The passed in index should be validated against the number of registered
files we have, it needs to be smaller than the index value to avoid going
one beyond the end.

Fixes: 78a861b94959 ("io_uring: add sync cancelation API through io_uring_register()")
Reported-by: Luo Likang <luolikang@nsfocus.com>
Signed-off-by: Jens Axboe <axboe@kernel.dk>
cve_desc: off-by-one in io_uring module.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one error in the bounds check. The check uses `fd > ctx->nr_user_files` (strictly greater) instead of `fd >= ctx->nr_user_files`. When `fd` equals `ctx->nr_user_files`, it passes the bounds check but represents an out-of-bounds index, since valid indices are 0 to nr_user_files-1. The subsequent `array_index_nospec` call uses `nr_user_files` as the upper bound (exclusive), but the prior check has already allowed the equal case through, leading to an out-of-bounds read from `io_fixed_file_slot`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `cd->flags` having both IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD and IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD_FIXED set, and `fd` equal to `ctx->nr_user_files`.
The bounds check `fd > ctx->nr_user_files` is evaluated. Since fd == nr_user_files, the condition is false (10 > 10 is false), so execution continues past the check.

`array_index_nospec(fd, ctx->nr_user_files)` is called. This macro is designed to sanitize speculation for indices < bound, but the actual value fd==bound is at the boundary. In practice, the non-speculative path can still yield index == nr_user_files.
`io_fixed_file_slot(&ctx->file_table, fd)` is called with fd=10 (nr_user_files), which accesses one element past the valid file table entries, resulting in an out-of-bounds read of `file_ptr`.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/4988
cve_desc: ImageMagick 7.1.0-27 is vulnerable to Buffer Overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow: image->depth is set from an attacker-controlled unsigned char (0-255) with no upper-bound validation. The quantum_info buffer is allocated assuming 32-bit quanta (via SetQuantumQuantum(quantum_info,32)), but `length` is computed by GetBytesPerRow using the unconstrained `image->depth`. If depth exceeds 32 (e.g., 64), `length` will be larger than the quantum pixel buffer size, and ReadBlobStream will write beyond the allocated `pixels` heap buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Image depth is read from file as an unsigned char and assigned without validation.
AcquireQuantumInfo allocates quantum_info and internal pixel buffer based on image properties.
SetQuantumQuantum sets the quantum size to 32 bits, so the internal pixel buffer is sized for 32-bit quanta per channel.
GetBytesPerRow computes length using the unconstrained depth of 64 bits per channel for 3 channels.

state.value: approximately columns * 3 * 8 bytes (much larger than quantum buffer)

step_by_step_execution.description: GetQuantumPixels returns a pointer to the internal quantum buffer sized for 32-bit quanta.

state.value: pointer to buffer of size ~columns*12 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: ReadBlobStream attempts to read `length` bytes into the `pixels` buffer, writing past the end of the allocated heap buffer.

commit_message:
[bgpd] cleanup, compact and consolidate capability parsing code

2007-07-26 Paul Jakma <paul.jakma@sun.com>

	* (general) Clean up and compact capability parsing slightly.
	  Consolidate validation of length and logging of generic TLV, and
	  memcpy of capability data, thus removing such from cap specifc
	  code (not always present or correct).
	* bgp_open.h: Add structures for the generic capability TLV header
	  and for the data formats of the various specific capabilities we
	  support.  Hence remove the badly named, or else misdefined, struct
	  capability.
	* bgp_open.c: (bgp_capability_vty_out) Use struct capability_mp_data.
	  Do the length checks *before* memcpy()'ing based on that length
	  (stored capability - should have been validated anyway on input,
	  but..).
	  (bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices) new function to validate (afi,safi)
	  which is about to be used as index into arrays, consolidates
	  several instances of same, at least one of which appeared to be
	  incomplete..
	  (bgp_capability_mp) Much condensed.
	  (bgp_capability_orf_entry) New, process one ORF entry
	  (bgp_capability_orf) Condensed. Fixed to process all ORF entries.
	  (bgp_capability_restart) Condensed, and fixed to use a
	  cap-specific type, rather than abusing capability_mp.
	  (struct message capcode_str) added to aid generic logging.
	  (size_t cap_minsizes[]) added to aid generic validation of
	  capability length field.
	  (bgp_capability_parse) Generic logging and validation of TLV
	  consolidated here. Code compacted as much as possible.
	* bgp_packet.c: (bgp_open_receive) Capability parsers now use
	  streams, so no more need here to manually fudge the input stream
	  getp.
	  (bgp_capability_msg_parse) use struct capability_mp_data. Validate
	  lengths /before/ memcpy. Use bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices.
	  (bgp_capability_receive) Exported for use by test harness.
	* bgp_vty.c: (bgp_show_summary) fix conversion warning
	  (bgp_show_peer) ditto
	* bgp_debug.h: Fix storage 'extern' after type 'const'.
        * lib/log.c: (mes_lookup) warning about code not being in
          same-number array slot should be debug, not warning. E.g. BGP
          has several discontigious number spaces, allocating from
          different parts of a space is not uncommon (e.g. IANA
          assigned versus vendor-assigned code points in some number
          space).
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read in the BGP daemon of FRRouting FRR before 8.4 may lead to a segmentation fault and denial of service. This occurs in bgp_capability_msg_parse in bgpd/bgp_packet.c.

vulnerabilities.description: The memcpy copies sizeof(struct capability) bytes from pnt+1 into cap, but at that point only 3 bytes from pnt (action, capability code, capability length) have been validated to exist in the buffer. The struct capability likely contains fields beyond code and length (e.g., the mpc sub-structure with afi/safi), making sizeof(struct capability) larger than 2. This means the memcpy can read past the end of the input buffer. The bounds check against cap.length happens only after the memcpy has already performed the over-read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a small length value, e.g. length=3, meaning the buffer has exactly 3 bytes (action, cap.code, cap.length).
The first bounds check verifies pnt + 3 <= end, which passes since the buffer is exactly 3 bytes.
action is read from *pnt, consuming 1 byte — this is safe.

memcpy copies sizeof(struct capability) bytes from pnt+1. The struct capability contains code (1 byte), length (1 byte), and a union including mpc (with afi and safi fields), so sizeof(struct capability) is likely >= 4 bytes. Only 2 bytes are available at pnt+1 (within the buffer bounds), resulting in an out-of-bounds read.
The out-of-bounds read has already occurred before the later cap.length validation check is reached.

state.value: contains data read from beyond buffer end

commit_message:
Initialize the max upload file size when quotas are enabled

Due to an unwanted check, files causing the quota to be exceeded
were deleted after the upload, but not during the upload.

The bug was introduced in 2009 in version 1.0.23

Spotted by @DroidTest, thanks!
cve_desc: In Pure-FTPd before 1.0.50, an incorrect max_filesize quota mechanism in the server allows attackers to upload files of unbounded size, which may lead to denial of service or a server hang. This occurs because a certain greater-than-zero test does not anticipate an initial -1 value. (Versions 1.0.23 through 1.0.49 are affected.)

vulnerabilities.description: In the FTPWHO block's else branch, the offset calculation `sl - sizeof shm_data_cur->filename - 1U` can suffer an unsigned integer underflow. When `sl` (the length of `name`) equals `sizeof shm_data_cur->filename`, the expression evaluates to `0 - 1U` which wraps around to SIZE_MAX due to unsigned arithmetic, resulting in a massive out-of-bounds read from `name`. Even without underflow, the `- 1U` is an off-by-one error that reads one byte before the intended position, and the result is never null-terminated.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives `name` parameter. Assume `name` has length exactly equal to `sizeof shm_data_cur->filename` (e.g., if filename buffer is 256 bytes, name is 256 chars long).

state.value: a string of length == sizeof shm_data_cur->filename

step_by_step_execution.description: The code passes `checknamesanity` and proceeds through the upload logic into the FTPWHO block, where `shm_data_cur != NULL`.
`sl` is computed as `strlen(name)`, which equals `sizeof shm_data_cur->filename`.
The condition `sl < sizeof shm_data_cur->filename` is false (they are equal), so the else branch is taken.
The offset is computed: `sl - sizeof shm_data_cur->filename - 1U` = `256 - 256 - 1` = `(size_t)0 - 1` = `SIZE_MAX` due to unsigned arithmetic.
memcpy reads `sizeof shm_data_cur->filename` bytes from `&name[SIZE_MAX]`, which is a massively out-of-bounds memory address, causing an out-of-bounds read (likely crash or information leak).

vulnerabilities.description: After `close(f)` is called, the file descriptor `f` is used in `FSTATFS(f, &statfsbuf)` (under SHOW_REAL_DISK_SPACE). Using a file descriptor after close is undefined behavior; the fd may have been reassigned to another file by a concurrent operation, leading to information disclosure from an unrelated file descriptor.

step_by_step_execution.description: The file `f` is opened successfully earlier in the function.
After the upload completes, `close(f)` is called.
Under the SHOW_REAL_DISK_SPACE preprocessor guard, `FSTATFS(f, &statfsbuf)` is called using the already-closed file descriptor `f`.

commit_message:
netfilter: nf_tables: disallow binding to already bound chain

Update nft_data_init() to report EINVAL if chain is already bound.

Fixes: d0e2c7de92c7 ("netfilter: nf_tables: add NFT_CHAIN_BINDING")
Reported-by: Gwangun Jung <exsociety@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/netfilter/nf_tables_api.c in the Linux kernel before 5.19.6. A denial of service can occur upon binding to an already bound chain.

	  capability.

	  Do the length checks *before* memcpy()'ing based on that length

	  but..).
	  (bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices) new function to validate (afi,safi)
	  which is about to be used as index into arrays, consolidates
	  several instances of same, at least one of which appeared to be
	  incomplete..
	  (bgp_capability_mp) Much condensed.
	  (bgp_capability_orf_entry) New, process one ORF entry
	  (bgp_capability_orf) Condensed. Fixed to process all ORF entries.
	  (bgp_capability_restart) Condensed, and fixed to use a
	  cap-specific type, rather than abusing capability_mp.
	  (struct message capcode_str) added to aid generic logging.
	  (size_t cap_minsizes[]) added to aid generic validation of
	  capability length field.
	  (bgp_capability_parse) Generic logging and validation of TLV
	  consolidated here. Code compacted as much as possible.
	* bgp_packet.c: (bgp_open_receive) Capability parsers now use
	  streams, so no more need here to manually fudge the input stream
	  getp.
	  (bgp_capability_msg_parse) use struct capability_mp_data. Validate
	  lengths /before/ memcpy. Use bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices.
	  (bgp_capability_receive) Exported for use by test harness.
	* bgp_vty.c: (bgp_show_summary) fix conversion warning
	  (bgp_show_peer) ditto
	* bgp_debug.h: Fix storage 'extern' after type 'const'.
        * lib/log.c: (mes_lookup) warning about code not being in
          same-number array slot should be debug, not warning. E.g. BGP
          has several discontigious number spaces, allocating from
          different parts of a space is not uncommon (e.g. IANA
          assigned versus vendor-assigned code points in some number
          space).
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read in the BGP daemon of FRRouting FRR before 8.4 may lead to a segmentation fault and denial of service. This occurs in bgp_capability_msg_parse in bgpd/bgp_packet.c.

vulnerabilities.description: The memcpy copies a fixed `sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)` bytes from `pnt + 3` regardless of the actual `hdr->length` value. The bounds check only validates that `hdr->length` bytes are available, but if `hdr->length` is smaller than `sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)`, the memcpy reads beyond the validated buffer boundary (`end`), causing a heap buffer over-read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a peer-supplied capability message buffer `pnt` of `length` bytes. `end` is set to `pnt + length`.
The loop checks `pnt + 3 > end` to ensure at least 3 bytes are available (action + 2-byte header). This passes.
action and hdr are read. hdr->length is attacker-controlled and can be set to e.g. 0.
The capability length check `(pnt + hdr->length + 3) > end` is evaluated. With hdr->length=0, this checks `pnt + 3 > end`, which was already validated and passes.

memcpy copies sizeof(struct capability_mp_data) bytes (typically 4 bytes: 2 for afi, 1 reserved, 1 safi) from pnt+3. However, only hdr->length (0) bytes past pnt+3 are guaranteed to be within the buffer. If pnt+3 is at or near `end`, this reads up to 4 bytes past the valid buffer.

state.value: pnt + 3 (at or near end of buffer)

commit_message:
CVE-2022-31214: fixing the fix, one more time

the previous commit "CVE-2022-31214: fixing the fix"
made private-etc=fonts,fonts and similar commands
fail with an error

fix that regression by tolerating already existing
directories
cve_desc: A Privilege Context Switching issue was discovered in join.c in Firejail 0.9.68. By crafting a bogus Firejail container that is accepted by the Firejail setuid-root program as a join target, a local attacker can enter an environment in which the Linux user namespace is still the initial user namespace, the NO_NEW_PRIVS prctl is not activated, and the entered mount namespace is under the attacker's control. In this way, the filesystem layout can be adjusted to gain root privileges through execution of available setuid-root binaries such as su or sudo.

commit_message:
udf: Restore i_lenAlloc when inode expansion fails

When we fail to expand inode from inline format to a normal format, we
restore inode to contain the original inline formatting but we forgot to
set i_lenAlloc back. The mismatch between i_lenAlloc and i_size was then
causing further problems such as warnings and lost data down the line.

Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: 7e49b6f2480c ("udf: Convert UDF to new truncate calling sequence")
Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Signed-off-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>

cve_desc: A Privilege Context Switching issue was discovered in join.c in Firejail 0.9.68. By crafting a bogus Firejail container that is accepted by the Firejail setuid-root program as a join target, a local attacker can enter an environment in which the Linux user namespace is still the initial user namespace, the NO_NEW_PRIVS prctl is not activated, and the entered mount namespace is under the attacker's control. vulnerabilities.description: TOCTOU race condition: when mkdir() fails with EEXIST (directory already exists), the code proceeds to call chmod() on the path. An attacker who controls the filesystem (e.g., inside a sandbox) can replace the existing directory with a symlink between the mkdir and chmod calls (or before chmod when EEXIST occurs). chmod follows symlinks, so the attacker can cause chmod to change permissions on an arbitrary file outside the intended directory tree.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function starts iterating over path components in src and dst simultaneously.
A path separator or null terminator is found in src, temporarily null-terminating it to form a complete path prefix.
stat(src, &s) succeeds and confirms the src component is a directory, so the code enters the directory-creation branch.

state.value: directory mode bits from src, e.g. 0755

step_by_step_execution.description: mkdir(dst, 0700) is called. If the directory already exists, it returns -1 with errno == EEXIST, and execution continues past the errExit check.
Between mkdir failing with EEXIST and the chmod call, an attacker replaces the existing directory at dst with a symlink pointing to an arbitrary target (e.g., /etc/shadow).

state.value: path that is now a symlink to /etc/shadow

step_by_step_execution.description: chmod(dst, s.st_mode) is called. Since chmod follows symlinks, it changes the permissions of the attacker's chosen target file to s.st_mode.

commit_message: Fix wrong boundary checks in inflections parser resulting in stack buffer over-read with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11. This vulnerability is capable of arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.description: In the delete branch, the memmove length is calculated as `l = *decoded_size - pos`, but it should be `*decoded_size - pos - 1`. Valid data occupies indices 0 through `*decoded_size - 1`. The memmove source starts at `decoded + pos + 1` and copies `*decoded_size - pos` bytes, reading up to index `pos + 1 + (*decoded_size - pos) - 1 = *decoded_size`, which is one byte past the valid data. This is an off-by-one out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `*decoded_size = N` (e.g., 5), `decoded` buffer with N valid bytes (indices 0..N-1). `pos` is initialized to N.
Rule byte c = 4 is read. Since c <= 4: mod = 'd' (c > 2), dir = '>' (c & 2 == 0). olddir = '<' != dir = '>', so pos = 0 (c & 2 == 0).
Next rule byte c is a printable character (e.g., 0x41 = 'A', which is > 20). Enters the else branch with mod == 'd'.
Since dir == '>', pos is NOT decremented. Source and length are computed: s = decoded + 1, d = decoded + 0, l = *decoded_size - 0 = N.
Bounds check passes: pos >= 0, l >= 0, s + l = decoded + N + 1 which is <= decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX for reasonable N. Assuming decoded[0] == 'A', the character match check passes.

state.value: decoded + N + 1 (one past valid data end)

step_by_step_execution.description: memmove(d, s, l) copies N bytes starting from decoded[1] through decoded[N]. Index decoded[N] is one past the valid data (valid range is 0..N-1), causing an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: decoded[1] through decoded[N], where decoded[N] is out-of-bounds

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5123: using invalid index when looking for spell suggestions

Problem:    Using invalid index when looking for spell suggestions.
Solution:   Do not decrement the index when it is zero.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: The length of `preword` (which can be up to MAXWLEN*3 = ~762 bytes due to compound words and splits) is cast to `char_u` (unsigned char, max 255). When the accumulated preword length exceeds 255 bytes (possible with compound/split words), the value wraps around, causing subsequent writes at `preword + sp->ts_prewordlen` to target incorrect (earlier) positions in the buffer. This can lead to out-of-bounds writes as the code continues appending data at a wrong offset, and corrupts earlier data in `preword`.

code_snippets: vim_strncpy(preword + sp->ts_prewordlen, tword + sp->ts_splitoff, sp->ts_twordlen - sp->ts_splitoff);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins processing, building compound or split words. `preword` is declared as `char_u preword[MAXWLEN * 3]` (approximately 762 bytes).

Through repeated compound word building or word splitting, the code accumulates words in `preword`. For example, through STATE_START compounding, `vim_strncpy(preword + sp->ts_prewordlen, tword + sp->ts_splitoff, ...)` appends words, and `STRCAT(preword, " ")` appends spaces between split words.

state.value: accumulating compound/split words, e.g., 'word1 word2 word3...'
growing with each compound/split, can exceed 255 after ~2 full-length words

step_by_step_execution.description: After appending to `preword`, the code executes `sp->ts_prewordlen = (char_u)STRLEN(preword)`. If `STRLEN(preword)` is, say, 300, the cast to `char_u` truncates it to 300 % 256 = 44.

On subsequent iterations, the code uses `sp->ts_prewordlen` as an offset into `preword` for writes, e.g., `STRCPY(preword + sp->ts_prewordlen, tword + sp->ts_splitoff)` or `make_case_word(tword + sp->ts_splitoff, preword + sp->ts_prewordlen, c)`. This writes to `preword[44]` instead of `preword[300]`, corrupting already-written data and potentially allowing further compound/split operations to push the actual string length beyond the `MAXWLEN * 3` buffer boundary.

code_snippets:
STRMOVE(p + tl, p + fl);
		    repextra += tl - fl;
vulnerabilities.description: In STATE_REP, the code replaces substrings in `fword` (passed as a pointer to a fixed-size MAXWLEN buffer) using REP items from .aff files. When the replacement (`ft_to`) is longer than the original (`ft_from`), `STRMOVE` shifts the tail of fword right, expanding it. Multiple nested REP replacements at different stack depths can cumulatively grow `fword` beyond its MAXWLEN buffer without any bounds checking, causing a stack or heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives `fword` which is a buffer of size MAXWLEN containing the bad word to check.

state.value: buffer of MAXWLEN bytes containing the misspelled word

step_by_step_execution.description: In STATE_REP, a REP item matches where `ft_from` is shorter than `ft_to` (e.g., ft_from='a' length 1, ft_to='ough' length 4). The code computes fl=1 and tl=4.

The code executes `STRMOVE(p + tl, p + fl)` which shifts the remainder of fword 3 bytes to the right, then copies the replacement with `mch_memmove(p, ftp->ft_to, tl)`. This grows fword by 3 bytes. repextra becomes 3.

The search goes deeper (++depth) and may encounter another REP match at a different position. Each such match can further expand fword. After several such expansions, the total length of fword exceeds MAXWLEN, writing beyond the allocated buffer.

state.value: can exceed MAXWLEN with no bounds check

commit_message: use mkstemp for sdb
cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

vulnerabilities.description: The EDITOR or VISUAL environment variable is read and passed unsanitized to system(). An attacker who controls these environment variables can inject arbitrary shell commands. The code only checks if the editor string contains a space to decide between execlp and system(), but any value with a space (e.g., "vi; malicious_command") will be passed to system(), enabling command injection.

step_by_step_execution.description: A temporary file is created via mkstemp and succeeds.
pi->language equals LANG_SINGULAR, so execution enters the else branch.
getenv("EDITOR") returns an attacker-controlled string containing a space and shell metacharacters, e.g. "vi; rm -rf /".
The procedure body exists, so it is written to the temp file and the file is closed.
fork() is called, returning 0 in the child process.
strchr(editor, ' ') finds a space in the editor string, so the else branch is taken.
A buffer is allocated and sprintf formats the editor and filename into it, then system() is called with the attacker-controlled string.
system() interprets the string via /bin/sh, executing the injected command.

commit_message: fix #2963 - oob write (1 byte) in pyc/marshal.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds write when getting data from PYC(python) files. A user opening a malicious PYC file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number 68948017423a12786704e54227b8b2f918c2fd27 contains a patch.

vulnerabilities.description: When `malloc(n1 + 1)` for `s1` fails and returns NULL, the function returns NULL without freeing the previously allocated `ret` object, causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: ret is allocated via RZ_NEW0(pyc_object)
n1 is read from buffer successfully, passes overflow check
malloc(n1 + 1) fails and returns NULL
Function returns NULL without freeing ret

vulnerabilities.description: When reading `n2` fails (error) or n2 overflows, the function returns NULL without freeing the previously allocated `ret` and `s1`, causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: ret is allocated and s1 is allocated and populated successfully
Reading n2 from buffer fails (error is true) or n2 == 0xFFFFFFFF triggering overflow check
Function returns NULL without freeing ret or s1

vulnerabilities.description: When `malloc(n2 + 1)` for `s2` fails, the function returns NULL without freeing the previously allocated `ret` and `s1`, causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: ret and s1 are allocated successfully
n2 is read successfully and passes overflow check
malloc(n2 + 1) fails and returns NULL
Function returns NULL without freeing ret or s1

commit_message:
ALSA: control: Re-order bounds checking in get_ctl_id_hash()

These two checks are in the reverse order so it might read one element
beyond the end of the array.  First check if the "i" is within bounds
before using it.

Fixes: 6ab55ec0a938 ("ALSA: control: Fix an out-of-bounds bug in get_ctl_id_hash()")
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/YwjgNh/gkG1hH7po@kili
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds access issue was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem. It could occur when the 'id->name' provided by the user did not end with '\0'. A privileged local user could pass a specially crafted name through ioctl() interface and crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5123: using invalid index when looking for spell suggestions

Problem:    Using invalid index when looking for spell suggestions.
Solution:   Do not decrement the index when it is zero.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: The length of `preword` (buffer size `MAXWLEN * 3`, typically 762 bytes) is stored via a cast to `char_u` (an unsigned 8-bit type), truncating any value above 255. This happens in multiple locations. When `preword` grows beyond 255 bytes through compound word building or word splitting, `ts_prewordlen` wraps around to a small value. Subsequent writes using this truncated offset (e.g., `make_case_word(..., preword + sp->ts_prewordlen, ...)`, `STRCPY(preword + sp->ts_prewordlen, ...)`, `vim_strncpy(preword + sp->ts_prewordlen, ...)`) write to incorrect positions, corrupting `preword` contents and potentially allowing the actual NUL-terminated string length to grow unboundedly, eventually overflowing the `preword` stack buffer.

code_snippets: vim_strncpy(preword + sp->ts_prewordlen, tword + sp->ts_splitoff, sp->ts_twordlen - sp->ts_splitoff);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the main loop. Through compound word building and word splitting, `preword` accumulates word fragments and spaces via STRCAT and STRCPY operations.

state.value: Initially empty, grows with each compound/split iteration

step_by_step_execution.description: During compound word handling in STATE_START, compound words are appended: `vim_strncpy(preword + sp->ts_prewordlen, tword + sp->ts_splitoff, sp->ts_twordlen - sp->ts_splitoff)`. During split handling, a space is appended: `STRCAT(preword, " ")`. After each such operation, `sp->ts_prewordlen = (char_u)STRLEN(preword)` is executed.

state.value: Grows incrementally with each compound/split word added

step_by_step_execution.description: When preword length exceeds 255 bytes (possible since buffer is 762 bytes and multiple compound/split words accumulate), the cast `(char_u)STRLEN(preword)` truncates to the low 8 bits. For example, length 260 becomes 4.

Subsequent operations use the truncated `ts_prewordlen` as an offset. For instance, `make_case_word(tword + sp->ts_splitoff, preword + sp->ts_prewordlen, c)` writes at offset 4 instead of 260, corrupting earlier data in preword. The NUL terminator from the previous string content at position 260 remains, so subsequent STRCAT/STRLEN operations see preword as still ~260 bytes long.

In subsequent iterations, STRCAT(preword, " ") appends at the actual NUL position (260+), and more compound/split words are appended. The ts_prewordlen remains corrupted (small value), so the append positions tracked by the code diverge further from reality. Since no bounds check against MAXWLEN*3 is performed before STRCAT or STRCPY operations, the string can grow past the 762-byte buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.

state.value: Can exceed MAXWLEN * 3 (762) without bounds checking
Incorrectly small due to repeated truncation

commit_message:
ip6_gre: fix ip6gre_err() invalid reads

Andrey Konovalov reported out of bound accesses in ip6gre_err()

If GRE flags contains GRE_KEY, the following expression
*(((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1)

accesses data ~40 bytes after the expected point, since
grehlen includes the size of IPv6 headers.

Let's use a "struct gre_base_hdr *greh" pointer to make this
code more readable.

p[1] becomes greh->protocol.
grhlen is the GRE header length.

Fixes: c12b395a4664 ("gre: Support GRE over IPv6")
Signed-off-by: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Reported-by: Andrey Konovalov <andreyknvl@google.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: The ip6gre_err function in net/ipv6/ip6_gre.c in the Linux kernel allows remote attackers to have unspecified impact via vectors involving GRE flags in an IPv6 packet, which trigger an out-of-bounds access.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when computing the GRE key for tunnel lookup. `grehlen` includes `offset` (the distance from skb->data to the GRE header), but it is used as if it only represents the GRE header length. The expression `((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1` indexes relative to `p` (which already points to `skb->data + offset`), so the final read address is `skb->data + offset + 4*(grehlen/4) - 4 = skb->data + 2*offset + <GRE_hdr_fields> - 4`. For a typical offset of 40 (IPv6 header), this reads ~40 bytes past the intended key position, well beyond the `grehlen` bytes guaranteed by `pskb_may_pull`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry. offset is the position of the GRE header in the ICMPv6 error payload. For an IPv6 inner header, offset is typically 40.
p is set to point to the GRE header at skb->data + offset.
flags are read from p[0]. Assume GRE_KEY is set but GRE_CSUM is not, and GRE_VERSION|GRE_ROUTING are not set.
pskb_may_pull(skb, grehlen) ensures 48 bytes are accessible from skb->data. Pointers ipv6h and p are refreshed.
Key lookup expression: ((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1 = ((__be32 *)(skb->data + 40)) + (48/4) - 1 = ((__be32 *)(skb->data + 40)) + 11. This dereferences skb->data + 40 + 44 = skb->data + 84.
The read at skb->data + 84 is 36 bytes beyond the 48 bytes guaranteed by pskb_may_pull, causing an out-of-bounds read. The correct key position should be skb->data + 44 (offset + 4).

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow when computing MTU in the ICMPV6_PKT_TOOBIG case. If be32_to_cpu(info) is less than offset, the subtraction underflows the __u32 variable `mtu`, resulting in a very large value (close to UINT32_MAX). The subsequent check `mtu < IPV6_MIN_MTU` does not catch this because the wrapped value is much larger than IPV6_MIN_MTU. The tunnel device MTU is then set to an extremely large, invalid value.

step_by_step_execution.description: An ICMPv6 Packet Too Big message is received with type == ICMPV6_PKT_TOOBIG. The info field contains an attacker-influenced MTU value from the ICMP message.
offset is the position of the GRE header, typically 40 for an IPv6 header.
mtu = be32_to_cpu(info) - offset. With info=0: mtu = 0 - 40, which wraps to 0xFFFFFFD8 (4294967256) as __u32.
Check mtu < IPV6_MIN_MTU (1280). Since 4294967256 > 1280, the check is false and mtu is not clamped.
t->dev->mtu is set to the underflowed value, corrupting the device MTU.

commit_message: Fixed bug #22965 (Crash in gd lib's ImageFillToBorder()).
cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

vulnerabilities.description: The function clamps x and y when they exceed the image dimensions but never checks for negative values. A negative y value passes both upper-bound checks and is then used directly in gdImageGetPixel and gdImageSetPixel calls, causing out-of-bounds memory access (read and write).

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a negative y value, e.g. y = -1. x is a valid value within [0, im->sx).
The check `x >= im->sx` is false, so x is unchanged.
The check `y >= im->sy` is false (since -1 < im->sy), so y remains -1. No check for y < 0 exists.
The loop `for (i = x; i >= 0; i--)` starts with i = 0. gdImageGetPixel(im, 0, -1) is called, performing an out-of-bounds read with a negative y coordinate.
If the pixel doesn't match the border value, gdImageSetPixel(im, 0, -1, color) is called, performing an out-of-bounds write with a negative y coordinate.

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

commit_message: Fixed bug #22965 (Crash in gd lib's ImageFillToBorder()).
cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

vulnerabilities.description: The function does not validate that the initial x and y parameters are within the image bounds (0 <= x < im->sx, 0 <= y < im->sy) before using them in gdImageGetPixel and gdImageSetPixel calls. If x >= im->sx or y < 0 or y >= im->sy, out-of-bounds memory reads and writes occur.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with y value that is negative or >= im->sy (e.g., y = -1 or y = im->sy). border >= 0, so the early return is not triggered.
The leftward seek loop begins at i = x. No bounds check on y is performed before calling gdImageGetPixel(im, i, y).
gdImageGetPixel(im, i, y) is called with an out-of-bounds y coordinate, causing an out-of-bounds read from image pixel data.
If the pixel doesn't match border, gdImageSetPixel(im, i, y, color) is called with the same out-of-bounds y, causing an out-of-bounds write to image pixel data.

vulnerabilities.description: The function is recursive with no depth limit. A carefully crafted image (e.g., a serpentine pattern) can cause recursion depth proportional to the number of pixels in the image, leading to stack exhaustion and a crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called on a large image with a pattern that causes alternating up/down recursive calls (e.g., a zigzag path without border pixels).
After filling a horizontal span, the function recurses into y-1 line for each non-border, non-color span found above.
Each recursive call may again find spans and recurse into y+1 or y-1, creating a recursion chain that can reach depth proportional to image area.
Stack space is exhausted, causing a segmentation fault / stack overflow crash (denial of service).

commit_message:
media: em28xx: initialize refcount before kref_get

The commit 47677e51e2a4("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct
em28xx after finishing all extensions") adds kref_get to many init
functions (e.g., em28xx_audio_init). However, kref_init is called too
late in em28xx_usb_probe, since em28xx_init_dev before will invoke
those init functions and call kref_get function. Then refcount bug
occurs in my local syzkaller instance.

Fix it by moving kref_init before em28xx_init_dev. This issue occurs
not only in dev but also dev->dev_next.

Fixes: 47677e51e2a4 ("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct em28xx after finishing all extensions")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
cve_desc: A flaw use after free in the Linux kernel video4linux driver was found in the way user triggers em28xx_usb_probe() for the Empia 28xx based TV cards. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.description: After em28xx_init_dev() succeeds for the primary device, usb_set_intfdata(intf, dev) has already stored a pointer to 'dev' in the USB interface. If the code later fails (e.g., em28xx_init_dev for dev->dev_next in the dual_ts path fails), execution jumps to err_free which calls kfree(dev). This leaves a dangling pointer in the USB interface's driver data (set via usb_set_intfdata), resulting in a use-after-free when the USB subsystem later accesses that pointer (e.g., during disconnect). Additionally, resources initialized by the first em28xx_init_dev call are not properly torn down.

step_by_step_execution.description: Device struct 'dev' is allocated with kzalloc and initialized.

state.value: valid pointer to newly allocated em28xx struct

step_by_step_execution.description: usb_set_intfdata(intf, dev) stores dev pointer in the USB interface's private data.
em28xx_init_dev(dev, udev, intf, nr) is called and succeeds, initializing internal device resources (registering v4l2 devices, i2c adapters, etc.).

state.value: fully initialized device with registered sub-resources

step_by_step_execution.description: Code enters the dual_ts branch because dev->board.has_dual_ts is true. em28xx_duplicate_dev(dev) succeeds and allocates dev->dev_next.

state.value: valid pointer to second em28xx struct

step_by_step_execution.description: em28xx_init_dev(dev->dev_next, ...) is called for the second tuner and fails, returning a non-zero retval.
Execution jumps to err_free, which calls kfree(dev->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc) and kfree(dev), freeing the primary device struct without unregistering it or clearing the USB interface data.
When the USB subsystem later invokes the disconnect callback or any other operation on the interface, it retrieves the dangling pointer via usb_get_intfdata(intf), leading to a use-after-free.

commit_message: fix #2963 - oob write (1 byte) in pyc/marshal.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds write when getting data from PYC(python) files. A user opening a malicious PYC file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number 68948017423a12786704e54227b8b2f918c2fd27 contains a patch.

vulnerabilities.description: When n1 is obtained from get_st32 and equals 0xFFFFFFFF (i.e., -1 as a signed int cast to ut32), the expression `n1 + 1` wraps around to 0 due to unsigned integer overflow. malloc(0) returns a small or zero-size allocation, but rz_buf_read then attempts to write up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into that allocation, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry. pyc->magic_int has a value where (pyc->magic_int & 0xffff) > 62061, so the else branch is taken.
n1 = get_st32(buffer, &error) is called. The buffer contains bytes representing -1 as a signed 32-bit integer, so n1 is assigned 0xFFFFFFFF.
malloc(n1 + 1) is called. n1 + 1 = 0xFFFFFFFF + 1 = 0 due to unsigned 32-bit wrap. malloc(0) returns a valid but tiny (or zero-size) heap allocation.
rz_buf_read(buffer, s1, n1) attempts to read 0xFFFFFFFF bytes from buffer into s1. Any bytes read are written past the bounds of the 0-byte allocation, causing a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Same integer overflow vulnerability as with n1, but for n2. If n2 is 0xFFFFFFFF, malloc(0) is called and rz_buf_read overflows the resulting allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: After successfully reading s1, the code reads n2 via get_st32, which returns 0xFFFFFFFF.
malloc(n2 + 1) is called. n2 + 1 overflows to 0. malloc(0) returns a valid but tiny allocation.
rz_buf_read(buffer, s2, n2) writes up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into s2, causing heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: When an error occurs reading n2, the function returns NULL without freeing s1 and ret, causing a memory leak. While not directly exploitable for code execution, repeated triggering can exhaust memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: s1 is successfully allocated and populated. ret is allocated.
get_ut8 or get_st32 for n2 sets error to true.
The error check returns NULL without freeing s1 or ret, leaking both allocations.

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message:
Bug 790784 - (CVE-2017-17784) heap overread in gbr parser / load_image.

We were assuming the input name was well formed, hence was
nul-terminated. As any data coming from external input, this has to be
thorougly checked.
Similar to commit 06d24a79af94837d615d0024916bb95a01bf3c59 but adapted
to older gimp-2-8 code.
cve_desc: In GIMP 2.8.22, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in load_image in plug-ins/common/file-gbr.c in the gbr import parser, related to mishandling of UTF-8 data.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the allocation size when a pattern follows a 1-byte brush. The original sanitization check validates `bh.width * bh.height * bh.bytes` against G_MAXSIZE with bh.bytes=1, but when a valid pattern is detected, a new buffer is allocated with `4 * bh.width * bh.height`. On 32-bit systems (where bh.width/bh.height are guint32), this multiplication can overflow, resulting in a small allocation followed by large out-of-bounds writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: File is opened and BrushHeader is read and byte-swapped.
Sanitization check is evaluated: G_MAXSIZE / 32768 / 32768 / 1. On a 32-bit system G_MAXSIZE=4294967295, result is 3, which is not < 1, so validation passes.
Initial brush data is read: size = 32768 * 32768 * 1 = 1073741824 bytes, allocated and read into brush_buf.
Since bh.bytes == 1, code attempts to read a following PatternHeader. A crafted file provides a valid pattern header with ph.width=32768, ph.height=32768, ph.bytes=3.

Pattern header validation passes (magic, version, dimensions match). Code computes new allocation: 4 * bh.width * bh.height = 4 * 32768 * 32768. In 32-bit unsigned arithmetic: 32768*32768 = 1073741824, then 4*1073741824 = 4294967296 which overflows guint32 to 0.

g_malloc(0) is called, returning a minimal (e.g., 1-byte) allocation. The code then loops ph.width*ph.height = 1073741824 times, writing 4 bytes per iteration into the undersized brush_buf, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Bug 790784 - (CVE-2017-17784) heap overread in gbr parser / load_image.

We were assuming the input name was well formed, hence was
nul-terminated. As any data coming from external input, this has to be
thorougly checked.
Similar to commit 06d24a79af94837d615d0024916bb95a01bf3c59 but adapted
to older gimp-2-8 code.
cve_desc: In GIMP 2.8.22, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in load_image in plug-ins/common/file-gbr.c in the gbr import parser, related to mishandling of UTF-8 data.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in allocation size when a GIMP pattern follows a 1-byte brush. The initial sanitization check validates `G_MAXSIZE / bh.width / bh.height / bh.bytes < 1` with `bh.bytes=1`, but later when a pattern is detected, the code allocates `4 * bh.width * bh.height` without re-checking for overflow. If `bh.width * bh.height` is large enough (greater than G_MAXSIZE/4 but less than G_MAXSIZE), the multiplication `4 * bh.width * bh.height` wraps around, resulting in a small allocation. Subsequent writes into this undersized buffer cause a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: File is opened and the BrushHeader is read. Fields are converted from network byte order.

Sanitization check is performed: bh.width > 0, bh.height > 0, bh.bytes == 1 (allowed), and G_MAXSIZE / bh.width / bh.height / bh.bytes >= 1. With bh.bytes=1, the check passes since bh.width * bh.height * 1 fits in gsize.
Version 2 path is taken. Name is read. The initial brush data buffer is allocated as size = bh.width * bh.height * 1 and read.
Since bh.bytes == 1, we enter the pattern-check path. A PatternHeader is read from the file and its fields match the required conditions (magic, version, same width/height, bytes=3).

The code computes `4 * bh.width * bh.height` for the new allocation. This multiplication overflows: 4 * 0x40000000 = 0x100000000 which wraps to 0 on 32-bit gsize (or a small value). g_malloc allocates a tiny buffer.
The loop iterates `ph.width * ph.height` (0x40000000) times, writing to `brush_buf + i * 4` and `brush_buf[i * 4 + 3]`, causing massive heap buffer overflow writes far beyond the allocated buffer.

commit_message: use mkstemp for sdb
cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow via sprintf. The string "/tmp/sd000000" is 13 characters + null terminator = 14 bytes allocated by omStrDup. sprintf writes the PID at offset 7. On 64-bit Linux, PIDs can reach 4194304 (7 digits). Writing 7 digits + null at offset 7 requires 15 bytes total, overflowing the 14-byte heap buffer by 1 byte.

step_by_step_execution.description: filename is allocated as a duplicate of "/tmp/sd000000"

state.value: heap buffer of 14 bytes (13 chars + null)

step_by_step_execution.description: sprintf writes the PID as a decimal string at offset 7 in filename. If PID is e.g. 4194304 (7 digits), it writes "4194304\0" (8 bytes) starting at offset 7.

state.value: needs 8 bytes (7 digits + null) = total offset 15

step_by_step_execution.description: The write exceeds the allocated buffer by 1 byte, causing a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Command injection via the EDITOR or VISUAL environment variable. When the environment variable contains a space, the value is concatenated with the filename and passed directly to system(), which invokes a shell. An attacker who controls the environment can set EDITOR to something like "vi ; malicious_command" to execute arbitrary commands.

step_by_step_execution.description: The EDITOR environment variable is read via getenv.
Since editor contains a space, the else branch is taken. A buffer is allocated and formatted with sprintf.
system(p) is called, which passes the string to /bin/sh for interpretation, executing the injected command.

vulnerabilities.description: Symlink race condition (TOCTOU) due to predictable temporary file name in /tmp. The filename /tmp/sd<PID> is entirely predictable. An attacker can create a symlink at that path before the program opens it, causing the program to write to or read from an arbitrary file.

step_by_step_execution.description: A predictable temporary filename is constructed using the PID.
The attacker creates a symlink at /tmp/sd<pid> pointing to a target file (e.g. ~/.bashrc) before fopen is called.
fopen opens the file for writing, following the symlink, and the program writes procedure body data to the attacker-chosen target file.

state.value: file handle to attacker-chosen target file

commit_message:
red fixes (#468)

* red fixes

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

vulnerabilities.description: The pipePath string (from user-controlled endpoint.name()) is copied into local.sun_path using strcpy without any length check. The sun_path field in sockaddr_un is a fixed-size buffer (typically 108 bytes on Linux). If pipePath exceeds this size, a stack buffer overflow occurs.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives an endpoint parameter and extracts the pipe path name.

state.value: arbitrary string from endpoint.name(), can be longer than 108 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: A sockaddr_un struct is declared on the stack, which contains sun_path as a fixed-size char array (typically 108 bytes).
strcpy copies the entire pipePath into local.sun_path without bounds checking, overflowing the stack buffer if pipePath.size() >= sizeof(local.sun_path).

state.value: overflowed beyond its 108-byte boundary, corrupting adjacent stack memory

commit_message:
server: reset meta context replies on starttls

Related to CVE-2021-3716, but not as severe.  No compliant client will
send NBD_CMD_BLOCK_STATUS unless it first negotiates
NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT.  If an attacker injects a premature
SET_META_CONTEXT, either the client will never notice (because it
never uses BLOCK_STATUS), or the client will overwrite the attacker's
attempt with the client's own SET_META_CONTEXT request after
encryption is enabled.  So I don't class this as having the potential
to trigger denial-of-service due to any protocol mismatch between
compliant client and server (I don't care what happens with
non-compliant clients).

Fixes: 26455d45 (server: protocol: Implement Block Status "base:allocation".)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in nbdkit due to to improperly caching plaintext state across the STARTTLS encryption boundary. A MitM attacker could use this flaw to inject a plaintext NBD_OPT_STRUCTURED_REPLY before proxying everything else a client sends to the server, potentially leading the client to terminate the NBD session. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

commit_message:
SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill: Fix crash on broken file

Issue #802
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in Poppler through 0.78.0. There is a divide-by-zero error in the function SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill at SplashOutputDev.cc.

code_snippets:
result_width = tBitmap->getWidth() * imgData.repeatX;
  result_height = tBitmap->getHeight() * imgData.repeatY;
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of result_width and result_height. tBitmap->getWidth() (up to 16384) multiplied by repeatX (which can be very large, derived from result_width / surface_width) can overflow a 32-bit int. The overflowed value is then passed to splash->drawImage() as width/height, potentially causing heap buffer overflow or other memory corruption when the image is rendered.

code_snippets: retValue = splash->drawImage(&tilingBitmapSrc, nullptr, &imgData, colorMode, true, result_width, result_height, matc, false, true) == splashOk;

step_by_step_execution.description: Width and height are computed from bbox, xStep and yStep must equal width and height to pass the early check.
CTM is concatenated and kx is derived. The while loop limits fabs(kx) and fabs(ky) to <= 16384, so surface_width is at most ceil(16384) = 16384.
The surface_width * surface_height check against 0x800000L passes (e.g., surface_width=16384, surface_height=1, product=16384 < 0x800000).
result_width is computed as (int) ceil(fabs(kx * width * (x1 - x0))). With large (x1 - x0) and appropriate kx/width, result_width can be close to INT_MAX.
repeatX = result_width / surface_width. With result_width near INT_MAX and surface_width=16384, repeatX can be ~131072.
The check repeatX * repeatY <= 4 passes since both are large (product >> 4). Execution continues.
After rendering, result_width = tBitmap->getWidth() * imgData.repeatX. This is 16384 * 131072 = 2^31, which overflows a 32-bit signed int, wrapping to a negative or small value.

The overflowed result_width and result_height are passed to splash->drawImage(), which may allocate an undersized buffer or misinterpret dimensions, leading to heap buffer overflow during image rendering.

commit_message:
random32: update the net random state on interrupt and activity

This modifies the first 32 bits out of the 128 bits of a random CPU's
net_rand_state on interrupt or CPU activity to complicate remote
observations that could lead to guessing the network RNG's internal
state.

Note that depending on some network devices' interrupt rate moderation
or binding, this re-seeding might happen on every packet or even almost
never.

In addition, with NOHZ some CPUs might not even get timer interrupts,
leaving their local state rarely updated, while they are running
networked processes making use of the random state.  For this reason, we
also perform this update in update_process_times() in order to at least
update the state when there is user or system activity, since it's the
only case we care about.

Reported-by: Amit Klein <aksecurity@gmail.com>
Suggested-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
Cc: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Cc: "Jason A. Donenfeld" <Jason@zx2c4.com>
Cc: Andy Lutomirski <luto@kernel.org>
Cc: Kees Cook <keescook@chromium.org>
Cc: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@linutronix.de>
Cc: Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Willy Tarreau <w@1wt.eu>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The Linux kernel through 5.7.11 allows remote attackers to make observations that help to obtain sensitive information about the internal state of the network RNG, aka CID-f227e3ec3b5c. This is related to drivers/char/random.c and kernel/time/timer.c.
