commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1540
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-40 Q16, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the function WritePNMImage of coders/pnm.c, which allows an attacker to cause a denial of service or possibly information disclosure via a crafted image file. This is related to SetGrayscaleImage in MagickCore/quantize.c.

vulnerabilities.description: The colormap is allocated with MaxColormapSize entries via AcquireImageColormap, but image->colors is incremented without any bounds check against MaxColormapSize. Since intensity values range over [0, MaxMap] (via ScaleQuantumToMap), and MaxMap can be larger than MaxColormapSize, the number of unique intensity values can exceed MaxColormapSize, causing an out-of-bounds write into image->colormap.

step_by_step_execution.description: The image is not PseudoClass, so the else branch allocates colormap_index with MagickMax(MaxColormapSize+1, MaxMap) entries.
colormap_index is initialized to -1 for MaxColormapSize entries, and AcquireImageColormap allocates image->colormap with MaxColormapSize entries. image->colors is set to 0.
In the parallel loop, for each pixel, intensity = ScaleQuantumToMap(GetPixelRed(image,q)) is computed, which maps to range [0, MaxMap].

If colormap_index[intensity] < 0, the critical section is entered. Inside, image->colormap[image->colors] is written to and image->colors is incremented. There is no check that image->colors < MaxColormapSize.

state.value: incremented each time a new unique intensity is found, no upper bound enforced

step_by_step_execution.description: If the image has more than MaxColormapSize unique intensity values (possible when MaxMap > MaxColormapSize), image->colors exceeds MaxColormapSize and the write to image->colormap[image->colors] is out of bounds.

commit_message: Fix oob write for dwarf with abbrev with count 0 (Fix #2083) (#2086)
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0; i < abbrev->count - 1; i++) {
		memset(&die->attr_values[i], 0, sizeof(die->attr_values[i]));
vulnerabilities.description: If abbrev->count is 0 (an unsigned type like size_t), then `abbrev->count - 1` wraps around to SIZE_MAX due to unsigned integer underflow. The loop condition `i < SIZE_MAX` is effectively always true, causing massive out-of-bounds reads and writes on `die->attr_values[i]` and `abbrev->defs[i]`.

code_snippets:
buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i],
			&die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with an abbrev declaration where abbrev->count is 0 (e.g., an abbreviation with no attribute definitions).
The loop condition is evaluated: `i < abbrev->count - 1`. Since count is an unsigned type and equals 0, `count - 1` wraps to SIZE_MAX.

The condition `0 < SIZE_MAX` is true, so the loop body executes. memset writes to die->attr_values[0], which may or may not be allocated, and continues to die->attr_values[1], die->attr_values[2], etc., far beyond any allocated buffer.

state.value: out-of-bounds access for any i beyond the allocated size of attr_values

step_by_step_execution.description: Each iteration also accesses abbrev->defs[i] out of bounds and calls parse_attr_value with invalid data, leading to heap buffer overflow writes and reads.

commit_message: Fixed an issue affecting recursions in JIT
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the get_recurse_data_length() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This issue affects recursions in JIT-compiled regular expressions caused by duplicate data transfers.

commit_message: Fixed an issue affecting recursions in JIT
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the get_recurse_data_length() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This issue affects recursions in JIT-compiled regular expressions caused by duplicate data transfers.

commit_message:
xfrm: xfrm_policy: fix a possible double xfrm_pols_put() in xfrm_bundle_lookup()

xfrm_policy_lookup() will call xfrm_pol_hold_rcu() to get a refcount of
pols[0]. This refcount can be dropped in xfrm_expand_policies() when
xfrm_expand_policies() return error. pols[0]'s refcount is balanced in
here. But xfrm_bundle_lookup() will also call xfrm_pols_put() with
num_pols == 1 to drop this refcount when xfrm_expand_policies() return
error.

This patch also fix an illegal address access. pols[0] will save a error
point when xfrm_policy_lookup fails. This lead to xfrm_pols_put to resolve
an illegal address in xfrm_bundle_lookup's error path.

Fix these by setting num_pols = 0 in xfrm_expand_policies()'s error path.

Fixes: 80c802f3073e ("xfrm: cache bundles instead of policies for outgoing flows")
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.18.14. xfrm_expand_policies in net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c can cause a refcount to be dropped twice.

commit_message: check vertexIndex which might be -1 for broken DXF
cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the expression `4*maxVertices` when `maxVertices` is a large value parsed from user input. If `maxVertices` is an int, `4*maxVertices` can wrap around to a small positive value (or zero), causing a tiny heap allocation. The subsequent initialization loop iterates `maxVertices` times, writing far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: groupCode is 90, so we enter the first branch to allocate vertices.
maxVertices is set from user-controlled input via toInt(groupValue). Attacker provides a large value such as 0x40000001 (1073741825).
maxVertices > 0 check passes, so allocation proceeds. The expression 4*maxVertices is computed as int arithmetic: 4 * 0x40000001 = 0x100000004, which truncates to 4 in a 32-bit int.
new double[4] is allocated — only 32 bytes of heap memory.

state.value: pointer to heap buffer of 4 doubles (32 bytes)

step_by_step_execution.description: The initialization loop runs from i=0 to i<1073741825, writing to vertices[i*4] through vertices[i*4+3]. After i=0, writes at index 4 and beyond are already out of bounds of the 4-element allocation, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
netfilter: nf_tables: disallow binding to already bound chain

Update nft_data_init() to report EINVAL if chain is already bound.

Fixes: d0e2c7de92c7 ("netfilter: nf_tables: add NFT_CHAIN_BINDING")
Reported-by: Gwangun Jung <exsociety@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/netfilter/nf_tables_api.c in the Linux kernel before 5.19.6. A denial of service can occur upon binding to an already bound chain.

vulnerabilities.description: The chain reference counter `chain->use` is incremented without checking for integer overflow. If an attacker can repeatedly trigger the NFT_JUMP or NFT_GOTO verdict path targeting the same chain, the `use` counter (typically a u32) can wrap around to zero. When it wraps, subsequent operations may believe the chain is unreferenced and free it, leading to a use-after-free condition on the chain object.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function parses the nested netlink attribute to extract the verdict code.
The switch statement enters the NFT_JUMP/NFT_GOTO case. A valid chain is looked up via tb[NFTA_VERDICT_CHAIN] or tb[NFTA_VERDICT_CHAIN_ID].
The checks for base chain, bound chain, and setelem binding all pass, so execution reaches chain->use++.
chain->use is incremented without any overflow check. After being called 2^32 times for the same chain, the counter wraps around to 0.
With chain->use at 0, subsequent deletion or cleanup logic considers the chain unreferenced and frees it, while existing rules still hold pointers to the chain, resulting in a use-after-free.

state.value: freed but still referenced by existing verdict data

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/4988
cve_desc: ImageMagick 7.1.0-27 is vulnerable to Buffer Overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: The quantum pixel buffer (`pixels`) is allocated internally by `AcquireQuantumInfo` based on `GetQuantumExtent`, but the actual number of bytes read into this buffer (`length`) is computed independently by `GetBytesPerRow`. The `extent` value is explicitly discarded with `(void) extent;`. Since `image->depth` comes directly from an untrusted file field (`cin.image.channel[0].bits_per_pixel`, an unsigned char 0-255), a crafted file can set `bits_per_pixel` to a large value (e.g., 64 or 128). This causes `GetBytesPerRow` to return a `length` larger than the internally allocated quantum pixel buffer. When `ReadBlobStream` is called for a non-memory-mapped blob, it writes `length` bytes into the `pixels` buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The CIN file header is read. The magic bytes are validated.
Image channel information is read from the file. `cin.image.channel[0].bits_per_pixel` is set from a single byte read from the attacker-controlled file, allowing any value 0-255.
Image dimensions and depth are set from the unvalidated channel info.
The sanity check `((MagickSizeType) image->columns*image->rows/8) > GetBlobSize(image)` passes because the division by 8 makes it a weak check (100*100/8 = 1250, easily satisfiable).
Quantum info is allocated. The internal pixel buffer is sized based on `GetQuantumExtent(image, quantum_info, RGBQuantum)` with the quantum set to 32 bits.

state.value: allocated with internal buffer sized for 32-bit quantum
computed from GetQuantumExtent, then explicitly discarded via (void) extent;

step_by_step_execution.description: `length` is computed by `GetBytesPerRow(image->columns, 3, image->depth, MagickTrue)` using the attacker-controlled depth. With depth=64 or 128, `GetBytesPerRow` computes a much larger row size than the quantum pixel buffer was allocated for.

state.value: much larger than quantum pixel buffer (proportional to depth * columns * channels)
sized for 32-bit quantum, smaller than length

step_by_step_execution.description: In the per-row loop, `ReadBlobStream(image, length, pixels, &count)` is called. For non-memory-mapped blobs, this reads `length` bytes into the `pixels` buffer, overflowing the heap-allocated quantum pixel buffer.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4646: using buffer line after it has been freed

Problem:    Using buffer line after it has been freed in old regexp engine.
Solution:   After getting mark get the line again.
cve_desc: Use after free in utf_ptr2char in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4646.

vulnerabilities.description: In the MULTIBYTECODE case, `rex.input += len` is executed unconditionally after the byte comparison loop, even when `status` is set to `RA_NOMATCH`. If the input string is shorter than `len` bytes (e.g., `rex.input` is at or near the NUL terminator), the pointer is advanced past the end of the valid string buffer. This corrupted pointer may then be dereferenced by subsequent regex operations before backtracking restores it, leading to an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The MULTIBYTECODE case begins. The operand `opnd` is a multi-byte character from the compiled regex pattern, and `len` is computed from `mb_ptr2len(opnd)`.

state.value: A valid multi-byte character sequence (e.g., 3-byte UTF-8 char)

step_by_step_execution.description: The code enters the non-composing path (either not UTF-8, or the operand is not a composing character). The byte-by-byte comparison loop runs: `for (i = 0; i < len; ++i) if (opnd[i] != rex.input[i])`.

state.value: Non-NUL first byte of multi-byte character

step_by_step_execution.description: At i=0, `opnd[0] != rex.input[0]` evaluates to true because the operand's first byte is non-NUL but the input is at NUL. Status is set to RA_NOMATCH and the comparison loop breaks.

Execution falls through to `rex.input += len` which is outside the if/else and executes unconditionally. The input pointer is advanced by `len` bytes past the NUL terminator, now pointing to out-of-bounds memory.

state.value: Points past the NUL terminator of the line buffer (out-of-bounds)

step_by_step_execution.description: The inner loop breaks due to `status != RA_CONT`. If the regstack has entries, the regstack processing loop runs. Certain regstack states (e.g., RS_STAR_LONG/RS_STAR_SHORT) may read `rex.input` (e.g., comparing `rex.input == rex.line`, or `*rex.input == rst->nextb`) before calling `reg_restore`, causing an out-of-bounds read from the corrupted pointer.

state.value: Invalid — points past end of line buffer

commit_message:
xfrm: xfrm_policy: fix a possible double xfrm_pols_put() in xfrm_bundle_lookup()

xfrm_policy_lookup() will call xfrm_pol_hold_rcu() to get a refcount of
pols[0]. This refcount can be dropped in xfrm_expand_policies() when
xfrm_expand_policies() return error. pols[0]'s refcount is balanced in
here. But xfrm_bundle_lookup() will also call xfrm_pols_put() with
num_pols == 1 to drop this refcount when xfrm_expand_policies() return
error.

This patch also fix an illegal address access. pols[0] will save a error
point when xfrm_policy_lookup fails. This lead to xfrm_pols_put to resolve
an illegal address in xfrm_bundle_lookup's error path.

Fix these by setting num_pols = 0 in xfrm_expand_policies()'s error path.

Fixes: 80c802f3073e ("xfrm: cache bundles instead of policies for outgoing flows")
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.18.14. xfrm_expand_policies in net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c can cause a refcount to be dropped twice.

commit_message:
net: xilinx_emaclite: Do not print real IOMEM pointer

Printing kernel pointers is discouraged because they might leak kernel
memory layout.  This fixes smatch warning:

drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c:1191 xemaclite_of_probe() warn:
 argument 4 to %08lX specifier is cast from pointer

Signed-off-by: YueHaibing <yuehaibing@huawei.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.13.3 makes it easier for attackers to defeat an ASLR protection mechanism because it prints a kernel pointer (i.e., the real IOMEM pointer).

commit_message:
mount: sanitize paths from non-root users

 $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
 mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

this is too promiscuous. It seems better to ignore on command line
specified paths which are not resolve-able for non-root users.

Fixed version:

  $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
  mount: /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2: Permission denied

  $ mount /dev/sda2
  mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

Note that this bug has no relation to mount(2) permissions evaluation
in suid mode. The way how non-root user specifies paths on command
line is completely irrelevant for comparison with fstab entries.

Signed-off-by: Karel Zak <kzak@redhat.com>
cve_desc: (a) mount and (b) umount in util-linux 2.14.1, 2.17.2, and probably other versions allow local users to determine the existence of restricted directories by (1) using the --guess-fstype command-line option or (2) attempting to mount a non-existent device, which generates different error messages depending on whether the directory exists.

commit_message:
SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill: Fix crash on broken file

Issue #802
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in Poppler through 0.78.0. There is a divide-by-zero error in the function SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill at SplashOutputDev.cc.

code_snippets:
result_width = tBitmap->getWidth() * imgData.repeatX;
  result_height = tBitmap->getHeight() * imgData.repeatY;
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow when computing result_width and result_height near the end of the function. Both are `int` types, and the multiplication of bitmap dimensions by repeat counts can overflow, producing a small or negative value. This corrupted value is then passed to `splash->drawImage()` as the image dimensions, potentially leading to heap buffer overflow or other memory corruption when the image is rendered.

code_snippets: retValue = splash->drawImage(&tilingBitmapSrc, nullptr, &imgData, colorMode, true, result_width, result_height, matc, false, true) == splashOk;

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with attacker-controlled PDF parameters including x0, y0, x1, y1, bbox, mat, ptm, xStep, yStep.

state.value: attacker-controlled integers, e.g., x0=0, y0=0, x1=100000, y1=100000

step_by_step_execution.description: width and height are computed from bbox, and the check xStep != width || yStep != height passes.
CTM is modified and kx, ky are computed. The code enters the branch where fabs(kx) < 1 && fabs(ky) < 1.

In the fabs(kx)<1 && fabs(ky)<1 branch, surface_width and surface_height are recalculated after scaling, and repeatX/repeatY are set to x1-x0, y1-y0. There is NO surface_width*surface_height size limit check in this branch.

The check `repeatX * repeatY <= 4` is evaluated. With repeatX=100000 and repeatY=100000, the int multiplication 100000*100000 = 10^10 overflows a 32-bit int, potentially wrapping to a small positive value (e.g., 1410065408 or some value > 4 on 32-bit, but could wrap to <=4 with different values). With carefully chosen values, this check can be bypassed.

state.value: overflowed int value, potentially <= 4 (bypasses check) or > 4 (passes check)

step_by_step_execution.description: A SplashBitmap is allocated with surface_width x surface_height. The function proceeds to render the pattern and reaches the final result_width/result_height computation.
result_width = tBitmap->getWidth() * imgData.repeatX overflows int (e.g. 50 * 100000 = 5000000, but with larger values like surface_width=50000 and repeatX=100000 => 5*10^9 overflows 32-bit int).

state.value: overflowed int value, could be negative or small positive
overflowed int value, could be negative or small positive

step_by_step_execution.description: The corrupted result_width and result_height are passed to splash->drawImage(), which uses them to determine how much data to read/write, leading to heap buffer overflow or out-of-bounds memory access.

vulnerabilities.description: Division by zero when both ctm[0] and ctm[1] are zero (or both matc[0] and matc[1] are zero). The condition `splashAbs(ctm[1]) > splashAbs(ctm[0])` is false when both are zero, leading to the else branch which divides by ctm[0] (which is zero).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with mat and CTM values such that after concatCTM, ctm[0] and ctm[1] are both 0.
The condition `splashAbs(ctm[1]) > splashAbs(ctm[0])` evaluates to false (0 > 0 is false), so the else branch executes.
In the else branch, `ky = ctm[3] - (ctm[1] * ctm[2]) / ctm[0]` performs division by zero (ctm[0] is 0).

commit_message:
red fixes (#468)

* red fixes

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

vulnerabilities.description: strncpy does not null-terminate the destination buffer when the source string length is >= sizeof(local.sun_path). If pipePath is 108 bytes or longer (the typical size of sun_path), local.sun_path will not be null-terminated. Subsequent use of local.sun_path as a C string in unlink(), bind(), and chmod() will read past the buffer boundary on the stack, causing an out-of-bounds read and potentially undefined behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with an endpoint whose name() returns a string of length >= sizeof(local.sun_path) (typically 108 bytes).

state.value: A string of 108 or more characters

step_by_step_execution.description: A sockaddr_un struct 'local' is declared on the stack. sun_path is a fixed-size char array (typically 108 bytes).

strncpy copies at most sizeof(local.sun_path) bytes from pipePath into local.sun_path. Because pipePath length >= 108, all 108 bytes are filled with non-null characters and no null terminator is written.

unlink(local.sun_path) is called, which treats local.sun_path as a null-terminated C string. It reads beyond the 108-byte sun_path buffer into adjacent stack memory looking for a null terminator, causing a stack buffer over-read.

state.value: not null-terminated; read goes out of bounds

commit_message:
Calcualte max nspace declarations correctly for XML writer

(raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common): Calculate max including for
each attribute a potential name and value.

Fixes Issues #0000617 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=617
and #0000618 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=618
cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

code_snippets:
size_t buf_length = XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN + lang_len + 1;
    unsigned char* buffer = RAPTOR_MALLOC(unsigned char*, buf_length + 1);
vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow when processing xml:lang. The buffer is sized assuming the language string needs no XML escaping (allocated as prefix_len + lang_len + 1 + 1). However, raptor_xml_escape_string can expand characters (e.g., '&' -> '&', '"' -> '"'), producing output longer than lang_len. Furthermore, the available space parameter passed to raptor_xml_escape_string is buf_length (lang_len + 11), which overstates the actual remaining buffer space after the prefix (lang_len + 2). If xml_language contains characters requiring XML escaping, the write will exceed the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins. nstack is non-NULL, element->xml_language is set to a string containing characters that require XML escaping, e.g. 'a"b' (length 3).
nspace_declarations is allocated based on nspace_max_count. Processing reaches the xml_language block because nstack is non-NULL and element->xml_language is non-NULL.
lang_len is computed as strlen of xml_language.
buf_length is computed as XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN (10) + lang_len (3) + 1 = 14. Buffer is allocated with buf_length + 1 = 15 bytes.
10 bytes of 'xml:lang="' are written to buffer. Pointer p is set to buffer + 10, leaving 5 bytes of space (indices 10-14).

raptor_xml_escape_string is called with the language string 'a"b' (3 bytes), output pointer p, and buffer size parameter buf_length=14 (overstated; actual remaining space is 5). The double-quote character '"' is escaped to '"' (6 bytes), so the output is 'a"b' = 8 bytes. This writes 8 bytes starting at buffer+10, reaching buffer+17, overflowing the 15-byte buffer by 3 bytes.
After the escaped write, *p++ = quote writes another byte beyond the buffer, and *p = '\0' writes yet another, further compounding the overflow.

commit_message:
Fix UAF in aaaa on arm/thumb switching ##crash

* Reported by @peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer tests_65185
* This is a logic fix, but not the fully safe as changes in the code
  can result on UAF again, to properly protect r2 from crashing we
  need to break the ABI and add refcounting to RRegItem, which can't
  happen in 5.6.x because of abi-compat rules
cve_desc: Use After Free in op_is_set_bp in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {
vulnerabilities.description: When `stack_rev` is true, the loop variable `i` (of type `size_t`, which is unsigned) is checked with `i >= to`. If `to` is 0 (which happens when `r_anal_cc_max_arg` returns 0), the condition `i >= 0` is always true for an unsigned type. When `i` reaches 0 and `i--` is executed, it wraps around to `SIZE_MAX`, creating an effectively infinite loop that accesses `r_type_func_args_type` and `r_type_func_args_name` with extremely large out-of-bounds index values.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with valid arguments. The first loop over `op->src` does not find a matching register or suitable delta, so `ptr` remains 0.
ESIL parsing succeeds, `ptr_end` is found, and `addr` is parsed to extract a valid hex pointer value. `ptr` gets a non-zero value.
Execution reaches the `*sign == '+'` branch. `isarg` evaluates to true.
`get_stack_var` returns NULL, so we proceed to guess variable names from function type info.
`r_anal_cc_arg(anal, fcn->cc, ST32_MAX)` returns "stack_rev", so `stack_rev` is set to true.
`r_type_func_guess` returns a valid `fname`. `r_type_func_args_count` returns some count (e.g. 3), so `from = cnt - 1 = 2`. `r_anal_cc_max_arg` returns 0, so `to = 0`.
The for loop starts with `i = 2`. The condition `i >= to` is `i >= 0`, which is always true for unsigned `size_t`. The loop iterates with i=2, i=1, i=0.
When `i == 0`, after the loop body executes, `i--` causes an unsigned integer underflow, wrapping `i` to `SIZE_MAX`.
The loop condition `i >= 0` is still true (always true for unsigned). `r_type_func_args_type` is called with an enormous index value, causing out-of-bounds reads or an infinite loop.

commit_message:
input-bmp: Increase header buffer in some cases

Signed-off-by: Peter Lemenkov <lemenkov@gmail.com>
cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

vulnerabilities.description: When Bitmap_File_Head.biSize is 108 or 124 (BMP Version 4 or 5), the code reads biSize-4 bytes (104 or 120 bytes) into a stack buffer of only 64 bytes, causing a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A 64-byte stack buffer is declared.
The file is opened and the BMP magic bytes and initial header are read and validated.
Bitmap_File_Head.biSize is read from the file. The attacker crafts a BMP v4 header so biSize == 108.
The code enters the branch for biSize == 108 || biSize == 124 and calls ReadOK(fd, buffer, Bitmap_File_Head.biSize - 4), which attempts to read 108 - 4 = 104 bytes into the 64-byte buffer.
ReadOK writes 104 bytes into the 64-byte stack buffer, overflowing it by 40 bytes and corrupting adjacent stack memory including the return address.

vulnerabilities.description: When fopen fails and fd is NULL, the code jumps to the cleanup label which calls fclose(fd) with a NULL pointer, causing undefined behavior (likely a crash).

step_by_step_execution.description: fopen is called and fails, returning NULL.
The !fd check is true, so the code logs an error and jumps to cleanup.
At the cleanup label, fclose(fd) is called with fd == NULL, which is undefined behavior.

commit_message: fix #2964 - double-free in bin_qnx.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to a double free in bobj.c:rz_bin_reloc_storage_free() when freeing relocations generated from qnx binary plugin. A user opening a malicious qnx binary could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. commit_message: check vertexIndex which might be -1 for broken DXF
cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.description: When groupCode==42 is processed before any groupCode==10 has been seen, vertexIndex is still -1 (its initial value set when groupCode==90 is handled). The check `vertexIndex<maxVertices` passes because -1 < maxVertices, but unlike the groupCode<=30 branch, there is no `vertexIndex>=0` check. This leads to an out-of-bounds write at `vertices[4*(-1) + 3]` = `vertices[-1]`.

step_by_step_execution.description: groupCode==90 is processed, maxVertices is set to a positive value, vertices array is allocated, and vertexIndex is set to -1.
Next call has groupCode==42, entering the else-if branch. Since groupCode!=10, vertexIndex is not incremented and remains -1.

The condition `groupCode==42 && vertexIndex<maxVertices` is evaluated: -1 < maxVertices is true. The code writes to vertices[4*(-1)+3] = vertices[-1], an out-of-bounds write before the allocated buffer.

vulnerabilities.description: maxVertices is derived from user input via toInt(groupValue). If maxVertices is large enough (e.g., 0x40000001 on a 32-bit int system), the expression `4*maxVertices` overflows to a small positive value (e.g., 4). The `new double[4*maxVertices]` allocates a tiny buffer, but the subsequent initialization loop iterates maxVertices times, writing far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: groupCode==90 is processed. maxVertices is set from user-controlled input to a value like 0x40000001 (1073741825).
The check maxVertices>0 passes. The expression 4*maxVertices is computed as an int: 4*0x40000001 = 0x100000004, which overflows 32-bit int to 4.
new double[4] allocates space for only 4 doubles (32 bytes).

The for loop iterates from i=0 to i<1073741825, writing 4 doubles per iteration. After the first iteration (i=1), the write to vertices[4] is already out of bounds, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
net: xilinx_emaclite: Do not print real IOMEM pointer

Printing kernel pointers is discouraged because they might leak kernel
memory layout.  This fixes smatch warning:

drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c:1191 xemaclite_of_probe() warn:
 argument 4 to %08lX specifier is cast from pointer

Signed-off-by: YueHaibing <yuehaibing@huawei.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.13.3 makes it easier for attackers to defeat an ASLR protection mechanism because it prints a kernel pointer (i.e., the real IOMEM pointer).

code_snippets:
res = platform_get_resource(ofdev, IORESOURCE_MEM, 0);
	lp->base_addr = devm_ioremap_resource(&ofdev->dev, res);
vulnerabilities.description: If platform_get_resource for IORESOURCE_MEM returns NULL, the NULL res pointer is passed directly to devm_ioremap_resource, which will dereference it (e.g., to access res->start). Unlike the IORESOURCE_IRQ case above, there is no NULL check on res before it is used.

step_by_step_execution.description: platform_get_resource(ofdev, IORESOURCE_MEM, 0) is called and returns NULL because the memory resource is not defined for the device.
res (NULL) is passed to devm_ioremap_resource, which internally dereferences the resource pointer, causing a NULL pointer dereference.
Even if devm_ioremap_resource somehow handled NULL and returned an error, the subsequent lines res->start and res->end would also dereference the NULL pointer.

commit_message: Fix segfault in older versions of OpenSSL (before 0.9.8i)
cve_desc: The openssl_encrypt function in ext/openssl/openssl.c in PHP 5.3.9 through 5.3.13 does not initialize a certain variable, which allows remote attackers to obtain sensitive information from process memory by providing zero bytes of input data.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the calculation of `outlen` when `data_len` is very large. If `data_len` is close to INT_MAX, adding `EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)` can cause an integer overflow, resulting in a very small `outlen` value. This leads to a small heap allocation via `emalloc(outlen + 1)`, but `EVP_EncryptUpdate` will then write up to `data_len + block_size` bytes into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: User supplies a very large `data` string, e.g., `data_len` close to INT_MAX (2147483647).
The cipher block size is retrieved, e.g., 16 for AES.
outlen is calculated as data_len + block_size. With data_len near INT_MAX, this overflows the int type, wrapping to a small or negative value.

state.value: data_len + 16 overflows int, e.g., becomes a small negative or positive number

step_by_step_execution.description: emalloc(outlen + 1) allocates a very small buffer (if outlen wrapped to a small positive value) or triggers undefined behavior.

state.value: pointer to a small heap allocation

step_by_step_execution.description: EVP_EncryptUpdate writes encrypted data into outbuf. The actual output size can be up to data_len + block_size bytes, far exceeding the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message: Fix segfault in older versions of OpenSSL (before 0.9.8i)
cve_desc: The openssl_encrypt function in ext/openssl/openssl.c in PHP 5.3.9 through 5.3.13 does not initialize a certain variable, which allows remote attackers to obtain sensitive information from process memory by providing zero bytes of input data.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the output buffer size calculation. If `data_len` is sufficiently large (close to INT_MAX), adding `EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)` can cause an integer overflow, resulting in a small allocation. The subsequent `EVP_DecryptUpdate` then writes beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: User calls openssl_decrypt with raw_input=true and a very large data string, so data_len is close to INT_MAX (e.g., 0x7FFFFFF0).
raw_input is true, so the base64 decode path is skipped. data_len remains the original large value.
outlen is computed as data_len + EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type). For a 16-byte block cipher, this is ~0x7FFFFFF0 + 16 = 0x80000000, which overflows a signed 32-bit int to a negative value.

state.value: negative value due to signed integer overflow (e.g., -2147483632)

step_by_step_execution.description: emalloc(outlen + 1) is called. The negative outlen + 1 is interpreted as a size_t, potentially wrapping or allocating a very small buffer depending on platform behavior.
EVP_DecryptUpdate writes decrypted data into outbuf using the original large data_len, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Fix UAF in aaaa on arm/thumb switching ##crash

* Reported by @peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer tests_65185
* This is a logic fix, but not the fully safe as changes in the code
  can result on UAF again, to properly protect r2 from crashing we
  need to break the ABI and add refcounting to RRegItem, which can't
  happen in 5.6.x because of abi-compat rules
cve_desc: Use After Free in op_is_set_bp in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {
vulnerabilities.description: When `stack_rev` is true and `to` is 0, the loop variable `i` (of type `size_t`, which is unsigned) will underflow when it decrements past 0. After `i` is 0 and the loop body executes, `i--` wraps `i` to `SIZE_MAX`. The condition `i >= to` (i.e., `SIZE_MAX >= 0`) remains true, so the loop continues with a massive index, leading to out-of-bounds reads via `r_type_func_args_type` and `r_type_func_args_name`.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++)

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with `sign` pointing to '+', so the `*sign == '+'` branch is taken.
`isarg` evaluates to true (the offset indicates an argument).
`r_anal_cc_arg(anal, fcn->cc, ST32_MAX)` returns "stack_rev", so `stack_rev` is set to true.
A function name `fname` is resolved successfully from `fcn->name`.
`to` is set to `r_anal_cc_max_arg(anal, fcn->cc)`, which can return 0 (no register arguments, all on stack).
`from` is set to `cnt - 1` where `cnt = r_type_func_args_count(...)`. Suppose `cnt` is 1, so `from` is 0.
The for-loop starts with `i = from = 0`. Condition `i >= to` is `0 >= 0` which is true. Loop body executes. If `sum_sz != frame_off`, the loop proceeds to the increment expression.
The increment expression `i--` is evaluated. Since `i` is `size_t` (unsigned), decrementing 0 wraps to SIZE_MAX.

The loop condition `i >= to` evaluates to `SIZE_MAX >= 0`, which is true. The loop continues with a massive out-of-range index passed to `r_type_func_args_type(anal->sdb_types, fname, i)`, causing out-of-bounds access.

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

vulnerabilities.description: The buffer `drc_name` is not null-terminated after `memcpy`, but `strchr` is called on it, which scans memory until it finds the target character or a null terminator. Since `drc_name` is an uninitialized stack buffer and only `nbytes` bytes are copied (without a null terminator), `strchr` can read beyond the copied region into uninitialized stack memory. If no '\n' or '\0' happens to exist in the remaining uninitialized portion of the array (or beyond it), `strchr` will perform an out-of-bounds read past the `drc_name` array.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `buf` containing data with no newline character, and `nbytes` is less than MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN (e.g., nbytes = 5).

state.value: uninitialized stack array of MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `nbytes >= MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN` is false, so execution continues.
`memcpy(drc_name, buf, nbytes)` copies 5 bytes from `buf` into `drc_name`. Bytes at indices 5 through MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN-1 remain uninitialized. No null terminator is written.

`strchr(drc_name, '
')` scans `drc_name` starting at index 0, looking for '
' or stopping at '\0'. Since the copied 5 bytes contain no '
', it continues into uninitialized memory at indices 5 and beyond. If no '
' or '\0' is found in the uninitialized region within the array, `strchr` reads beyond the array boundary, causing an out-of-bounds read on the stack.

state.value: result of strchr scanning into and potentially past uninitialized stack memory

  #5  0x00007ffff7b69146 in virHashForEach (table=0x7fff9814b700 = {...}, iter=0x7ffff7cb149e <virNodeDeviceObjListExportCallback>, opaque=0x7fffe2ffc6a0) at ../src/util/virhash.c:394

cve_desc: A flaw was found in libvirt in the virConnectListAllNodeDevices API in versions before 7.0.0. It only affects hosts with a PCI device and driver that supports mediated devices (e.g., GRID driver). commit_message:
patch 9.0.0025: accessing beyond allocated memory with the cmdline window

Problem:    Accessing beyond allocated memory when using the cmdline window in
            Ex mode.
Solution:   Use "*" instead of "'<,'>" for Visual mode.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Write in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.description: When has_visual_range is TRUE, eap->cmd > cmd_start, and use_plus_cmd is TRUE, the code copies the content from cmd_start to orig_cmd and then appends the 7-byte string "'<,'>+\0". The original buffer starting at orig_cmd has 5 + len + 1 bytes (the 5-char prefix "'<,'>" plus the remaining content plus NUL), but the write requires len + 7 bytes, resulting in a 1-byte out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is entered with eap->cmd pointing to a string like "'<,'> " (the visual range prefix followed by whitespace, with the string being exactly 7 characters + NUL = 8 bytes from orig_cmd).
The STRNCMP check matches "'<,'>", so eap->cmd is advanced by 5 and cmd_start is set. has_visual_range becomes TRUE.
In the for loop, the while loop skips the two space characters, advancing eap->cmd to the NUL terminator.

state.value: pointing to NUL (2 positions past cmd_start)

step_by_step_execution.description: The condition *eap->cmd == NUL && exmode_active (and other conditions) is met, so use_plus_cmd is set to TRUE and the loop breaks.
In the has_visual_range block, since eap->cmd > cmd_start and use_plus_cmd is TRUE, len is computed as STRLEN(cmd_start) = 2 (the two spaces).

mch_memmove copies 2 bytes from cmd_start to orig_cmd. Then STRCPY writes "'<,'>+" (6 chars + NUL = 7 bytes) at orig_cmd + 2. Total bytes written: 2 + 7 = 9, but the buffer is only 8 bytes, causing a 1-byte heap/stack buffer overflow.

commit_message:
iwlwifi: pcie: fix rb_allocator workqueue allocation

We don't handle failures in the rb_allocator workqueue allocation
correctly. To fix that, move the code earlier so the cleanup is
easier and we don't have to undo all the interrupt allocations in
this case.

Signed-off-by: Johannes Berg <johannes.berg@intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Luca Coelho <luciano.coelho@intel.com>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/intel/iwlwifi/pcie/trans.c in the Linux kernel 5.2.14 does not check the alloc_workqueue return value, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
Avoid possible segfault when handling networkstatus vote with bad flavor

Fix for 6530; fix on 0.2.2.6-alpha.
cve_desc: Use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c in Tor before 0.2.2.38 might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (daemon crash) via vectors related to failed DNS requests.

vulnerabilities.description: When processing K_CONTACT or K_VOTE_DIGEST tokens, the code checks if `voter` is NULL but if the check fails (voter is NULL), it logs a warning and goes to `goto err`. However, if a K_CONTACT or K_VOTE_DIGEST token appears before any K_DIR_SOURCE token, `voter` is NULL and the NULL check correctly catches it. This is actually handled. But there's a more subtle issue: if no K_DIR_SOURCE token is encountered but tokens of other types are processed, the `voter` variable remains NULL. The real issue is that `voter->contact` or `voter->vote_digest` access would be a NULL dereference if the `!voter` check weren't there. The checks are present, so this path is guarded.

step_by_step_execution.description: This is actually guarded by the `if (!voter || voter->contact)` check, so not exploitable. Removing from findings.

vulnerabilities.description: For consensus type documents, `tok->object_size` is checked against INT_MAX and SIZE_T_CEILING, but earlier there's a check `tok->object_size < 128 || tok->object_size > 512` which bounds the size to [128, 512]. So the INT_MAX check is redundant but not a vulnerability. No real issue here.

step_by_step_execution.description: The object_size is bounded to [128, 512] by an earlier check, so the cast to int is safe. Not a real vulnerability.

commit_message:
seq_file: disallow extremely large seq buffer allocations

There is no reasonable need for a buffer larger than this, and it avoids
int overflow pitfalls.

Fixes: 058504edd026 ("fs/seq_file: fallback to vmalloc allocation")
Suggested-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
Reported-by: Qualys Security Advisory <qsa@qualys.com>
Signed-off-by: Eric Sandeen <sandeen@redhat.com>
Cc: stable@kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: fs/seq_file.c in the Linux kernel 3.16 through 5.13.x before 5.13.4 does not properly restrict seq buffer allocations, leading to an integer overflow, an Out-of-bounds Write, and escalation to root by an unprivileged user, aka CID-8cae8cd89f05.

commit_message: Fix use-after-free in PlayerGeneric destructor
cve_desc: PlayerGeneric.cpp in MilkyTracker through 1.02.00 has a use-after-free in the PlayerGeneric destructor.

vulnerabilities.description: When both `mixer` and `player` are non-null, `mixer` is deleted first, and then the code dereferences the already-freed `mixer` pointer via `mixer->isActive()` and potentially `mixer->isDeviceRemoved()` and `mixer->removeDevice()`. This is a use-after-free vulnerability.

step_by_step_execution.description: The destructor is entered with both `mixer` and `player` being non-null pointers to valid objects.

state.value: non-null, points to a valid object
non-null, points to a valid object

step_by_step_execution.description: The condition `if (mixer)` is true, so `delete mixer` is executed, freeing the memory pointed to by `mixer`.

state.value: non-null, but points to freed memory (dangling pointer)

step_by_step_execution.description: The condition `if (player)` is true since `player` is non-null, so the body of the if-block is entered.
`mixer->isActive()` is called on the dangling pointer, resulting in a use-after-free. The program reads from or dispatches a virtual call on freed memory.

commit_message:
Avoid possible segfault when handling networkstatus vote with bad flavor

Fix for 6530; fix on 0.2.2.6-alpha.
cve_desc: Use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c in Tor before 0.2.2.38 might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (daemon crash) via vectors related to failed DNS requests.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on tok->args[2]. When tok->n_args is exactly 2 (which satisfies the `tok->n_args > 1` check), accessing tok->args[2] reads past the end of the args array. The error message incorrectly uses index 2 instead of index 1.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function parses the K_NETWORK_STATUS_VERSION token from the input string.

The code checks if tok->n_args > 1. A valid network-status-version line like 'network-status-version 3 microdesc' would have n_args == 2, passing this check. However, a line with exactly 2 args (e.g., 'network-status-version 3 unknownflavor') also passes.
networkstatus_parse_flavor_name(tok->args[1]) is called with the second argument (e.g., 'unknownflavor'). If the flavor is unrecognized, it returns -1.
Because flavor < 0, the error logging branch is entered. The log_warn call accesses tok->args[2], but since n_args is 2, only indices 0 and 1 are valid. Index 2 is an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: out-of-bounds access (only indices 0 and 1 are valid)

commit_message:
Fixed int overflow in PresentationContext_new

Thanks to hac425 CVE-2020-11038
cve_desc: In FreeRDP less than or equal to 2.0.0, an Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow exists. When using /video redirection, a manipulated server can instruct the client to allocate a buffer with a smaller size than requested due to an integer overflow in size calculation. With later messages, the server can manipulate the client to write data out of bound to the previously allocated buffer. This has been patched in 2.1.0.

vulnerabilities.description: The expression `width * height * 4` is computed using UINT32 arithmetic and can overflow, resulting in a much smaller allocation than expected. Subsequent operations (e.g., H.264 decoding into surfaceData) would then write beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with attacker-influenced width and height parameters (both UINT32).
The expression width * height * 4 is evaluated in 32-bit arithmetic: 65536 * 65536 * 4 = 0x400000000, which overflows UINT32 to 0.
BufferPool_Take allocates a buffer of size 0 (or a very small buffer), returning a valid pointer.
Later usage (H.264 decode output, YUV conversion) writes width*height*4 bytes worth of pixel data into ret->surfaceData, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: up to 65536*65536*4 bytes into a 0-byte buffer

commit_message: SERVER-59071 Treat '$sample' as unsharded when connecting directly to shards
cve_desc: An authorized user may trigger an invariant which may result in denial of service or server exit if a relevant aggregation request is sent to a shard. Usually, the requests are sent via mongos and special privileges are required in order to know the address of the shards and to log in to the shards of an auth enabled environment.

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.description: The pipe_buffer's `flags` field is never initialized when a new buffer entry is set up. This means `buf->flags` retains whatever value it had from a previous use of that pipe ring slot. If a prior operation (e.g., a splice or pipe read/write cycle) left `PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE` set on that slot, a subsequent `pipe_write()` call will see the flag and merge user-controlled data directly into the page cache page referenced by `buf->page`. This allows an unprivileged user to overwrite the contents of read-only files (the Dirty Pipe vulnerability, CVE-2022-0847).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a page from the page cache (e.g., via splice from a file) and a valid pipe iterator. `bytes` is clamped to `i->count` and is non-zero.

state.value: a page cache page from a read-only file

step_by_step_execution.description: `off` is read from `i->iov_offset`. Assume `off == 0`, so the merge/increment block is skipped entirely.
`pipe_full()` is checked and returns false, so execution continues to initialize the buffer.
The buffer fields `ops`, `page`, `offset`, and `len` are assigned. However, `buf->flags` is NOT written to. It retains its stale value from a previous use of this pipe ring slot.

state.value: stale value, potentially PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE from a prior anonymous pipe write
page cache page of a read-only file

step_by_step_execution.description: A subsequent `pipe_write()` by the user sees `PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE` on this buffer slot. It merges attacker-controlled data directly into `buf->page`, which is a page cache page, thereby overwriting read-only file contents.

state.value: attacker data written into read-only page cache page

Usually, the requests are sent via mongos and special privileges are required in order to know the address of the shards and to log in to the shards of an auth enabled environment.

commit_message:
Fix oobread in dyldcache ##crash

* Reported by @hdthky via huntr.dev
* Reproducers: poc1
* BountyID: 8ae2c61a-2220-47a5-bfe8-fe6d41ab1f82
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of inducing denial of service.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++) {
				bool printing = !deps[j];
vulnerabilities.description: The `deps` array is allocated using `cache->hdr->imagesCount` (i.e., `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`), but in the loop over multiple headers (`for i = 0..n_hdr`), it is indexed with `j` up to `hdr->imagesCount` where `hdr = &cache->hdr[i]`. If a subsequent header has a larger `imagesCount` than the first header, accessing `deps[j]` results in an out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `deps` array is allocated based on the first header's imagesCount.
The outer loop iterates over all headers: for i = 0 to cache->n_hdr - 1.
For some i > 0, hdr = &cache->hdr[i] may have a larger imagesCount than cache->hdr[0].imagesCount.
The inner loop iterates j from 0 to hdr->imagesCount - 1, and accesses deps[j] which is out of bounds when j >= cache->hdr[0].imagesCount.

vulnerabilities.description: The `dep_index` value derived from `depArray[k] & 0x7fff` (up to 32767) is used to index into both `deps` (size `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`) and `img` (size `hdr->imagesCount`) without any bounds check. A malformed cache file can cause out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: depArray is read from the cache file, containing attacker-controlled ut16 values.
dep_index is computed as depArray[k] & 0x7fff, yielding a value up to 32767.
deps[dep_index]++ is executed. If dep_index >= cache->hdr[0].imagesCount, this is an out-of-bounds write.
img[dep_index] is passed to get_lib_name, which is an out-of-bounds read if dep_index >= hdr->imagesCount.

vulnerabilities.description: The loop over depArray uses `extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex` as the starting index and terminates only when `depArray[k] == 0xffff`. There is no bounds check ensuring `k < cache->accel->depListCount`, so a malformed cache without the sentinel value causes an out-of-bounds read on depArray.

step_by_step_execution.description: depArray is allocated with cache->accel->depListCount elements.
extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex is read from attacker-controlled cache data.
The loop starts at that index and increments k until depArray[k] == 0xffff. If the start index is already >= depListCount, or no 0xffff sentinel exists within bounds, k exceeds the allocation size.

commit_message:
Fix oobread in dyldcache ##crash

* Reported by @hdthky via huntr.dev
* Reproducers: poc1
* BountyID: 8ae2c61a-2220-47a5-bfe8-fe6d41ab1f82
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of inducing denial of service.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++) {
				bool printing = !deps[j];
vulnerabilities.description: The `deps` array is allocated based on `cache->hdr->imagesCount` (i.e., cache->hdr[0].imagesCount), but is then indexed by `j` which iterates up to `hdr->imagesCount` for each header in the loop (`hdr = &cache->hdr[i]`). If any subsequent header has a larger `imagesCount` than the first header, `deps[j]` will be an out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `deps` array is allocated with size `cache->hdr->imagesCount`, which refers to `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`.
The outer loop iterates over all headers: `for (i = 0; i < cache->n_hdr; i++)`. For each iteration, `hdr = &cache->hdr[i]`.
When i > 0, `hdr->imagesCount` can be larger than `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`.
The inner loop `for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++)` iterates j beyond the allocated size of `deps`.
`deps[j]` is accessed for read (`!deps[j]`) and write (`deps[j]++`), causing an out-of-bounds access.

vulnerabilities.description: The loop over `depArray` starting at `extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex` checks only for sentinel value 0xffff but never checks if `k < cache->accel->depListCount`, allowing out-of-bounds read on `depArray`. Additionally, `dep_index` (derived from `depArray[k] & 0x7fff`, up to 32767) is used to index both `deps` and `img` without bounds checking, allowing out-of-bounds access on both arrays.

step_by_step_execution.description: depArray is allocated with `cache->accel->depListCount` elements and filled from the cache buffer.
The loop starts at `k = extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex` and increments k until `depArray[k] == 0xffff`.
If the sentinel 0xffff is missing or placed beyond the array, `k` exceeds `cache->accel->depListCount - 1`, reading out-of-bounds from depArray.
dep_index is computed as `depArray[k] & 0x7fff`, yielding a value up to 32767. This is used to index `deps` (size cache->hdr[0].imagesCount) and `img` (size hdr->imagesCount) without bounds checking.
`deps[dep_index]++` and `img[dep_index]` access memory out-of-bounds.

commit_message: ITS#9454 fix issuerAndThisUpdateCheck
cve_desc: In OpenLDAP through 2.4.57 and 2.5.x through 2.5.1alpha, an assertion failure in slapd can occur in the issuerAndThisUpdateCheck function via a crafted packet, resulting in a denial of service (daemon exit) via a short timestamp. This is related to schema_init.c and checkTime.

vulnerabilities.description: When the issuer quoted-string scanning loop terminates without finding a closing double-quote (i.e., is->bv_len reaches x.bv_len), the code unconditionally subtracts is->bv_len + 1 from x.bv_len. Since ber_len_t is unsigned and is->bv_len equals x.bv_len, the subtraction (x.bv_len - x.bv_len - 1) wraps around to a very large value. This leads to subsequent out-of-bounds reads as the parser continues with a corrupted length, and also x.bv_val is advanced past the valid buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function checks that in->bv_len is at least STRLENOF("{issuer \"\",thisUpdate \"YYMMDDhhmmssZ\"}") (approximately 39). We provide a valid-length input like: {issuer rdnSequence:"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA} (padded to sufficient length, with no closing double-quote inside the content).
The opening '{' and closing '}' are verified. x.bv_val is advanced past '{' and x.bv_len is reduced by 2.

The parser matches 'issuer', skips spaces, matches 'rdnSequence:', and finds the opening '"'. x.bv_val and x.bv_len are adjusted. is->bv_val is set to x.bv_val (after the quote) and is->bv_len is set to 0.

state.value: points to characters after opening quote
remaining bytes after opening quote (all A's, no closing quote present)

step_by_step_execution.description: The quote-scanning loop iterates: for ( ; is->bv_len < x.bv_len; ). Since none of the characters are '"', is->bv_len is incremented each iteration until is->bv_len == x.bv_len, at which point the loop exits normally (condition false) without finding a closing quote.

state.value: x.bv_len (equal to remaining bytes, no quote found)

step_by_step_execution.description: The code executes x.bv_val += is->bv_len + 1 (pointer goes past the valid buffer) and x.bv_len -= is->bv_len + 1. Since is->bv_len == x.bv_len and ber_len_t is unsigned, this computes 0 - 1 which wraps to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF on 64-bit).

state.value: points past the end of the original buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: Subsequent code (space-eating loops, strncasecmp calls, etc.) continues to read from x.bv_val with the corrupted x.bv_len, causing out-of-bounds memory reads.

vulnerabilities.description: In the issuer quote-scanning loop, when a double-quote character is found at is->bv_val[is->bv_len], the code immediately accesses is->bv_val[is->bv_len+1] without checking that is->bv_len+1 < x.bv_len. When is->bv_len == x.bv_len - 1, this reads one byte past the tracked region. While this byte is typically within the original buffer (the '}' character), it constitutes an off-by-one out-of-bounds read relative to the logical parsing boundary, and if is->bv_val[is->bv_len+1] happens to be '"' (which could happen in certain buffer layouts), it causes is->bv_len += 2, overshooting x.bv_len and triggering further issues.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input is crafted so that the last character of the region tracked by x (before '}') is a double-quote, positioned at is->bv_val[x.bv_len - 1].
The loop reaches is->bv_len == x.bv_len - 1. The condition is->bv_len < x.bv_len is true so the loop body executes.
is->bv_val[is->bv_len] is '"', so the code checks is->bv_val[is->bv_len + 1], i.e. is->bv_val[x.bv_len]. This index is outside the valid parsing region tracked by x, reading one byte beyond.

commit_message:
conf: Fix segfault when parsing mdev types

Commit f1b0890 introduced a potential crash due to incorrect operator
precedence when accessing an element from a pointer to an array.

Backtrace below:

Signed-off-by: Jonathon Jongsma <jjongsma@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Ján Tomko <jtomko@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Ján Tomko <jtomko@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in libvirt in the virConnectListAllNodeDevices API in versions before 7.0.0. It only affects hosts with a PCI device and driver that supports mediated devices (e.g., GRID driver). This flaw could be used by an unprivileged client with a read-only connection to crash the libvirt daemon by executing the 'nodedev-list' virsh command. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

vulnerabilities.description: Operator precedence bug causes out-of-bounds memory access. The expression `*mdev_types[i]` is parsed as `*(mdev_types[i])` due to `[]` having higher precedence than unary `*`. The intended expression is `(*mdev_types)[i]`. When `i >= 1`, `mdev_types[i]` reads memory past the single `virMediatedDeviceTypePtr *` pointer that `mdev_types` points to, resulting in an out-of-bounds read (and then the dereferenced garbage value is passed to the free function, potentially causing a use-after-free or arbitrary free).

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `mdev_types` pointing to a valid `virMediatedDeviceTypePtr *` (a pointer to an array of type pointers), and `*nmdev_types` is >= 2 (indicating a refresh with previous data).

state.value: pointer to a heap-allocated array of virMediatedDeviceTypePtr

step_by_step_execution.description: The for-loop begins with i=0. The expression `*mdev_types[0]` is evaluated as `*(mdev_types[0])` which equals `*(*mdev_types)`, i.e. `(*mdev_types)[0]`. This is correct for i=0 by coincidence.

state.value: equivalent to (*mdev_types)[0], a valid type pointer

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop increments i to 1. Now `*mdev_types[1]` is evaluated as `*(mdev_types[1])`. `mdev_types` is a `virMediatedDeviceTypePtr **` parameter (pointer to a single stack/heap variable). `mdev_types[1]` reads memory adjacent to that variable, which is out-of-bounds.

state.value: out-of-bounds read past the mdev_types pointer, undefined/garbage value

step_by_step_execution.description: The garbage pointer from the out-of-bounds read is dereferenced (`*mdev_types[1]`) and passed to `virMediatedDeviceTypeFree`, which will attempt to free arbitrary memory.

state.value: garbage/undefined pointer value from OOB read

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4418: crash when using special multi-byte character

Problem:    Crash when using special multi-byte character.
Solution:   Don't use isalpha() for an arbitrary character.
cve_desc: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4418.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow when a directory entry name (dp->d_name) exceeds BASENAMELEN characters. The buffer is allocated with size STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5, assuming filenames are at most BASENAMELEN bytes. However, on some filesystems, filenames returned by readdir() can exceed BASENAMELEN. The unchecked STRCPY of dp->d_name into buf at position s, followed by appending path_end (and possibly "/**" in the starstar case), can write past the end of the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Buffer buf is allocated with size STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5. For example, if path is "dir/**/rest", STRLEN(path) is 11, and BASENAMELEN is 255, buffer is 271 bytes.

The while loop parses path, finding the wildcard component. s points to the start of the wildcard part in buf (e.g., after "dir/"), e points to its end. path_end points to the remaining path after the wildcard component (e.g., "/rest").
opendir is called on the directory prefix. readdir() returns a directory entry dp whose d_name is longer than BASENAMELEN on a filesystem that supports longer names (e.g., 300 bytes).

STRCPY(s, dp->d_name) copies the 300-byte filename into buf starting at position s. Since the buffer was sized assuming names are at most BASENAMELEN, this writes (s - buf) + 300 bytes, which can exceed the allocated buffer size of STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5.
This is already an overflow. Subsequently, STRCPY(buf + len, "/**") and STRCPY(buf + len + 3, path_end) write even further past the buffer end, worsening the heap corruption.

commit_message:
Fix bug 697459 Buffer overflow in fill_threshold_buffer

There was an overflow check for ht_buffer size, but none for the larger
threshold_buffer. Note that this file didn't fail on Windows because the
combination of the ht_buffer and the size of the (miscalculated due to
overflow) threshold_buffer would have exceeded the 2Gb limit.
cve_desc: The fill_threshhold_buffer function in base/gxht_thresh.c in Artifex Software, Inc. Ghostscript 9.20 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted PostScript document.

io_uring: reexpand under-reexpanded iters

[   74.211232] BUG: KASAN: stack-out-of-bounds in iov_iter_revert+0x809/0x900
[   74.212778] Read of size 8 at addr ffff888025dc78b8 by task
syz-executor.0/828
[   74.214756] CPU: 0 PID: 828 Comm: syz-executor.0 Not tainted
5.14.0-rc3-next-20210730 #1
[   74.216525] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996),
BIOS rel-1.14.0-0-g155821a1990b-prebuilt.qemu.org 04/01/2014
[   74.219033] Call Trace:
[   74.219683]  dump_stack_lvl+0x8b/0xb3
[   74.220706]  print_address_description.constprop.0+0x1f/0x140
[   74.224226]  kasan_report.cold+0x7f/0x11b
[   74.226085]  iov_iter_revert+0x809/0x900
[   74.227960]  io_write+0x57d/0xe40
[   74.232647]  io_issue_sqe+0x4da/0x6a80
[   74.242578]  __io_queue_sqe+0x1ac/0xe60
[   74.245358]  io_submit_sqes+0x3f6e/0x76a0
[   74.248207]  __do_sys_io_uring_enter+0x90c/0x1a20
[   74.257167]  do_syscall_64+0x3b/0x90
[   74.257984]  entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x44/0xae

old_size = iov_iter_count();
...
iov_iter_revert(old_size - iov_iter_count());

If iov_iter_revert() is done base on the initial size as above, and the
iter is truncated and not reexpanded in the middle, it miscalculates
borders causing problems. This trace is due to no one reexpanding after
generic_write_checks().

Now iters store how many bytes has been truncated, so reexpand them to
the initial state right before reverting.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Reported-by: Palash Oswal <oswalpalash@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Sudip Mukherjee <sudipm.mukherjee@gmail.com>

cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s io_uring module in the way a user triggers the io_read() function with some special parameters. This flaw allows a local user to read some memory out of bounds.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0018: going over the end of the typahead

Problem:    Going over the end of the typahead.
Solution:   Put a NUL after the typeahead.
cve_desc: Integer Overflow or Wraparound in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.description: When buf is not NULL, tp points to buf + offset and len = *buflen - offset. The NUL write tp[len] = NUL writes at buf[offset + (*buflen - offset)] = buf[*buflen]. If *buflen equals bufsize (the allocated size of buf), this writes one byte past the end of the buffer, causing an out-of-bounds write. The function never checks that *buflen < bufsize before this write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the for loop with offset = 0 and buf != NULL.

state.value: non-NULL pointer to buffer of size bufsize

step_by_step_execution.description: Since buf is not NULL, tp is set to buf + offset and len is computed as *buflen - offset.
The check offset >= *buflen does not break out of the loop since offset (0) < *buflen.

state.value: equal to bufsize (the full allocated buffer size)

step_by_step_execution.description: tp[len] = NUL writes a NUL byte at buf[*buflen]. Since *buflen == bufsize, this writes at buf[bufsize], which is one byte past the allocated buffer.

state.value: buf[bufsize] — one byte out of bounds

commit_message:
openvswitch: fix OOB access in reserve_sfa_size()

Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and
reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, if next_offset is
greater than MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE, the function reserve_sfa_size() does
not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, but it allocates MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE
bytes increasing actions_len by req_size. This can then lead to an OOB
write access, especially when further actions need to be copied.

Fix it by rearranging the flow action size check.

KASAN splat below:

==================================================================
BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in reserve_sfa_size+0x1ba/0x380 [openvswitch]
Write of size 65360 at addr ffff888147e4001c by task handler15/836

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: f28cd2af22a0 ("openvswitch: fix flow actions reallocation")
Signed-off-by: Paolo Valerio <pvalerio@redhat.com>
Acked-by: Eelco Chaudron <echaudro@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An integer coercion error was found in the openvswitch kernel module. Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, the reserve_sfa_size() function does not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, potentially leading to an out-of-bounds write access. This flaw allows a local user to crash or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in `next_offset + req_size` calculation. `next_offset` is an `int` and `req_size` is a `size_t`. The expression `next_offset + req_size` used in comparisons and the `max()` call can overflow or produce unexpected results due to mixed signed/unsigned arithmetic. Additionally, `new_acts_size` is an `int` which can overflow when computing `max(next_offset + req_size, ksize(*sfa) * 2)`, potentially leading to a small allocation followed by a large `memcpy` or out-of-bounds write via the returned pointer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function computes `req_size = NLA_ALIGN(attr_len)`. If `attr_len` is very large, `req_size` could be a large value after alignment.

Compute `next_offset` as an `int` from `offsetof(struct sw_flow_actions, actions) + (*sfa)->actions_len`. If `actions_len` is already large from prior calls, `next_offset` could be large or even overflow as a signed int.

state.value: large int, potentially wrapping negative on overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `req_size <= (ksize(*sfa) - next_offset)` involves subtracting a potentially negative `next_offset` (due to int overflow) from `ksize()` (a `size_t`), producing a very large unsigned value, causing the check to pass and jumping to `out` without reallocation.

state.value: wraps to very large size_t if next_offset is negative
appears smaller than the wrapped value, check passes

step_by_step_execution.description: At `out`, `(*sfa)->actions_len += req_size` increases `actions_len` further, and the function returns a pointer at `next_offset` from `*sfa`, which may point outside the allocated buffer.

commit_message:
i2c-ddc: fix oob read

Suggested-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>
Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Message-id: 20190108102301.1957-1-kraxel@redhat.com
cve_desc: QEMU, through version 2.10 and through version 3.1.0, is vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read of up to 128 bytes in the hw/i2c/i2c-ddc.c:i2c_ddc() function. A local attacker with permission to execute i2c commands could exploit this to read stack memory of the qemu process on the host.

vulnerabilities.description: If s->reg is not properly bounded, repeated calls to i2c_ddc_rx will increment s->reg beyond the size of s->edid_blob, causing an out-of-bounds read. There is no bounds check on s->reg before indexing into s->edid_blob.

step_by_step_execution.description: i2c_ddc_rx is called, s->reg has some current value
s->edid_blob[s->reg] is accessed without any bounds check against the size of edid_blob

state.value: could be out of bounds if incremented past array size by repeated calls

step_by_step_execution.description: s->reg is incremented unconditionally, allowing further out-of-bounds access on subsequent calls

state.value: previous value + 1, no wrapping or clamping

commit_message:
Fixed int overflow in PresentationContext_new

Thanks to hac425 CVE-2020-11038
cve_desc: In FreeRDP less than or equal to 2.0.0, an Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow exists. When using /video redirection, a manipulated server can instruct the client to allocate a buffer with a smaller size than requested due to an integer overflow in size calculation. With later messages, the server can manipulate the client to write data out of bound to the previously allocated buffer. This has been patched in 2.1.0.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `s`. The expression `width * height * 4ULL` evaluates `width * height` first using 32-bit unsigned arithmetic (both operands are UINT32), which can silently wrap around to a small value. Only then is the result promoted to 64-bit for the multiplication by 4ULL. This means `s` can be much smaller than the actual required buffer size. The subsequent check against INT32_MAX does not catch the already-truncated value, leading to an undersized allocation in `BufferPool_Take`. When the surface data is later written (e.g., during YUV/H.264 decoding into `ret->surfaceData`), a heap buffer overflow occurs.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with, e.g., width = 0x10000 (65536) and height = 0x10000 (65536). The true required size is 65536 * 65536 * 4 = 0x40000000000 bytes.
The expression `width * height` is evaluated. Both operands are UINT32, so the multiplication is performed in 32-bit arithmetic: 0x10000 * 0x10000 = 0x100000000 which wraps to 0 in UINT32.
The intermediate result 0 is then promoted to unsigned long long and multiplied by 4ULL, giving s = 0.
The check `s > INT32_MAX` evaluates to false (0 <= INT32_MAX), so execution continues.
BufferPool_Take allocates a buffer of size 0 (or a minimal allocation) for surfaceData.
Later, H.264/YUV decoding writes width*height*4 bytes (the true amount) into ret->surfaceData, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0240: crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file

Problem:    Crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file.
Solution:   Check for an empty word tree.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0240.

vulnerabilities.description: When traversing the trie, `depth` is incremented without any bounds check against MAXWLEN. If the trie contains a path longer than MAXWLEN levels, `depth` will exceed the array bounds of `arridx`, `curi`, `tword`, and `wordcount` (all sized MAXWLEN), causing stack-based buffer overflows.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function begins, local stack arrays are declared with size MAXWLEN.
The while loop iterates, and for each non-NUL byte in the trie, the else branch is taken which increments depth and writes to the arrays at the new depth index.

state.value: incremented by 1 each level deeper in the trie

step_by_step_execution.description: If the input trie (slang->sl_fbyts / sl_fidxs) contains a chain of non-NUL bytes longer than MAXWLEN-1, depth reaches MAXWLEN-1 and the code executes tword[depth++] = c, writing at index MAXWLEN-1 (valid) but then incrementing depth to MAXWLEN.
Immediately after, arridx[depth], curi[depth], and wordcount[depth] are written with depth == MAXWLEN, which is one past the end of the arrays, causing a stack buffer overflow.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0240: crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file

Problem:    Crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file.
Solution:   Check for an empty word tree.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0240.

vulnerabilities.description: If the trie depth exceeds MAXWLEN - 1, the code increments `depth` past the bounds of the fixed-size arrays `arridx`, `curi`, `tword`, and `wordcount` (all declared as size MAXWLEN). There is no bounds check on `depth` before the increment and subsequent writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function starts, depth is initialized to 0. All stack-allocated arrays are of size MAXWLEN.
The while loop iterates, and for each non-NUL byte (normal character) in the trie, the else branch is taken which increments depth.

state.value: increments by 1 each time a non-NUL byte is encountered at the current trie node

step_by_step_execution.description: After MAXWLEN - 1 iterations of the else branch, depth reaches MAXWLEN - 1. On the next non-NUL byte, `tword[depth++] = c` writes to tword[MAXWLEN-1] (last valid index) and then depth becomes MAXWLEN.

Immediately after the increment, `arridx[depth] = idxs[n]` writes to arridx[MAXWLEN], which is one element past the end of the array. Similarly, `curi[depth] = 1` and `wordcount[depth] = 0` also write out of bounds.
