cve_desc: ntpq in NTP 4.2.x before 4.2.8p4, and 4.3.x before 4.3.77 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via crafted mode 6 response packets.

commit_message: Fixed bug #68665 (Invalid free)
cve_desc: The apprentice_load function in libmagic/apprentice.c in the Fileinfo component in PHP through 5.6.4 attempts to perform a free operation on a stack-based character array, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption or application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via unknown vectors.  NOTE: this is disputed by the vendor because the standard erealloc behavior makes the free operation unreachable

Integer underflow in the cli_scanpe function in pe.c in ClamAV before 0.97.8 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a skewed offset larger than the size of the PE section in a UPX packed executable, which triggers an out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.description: When `len` is 1, the buffer `name` is allocated with 1 byte. The loop executes once (i=0), then i becomes 2. After the loop, `name[i/2]` writes `'\0'` to `name[1]`, which is one byte past the end of the allocated buffer (off-by-one heap buffer overflow).

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `len=1` (a non-zero value, so the NULL check is bypassed). `len` is also not greater than 400, so it stays 1.
cli_malloc(len) allocates a 1-byte buffer.
fread successfully reads 1 byte into the buffer.

state.value: 1-byte buffer with data read from file

step_by_step_execution.description: The for loop starts with i=0. Since 0 < 1 (len), the body executes: name[0/2] = name[0], i.e., name[0] = name[0]. Then i is incremented to 2.
Loop condition: i(2) < len(1) is false, so the loop exits with i=2.
name[i/2] = '\0' writes to name[2/2] = name[1]. The buffer is only 1 byte (valid index 0), so this is a 1-byte heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
'mytx' in bug report #1723194 (http://curl.haxx.se/bug/view.cgi?id=1723194)
pointed out that the warnf() function in the curl tool didn't properly deal
with the cases when excessively long words were used in the string to chop
up.
cve_desc: Curl versions 7.14.1 through 7.61.1 are vulnerable to a heap-based buffer over-read in the tool_msgs.c:voutf() function that may result in information exposure and denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: vsnprintf returns the number of characters that *would* have been written if the buffer were large enough, not the number actually written. If the formatted string exceeds 255 characters, `len` will be larger than the actual content in `print_buffer` (which is truncated to 255 chars). The while loop iterates based on this inflated `len`, causing `ptr` to advance past the end of `print_buffer`, resulting in out-of-bounds reads via `fwrite` and `fputs`.

step_by_step_execution.description: vsnprintf is called with print_buffer of size 256. If the formatted output would be, e.g., 500 characters, vsnprintf writes 255 chars + null terminator into print_buffer but returns 500.
ptr is set to print_buffer and the while loop begins since len (500) > 0.
In the first iteration, since len > WARN_TEXTWIDTH, the code writes WARN_TEXTWIDTH characters and advances ptr by cut+1, decreasing len by cut. This repeats for subsequent iterations.

After ptr has advanced past 255 bytes (the actual content), the loop continues because len is still > 0. ptr now points beyond print_buffer, and subsequent fwrite/fputs calls read out-of-bounds memory from the stack.

state.value: past end of print_buffer (out of bounds)

vulnerabilities.description: va_start is called twice on the same va_list without an intervening va_end, which is undefined behavior per the C standard. This could lead to incorrect argument parsing or crashes depending on the platform.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with variadic arguments. va_start(ap, fmt) is invoked.

state.value: initialized to point to first variadic argument

step_by_step_execution.description: va_start(ap, fmt) is invoked a second time without va_end, which is undefined behavior.

state.value: undefined state — double initialization without cleanup

step_by_step_execution.description: vsnprintf uses the va_list in undefined state, potentially reading incorrect arguments or causing a crash.

commit_message:
netfilter: nf_tables_offload: incorrect flow offload action array size

immediate verdict expression needs to allocate one slot in the flow offload
action array, however, immediate data expression does not need to do so.

fwd and dup expression need to allocate one slot, this is missing.

Add a new offload_action interface to report if this expression needs to
allocate one slot in the flow offload action array.

Fixes: be2861dc36d7 ("netfilter: nft_{fwd,dup}_netdev: add offload support")
Reported-and-tested-by: Nick Gregory <Nick.Gregory@Sophos.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>
cve_desc: net/netfilter/nf_dup_netdev.c in the Linux kernel 5.4 through 5.6.10 allows local users to gain privileges because of a heap out-of-bounds write. This is related to nf_tables_offload.

commit_message:
nfc: st21nfca: Fix potential buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION

It appears that there are some buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION.
This happens because the length parameters that are passed to memcpy
come directly from skb->data and are not guarded in any way.

Signed-off-by: Jordy Zomer <jordy@pwning.systems>
Reviewed-by: Krzysztof Kozlowski <krzysztof.kozlowski@canonical.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: st21nfca_connectivity_event_received in drivers/nfc/st21nfca/se.c in the Linux kernel through 5.16.12 has EVT_TRANSACTION buffer overflows because of untrusted length parameters.

vulnerabilities.description: The aid_len and params_len fields are read directly from attacker-controlled skb data without validation against the allocated buffer size (skb->len - 2). An attacker can set skb->data[1] (aid_len) to a value larger than what was allocated, causing a heap buffer overflow in the first memcpy into transaction->aid. Similarly, params_len is unchecked and can overflow transaction->params. Additionally, the initial length check uses '&&' instead of '||', so if skb->data[0] equals NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG, the length check is bypassed even for very short skbs.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with event == ST21NFCA_EVT_TRANSACTION, and an skb crafted by an attacker with skb->len = NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH + 2 (e.g., 7), skb->data[0] = NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG, and skb->data[1] = 0xFF (255).
The guard check is evaluated: (skb->len < NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH + 2) is false (7 is not < 7), so the entire '&&' condition is false regardless of the tag check. Execution continues past the check.
devm_kzalloc allocates skb->len - 2 = 5 bytes for the transaction structure.
transaction->aid_len is set from skb->data[1] = 255.

memcpy copies 255 bytes from &skb->data[2] into transaction->aid. The destination buffer (transaction) is only 5 bytes. This causes a massive heap buffer overflow, writing up to 255 bytes past the allocated 5-byte buffer.

commit_message: lib: Protect against malicious namespace declarations (CVE-2022-25236)
cve_desc: xmlparse.c in Expat (aka libexpat) before 2.4.5 allows attackers to insert namespace-separator characters into namespace URIs.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read from `directory_table`. The outer `while(bytes < size)` check only guards the start of each iteration, but within the loop body `bytes` is incremented multiple times (for the dir header, dir entry, and name) without re-checking against `size`. Furthermore, the inner `while(dir_count--)` loop can iterate up to SQUASHFS_DIR_COUNT times without ever re-checking `bytes < size`, allowing reads far beyond the valid range of `directory_table`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The outer while loop checks `bytes < size`. Assume `bytes` is just barely less than `size` (e.g., `bytes = size - 1`).

A directory header is read via memcpy from `directory_table + bytes` with sizeof(dirh) bytes. Since bytes = size-1, this already reads beyond the intended directory data region, but there's no bounds check preventing it.

state.value: size - 1 (reading sizeof(dirh) bytes past this point)

step_by_step_execution.description: `bytes` is incremented by `sizeof(dirh)`, now `bytes` is well past `size`.
The inner `while(dir_count--)` loop begins. `dir_count` can be up to SQUASHFS_DIR_COUNT. There is no check of `bytes < size` before each inner iteration.
Inside the inner loop, `memcpy(dire, directory_table + bytes, sizeof(*dire))` reads from `directory_table` at an offset beyond valid data, causing an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: well beyond size, potentially beyond directory_table allocation

step_by_step_execution.description: `dire->size` is now attacker-influenced from the OOB read. Then `memcpy(dire->name, directory_table + bytes, dire->size + 1)` reads up to SQUASHFS_NAME_LEN bytes from an out-of-bounds location.

state.value: arbitrary value from OOB memory (checked < SQUASHFS_NAME_LEN but value itself is from OOB data)

commit_message: [TALOS-CAN-0063] avoid buffer overrun in ntpq
cve_desc: ntpq in NTP 4.2.x before 4.2.8p4, and 4.3.x before 4.3.77 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via crafted mode 6 response packets.

vulnerabilities.description: If `atoascii` fills `bv` to its full capacity (`sizeof(bv)` which is `2 * MAXVALLEN`), the null terminator will be at index `2 * MAXVALLEN - 1`. Then `strlen(bv)` returns `2 * MAXVALLEN - 1`, and the write `bv[len+1] = '\0'` writes at index `2 * MAXVALLEN`, which is one byte past the end of the `bv` buffer. This is a stack-based off-by-one buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function declares a stack buffer `bv` of size `2 * MAXVALLEN`.
`nextvar` returns a name/value pair where the value is long enough that after processing by `atoascii`, the output fills `bv` to capacity.

state.value: a string of sufficient length to fill bv

step_by_step_execution.description: The `fmt` switch processes the value but fails to decode it (e.g., a TS format with an invalid timestamp), setting `output_raw = '?'`.

`atoascii(value, MAXVALLEN, bv, sizeof(bv))` is called. It converts up to MAXVALLEN bytes of `value` into `bv`, potentially filling all `2 * MAXVALLEN` bytes (e.g., if non-printable characters are expanded). The resulting string uses the full buffer, with the null terminator at index `2 * MAXVALLEN - 1`.
Since `output_raw` is '?' (not '*'), the code enters the if-block. `len = strlen(bv)` computes the length.
`bv[len] = output_raw` writes '?' at index `2 * MAXVALLEN - 1` (the last valid index, overwriting the null terminator). This is in-bounds.
`bv[len+1] = '\0'` writes a null byte at index `2 * MAXVALLEN`, which is one byte beyond the allocated buffer size, causing a stack buffer overflow.

commit_message: libarchive: sanitize filenames before extracting
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in File Roller 3.6.x before 3.6.4, 3.8.x before 3.8.3, and 3.9.x before 3.9.3, when libarchive is used, allows remote attackers to create arbitrary files via a crafted archive that is not properly handled in a "Keep directory structure" action, related to fr-archive-libarchive.c and fr-window.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When g_file_query_info returns NULL (info == NULL) and the error is not G_IO_ERROR_NOT_FOUND, the code calls g_object_unref(info) on a NULL pointer, causing a NULL pointer dereference crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: The code enters the skip_older / overwrite check block.
g_file_query_info is called and fails with an error other than G_IO_ERROR_NOT_FOUND (e.g., permission denied). It returns NULL.
The code enters the else branch (info == NULL).
The error does not match G_IO_ERROR_NOT_FOUND, so the inner if-block is entered. The code stores the error and then calls g_object_unref(info) where info is NULL.

code_snippets:
case AE_IFLNK:
	if (! g_file_make_symbolic_link (file, archive_entry_symlink (entry), cancellable, &local_error)) {
vulnerabilities.description: A malicious archive can contain a symlink entry pointing to an arbitrary location (e.g., "/") followed by a regular file entry whose path traverses the symlink. The symlink is created first within the destination directory, and then a subsequent regular file entry is written through that symlink, allowing writing to arbitrary locations outside the intended destination (Zip Slip via symlink).

code_snippets: ostream = (GOutputStream *) g_file_replace (file, NULL, FALSE, G_FILE_CREATE_REPLACE_DESTINATION, cancellable, &load_data->error);

step_by_step_execution.description: Archive iteration encounters a symlink entry (AE_IFLNK) with pathname e.g. 'evil_link' and symlink target e.g. '/etc'.
file is resolved as a child of extract_data->destination, e.g. /tmp/dest/evil_link.
g_file_make_symbolic_link creates the symlink /tmp/dest/evil_link -> /etc. No validation of the symlink target is performed.
In the next loop iteration, a regular file entry (AE_IFREG) with pathname e.g. 'evil_link/passwd' is encountered.
file is resolved as g_file_get_child(destination, 'evil_link/passwd'), which resolves through the symlink to /etc/passwd.
g_file_replace opens /etc/passwd for writing and archive data is written to it, overwriting a file outside the destination.

commit_message:
'mytx' in bug report #1723194 (http://curl.haxx.se/bug/view.cgi?id=1723194)
pointed out that the warnf() function in the curl tool didn't properly deal
with the cases when excessively long words were used in the string to chop
up.
cve_desc: Curl versions 7.14.1 through 7.61.1 are vulnerable to a heap-based buffer over-read in the tool_msgs.c:voutf() function that may result in information exposure and denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: vsnprintf returns the number of characters that *would* have been written if the buffer were large enough (excluding null terminator). If the formatted string exceeds 255 characters, `len` will be larger than the actual content in `print_buffer` (which is truncated to 255 chars + null). The while loop uses `len` to control iteration, so when `len` > 255, the code will eventually read past the end of the stack buffer `print_buffer` via `fputs(ptr, ...)` or `fwrite(ptr, ...)`, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: A format string that expands to more than 255 characters is passed to warnf.
vsnprintf writes at most 255 chars + null into print_buffer, but returns the full would-be length.

state.value: 255 chars of content + null terminator (256 bytes total)

step_by_step_execution.description: ptr is set to print_buffer and the while loop begins with len=500.

In early iterations, legitimate content is written. ptr advances and len decreases. After processing ~255 characters worth of content, ptr points near the end of the valid string in print_buffer but len is still > 0 (around 245 remaining).

state.value: past the null terminator or near end of print_buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: fputs or fwrite reads from ptr which is now beyond the valid content of print_buffer, reading stack memory out-of-bounds. This leaks stack data to config->errors.

vulnerabilities.description: When the word-wrap search loop finds no space (cut reaches 0 and ptr[0] is not a space), cut=0. Then `len -= cut` means `len -= 0`, so len does not decrease. However, `ptr += cut+1` advances ptr by 1. This means len never decreases while ptr keeps advancing, causing the loop to read far beyond the buffer until len finally underflows or other memory corruption occurs.

step_by_step_execution.description: A formatted string with a long run of non-space characters (longer than WARN_TEXTWIDTH) is passed.

state.value: points to a segment with no spaces

step_by_step_execution.description: The word-wrap search loop decrements cut to 0 without finding a space.
fwrite writes 1 byte, ptr advances by 1, but len -= 0 keeps len unchanged.
On next iteration, the same thing happens: cut goes to 0, ptr advances by 1, len stays the same. This repeats indefinitely, causing ptr to read well past the end of print_buffer.

commit_message: libarchive: sanitize filenames before extracting
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in File Roller 3.6.x before 3.6.4, 3.8.x before 3.8.3, and 3.9.x before 3.9.3, when libarchive is used, allows remote attackers to create arbitrary files via a crafted archive that is not properly handled in a "Keep directory structure" action, related to fr-archive-libarchive.c and fr-window.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When `g_file_query_info` returns NULL (meaning `info` is NULL) and the error is not G_IO_ERROR_NOT_FOUND, the code calls `g_object_unref(info)` on a NULL pointer, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop processes an archive entry whose pathname is requested for extraction.

state.value: a valid pathname requested for extraction

step_by_step_execution.description: The code enters the skip_older/overwrite check block because one of extract_data->skip_older or !extract_data->overwrite is true.
g_file_query_info is called and fails with an error other than G_IO_ERROR_NOT_FOUND (e.g. permission denied).
The code enters the else branch (info == NULL), checks the error type, and since it doesn't match NOT_FOUND, enters the inner if block.
g_object_unref(info) is called with info being NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.description: A malicious archive can contain a symlink entry pointing to an arbitrary directory (e.g., /etc) followed by a regular file entry whose path traverses through that symlink. The symlink target from `archive_entry_symlink(entry)` is used without validation, enabling an attacker to write files to arbitrary locations outside the extraction destination (directory traversal via symlink).

code_snippets: ostream = (GOutputStream *) g_file_replace (file, NULL, FALSE, G_FILE_CREATE_REPLACE_DESTINATION, cancellable, &load_data->error);

step_by_step_execution.description: Archive contains a symlink entry, e.g. pathname='evil_link' with symlink target '/etc'.
_g_path_get_relative_basename_safe passes because 'evil_link' is a simple relative name. file is set to destination/evil_link.
g_file_make_symbolic_link creates a symlink at destination/evil_link pointing to /etc.
In the next iteration, archive contains a regular file entry with pathname='evil_link/shadow'.
_g_path_get_relative_basename_safe passes because the relative path 'evil_link/shadow' looks safe. file is set to destination/evil_link/shadow.
Since destination/evil_link is a symlink to /etc, g_file_replace resolves destination/evil_link/shadow to /etc/shadow, writing arbitrary content to a sensitive file.

commit_message:
server: reset meta context replies on starttls

Related to CVE-2021-3716, but not as severe.  No compliant client will
send NBD_CMD_BLOCK_STATUS unless it first negotiates
NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT.  If an attacker injects a premature
SET_META_CONTEXT, either the client will never notice (because it
never uses BLOCK_STATUS), or the client will overwrite the attacker's
attempt with the client's own SET_META_CONTEXT request after
encryption is enabled.  So I don't class this as having the potential
to trigger denial-of-service due to any protocol mismatch between
compliant client and server (I don't care what happens with
non-compliant clients).

Fixes: 26455d45 (server: protocol: Implement Block Status "base:allocation".)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in nbdkit due to to improperly caching plaintext state across the STARTTLS encryption boundary. A MitM attacker could use this flaw to inject a plaintext NBD_OPT_STRUCTURED_REPLY before proxying everything else a client sends to the server, potentially leading the client to terminate the NBD session. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

commit_message:
glob: fix heap buffer overflow

* lib/glob.c (glob): Fix off-by-one error introduced into
glibc in commit dd7d45e838a42b0ed470c44b55901ea98d0c2bab
dated 1997-10-29 20:33:40.  Problem reported by Tim Rühsen in:
https://sourceware.org/bugzilla/show_bug.cgi?id=22320
Fix suggested by Bruno Haible.
cve_desc: The GNU C Library (aka glibc or libc6) before 2.27 contains an off-by-one error leading to a heap-based buffer overflow in the glob function in glob.c, related to the processing of home directories using the ~ operator followed by a long string.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one heap buffer overflow when extracting a username from a tilde expression like "~username/path". The buffer is allocated as `end_name - dirname` bytes, but `mempcpy` copies `end_name - dirname` bytes starting from `dirname + 1` (filling the buffer completely), and then a null terminator is written one byte past the end of the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a pattern like "~username/file" and flags including GLOB_TILDE. The pattern contains a '/' so `filename` is set to the '/' in the pattern, and `dirname` is constructed as "~username".
The tilde expansion branch is entered because dirname[0] == '~'. Since dirname[1] != '\0' and dirname[1] != '/', the else branch (user-specific home directory lookup) is taken.

`end_name = strchr(dirname, '/')` is called. Since dirname is "~username" (no slash), end_name is NULL initially. But wait—dirname was copied from pattern up to filename pointer. Let me reconsider: dirname = "~username" with no slash, so end_name = NULL. In that case user_name = dirname + 1 and no allocation happens. Let me reconsider the scenario where the dirname DOES contain a slash.

state.value: NULL when dirname has no slash

step_by_step_execution.description: Actually, re-examining: dirname is set by copying `dirlen` bytes from pattern. If pattern is "~user/sub/file", then filename points to last '/', dirname becomes "~user/sub", and end_name = strchr(dirname, '/') points to the first '/' in dirname.
`end_name = strchr(dirname, '/')` finds the '/' after "~user", so end_name points to the '/' at position 5 in dirname. `end_name - dirname` = 5.
unescape is NULL (no backslashes in dirname before end_name, assuming GLOB_NOESCAPE or no backslashes). The buffer `newp` is allocated with `malloc(end_name - dirname)` = `malloc(5)`.

`mempcpy(newp, dirname + 1, end_name - dirname)` copies 5 bytes from dirname+1 ("user/") into newp. This fills all 5 bytes of the buffer. mempcpy returns a pointer to newp+5, which is one byte past the end of the allocation.
The null terminator is written: `*(char*)mempcpy(...) = '\0'` writes to newp[5], which is one byte beyond the 5-byte allocation. This is a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the realloc size calculation. The total dir->dir_count across all directory headers is unbounded (only per-header count is checked against SQUASHFS_DIR_COUNT). A crafted filesystem image with many directory headers can cause dir->dir_count to grow very large, making `(dir->dir_count + DIR_ENT_SIZE) * sizeof(struct dir_ent)` overflow to a small value. The subsequent realloc allocates a small buffer, but writes to `dir->dirs[dir->dir_count]` access memory far beyond the allocation, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads an inode and allocates a dir struct, initializing dir->dir_count to 0.

size is computed from attacker-controlled inode data: `size = (*i)->data + bytes - 3`. A crafted filesystem can set (*i)->data to a very large value, making the outer while loop iterate many times over many directory headers.
The outer while loop reads directory headers. Each header's dir_count is checked against SQUASHFS_DIR_COUNT, but the total accumulated dir->dir_count across all headers has no upper bound.

state.value: accumulates across many headers, can approach SIZE_MAX / UINT_MAX

step_by_step_execution.description: When dir->dir_count is large enough, the expression `(dir->dir_count + DIR_ENT_SIZE) * sizeof(struct dir_ent)` overflows, wrapping around to a small value. realloc allocates a small buffer.

state.value: small value due to integer overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: The code writes to dir->dirs[dir->dir_count] via strcpy and direct assignments, writing far out of bounds of the undersized heap allocation.

commit_message:
nfc: st21nfca: Fix potential buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION

It appears that there are some buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION.
This happens because the length parameters that are passed to memcpy
come directly from skb->data and are not guarded in any way.

Signed-off-by: Jordy Zomer <jordy@pwning.systems>
Reviewed-by: Krzysztof Kozlowski <krzysztof.kozlowski@canonical.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: st21nfca_connectivity_event_received in drivers/nfc/st21nfca/se.c in the Linux kernel through 5.16.12 has EVT_TRANSACTION buffer overflows because of untrusted length parameters.

vulnerabilities.description: The length validation uses logical AND (&&) instead of logical OR (||). This means if the skb is very short (e.g., 1 byte) but skb->data[0] happens to equal NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG, the check passes. Subsequent accesses to skb->data[1], skb->data[2], skb->data[aid_len+2], skb->data[aid_len+3], etc. will read out of bounds of the skb buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with event == ST21NFCA_EVT_TRANSACTION and an skb with skb->len == 2 and skb->data[0] == NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG (0x81).

The guard condition is evaluated: skb->len (2) < NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH + 2 (7) is TRUE, but skb->data[0] != NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG is FALSE. Since TRUE && FALSE == FALSE, the early return is NOT taken.
devm_kzalloc allocates a transaction structure of size skb->len - 2 = 0 bytes (or the minimum allocation). Allocation succeeds.
transaction->aid_len = skb->data[1] is read. skb->data[1] is within the 2-byte buffer, so aid_len gets a value. Suppose skb->data[1] = 5.
The aid_len check (aid_len > sizeof(transaction->aid)) passes since 5 <= 16 (typical sizeof aid).
memcpy(transaction->aid, &skb->data[2], 5) reads 5 bytes starting at skb->data[2], but skb->len is only 2. This is a heap buffer over-read.

vulnerabilities.description: Even when skb->len passes the initial (flawed) check, there is no validation that skb->len is large enough to cover accesses at offsets aid_len+2 and aid_len+3. An attacker can provide an skb where aid_len is valid but the buffer doesn't extend to aid_len+4, causing out-of-bounds reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function called with event == ST21NFCA_EVT_TRANSACTION, skb->len == 8, skb->data[0] == 0x81, skb->data[1] == 5 (aid_len).
Initial check: skb->len (8) < NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH + 2 (7) is FALSE, so the overall && condition is FALSE and we proceed (correct path).
transaction allocated, transaction->aid_len = 5. The sizeof check passes (5 <= 16). memcpy copies 5 bytes from skb->data[2..6] — within bounds (skb->len=8).
Access skb->data[transaction->aid_len + 2] = skb->data[7] — within bounds (index 7, len 8). But skb->data[transaction->aid_len + 3] = skb->data[8] — out of bounds (index 8, len 8).

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.description: The inner `while(dir_count--)` loop reads from `directory_table + bytes` without re-checking that `bytes` is still within the valid range (`bytes < size`) or within the bounds of the `directory_table` buffer. The outer `while(bytes < size)` check only guards the beginning of each directory header iteration, but the inner loop can advance `bytes` well past `size` (and potentially past the end of `directory_table`) when processing multiple directory entries from a corrupted/malicious filesystem image. This leads to out-of-bounds heap reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function starts, reads an inode and allocates the `dir` structure. Assume a crafted filesystem where `(*i)->data` (directory size) is small but the directory header's `dirh.count` is large.
`bytes` is computed from `lookup_entry` plus `(*i)->offset`, and `size` is `(*i)->data + bytes`. The outer while loop begins with `bytes < size` being true.

A directory header is read from `directory_table + bytes`. `dir_count` is set to `dirh.count + 1`. With a crafted filesystem, `dirh.count` could be up to SQUASHFS_DIR_COUNT (e.g. 256), so `dir_count` could be large. The check `dir_count > SQUASHFS_DIR_COUNT` passes.

The inner `while(dir_count--)` loop begins. On each iteration it does `memcpy(dire, directory_table + bytes, sizeof(*dire))` and then `memcpy(dire->name, directory_table + bytes, dire->size + 1)`, advancing `bytes` each time. There is NO check that `bytes < size` inside this inner loop.

state.value: grows with each iteration, quickly exceeding `size` (120)

step_by_step_execution.description: After a few iterations, `bytes` exceeds `size` and even potentially exceeds the allocated size of `directory_table`. The memcpy calls read from `directory_table + bytes` which is now out of bounds, causing a heap buffer over-read.

state.value: well beyond `size` and potentially beyond `directory_table` allocation

commit_message: commit bash-20190628 snapshot
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in disable_priv_mode in shell.c in GNU Bash through 5.0 patch 11. By default, if Bash is run with its effective UID not equal to its real UID, it will drop privileges by setting its effective UID to its real UID. However, it does so incorrectly. On Linux and other systems that support "saved UID" functionality, the saved UID is not dropped. An attacker with command execution in the shell can use "enable -f" for runtime loading of a new builtin, which can be a shared object that calls setuid() and therefore regains privileges. However, binaries running with an effective UID of 0 are unaffected.

commit_message:
aaef on arm/thumb switches causes uaf ##crash

* Reported by peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: poc_uaf_r_reg_get
cve_desc: Use After Free in r_reg_get_name_idx in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

vulnerabilities.description: Multiple early return paths after `buf` (and later `spname`) have been allocated lead to memory leaks. The `buf` allocated via `malloc` is never freed on at least three early-return paths, and `spname` allocated via `strdup` is never freed on the `pcname` check return path.

code_snippets: return;  /* after 'Cannot find program counter register' */

step_by_step_execution.description: buf is allocated with malloc((size_t)iend + 2)
r_io_read_at reads data into buf
ESIL is checked; if NULL, aei command is run and ESIL re-checked. If still NULL, function returns without freeing buf.
Alternatively, if ESIL is valid, kspname is checked. If empty, function returns without freeing buf.
Alternatively, after spname=strdup(kspname), kpcname is checked. If NULL or empty, function returns without freeing buf or spname.

commit_message:
Do not call http_hdr_flags() on pseudo-headers

In http_EstimateWS(), all headers are passed to the http_isfiltered()
function to calculate how many bytes is needed to serialize the entire
struct http. http_isfiltered() will check the headers for whether they are
going to be filtered out later and if so skip them.

However http_isfiltered() would attempt to treat all elements of struct
http as regular headers with an implicit structure. That does not hold for
the first three pseudo-header entries, which would lead to asserts in
later steps.

This patch skips the filter step for pseudo-headers.

Fixes: #3830
cve_desc: In Varnish Cache 7.0.0, 7.0.1, 7.0.2, and 7.1.0, it is possible to cause the Varnish Server to assert and automatically restart through forged HTTP/1 backend responses. An attack uses a crafted reason phrase of the backend response status line. This is fixed in 7.0.3 and 7.1.1.

commit_message:
Fix XkbSelectEvents() integer underflow

CVE-2020-14361 ZDI-CAN 11573

This vulnerability was discovered by:
Jan-Niklas Sohn working with Trend Micro Zero Day Initiative

Signed-off-by: Matthieu Herrb <matthieu@herrb.eu>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in X.Org Server before xorg-x11-server 1.20.9. An Integer underflow leading to heap-buffer overflow may lead to a privilege escalation vulnerability. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to data confidentiality and integrity as well as system availability.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow in dataLeft when processing events with size=1 (XkbBellNotify, XkbActionMessage, XkbCompatMapNotify). The length check uses the original size (1), verifying dataLeft >= 2. However, the else branch then sets size=2, causing the pointer to advance by 4 and dataLeft to be decremented by 4. If dataLeft is 2 or 3, the unsigned subtraction wraps around to a huge value, allowing subsequent loop iterations to read/write out-of-bounds memory (swapping bytes beyond the request buffer).

step_by_step_execution.description: Client sends a request with affectWhich having both XkbBellNotify and XkbControlsNotify bits set (neither in selectAll nor clear), and extra data of exactly 2 bytes after the fixed header.

state.value: points to start of extra data after xkbSelectEventsReq

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop iterates. XkbBellNotify (ndx=8) is encountered first (assuming bit ordering). size is set to 1.
Length check: dataLeft < (size * 2) → 2 < 2 is false, so check passes.
Since size is not 2 or 4, the else branch executes, setting size = 2. No byte swapping occurs.
Pointer advances by size*2 = 4 bytes. dataLeft -= 4. Since dataLeft is unsigned and was 2, this wraps around.

state.value: advanced 4 bytes (2 bytes past allocated data)

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop continues to XkbControlsNotify (ndx=3). size is set to 4. The check dataLeft < 8 is false (dataLeft is ~4 billion). The code then calls swapl on from.c32[0] and from.c32[1], which are out-of-bounds of the original request buffer.

cve_desc: A use-after-free exists in the Linux Kernel in tc_new_tfilter that could allow a local attacker to gain privilege escalation. The exploit requires unprivileged user namespaces. We recommend upgrading past commit 04c2a47ffb13c29778e2a14e414ad4cb5a5db4b5

commit_message:
net: sched: fix use-after-free in tc_new_tfilter()

Whenever tc_new_tfilter() jumps back to replay: label,
we need to make sure @q and @chain local variables are cleared again,
or risk use-after-free as in [1]

For consistency, apply the same fix in tc_ctl_chain()

commit_message:
Do not call http_hdr_flags() on pseudo-headers

In http_EstimateWS(), all headers are passed to the http_isfiltered()
function to calculate how many bytes is needed to serialize the entire
struct http. http_isfiltered() will check the headers for whether they are
going to be filtered out later and if so skip them.

However http_isfiltered() would attempt to treat all elements of struct
http as regular headers with an implicit structure. That does not hold for
the first three pseudo-header entries, which would lead to asserts in
later steps.

This patch skips the filter step for pseudo-headers.

Fixes: #3830
cve_desc: In Varnish Cache 7.0.0, 7.0.1, 7.0.2, and 7.1.0, it is possible to cause the Varnish Server to assert and automatically restart through forged HTTP/1 backend responses. An attack uses a crafted reason phrase of the backend response status line. This is fixed in 7.0.3 and 7.1.1.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.description: The inner while(dir_count--) loop reads directory entries from directory_table without re-checking that 'bytes' remains within the valid range (bytes < size). A corrupted filesystem image can provide a large dir_count in the directory header, causing the inner loop to read from directory_table at offsets beyond the valid data region, resulting in out-of-bounds heap reads. The outer loop only checks 'bytes < size' at its top, but once the inner loop is entered, it iterates dir_count times without any bounds validation on 'bytes'.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads an inode and computes start/bytes/size for directory table traversal.
The outer while loop checks bytes < size, which passes on the first iteration.
A directory header is read via memcpy from directory_table + bytes, and dir_count is set to dirh.count + 1. A corrupted filesystem can set dirh.count up to SQUASHFS_DIR_COUNT - 1 (checked below).
The inner while(dir_count--) loop begins iterating. There is no check that 'bytes' is still less than 'size' or within the bounds of the directory_table allocation before each memcpy.

state.value: Decremented each iteration, can still be large
Incremented by sizeof(*dire) + dire->size + 1 each iteration, can exceed size

step_by_step_execution.description: On a subsequent inner-loop iteration, bytes may have grown past size and past the allocated length of directory_table. The memcpy reads sizeof(sdire) or sizeof(*dire) bytes from directory_table + bytes, causing an out-of-bounds heap read.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4074: going over the end of NameBuff

Problem:    Going over the end of NameBuff.
Solution:   Check length when appending a space.
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Heap-based Buffer Overflow

vulnerabilities.description: When the translated buffer name stored in NameBuff is close to MAXPATHL in length, the unconditional write `*(p + len++) = ' '` can write past the end of the NameBuff buffer without any bounds check. Additionally, the null terminator is not placed after this write, so subsequent STRLEN calls may read out of bounds. The vim_snprintf calls that follow use `MAXPATHL - len` as the size argument, which can become zero or wrap to a large value if len >= MAXPATHL, failing to null-terminate the string.

step_by_step_execution.description: get_trans_bufname fills NameBuff with the buffer name. NameBuff has a size of MAXPATHL+1 bytes.

state.value: NameBuff (points to global buffer of MAXPATHL+1 bytes)

step_by_step_execution.description: len is computed as STRLEN(p). If the buffer name is very long, len can be MAXPATHL-1 or MAXPATHL.
The condition `bt_help(...) || wp->w_p_pvw || bufIsChanged(...) || b_p_ro` is checked. If any is true (e.g. buffer is changed), execution enters the block.

*(p + len++) = ' ' writes a space character at offset len in the buffer without checking if len < MAXPATHL. If len was MAXPATHL-1, this overwrites the null terminator; if len was MAXPATHL, this writes one byte past the allocated buffer. len is incremented.

state.value: ' ' (space written at or beyond buffer boundary)

step_by_step_execution.description: The string is no longer null-terminated within the buffer bounds. Then vim_snprintf is called with size MAXPATHL - len, which is 0 or negative (wrapping to huge size_t). In the case of 0, no null terminator is written.
STRLEN(p + len) is called on the non-null-terminated buffer, reading out of bounds until a zero byte is found in memory.

state.value: points at or past end of NameBuff, no guaranteed null terminator

commit_message: Bug 705156(1): Avoid double free of PCL error buffer
cve_desc: A vulnerability classified as problematic was found in GhostPCL 9.55.0. This vulnerability affects the function chunk_free_object of the file gsmchunk.c. The manipulation with a malicious file leads to a memory corruption. The attack can be initiated remotely but requires user interaction. The exploit has been disclosed to the public as a POC and may be used. It is recommended to apply the patches to fix this issue.

commit_message: Avoid maxrlvls more than upper bound to cause heap-buffer-overflow
cve_desc: There's a flaw in jasper's jpc encoder in versions prior to 2.0.23. Crafted input provided to jasper by an attacker could cause an arbitrary out-of-bounds write. This could potentially affect data confidentiality, integrity, or application availability.

vulnerabilities.description: The user-controlled `maxrlvls` value from option parsing is used directly as a loop bound to write into fixed-size arrays `tccp->prcwidthexpns` and `tccp->prcheightexpns` before any bounds validation occurs. The validation check (`if (cp->tccp.maxrlvls < 1)`) happens much later in the function, after the out-of-bounds write has already occurred.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function allocates and initializes the `cp` structure. `tccp->maxrlvls` is set to the default value of 6.
The option string is parsed. When OPT_MAXRLVLS is encountered, `tccp->maxrlvls` is set from user input via `atoi()`. An attacker can provide a very large value (e.g., 10000).

After option parsing completes, the loop `for (rlvlno = 0; rlvlno < tccp->maxrlvls; ++rlvlno)` executes, writing `prcwidthexpn` and `prcheightexpn` into `tccp->prcwidthexpns[rlvlno]` and `tccp->prcheightexpns[rlvlno]` for each iteration.

The arrays `prcwidthexpns` and `prcheightexpns` are fixed-size arrays within the `jpc_enc_tccp_t` structure. Writing at indices far beyond their allocated size corrupts adjacent memory (heap buffer overflow).
The bounds check `if (cp->tccp.maxrlvls < 1)` occurs much later in the function, after the overflow has already happened.

commit_message: Fix: index entry label not being zero-terminated with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11.

vulnerabilities.description: The `control_bytes` pointer is initialized to a position within the buffer and is subsequently incremented (via `control_bytes++`) every time a tag with `control_byte == 1` is encountered. There is no bounds check ensuring that `control_bytes` remains within the valid buffer region. If the parsed data (tagx) specifies multiple tags with `control_byte == 1`, the pointer can advance past the end of the buffer data, and a subsequent read of `control_bytes[0]` results in a heap-based out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a valid `indx`, `tagx` with a large `tags_count` containing multiple tags where `control_byte == 1`, and a buffer `buf`.

state.value: e.g. 10, with many tags having control_byte == 1
e.g. 1 (only 1 actual control byte in the buffer)

step_by_step_execution.description: The buffer position is set and entry validation passes. `buf->maxlen` is restricted to the entry boundary.
Label is read and stored. Then `control_bytes` is set to the current buffer position.

state.value: buf->data + buf->offset (points to 1 control byte in the buffer)

step_by_step_execution.description: `mobi_buffer_seek(buf, tagx->control_byte_count)` advances `buf->offset` past the control bytes.
The while loop iterates over `tagx->tags`. On the first tag with `control_byte == 1`, `control_bytes` is incremented.

state.value: buf->data + buf->offset (original) + 1, now past the single valid control byte

step_by_step_execution.description: On a subsequent tag (with `control_byte != 1`), `control_bytes[0]` is read. Since `control_bytes` has been incremented beyond the valid control byte region, this reads out-of-bounds memory from the heap.

state.value: reads memory beyond the allocated/valid buffer region

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the realloc size calculation. The expression `(dir->dir_count + DIR_ENT_SIZE) * sizeof(struct dir_ent)` can overflow on 32-bit systems when `dir->dir_count` becomes very large. Since `size` is derived from attacker-controlled filesystem data (`(*i)->data`), the outer while loop can iterate many times, growing `dir->dir_count` to a value where the multiplication wraps around to a small number. This causes realloc to allocate a small buffer, after which subsequent writes via `strcpy` and field assignments write out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The inode is read and `(*i)->data` is set from the (attacker-controlled) filesystem image. `size` is computed as `(*i)->data + bytes - 3`.

state.value: Attacker-controlled, very large value (e.g., close to INT_MAX)
Very large int value, causing the while loop to iterate many times

step_by_step_execution.description: The outer `while(bytes < size)` loop begins iterating over directory headers. Each header can contain up to SQUASHFS_DIR_COUNT entries.

After processing many directory entries across many loop iterations, `dir->dir_count` grows very large. When `(dir->dir_count % DIR_ENT_SIZE) == 0`, realloc is called with `(dir->dir_count + DIR_ENT_SIZE) * sizeof(struct dir_ent)`.

state.value: Large value approaching SIZE_MAX / sizeof(struct dir_ent) on 32-bit systems
Overflows to a small value due to integer wraparound

step_by_step_execution.description: realloc returns a small heap buffer. Subsequent writes via `strcpy(dir->dirs[dir->dir_count].name, dire->name)` and other field assignments write far beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: Points to a small heap allocation (due to integer overflow in realloc size)
Large value, so dir->dirs[dir->dir_count] accesses memory well past the allocation

commit_message:
sungem: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Mark Cave-Ayland <mark.cave-ayland@ilande.co.uk>
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Alistair Francis <alistair.francis@wdc.com>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

commit_message:
jp2_dec: fix `numchans` mixup

When iterating over `dec->cdef->data.cdef.ents`, we need to use its
`numchans` variable, not the one in `jp2_dec_t`.

Fixes CVE-2018-19543
Fixes CVE-2017-9782

Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/13
Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/18
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/140
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/182
cve_desc: JasPer 2.0.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer over-read and application crash) via a crafted image, related to the jp2_decode function in libjasper/jp2/jp2_dec.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When a CMAP box is present but no CDEF box is present, `dec->cdef` is NULL. The code unconditionally dereferences `dec->cdef` to assign `cdefd`, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The decoder `dec` is created and its fields are initialized (all box pointers default to 0/NULL).
During box parsing, a CMAP box is encountered and stored in dec->cmap, but no CDEF box is encountered, so dec->cdef remains NULL.
The code reaches the `if (!dec->cmap)` branch and takes the else branch since dec->cmap is non-NULL.
Inside the else branch, `cdefd = &dec->cdef->data.cdef;` is executed, dereferencing the NULL pointer dec->cdef.

vulnerabilities.description: The return value of `jas_alloc2` for `lutents` is not checked for NULL. If the allocation fails, the subsequent loop dereferences a NULL pointer when writing to `lutents[i]`.

step_by_step_execution.description: A CMAP box is present and a CMAP entry has map type JP2_CMAP_PALETTE, so the palette mapping branch is entered.
jas_alloc2 is called to allocate memory for lutents. Under memory pressure or with a large numlutents value, this returns NULL.
The for loop iterates and writes to lutents[0], dereferencing the NULL pointer.

commit_message:
bpf: Fix integer overflow in prealloc_elems_and_freelist()

In prealloc_elems_and_freelist(), the multiplication to calculate the
size passed to bpf_map_area_alloc() could lead to an integer overflow.
As a result, out-of-bounds write could occur in pcpu_freelist_populate()
as reported by KASAN:

[...]
[   16.968613] BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.969408] Write of size 8 at addr ffff888104fc6ea0 by task crash/78
[   16.970038]
[   16.970195] CPU: 0 PID: 78 Comm: crash Not tainted 5.15.0-rc2+ #1
[   16.970878] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS 1.13.0-1ubuntu1.1 04/01/2014
[   16.972026] Call Trace:
[   16.972306]  dump_stack_lvl+0x34/0x44
[   16.972687]  print_address_description.constprop.0+0x21/0x140
[   16.973297]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.973777]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.974257]  kasan_report.cold+0x7f/0x11b
[   16.974681]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975190]  pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975669]  stack_map_alloc+0x209/0x2a0
[   16.976106]  __sys_bpf+0xd83/0x2ce0
[...]

The possibility of this overflow was originally discussed in [0], but
was overlooked.

Fix the integer overflow by changing elem_size to u64 from u32.

  [0] https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/728b238e-a481-eb50-98e9-b0f430ab01e7@gmail.com/

Fixes: 557c0c6e7df8 ("bpf: convert stackmap to pre-allocation")
Signed-off-by: Tatsuhiko Yasumatsu <th.yasumatsu@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/20210930135545.173698-1-th.yasumatsu@gmail.com
cve_desc: prealloc_elems_and_freelist in kernel/bpf/stackmap.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.12 allows unprivileged users to trigger an eBPF multiplication integer overflow with a resultant out-of-bounds write.

vulnerabilities.description: The multiplication `elem_size * smap->map.max_entries` can overflow. `elem_size` is u64 and `max_entries` is u32, so the multiplication is done in u64. However, if `value_size` and `max_entries` are large enough, the product can wrap around, causing a smaller-than-expected allocation. Subsequent use via `pcpu_freelist_populate` would then access memory beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: elem_size is computed as sizeof(struct stack_map_bucket) + (u64)smap->map.value_size. With a large value_size (e.g., close to U32_MAX), elem_size can be very large.

The product elem_size * smap->map.max_entries is computed in u64 arithmetic. If both are large enough (e.g., elem_size ~ 2^32 and max_entries ~ 2^32), the result wraps around modulo 2^64, yielding a small value.

state.value: Wraps around to a small value due to u64 overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: bpf_map_area_alloc allocates a small buffer based on the overflowed size.

state.value: Points to a small heap allocation

step_by_step_execution.description: pcpu_freelist_populate iterates max_entries times, stepping by elem_size bytes through smap->elems, writing well beyond the allocated buffer.

state.value: Out-of-bounds writes past the small allocation

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read from directory_table. The outer loop checks 'bytes < size', but the inner loop iterates 'dir_count' times without re-checking 'bytes' against 'size' or the actual allocated size of 'directory_table'. A corrupted filesystem image with a 'dirh.count' larger than the remaining data can cause memcpy to read beyond the bounds of 'directory_table'. Additionally, 'size' itself is derived from inode data ((*i)->data) which can be corrupted, allowing it to exceed the actual allocated size of directory_table.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads an inode and computes the starting byte offset into directory_table.
'size' is computed from inode data, which can be attacker-controlled in a corrupted filesystem image.

The outer while loop checks 'bytes < size' and reads a directory header, incrementing bytes by sizeof(dirh). dir_count is set from dirh.count + 1, which can be up to SQUASHFS_DIR_COUNT (an attacker-controlled value from the filesystem image).

The inner while loop iterates dir_count times. Each iteration reads from directory_table + bytes via memcpy without checking if bytes still falls within the allocated size of directory_table or within 'size'. If dir_count claims more entries than actually exist, bytes grows past valid data.

state.value: continuously incrementing by sizeof(*dire) + dire->size + 1 per iteration, potentially exceeding directory_table bounds

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy reads sizeof(sdire) or sizeof(*dire) bytes from directory_table + bytes, which may now be out of bounds of the directory_table allocation, causing a heap buffer over-read.

commit_message:
nfc: nfcmrvl: main: reorder destructive operations in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev to avoid bugs

There are destructive operations such as nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort and
gpio_free in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev. The resources such as firmware,
gpio and so on could be destructed while the upper layer functions such as
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start and nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame is executing, which leads
to double-free, use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs.

There are three situations that could lead to double-free bugs.

The first situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 release_firmware()           |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  kfree(fw) //(1)             |    fw_dnld_over
                              |     release_firmware
  ...                         |      kfree(fw) //(2)
                              |     ...

The second situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |
 mod_timer                    |
 (wait a time)                |
 fw_dnld_timeout              |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
   fw_dnld_over               |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
    release_firmware          |    fw_dnld_over
     kfree(fw) //(1)          |     release_firmware
     ...                      |      kfree(fw) //(2)

The third situation is shown below:

       (Thread 1)               |       (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame          |
 if(..->fw_download_in_progress)|
  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_recv_frame    |
   queue_work                   |
                                |
fw_dnld_rx_work                 | nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 fw_dnld_over                   |  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  release_firmware              |   fw_dnld_over
   kfree(fw) //(1)              |    release_firmware
                                |     kfree(fw) //(2)

The firmware struct is deallocated in position (1) and deallocated
in position (2) again.

The crash trace triggered by POC is like below:

BUG: KASAN: double-free or invalid-free in fw_dnld_over
Call Trace:
  kfree
  fw_dnld_over
  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
  nci_uart_tty_close
  tty_ldisc_kill
  tty_ldisc_hangup
  __tty_hangup.part.0
  tty_release
  ...

What's more, there are also use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs
in nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start. If we deallocate firmware struct, gpio or
set null to the members of priv->fw_dnld in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev,
then, we dereference firmware, gpio or the members of priv->fw_dnld in
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start, the UAF or NPD bugs will happen.

This patch reorders destructive operations after nci_unregister_device
in order to synchronize between cleanup routine and firmware download
routine.

The nci_unregister_device is well synchronized. If the device is
detaching, the firmware download routine will goto error. If firmware
download routine is executing, nci_unregister_device will wait until
firmware download routine is finished.

Fixes: 3194c6870158 ("NFC: nfcmrvl: add firmware download support")
Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A flaw in Linux Kernel found in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev() in drivers/nfc/nfcmrvl/main.c can lead to use after free both read or write when non synchronized between cleanup routine and firmware download routine.

commit_message:
bpf: Fix integer overflow in prealloc_elems_and_freelist()

In prealloc_elems_and_freelist(), the multiplication to calculate the
size passed to bpf_map_area_alloc() could lead to an integer overflow.
As a result, out-of-bounds write could occur in pcpu_freelist_populate()
as reported by KASAN:

[...]
[   16.968613] BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.969408] Write of size 8 at addr ffff888104fc6ea0 by task crash/78
[   16.970038]
[   16.970195] CPU: 0 PID: 78 Comm: crash Not tainted 5.15.0-rc2+ #1
[   16.970878] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS 1.13.0-1ubuntu1.1 04/01/2014
[   16.972026] Call Trace:
[   16.972306]  dump_stack_lvl+0x34/0x44
[   16.972687]  print_address_description.constprop.0+0x21/0x140
[   16.973297]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.973777]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.974257]  kasan_report.cold+0x7f/0x11b
[   16.974681]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975190]  pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975669]  stack_map_alloc+0x209/0x2a0
[   16.976106]  __sys_bpf+0xd83/0x2ce0
[...]

The possibility of this overflow was originally discussed in [0], but
was overlooked.

Fix the integer overflow by changing elem_size to u64 from u32.

  [0] https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/728b238e-a481-eb50-98e9-b0f430ab01e7@gmail.com/

Fixes: 557c0c6e7df8 ("bpf: convert stackmap to pre-allocation")
Signed-off-by: Tatsuhiko Yasumatsu <th.yasumatsu@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/20210930135545.173698-1-th.yasumatsu@gmail.com
cve_desc: prealloc_elems_and_freelist in kernel/bpf/stackmap.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.12 allows unprivileged users to trigger an eBPF multiplication integer overflow with a resultant out-of-bounds write.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the size calculation `elem_size * smap->map.max_entries` can cause a smaller-than-expected buffer to be allocated. The subsequent `pcpu_freelist_populate` call then treats this undersized buffer as if it contains `max_entries` elements of `elem_size` bytes each, leading to out-of-bounds access.

step_by_step_execution.description: elem_size is computed as sizeof(struct stack_map_bucket) + smap->map.value_size. Both are u32 values, so elem_size is u32.

The product `elem_size * smap->map.max_entries` is computed as a u32 multiplication (or u64 depending on architecture, but on 32-bit it wraps). If both are large enough the multiplication overflows, yielding a small value.

state.value: wraps to a small value, e.g. 0x01000100 on 32-bit or similar overflow scenarios

step_by_step_execution.description: bpf_map_area_alloc allocates a buffer based on the overflowed (small) size. The allocation succeeds with an undersized buffer.

pcpu_freelist_populate iterates over max_entries elements, each of elem_size bytes, starting from smap->elems. Since the buffer is smaller than max_entries * elem_size, this accesses memory beyond the allocated region.

commit_message:
jp2_dec: fix `numchans` mixup

When iterating over `dec->cdef->data.cdef.ents`, we need to use its
`numchans` variable, not the one in `jp2_dec_t`.

Fixes CVE-2018-19543
Fixes CVE-2017-9782

Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/13
Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/18
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/140
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/182
cve_desc: JasPer 2.0.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer over-read and application crash) via a crafted image, related to the jp2_decode function in libjasper/jp2/jp2_dec.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When a CMAP box is present (dec->cmap is non-null), the code unconditionally dereferences dec->cdef to assign cdefd, without checking if dec->cdef is NULL. The CDEF box is optional and may not be present in the input stream, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The decoder object is created and box parsing begins.
During box parsing, a CMAP box is encountered and stored, but no CDEF box is found in the stream.

state.value: non-NULL (valid PCLR box, required when CMAP present)

step_by_step_execution.description: After all checks pass (CMAP sanity check, channel-to-component LUT allocation), the code enters the `else` branch for dec->cmap being non-null.
The code dereferences dec->cdef without a NULL check, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.description: When a CMAP box is present and a palette-mapped channel is being processed, jas_alloc2 is called to allocate lutents. If the allocation fails (returns NULL), the code immediately dereferences the NULL pointer in the for loop without any check.

step_by_step_execution.description: The decoder enters the CMAP processing branch with a palette-mapped channel entry (cmapent->map == JP2_CMAP_PALETTE).

state.value: a large value or system is low on memory

step_by_step_execution.description: jas_alloc2 is called to allocate memory for lutents but fails and returns NULL.
The for loop iterates and writes to lutents[0], dereferencing the NULL pointer.

commit_message:
Sanitize inputs to bookmark-cmd (#591)

Newsbeuter didn't properly shell-escape the arguments passed to
bookmarking command, which allows a remote attacker to perform remote
code execution by crafting an RSS item whose title and/or URL contain
something interpretable by the shell (most notably subshell
invocations.)

This has been reported by Jeriko One <jeriko.one@gmx.us>, complete with
PoC and a patch.

This vulnerability was assigned CVE-2017-12904.
cve_desc: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command in bookmarking function of Newsbeuter versions 0.7 through 2.9 allows remote attackers to perform user-assisted code execution by crafting an RSS item that includes shell code in its title and/or URL.

commit_message:
gsocketclient: Fix criticals

This ensures the parent GTask is kept alive as long as an enumeration
is running and trying to connect.

Closes #1646
Closes #1649
cve_desc: gio/gsocketclient.c in GNOME GLib 2.59.2 does not ensure that a parent GTask remains alive during the execution of a connection-attempting enumeration, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (g_socket_client_connected_callback mishandling and application crash) via a crafted web site, as demonstrated by GNOME Web (aka Epiphany).

commit_message:
i2c: ismt: prevent memory corruption in ismt_access()

The "data->block[0]" variable comes from the user and is a number
between 0-255.  It needs to be capped to prevent writing beyond the end
of dma_buffer[].

Fixes: 5e9a97b1f449 ("i2c: ismt: Adding support for I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL")
Reported-and-tested-by: Zheyu Ma <zheyuma97@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Mika Westerberg <mika.westerberg@linux.intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel Intel’s iSMT SMBus host controller driver in the way it handled the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case (via the ioctl I2C_SMBUS) with malicious input data. This flaw could allow a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write case, data->block[0] is not validated against I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX before being used to compute dma_size and the memcpy length. Since dma_size is a u8, when data->block[0] is 255, dma_size wraps to 0 (255+1=0 in u8), and then dma_size-1 wraps to 255, causing a 255-byte memcpy into dma_buffer which overflows the priv->buffer. Other cases (BLOCK_PROC_CALL and I2C_BLOCK_DATA) explicitly validate or clamp data->block[0], but BLOCK_DATA write does not.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with size = I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA and read_write = I2C_SMBUS_WRITE, with data->block[0] set to 255 by the caller.
dma_buffer is computed as PTR_ALIGN(&priv->buffer[0], 16), pointing into a fixed-size buffer.
Execution enters the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write branch. dma_size is computed as data->block[0] + 1. Since dma_size is u8, 255 + 1 wraps to 0.

memcpy is called with length dma_size - 1. Since dma_size is u8 with value 0, 0-1 wraps to 255 (unsigned underflow). This copies 255 bytes from data->block[1] into dma_buffer[1], overflowing the priv->buffer.
Since dma_size == 0, the DMA mapping block is skipped entirely, but the buffer overflow has already occurred in the memcpy above.

commit_message:
cgroup: verify that source is a string

The following sequence can be used to trigger a UAF:

    int fscontext_fd = fsopen("cgroup");
    int fd_null = open("/dev/null, O_RDONLY);
    int fsconfig(fscontext_fd, FSCONFIG_SET_FD, "source", fd_null);
    close_range(3, ~0U, 0);

The cgroup v1 specific fs parser expects a string for the "source"
parameter.  However, it is perfectly legitimate to e.g.  specify a file
descriptor for the "source" parameter.  The fs parser doesn't know what
a filesystem allows there.  So it's a bug to assume that "source" is
always of type fs_value_is_string when it can reasonably also be
fs_value_is_file.

This assumption in the cgroup code causes a UAF because struct
fs_parameter uses a union for the actual value.  Access to that union is
guarded by the param->type member.  Since the cgroup paramter parser
didn't check param->type but unconditionally moved param->string into
fc->source a close on the fscontext_fd would trigger a UAF during
put_fs_context() which frees fc->source thereby freeing the file stashed
in param->file causing a UAF during a close of the fd_null.

Fix this by verifying that param->type is actually a string and report
an error if not.

In follow up patches I'll add a new generic helper that can be used here
and by other filesystems instead of this error-prone copy-pasta fix.
But fixing it in here first makes backporting a it to stable a lot
easier.

Fixes: 8d2451f4994f ("cgroup1: switch to option-by-option parsing")
Reported-by: syzbot+283ce5a46486d6acdbaf@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Cc: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Cc: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
Cc: Dmitry Vyukov <dvyukov@google.com>
Cc: <stable@kernel.org>
Cc: syzkaller-bugs <syzkaller-bugs@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Christian Brauner <christian.brauner@ubuntu.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A use-after-free flaw was found in cgroup1_parse_param in kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c in the Linux kernel's cgroup v1 parser. A local attacker with a user privilege could cause a privilege escalation by exploiting the fsconfig syscall parameter leading to a container breakout and a denial of service on the system.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-2qpg-f6wf-w984
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming STUN message contains an ERROR-CODE attribute, the header length is not checked before performing a subtraction operation, potentially resulting in an integer underflow scenario. This issue affects all users that use STUN. A malicious actor located within the victim’s network may forge and send a specially crafted UDP (STUN) message that could remotely execute arbitrary code on the victim’s machine. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The attribute header length (`attr->hdr.length`) is read directly from the attacker-controlled network buffer without validating that it is at least 4. If `attr->hdr.length` is less than 4, the subtraction `attr->hdr.length - 4` causes an integer underflow, resulting in a very large (or negative) `value.slen`. This is then passed to `pj_strdup`, which can cause a heap buffer over-read and potentially a massive heap allocation or crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives an attacker-controlled buffer `buf` containing a STUN attribute.
`GETATTRHDR(buf, &attr->hdr)` reads the attribute header from `buf`, including `attr->hdr.length`, which is attacker-controlled. An attacker sets the length field to a value less than 4, e.g., 0.
`value.slen = attr->hdr.length - 4` is computed. With `attr->hdr.length = 0`, the result is -4 if slen is signed, or wraps to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFC) if treated as unsigned.

`pj_strdup(pool, &attr->reason, &value)` is called, which attempts to allocate and copy `value.slen` bytes from the buffer. This leads to a heap buffer over-read (reading far beyond the legitimate buffer) and/or an excessively large memory allocation.

commit_message: Fix https://github.com/Exiv2/exiv2/issues/55
cve_desc: In Exiv2 0.26, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the Exiv2::Image::byteSwap4 function in image.cpp. Remote attackers can exploit this vulnerability to disclose memory data or cause a denial of service via a crafted TIFF file.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in `size*count + pad + 20`. The multiplication `size*count` and subsequent addition are performed in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic before the cast to `long long`. If `size*count` wraps around, a small buffer is allocated, but subsequent reads and accesses use the original (large) `count` and `size` values, leading to heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function reads dirLength from the IFD header. Assume dirLength is a valid small value (e.g. 1).

For the single IFD entry, tag, type, count, and offset are read from the 12-byte directory entry. An attacker controls the file so that type is a valid 8-byte type (e.g. rational, size=8) and count is 0x20000001.
kount is computed. Since count > 5 and it's not a string/XMP/ICC type, kount = 5.

The allocation size is computed: `size*count` = 8 * 0x20000001 = 0x100000008, which truncates to 0x8 in 32-bit arithmetic. Then `0x8 + pad(0) + 20 = 28`. The cast to long long happens after the overflow.
The check `allocate > io.size()` passes if the file is larger than 28 bytes, so no exception is thrown.
A buffer of only 28 bytes is allocated via `DataBuf buf(allocate)`.

`bOffsetIsPointer = count*size > 4` evaluates to `8 > 4 = true` (same overflow). `io.read(buf.pData_, count*size)` reads only 8 bytes (overflowed value) into the 28-byte buffer. This particular read is within bounds.

In the print loop for isRationalType, the loop iterates k from 0 to kount-1 (0 to 4). It accesses `byteSwap4(buf, k*size+0, bSwap)` and `byteSwap4(buf, k*size+4, bSwap)`. At k=4: offset = 4*8+4 = 36, accessing buf.pData_[36..39]. The buffer is only 28 bytes, causing a heap-based out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.description: When isPrintXMP returns true, `buf.pData_[count]` is written with 0 as a null terminator. With the integer overflow in allocation, the buffer can be far smaller than `count`, resulting in a massive out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: An IFD entry is read where tag triggers isPrintXMP (e.g. XMP tag) and type is a string type (size=1, pad=1). The attacker sets count to 0xFFFFFFFF.
For isPrintXMP, kount = count = 0xFFFFFFFF. Allocation: `size*count + pad + 20` = `1*0xFFFFFFFF + 1 + 20` = `0xFFFFFFFF + 21`. In uint32_t arithmetic this overflows to 20.
The check `allocate > io.size()` may pass if io.size() >= 20.
A 20-byte buffer is allocated.
`buf.pData_[count] = buf.pData_[0xFFFFFFFF] = 0` writes far beyond the 20-byte heap buffer, causing a heap-based out-of-bounds write.

commit_message:
cgroup: verify that source is a string

The following sequence can be used to trigger a UAF:

    int fscontext_fd = fsopen("cgroup");
    int fd_null = open("/dev/null, O_RDONLY);
    int fsconfig(fscontext_fd, FSCONFIG_SET_FD, "source", fd_null);
    close_range(3, ~0U, 0);

The cgroup v1 specific fs parser expects a string for the "source"
parameter.  However, it is perfectly legitimate to e.g.  specify a file
descriptor for the "source" parameter.  The fs parser doesn't know what
a filesystem allows there.  So it's a bug to assume that "source" is
always of type fs_value_is_string when it can reasonably also be
fs_value_is_file.

This assumption in the cgroup code causes a UAF because struct
fs_parameter uses a union for the actual value.  Access to that union is
guarded by the param->type member.  Since the cgroup paramter parser
didn't check param->type but unconditionally moved param->string into
fc->source a close on the fscontext_fd would trigger a UAF during
put_fs_context() which frees fc->source thereby freeing the file stashed
in param->file causing a UAF during a close of the fd_null.

Fix this by verifying that param->type is actually a string and report
an error if not.

In follow up patches I'll add a new generic helper that can be used here
and by other filesystems instead of this error-prone copy-pasta fix.
But fixing it in here first makes backporting a it to stable a lot
easier.

Fixes: 8d2451f4994f ("cgroup1: switch to option-by-option parsing")
