A local attacker with a user privilege could cause a privilege escalation by exploiting the fsconfig syscall parameter leading to a container breakout and a denial of service on the system.

commit_message: Avoid maxrlvls more than upper bound to cause heap-buffer-overflow
cve_desc: There's a flaw in jasper's jpc encoder in versions prior to 2.0.23. Crafted input provided to jasper by an attacker could cause an arbitrary out-of-bounds write. This could potentially affect data confidentiality, integrity, or application availability.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in hsteplcm/vsteplcm multiplication loop. The variables hsteplcm and vsteplcm (uint_fast32_t) are multiplied by each component's step value in a loop without overflow checking. The overflowed value is then used to multiply cp->imgareatlx and cp->imgareatly, leading to incorrect geometry calculations that propagate to tile grid and reference grid computations.

step_by_step_execution.description: hsteplcm is initialized to 1
Loop iterates over image components, multiplying hsteplcm by each component's horizontal step. With e.g. 3 components each having large step values like 65536, the product overflows uint_fast32_t.

state.value: overflowed/wrapped value, e.g. 0 if steps multiply to 2^32

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides imgareatlx via OPT_IMGAREAOFFX option, e.g. value 10
cp->imgareatlx is multiplied by the overflowed hsteplcm, producing an incorrect (potentially 0 or small) value
refgrdwidth is computed from overflowed imgareatlx, leading to incorrect tile grid calculations downstream

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the multiplication of numhtiles * numvtiles. If tilewidth and tileheight are set to small values (e.g., 1) via options and the image/grid dimensions are large, both numhtiles and numvtiles can be large. Their product can overflow uint_fast32_t, resulting in a much smaller numtiles value. This value is likely used for subsequent memory allocations, leading to undersized buffers and heap buffer overflows.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides tilewidth=1 and tileheight=1 via options
refgrdwidth and refgrdheight are computed based on image dimensions, e.g., a large image could yield refgrdwidth=70000, refgrdheight=70000
numhtiles = CEILDIV(refgrdwidth - tilegrdoffx, tilewidth) = large value, numvtiles similarly large
numtiles = numhtiles * numvtiles overflows uint_fast32_t (70000*70000 = 4,900,000,000 > 2^32)

state.value: wrapped/truncated value, much smaller than expected 4.9 billion

vulnerabilities.description: Using atoi() to parse user-provided strings into values assigned to unsigned integer fields (uint_fast32_t). atoi() returns a signed int and performs no error checking. A negative input value will be implicitly converted to a very large unsigned value, potentially bypassing subsequent validation checks or causing unexpected behavior in geometry calculations.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides option string with tilewidth=-1
atoi returns -1 as a signed int
The signed int -1 is implicitly converted to uint_fast32_t, wrapping to a very large value (e.g., UINT_FAST32_MAX)
This very large tilewidth is used in subsequent tile grid calculations, potentially causing incorrect results or further overflows

commit_message:
ax25: Fix UAF bugs in ax25 timers

There are race conditions that may lead to UAF bugs in
ax25_heartbeat_expiry(), ax25_t1timer_expiry(), ax25_t2timer_expiry(),
ax25_t3timer_expiry() and ax25_idletimer_expiry(), when we call
ax25_release() to deallocate ax25_dev.

One of the UAF bugs caused by ax25_release() is shown below:

      (Thread 1)                    |      (Thread 2)
ax25_dev_device_up() //(1)          |
...                                 | ax25_kill_by_device()
ax25_bind()          //(2)          |
ax25_connect()                      | ...
 ax25_std_establish_data_link()     |
  ax25_start_t1timer()              | ax25_dev_device_down() //(3)
   mod_timer(&ax25->t1timer,..)     |
                                    | ax25_release()
   (wait a time)                    |  ...
                                    |  ax25_dev_put(ax25_dev) //(4)FREE
   ax25_t1timer_expiry()            |
    ax25->ax25_dev->values[..] //USE|  ...
     ...                            |

We increase the refcount of ax25_dev in position (1) and (2), and
decrease the refcount of ax25_dev in position (3) and (4).
The ax25_dev will be freed in position (4) and be used in
ax25_t1timer_expiry().

The fail log is shown below:
==============================================================

[  106.116942] BUG: KASAN: use-after-free in ax25_t1timer_expiry+0x1c/0x60
[  106.116942] Read of size 8 at addr ffff88800bda9028 by task swapper/0/0
[  106.116942] CPU: 0 PID: 0 Comm: swapper/0 Not tainted 5.17.0-06123-g0905eec574
[  106.116942] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS rel-14
[  106.116942] Call Trace:
...
[  106.116942]  ax25_t1timer_expiry+0x1c/0x60
[  106.116942]  call_timer_fn+0x122/0x3d0
[  106.116942]  __run_timers.part.0+0x3f6/0x520
[  106.116942]  run_timer_softirq+0x4f/0xb0
[  106.116942]  __do_softirq+0x1c2/0x651
...

This patch adds del_timer_sync() in ax25_release(), which could ensure
that all timers stop before we deallocate ax25_dev.

Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: Paolo Abeni <pabeni@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s Amateur Radio AX.25 protocol functionality in the way a user connects with the protocol. This flaw allows a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.description: In the AX25_STATE_3/AX25_STATE_4 case, the code dereferences ax25->ax25_dev (via ax25_dev) without first checking if it is NULL. The NULL check for ax25_dev only occurs later in the function (after the switch statement). If ax25_dev is NULL, this dereference causes a NULL pointer dereference / kernel oops.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function obtains the sock pointer and the ax25 control block.
ax25_dev is read from ax25->ax25_dev. This value can be NULL if no device is associated.
The code enters the SOCK_SEQPACKET branch and matches AX25_STATE_3 or AX25_STATE_4.
The code dereferences ax25->ax25_dev->values[AX25_VALUES_PROTOCOL] without checking ax25_dev for NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
Fix: Worked around buffer overflows in URL auth's cURL interface

This is only a workaround that keeps compatibility with 2.4.x mainline.
A real fix has been implemented in 2.5.x (master).
cve_desc: A buffer overflow was discovered in the URL-authentication backend of the Icecast before 2.4.4. If the backend is enabled, then any malicious HTTP client can send a request for that specific resource including a crafted header, leading to denial of service and potentially remote code execution.

commit_message:
tpm2: Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it

Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it. This is necessary since
an OBJECT may also be used as a HASH_OBJECT via the ANY_OBJECT
union and that HASH_OBJECT can leave bad size inidicators in TPM2B
buffer in the OBJECT. To get rid of this problem we reset the whole
OBJECT to 0 before using it. This is as if the memory for the
OBJECT was just initialized.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the libtpms code that may cause access beyond the boundary of internal buffers. The vulnerability is triggered by specially-crafted TPM2 command packets that then trigger the issue when the state of the TPM2's volatile state is written. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability. This issue affects libtpms versions before 0.8.5, before 0.7.9 and before 0.6.6.

commit_message:
tpm2: Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it

Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it. This is necessary since
an OBJECT may also be used as a HASH_OBJECT via the ANY_OBJECT
union and that HASH_OBJECT can leave bad size inidicators in TPM2B
buffer in the OBJECT. To get rid of this problem we reset the whole
OBJECT to 0 before using it. This is as if the memory for the
OBJECT was just initialized.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the libtpms code that may cause access beyond the boundary of internal buffers. The vulnerability is triggered by specially-crafted TPM2 command packets that then trigger the issue when the state of the TPM2's volatile state is written. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability. This issue affects libtpms versions before 0.8.5, before 0.7.9 and before 0.6.6.

commit_message: commit bash-20190628 snapshot
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in disable_priv_mode in shell.c in GNU Bash through 5.0 patch 11. By default, if Bash is run with its effective UID not equal to its real UID, it will drop privileges by setting its effective UID to its real UID. However, it does so incorrectly. On Linux and other systems that support "saved UID" functionality, the saved UID is not dropped. An attacker with command execution in the shell can use "enable -f" for runtime loading of a new builtin, which can be a shared object that calls setuid() and therefore regains privileges. However, binaries running with an effective UID of 0 are unaffected.

vulnerabilities.description: When setuid() fails (and EXIT_ON_SETUID_FAILURE is not defined, or the error is not EAGAIN), the function logs an error but continues execution. It then unconditionally updates current_user.euid and current_user.egid to the unprivileged values, causing the program to believe privileges were successfully dropped when they were not. The process continues running with elevated privileges while the internal state indicates otherwise.

step_by_step_execution.description: setuid(current_user.uid) is called to drop privileges, but it fails (returns < 0) with an errno other than EAGAIN, or EXIT_ON_SETUID_FAILURE is not defined.
sys_error logs the failure message, but execution is not halted. The #if defined(EXIT_ON_SETUID_FAILURE) block may not be compiled in, or errno is not EAGAIN, so exit() is not called.
setgid(current_user.gid) is called, which may or may not succeed, but regardless execution continues.

current_user.euid is unconditionally set to current_user.uid, and current_user.egid is set to current_user.gid. The program's internal bookkeeping now indicates unprivileged operation, but the actual process privileges were never dropped.

vulnerabilities.description: setuid() is called before setgid(). If the process is running as root and setuid() succeeds in dropping to a non-root uid, the subsequent setgid() call may fail because the process no longer has the privilege to change its group ID. The correct order is to call setgid() first, then setuid().

step_by_step_execution.description: The process is running with euid=0 (root) and needs to drop to an unprivileged uid and gid.
setuid(current_user.uid) is called first and succeeds, dropping the effective uid to an unprivileged user.
setgid(current_user.gid) is called, but since the process is no longer root, it may fail (EPERM) if the target gid is different from the current gid.
The error is logged but execution continues. current_user.egid is set to current_user.gid even though setgid failed. The process retains its old (possibly privileged) gid.

commit_message: Fix https://github.com/Exiv2/exiv2/issues/55
cve_desc: In Exiv2 0.26, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the Exiv2::Image::byteSwap4 function in image.cpp. Remote attackers can exploit this vulnerability to disclose memory data or cause a denial of service via a crafted TIFF file.

code_snippets: DataBuf  buf(size*count + pad+20);  // allocate a buffer
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the expression `size*count` used for buffer allocation. When `size` (up to 8) and `count` (attacker-controlled from file data) multiply to exceed UINT32_MAX, the result wraps around to a small value, causing a small heap buffer to be allocated. Subsequent operations that use the original `count` value (e.g., `buf.pData_[count]=0` in the XMP path, or `out.write(..., count)` in the ICC path) then access memory far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function reads a 12-byte IFD entry from the file and parses tag, type, count, and offset.
The tag matches XMP tag, so isPrintXMP returns true and kount is set to count (the full value).

Buffer allocation: size*count + pad + 20 = 1*0xFFFFFF00 + 1 + 20 = 0xFFFFFF15. While this specific value doesn't overflow, with count=0xFFFFFFEC, size*count + pad + 20 = 0xFFFFFFEC + 1 + 20 = 0x00000001 (overflow wraps). A tiny buffer is allocated.

bOffsetIsPointer is checked: count*size = 0xFFFFFFEC > 4, so the code seeks to offset and calls io.read(buf.pData_, count*size). This attempts to read ~4GB into a 1-byte buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

Even if the read is short (io.read returns fewer bytes), the code later executes buf.pData_[count]=0, writing a null byte at offset 0xFFFFFFEC from the start of a 1-byte buffer — a heap out-of-bounds write.

state.value: Write at offset 0xFFFFFFEC past a 1-byte allocation

commit_message:
nfc: nfcmrvl: main: reorder destructive operations in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev to avoid bugs

There are destructive operations such as nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort and
gpio_free in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev. The resources such as firmware,
gpio and so on could be destructed while the upper layer functions such as
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start and nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame is executing, which leads
to double-free, use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs.

There are three situations that could lead to double-free bugs.

The first situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 release_firmware()           |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  kfree(fw) //(1)             |    fw_dnld_over
                              |     release_firmware
  ...                         |      kfree(fw) //(2)
                              |     ...

The second situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |
 mod_timer                    |
 (wait a time)                |
 fw_dnld_timeout              |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
   fw_dnld_over               |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
    release_firmware          |    fw_dnld_over
     kfree(fw) //(1)          |     release_firmware
     ...                      |      kfree(fw) //(2)

The third situation is shown below:

       (Thread 1)               |       (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame          |
 if(..->fw_download_in_progress)|
  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_recv_frame    |
   queue_work                   |
                                |
fw_dnld_rx_work                 | nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 fw_dnld_over                   |  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  release_firmware              |   fw_dnld_over
   kfree(fw) //(1)              |    release_firmware
                                |     kfree(fw) //(2)

The firmware struct is deallocated in position (1) and deallocated
in position (2) again.

The crash trace triggered by POC is like below:

BUG: KASAN: double-free or invalid-free in fw_dnld_over
Call Trace:
  kfree
  fw_dnld_over
  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
  nci_uart_tty_close
  tty_ldisc_kill
  tty_ldisc_hangup
  __tty_hangup.part.0
  tty_release
  ...

What's more, there are also use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs
in nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start. If we deallocate firmware struct, gpio or
set null to the members of priv->fw_dnld in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev,
then, we dereference firmware, gpio or the members of priv->fw_dnld in
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start, the UAF or NPD bugs will happen.

This patch reorders destructive operations after nci_unregister_device
in order to synchronize between cleanup routine and firmware download
routine.

The nci_unregister_device is well synchronized. If the device is
detaching, the firmware download routine will goto error. If firmware
download routine is executing, nci_unregister_device will wait until
firmware download routine is finished.

Fixes: 3194c6870158 ("NFC: nfcmrvl: add firmware download support")
Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A flaw in Linux Kernel found in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev() in drivers/nfc/nfcmrvl/main.c can lead to use after free both read or write when non synchronized between cleanup routine and firmware download routine.

vulnerabilities.description: nci_unregister_device(ndev) is called first, which frees the nfc_dev associated with the nci_dev (via nfc_unregister_device/nfc_release_device). Immediately after, the code dereferences priv->ndev->nfc_dev->fw_download_in_progress, accessing memory that has already been freed.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is entered with a valid priv pointer. ndev is set to priv->ndev.
nci_unregister_device(ndev) is called, which internally calls nfc_unregister_device and eventually frees ndev->nfc_dev.
The code dereferences priv->ndev->nfc_dev->fw_download_in_progress, accessing the already-freed nfc_dev structure.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-2qpg-f6wf-w984
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming STUN message contains an ERROR-CODE attribute, the header length is not checked before performing a subtraction operation, potentially resulting in an integer underflow scenario. This issue affects all users that use STUN. A malicious actor located within the victim’s network may forge and send a specially crafted UDP (STUN) message that could remotely execute arbitrary code on the victim’s machine. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

commit_message:
rgw: reject control characters in response-header actions

S3 GetObject permits overriding response header values, but those inputs
need to be validated to insure only characters that are valid in an HTTP
header value are present.

Credit: Initial vulnerability discovery by William Bowling (@wcbowling)
Credit: Further vulnerability discovery by Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>
Signed-off-by: Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the Ceph Object Gateway, where it supports request sent by an anonymous user in Amazon S3. This flaw could lead to potential XSS attacks due to the lack of proper neutralization of untrusted input.

commit_message:
af_key: Do not call xfrm_probe_algs in parallel

When namespace support was added to xfrm/afkey, it caused the
previously single-threaded call to xfrm_probe_algs to become
multi-threaded.  This is buggy and needs to be fixed with a mutex.

Reported-by: Abhishek Shah <abhishek.shah@columbia.edu>
Fixes: 283bc9f35bbb ("xfrm: Namespacify xfrm state/policy locks")
Signed-off-by: Herbert Xu <herbert@gondor.apana.org.au>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: A race condition was found in the Linux kernel's IP framework for transforming packets (XFRM subsystem) when multiple calls to xfrm_probe_algs occurred simultaneously. This flaw could allow a local attacker to potentially trigger an out-of-bounds write or leak kernel heap memory by performing an out-of-bounds read and copying it into a socket.

commit_message:
ax25: Fix UAF bugs in ax25 timers

There are race conditions that may lead to UAF bugs in
ax25_heartbeat_expiry(), ax25_t1timer_expiry(), ax25_t2timer_expiry(),
ax25_t3timer_expiry() and ax25_idletimer_expiry(), when we call
ax25_release() to deallocate ax25_dev.

One of the UAF bugs caused by ax25_release() is shown below:

      (Thread 1)                    |      (Thread 2)
ax25_dev_device_up() //(1)          |
...                                 | ax25_kill_by_device()
ax25_bind()          //(2)          |
ax25_connect()                      | ...
 ax25_std_establish_data_link()     |
  ax25_start_t1timer()              | ax25_dev_device_down() //(3)
   mod_timer(&ax25->t1timer,..)     |
                                    | ax25_release()
   (wait a time)                    |  ...
                                    |  ax25_dev_put(ax25_dev) //(4)FREE
   ax25_t1timer_expiry()            |
    ax25->ax25_dev->values[..] //USE|  ...
     ...                            |

We increase the refcount of ax25_dev in position (1) and (2), and
decrease the refcount of ax25_dev in position (3) and (4).
The ax25_dev will be freed in position (4) and be used in
ax25_t1timer_expiry().

The fail log is shown below:
==============================================================

[  106.116942] BUG: KASAN: use-after-free in ax25_t1timer_expiry+0x1c/0x60
[  106.116942] Read of size 8 at addr ffff88800bda9028 by task swapper/0/0
[  106.116942] CPU: 0 PID: 0 Comm: swapper/0 Not tainted 5.17.0-06123-g0905eec574
[  106.116942] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS rel-14
[  106.116942] Call Trace:
...
[  106.116942]  ax25_t1timer_expiry+0x1c/0x60
[  106.116942]  call_timer_fn+0x122/0x3d0
[  106.116942]  __run_timers.part.0+0x3f6/0x520
[  106.116942]  run_timer_softirq+0x4f/0xb0
[  106.116942]  __do_softirq+0x1c2/0x651
...

This patch adds del_timer_sync() in ax25_release(), which could ensure
that all timers stop before we deallocate ax25_dev.

Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: Paolo Abeni <pabeni@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s Amateur Radio AX.25 protocol functionality in the way a user connects with the protocol. This flaw allows a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.description: In AX25_STATE_3 and AX25_STATE_4, the code dereferences `ax25->ax25_dev` without first checking if it is NULL. The local variable `ax25_dev` is assigned from `ax25->ax25_dev` earlier and can be NULL (the later `if (ax25_dev)` guard proves this is an expected possibility). This leads to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function retrieves the sock and ax25_cb, then reads ax25_dev from ax25->ax25_dev.
The code checks sk->sk_type == SOCK_SEQPACKET, which is true.
The code enters the switch on ax25->state and matches AX25_STATE_3 or AX25_STATE_4.
The code dereferences ax25->ax25_dev->values[AX25_VALUES_PROTOCOL] without a NULL check, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message: Fix bug #79037 (global buffer-overflow in `mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar`)
cve_desc: When using certain mbstring functions to convert multibyte encodings, in PHP versions 7.2.x below 7.2.27, 7.3.x below 7.3.14 and 7.4.x below 7.4.2 it is possible to supply data that will cause function mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar to read past the allocated buffer. This may lead to information disclosure or crash.

vulnerabilities.description: If the for-loop completes without finding a matching range (no `break`), `k` equals the table size `sizeof(cp950_pua_tbl)/(sizeof(unsigned short)*4)`. The code then accesses `cp950_pua_tbl[k]` out of bounds, leading to an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with filter->from->no_encoding == mbfl_no_encoding_cp950 and filter->status == 1 (processing second byte of a DBCS character).

filter->cache (c1) is set to a value in one of the qualifying ranges (e.g., 0xFA), and c is a valid second byte (e.g., 0x40). The index w is computed and big5_ucs_table[w] returns 0 (no mapping found), so w <= 0.
The CP950 PUA conditions are met: w <= 0, c1 is in range [0xFA, 0xFE], and c is in range (0x39, 0x7F). c2 is computed as c1 << 8 | c.
The for-loop iterates over all entries in cp950_pua_tbl looking for a range that contains c2. If c2 does not match any entry's [2]..[3] range, the loop completes without breaking.

state.value: sizeof(cp950_pua_tbl)/(sizeof(unsigned short)*4) (one past the last valid index)

step_by_step_execution.description: The code accesses cp950_pua_tbl[k][2] with k out of bounds, reading memory beyond the array.

commit_message:
KVM: PPC: Book3S: Fix H_RTAS rets buffer overflow

The kvmppc_rtas_hcall() sets the host rtas_args.rets pointer based on
the rtas_args.nargs that was provided by the guest. That guest nargs
value is not range checked, so the guest can cause the host rets pointer
to be pointed outside the args array. The individual rtas function
handlers check the nargs and nrets values to ensure they are correct,
but if they are not, the handlers store a -3 (0xfffffffd) failure
indication in rets[0] which corrupts host memory.

Fix this by testing up front whether the guest supplied nargs and nret
would exceed the array size, and fail the hcall directly without storing
a failure indication to rets[0].

Also expand on a comment about why we kill the guest and try not to
return errors directly if we have a valid rets[0] pointer.

Fixes: 8e591cb72047 ("KVM: PPC: Book3S: Add infrastructure to implement kernel-side RTAS calls")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v3.10+
Reported-by: Alexey Kardashevskiy <aik@ozlabs.ru>
Signed-off-by: Nicholas Piggin <npiggin@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
cve_desc: arch/powerpc/kvm/book3s_rtas.c in the Linux kernel through 5.13.5 on the powerpc platform allows KVM guest OS users to cause host OS memory corruption via rtas_args.nargs, aka CID-f62f3c20647e.

commit_message: LibVNCClient: really fix #250
cve_desc: LibVNC before commit 7b1ef0ffc4815cab9a96c7278394152bdc89dc4d contains heap out-of-bound write vulnerability inside structure in VNC client code that can result remote code execution

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in `hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8))` can cause the buffer size check to pass with a very large nSubrects value. The multiplication wraps around to a small value, so ReadFromRFBServer reads a small amount into client->buffer, but the subsequent loop iterates hdr.nSubrects times (the original large value), reading far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: hdr.nSubrects is read from the server and byte-swapped. An attacker-controlled server provides a large value.

state.value: A large uint32_t value, e.g. 0x40000000 when BPP=32 (so stride is 4+4=8)

step_by_step_execution.description: The size check computes hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP/8)). With nSubrects=0x40000000 and stride=8, this is 0x40000000 * 8 = 0x200000000, which overflows a 32-bit unsigned integer to 0.
The check `0 > RFB_BUFFER_SIZE` is false, so execution continues. ReadFromRFBServer is called with size 0, which succeeds reading 0 bytes into client->buffer.
The for loop iterates hdr.nSubrects (0x40000000) times, reading from ptr which starts at client->buffer. Each iteration advances ptr by (4 + BPP/8) bytes, quickly going out of bounds of the buffer.

commit_message:
tipc: fix size validations for the MSG_CRYPTO type

The function tipc_crypto_key_rcv is used to parse MSG_CRYPTO messages
to receive keys from other nodes in the cluster in order to decrypt any
further messages from them.
This patch verifies that any supplied sizes in the message body are
valid for the received message.

Fixes: 1ef6f7c9390f ("tipc: add automatic session key exchange")
Signed-off-by: Max VA <maxv@sentinelone.com>
Acked-by: Ying Xue <ying.xue@windriver.com>
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Acked-by: Jon Maloy <jmaloy@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/tipc/crypto.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.16. The Transparent Inter-Process Communication (TIPC) functionality allows remote attackers to exploit insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes for the MSG_CRYPTO message type.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow: the buffer `skey` is allocated with `size` bytes (from the message header), but `skey->keylen` is read from the attacker-controlled message data and used as the length for `memcpy` into `skey->key` *before* the sanity check validates that `size == tipc_aead_key_size(skey)`. An attacker can craft a message where `keylen` exceeds the remaining space in the allocated buffer, causing an out-of-bounds write on the kernel heap.

step_by_step_execution.description: Message data size and data pointer are extracted from the header.
skey is allocated with `size` bytes via kmalloc.
skey->keylen is read from the message payload. This value is attacker-controlled.
memcpy copies `skey->keylen` (1024) bytes into `skey->key`, which starts at offset TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME + sizeof(__be32) within the 48-byte allocation. This writes far beyond the allocated buffer.
The sanity check `size != tipc_aead_key_size(skey)` occurs after the overflowing memcpy, so it cannot prevent the overflow.

state.value: already corrupted heap memory beyond allocation

commit_message:
Sanitize inputs to bookmark-cmd (#591)

Newsbeuter didn't properly shell-escape the arguments passed to
bookmarking command, which allows a remote attacker to perform remote
code execution by crafting an RSS item whose title and/or URL contain
something interpretable by the shell (most notably subshell
invocations.)

This has been reported by Jeriko One <jeriko.one@gmx.us>, complete with
PoC and a patch.

This vulnerability was assigned CVE-2017-12904.
cve_desc: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command in bookmarking function of Newsbeuter versions 0.7 through 2.9 allows remote attackers to perform user-assisted code execution by crafting an RSS item that includes shell code in its title and/or URL.

code_snippets:
std::string cmdline = strprintf::fmt("%s '%s' %s %s %s",
		                                       bookmark_cmd,
		                                       utils::replace_all(url,"'", "%27"),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(title)),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(description)),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(feed_title)));
vulnerabilities.description: The title, description, and feed_title parameters are passed through stfl::quote (an STFL UI escaping function, not a shell escaping function) and quote_empty, then interpolated into a shell command line. An attacker who controls RSS feed titles or descriptions can inject arbitrary shell commands. For example, a feed title containing $(malicious_command) or backtick-enclosed commands would be executed by /bin/sh. The url parameter is partially protected by replacing single quotes and wrapping in single quotes, but the other three parameters lack proper shell escaping.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives user/feed-controlled parameters: url, title, description, feed_title.
bookmark_cmd is retrieved from config and is non-empty, so execution proceeds to command construction.

The url is sanitized by replacing single quotes with %27 and wrapped in single quotes. However, title is only passed through stfl::quote (STFL UI escaping) and quote_empty, neither of which perform shell escaping.

state.value: $(rm -rf /) (shell metacharacters preserved since stfl::quote only escapes STFL special characters like < > &)

step_by_step_execution.description: The command line is constructed with the unsanitized title interpolated directly into the shell command string.
The command is executed via /bin/sh -c (non-interactive path) or utils::run_interactively, and the shell interprets the injected command substitution, executing arbitrary commands.

commit_message: Fix bug #79037 (global buffer-overflow in `mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar`)
cve_desc: When using certain mbstring functions to convert multibyte encodings, in PHP versions 7.2.x below 7.2.27, 7.3.x below 7.3.14 and 7.4.x below 7.4.2 it is possible to supply data that will cause function mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar to read past the allocated buffer. This may lead to information disclosure or crash.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on cp950_pua_tbl. If `is_in_cp950_pua(c1, c)` returns true but the for-loop over cp950_pua_tbl does not find a matching entry (i.e., no `break` is hit), `k` will equal `sizeof(cp950_pua_tbl)/(sizeof(unsigned short)*4)`, which is one past the last valid index. The code then accesses `cp950_pua_tbl[k][0]`, `cp950_pua_tbl[k][2]`, and `cp950_pua_tbl[k][3]` out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with filter->from->no_encoding == mbfl_no_encoding_cp950 and filter->status == 1 (processing DBCS second byte).
c1 is loaded from filter->cache (the lead byte), and c is the trail byte. The condition `(c > 0x39 && c < 0x7f) | (c > 0xa0 && c < 0xff)` is true, so we enter the DBCS decoding branch.

state.value: trail byte in valid DBCS range

step_by_step_execution.description: w is computed from big5_ucs_table lookup but results in w <= 0 (no mapping found).
is_in_cp950_pua(c1, c) returns true, so the CP950 PUA block is entered.

The for-loop iterates over all entries in cp950_pua_tbl looking for c2 >= cp950_pua_tbl[k][2] && c2 <= cp950_pua_tbl[k][3]. If is_in_cp950_pua's logic is inconsistent with the table entries (e.g., a gap between ranges), no entry matches and the loop completes without breaking.

state.value: sizeof(cp950_pua_tbl)/(sizeof(unsigned short)*4) — one past last valid index

step_by_step_execution.description: After the loop, cp950_pua_tbl[k][2] and cp950_pua_tbl[k][0] are accessed with k out of bounds, reading memory beyond the array.

state.value: reads memory past end of array

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4563: "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line

Problem:    "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line.
Solution:   Adjust "badlen".
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow occurs in vim in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4563.

vulnerabilities.description: Buffer overflow in the stack-allocated `wcopy[MAXWLEN + 2]`. The first `vim_strncpy` can write up to MAXWLEN bytes (setting `st_wordlen` close to MAXWLEN). The second `vim_strncpy` writes at offset `stp->st_wordlen` with up to `sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen` additional bytes. If `st_wordlen + (su_badlen - st_orglen)` exceeds `MAXWLEN + 2`, the write goes past the end of `wcopy`, causing a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the suggestion display loop with `sug.su_ga.ga_len > 0` and `count == 0`, iterating over suggestions.

state.value: uninitialized stack buffer of size MAXWLEN + 2

step_by_step_execution.description: For a given suggestion `stp`, `vim_strncpy(wcopy, stp->st_word, MAXWLEN)` copies the suggested word into `wcopy`. The suggested word can be up to MAXWLEN characters long.

state.value: contains st_word, up to MAXWLEN bytes filled

step_by_step_execution.description: The condition `sug.su_badlen > stp->st_orglen` is true, meaning the bad word is longer than the portion being replaced by the suggestion. The remainder must be appended.

state.value: length of bad word in visual selection, e.g. a large value
portion of bad word the suggestion replaces, smaller than su_badlen

step_by_step_execution.description: The second `vim_strncpy` writes at `wcopy + stp->st_wordlen`. If `st_wordlen` is close to MAXWLEN (e.g., MAXWLEN) and `su_badlen - st_orglen` is greater than 2, the write destination `wcopy + st_wordlen + (su_badlen - st_orglen)` exceeds `wcopy + MAXWLEN + 2`, overflowing the stack buffer.

commit_message:
USB: gadget: validate interface OS descriptor requests

Stall the control endpoint in case provided index exceeds array size of
MAX_CONFIG_INTERFACES or when the retrieved function pointer is null.

Signed-off-by: Szymon Heidrich <szymon.heidrich@gmail.com>
Cc: stable@kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in drivers/usb/gadget/composite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.10. The USB Gadget subsystem lacks certain validation of interface OS descriptor requests (ones with a large array index and ones associated with NULL function pointer retrieval). Memory corruption might occur.

vulnerabilities.description: If bos_desc() returns a negative error code, the result is cast to u16 (becoming a very large positive number, e.g., -ENOMEM = -12 becomes 65524). The min() then selects w_length, making 'value' positive. This causes the code to proceed to queue a response of w_length bytes from req->buf, potentially leaking kernel heap memory contents (information disclosure).

step_by_step_execution.description: A USB GET_DESCRIPTOR request arrives for USB_DT_BOS with a non-zero w_length (e.g., 255).
The gadget is superspeed or lpm_capable, so the USB_DT_BOS case is entered and bos_desc(cdev) is called.
bos_desc() returns a negative error code (e.g., -ENOMEM = -12).
The negative value is cast to u16 in the min() macro: (u16)(-12) = 65524. min(255, 65524) = 255. value is set to 255.

Since value (255) >= 0 and != USB_GADGET_DELAYED_STATUS, the code proceeds to check_value where req->length is set to 255 and the request is queued, sending 255 bytes from req->buf back to the USB host. This buffer was never populated by bos_desc, so stale heap data is leaked.

commit_message:
Fix oobread segfault in java arith8.class ##crash

* Reported by Cen Zhang via huntr.dev
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when reading `number_of_classes` from `buffer` at offset 6 without verifying that `sz` is large enough (at least 8 bytes). If `sz` is less than 8, the `R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT` macro reads 2 bytes past the end of the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a buffer and sz. sz can be as small as 6 (just enough for the default attribute header) or even smaller.
`r_bin_java_default_attr_new` is called, and offset is set to 6.
attr is checked for NULL and is not NULL, so execution continues.
`R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT(buffer, offset)` reads 2 bytes from buffer at offset 6. Since sz can be 6 or 7, this reads 1 or 2 bytes beyond the buffer boundary, causing an out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.description: Memory leak of `icattr->flags_str` (and the just-allocated `icattr->name`) when the name resolution fails. The code allocates `icattr->name` via `r_str_dup`, sets `icattr->flags_str` earlier via `retrieve_class_method_access_string`, then calls `free(icattr)` without freeing these string members first.

step_by_step_execution.description: Inside the loop, an `icattr` is allocated and its fields are populated, including `flags_str` from `retrieve_class_method_access_string`.
Both `r_bin_java_get_item_name_from_bin_cp_list` calls for `inner_name_idx` and `inner_class_info_idx` return NULL.
`icattr->name` is assigned `r_str_dup(NULL, "NULL")`, a new heap allocation. Then `free(icattr)` is called, freeing only the struct but not `icattr->name` or `icattr->flags_str`.

commit_message:
cgroup-v1: Require capabilities to set release_agent

The cgroup release_agent is called with call_usermodehelper.  The function
call_usermodehelper starts the release_agent with a full set fo capabilities.
Therefore require capabilities when setting the release_agaent.

Reported-by: Tabitha Sable <tabitha.c.sable@gmail.com>
Tested-by: Tabitha Sable <tabitha.c.sable@gmail.com>
Fixes: 81a6a5cdd2c5 ("Task Control Groups: automatic userspace notification of idle cgroups")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v2.6.24+
Signed-off-by: "Eric W. Biederman" <ebiederm@xmission.com>
Signed-off-by: Tejun Heo <tj@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel’s cgroup_release_agent_write in the kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c function. This flaw, under certain circumstances, allows the use of the cgroups v1 release_agent feature to escalate privileges and bypass the namespace isolation unexpectedly.

commit_message:
net/mlx5: prevent memory leak in mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq

In mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq if mlx5_vector2eqn fails the allocated
memory should be released.

Fixes: 537a50574175 ("net/mlx5: FPGA, Add high-speed connection routines")
Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Saeed Mahameed <saeedm@mellanox.com>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq() function in drivers/net/ethernet/mellanox/mlx5/core/fpga/conn.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering mlx5_vector2eqn() failures, aka CID-c8c2a057fdc7.

vulnerabilities.description: When mlx5_vector2eqn() fails, the code jumps to err_cqwq which only calls mlx5_wq_destroy but does not free the memory allocated by kvzalloc for 'in', causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: mlx5_cqwq_create succeeds, so execution continues past the first error check.
kvzalloc allocates memory for 'in' and succeeds (non-NULL).

state.value: valid pointer to allocated memory of size inlen

step_by_step_execution.description: mlx5_vector2eqn is called and returns a non-zero error code.
The error check triggers 'goto err_cqwq', skipping kvfree(in).

state.value: still points to allocated memory, never freed

step_by_step_execution.description: err_cqwq label: mlx5_wq_destroy is called, then function returns err. The memory pointed to by 'in' is leaked.

commit_message:
gsocketclient: Fix criticals

This ensures the parent GTask is kept alive as long as an enumeration
is running and trying to connect.

Closes #1646
Closes #1649
cve_desc: gio/gsocketclient.c in GNOME GLib 2.59.2 does not ensure that a parent GTask remains alive during the execution of a connection-attempting enumeration, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (g_socket_client_connected_callback mishandling and application crash) via a crafted web site, as demonstrated by GNOME Web (aka Epiphany).

vulnerabilities.description: When `data` is NULL (which the comment explicitly states can happen: "data is NULL once the task is completed"), and the connection attempt fails (`g_socket_connection_connect_finish` returns FALSE), and the attempt's cancellable is not cancelled, the code dereferences `data` (a NULL pointer) to access `data->connectable` and passes `data` to `set_last_error` before the `if (data)` NULL check that appears later in the same error-handling block.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters. `data` is assigned from `attempt->data`.

state.value: NULL (task already completed, another attempt set data to NULL)

step_by_step_execution.description: First NULL check: `if (data && g_task_return_error_if_cancelled(data->task))` — since `data` is NULL, this condition short-circuits to false, so execution continues.
Timeout source cleanup proceeds (does not use `data`).
`g_socket_connection_connect_finish` returns FALSE (connection failed), so we enter the error-handling block.
`g_cancellable_is_cancelled(attempt->cancellable)` returns FALSE (the attempt was not explicitly cancelled), so we enter the `if` branch.
`clarify_connect_error(error, data->connectable, attempt->address)` is called, dereferencing `data` which is NULL. This is a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
KVM: PPC: Book3S: Fix H_RTAS rets buffer overflow

The kvmppc_rtas_hcall() sets the host rtas_args.rets pointer based on
the rtas_args.nargs that was provided by the guest. That guest nargs
value is not range checked, so the guest can cause the host rets pointer
to be pointed outside the args array. The individual rtas function
handlers check the nargs and nrets values to ensure they are correct,
but if they are not, the handlers store a -3 (0xfffffffd) failure
indication in rets[0] which corrupts host memory.

Fix this by testing up front whether the guest supplied nargs and nret
would exceed the array size, and fail the hcall directly without storing
a failure indication to rets[0].

Also expand on a comment about why we kill the guest and try not to
return errors directly if we have a valid rets[0] pointer.

Fixes: 8e591cb72047 ("KVM: PPC: Book3S: Add infrastructure to implement kernel-side RTAS calls")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v3.10+
Reported-by: Alexey Kardashevskiy <aik@ozlabs.ru>
Signed-off-by: Nicholas Piggin <npiggin@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
cve_desc: arch/powerpc/kvm/book3s_rtas.c in the Linux kernel through 5.13.5 on the powerpc platform allows KVM guest OS users to cause host OS memory corruption via rtas_args.nargs, aka CID-f62f3c20647e.

vulnerabilities.description: The guest-controlled field `args.nargs` is used as an array index into the fixed-size `args.args[]` array without any bounds checking. A malicious guest can set `nargs` to a value larger than the `args.args` array size, causing `args.rets` to point outside the `args` structure on the kernel stack. Subsequent reads/writes through `args.rets` in the handler (and the later `kvm_write_guest` that copies the entire struct back) would operate on out-of-bounds stack memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: The guest physical address of the RTAS args structure is read from guest register r4.

state.value: guest-controlled physical address masked by KVM_PAM

step_by_step_execution.description: The entire `rtas_args` structure is copied from guest memory into the local stack variable `args`.

`args.nargs` is converted from big-endian and used as an index into `args.args[]` with no bounds check. If nargs > array size, `args.rets` points past the end of `args.args` and potentially past the `args` struct itself on the stack.

The token handler `d->handler->handler(vcpu, &args)` is invoked. Handlers typically read from and write to `args.rets[0]`, `args.rets[1]`, etc., which now point to out-of-bounds memory on the kernel stack, allowing stack corruption.

state.value: out-of-bounds write target on kernel stack

step_by_step_execution.description: After handler returns, `args` (including corrupted out-of-bounds data that may have been written) is written back to guest memory via `kvm_write_guest`, potentially leaking kernel stack data to the guest.

state.value: struct on stack, potentially with out-of-bounds modifications

Usually, the requests are sent via mongos and special privileges are required in order to know the address of the shards and to log in to the shards of an auth enabled environment.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4563: "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line

Problem:    "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line.
Solution:   Adjust "badlen".
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow occurs in vim in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4563.

vulnerabilities.description: Stack-based buffer overflow in `wcopy`. The buffer `wcopy` is sized `MAXWLEN + 2`, and the first `vim_strncpy` can write up to `MAXWLEN` characters (plus NUL). The second `vim_strncpy` appends at offset `stp->st_wordlen` with up to `sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen` additional characters. If `stp->st_wordlen` is close to `MAXWLEN` and `sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen` is greater than 1, the combined write exceeds the `MAXWLEN + 2` byte buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The stack buffer `wcopy` is declared with a fixed size of `MAXWLEN + 2` bytes.
The suggestion list is generated via `spell_find_suggest`. A suggestion `stp` is iterated where `stp->st_word` has length `stp->st_wordlen` which can be up to MAXWLEN.

state.value: length of original text replaced by this suggestion, can be small (e.g., 1)
length of the bad word from visual selection or spell checker, can be larger than st_orglen (e.g., st_orglen + 5)

step_by_step_execution.description: `vim_strncpy(wcopy, stp->st_word, MAXWLEN)` copies up to MAXWLEN characters of the suggested word into `wcopy`. This itself is safe within the buffer.

state.value: contains stp->st_word, up to MAXWLEN chars + NUL

step_by_step_execution.description: The condition `sug.su_badlen > stp->st_orglen` is true, so the remainder of the bad word is appended. `vim_strncpy(wcopy + stp->st_wordlen, ..., sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen)` writes at offset `stp->st_wordlen` (= MAXWLEN), writing up to `sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen` characters plus a NUL terminator.
The total write (MAXWLEN + 6 in this example) exceeds the buffer size (MAXWLEN + 2), causing a stack buffer overflow that can overwrite adjacent stack variables including the return address.

state.value: 4 bytes beyond buffer in this example

commit_message:
USB: gadget: validate interface OS descriptor requests

Stall the control endpoint in case provided index exceeds array size of
MAX_CONFIG_INTERFACES or when the retrieved function pointer is null.

Signed-off-by: Szymon Heidrich <szymon.heidrich@gmail.com>
Cc: stable@kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in drivers/usb/gadget/composite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.10. The USB Gadget subsystem lacks certain validation of interface OS descriptor requests (ones with a large array index and ones associated with NULL function pointer retrieval). Memory corruption might occur.

vulnerabilities.description: If bos_desc() returns a negative error code, the value is cast to u16 (e.g., -1 becomes 65535) before being passed to min(). This results in 'value' being set to w_length (a positive number), causing the code to proceed to check_value and queue a response of w_length bytes from req->buf without the buffer having been properly filled. This leads to an information leak of stale kernel heap data. Other descriptor cases (USB_DT_CONFIG, USB_DT_STRING) correctly check 'if (value >= 0)' before the min() call, but USB_DT_BOS does not.

step_by_step_execution.description: A USB IN control request is received with bRequestType=USB_DIR_IN, bRequest=USB_REQ_GET_DESCRIPTOR, wValue high byte = USB_DT_BOS, and w_length set to some value (e.g. 256, which is <= USB_COMP_EP0_BUFSIZ).
The code enters the USB_DT_BOS case. gadget_is_superspeed() or gadget->lpm_capable is true, so the block is entered.
bos_desc(cdev) is called and returns a negative error code, e.g., -ENOMEM (-12).
value is cast to u16 in min(w_length, (u16) value). Casting -12 to u16 yields 65524 (0xFFF4). min(256, 65524) = 256.

Execution reaches check_value. Since value (256) >= 0 and != USB_GADGET_DELAYED_STATUS, the response path is taken. req->length is set to 256, and composite_ep0_queue sends 256 bytes from req->buf to the host, even though bos_desc failed and did not populate the buffer properly.

commit_message:
Fix oobread segfault in java arith8.class ##crash

* Reported by Cen Zhang via huntr.dev
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Memory leak: `attr` is allocated by `r_bin_java_default_attr_new` before the bounds check. If the bounds check `buf_offset + offset + 8 > sz` fails, the function returns NULL without freeing `attr`. The NULL check for `attr` comes *after* this early return, so a non-NULL `attr` is leaked.

step_by_step_execution.description: attr is allocated via r_bin_java_default_attr_new, which returns a non-NULL pointer on success.
offset is incremented by 6.
The bounds check `buf_offset + offset + 8 > sz` evaluates to true (e.g., buf_offset=0, offset=6, sz=10, so 0+6+8=14 > 10).
The function returns NULL without freeing the previously allocated `attr`, causing a memory leak.

vulnerabilities.description: Memory leak: When the final name lookup fails, `icattr->flags_str` (allocated by `retrieve_class_method_access_string`) and `icattr->name` (just allocated by `r_str_dup`) are leaked because only `free(icattr)` is called instead of a proper destructor that also frees the member strings.

step_by_step_execution.description: A new icattr is allocated with R_NEW0 and its fields are populated from the buffer.
icattr->flags_str is set by retrieve_class_method_access_string, allocating a string on the heap.
The first name lookup via inner_name_idx fails (returns NULL for icattr->name).
The fallback lookup via inner_class_info_idx also fails to produce a name.
icattr->name is set to r_str_dup(NULL, "NULL"), allocating a new heap string.
free(icattr) is called, freeing the struct but not icattr->name or icattr->flags_str, leaking both.

commit_message:
fix(plugin): Add default limits for chunks and message size

Based on a reported DoS vulnerability reported by Team82 (Claroty
Research).
cve_desc: The package open62541/open62541 before 1.2.5, from 1.3-rc1 and before 1.3.1 are vulnerable to Denial of Service (DoS) due to a missing limitation on the number of received chunks - per single session or in total for all concurrent sessions. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending an unlimited number of huge chunks (e.g. 2GB each) without sending the Final closing chunk.

commit_message:
cadence_gem: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Alexander Bulekov <alxndr@bu.edu>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

commit_message:
study_chunk: avoid mutating regexp program within GOSUB

gh16947 and gh17743: studying GOSUB may restudy in an inner call
(via a mix of recursion and enframing) something that an outer call
is in the middle of looking at.  Let the outer frame deal with it.

(CVE-2020-12723)

(cherry picked from commit c4033e740bd18d9fbe3456a9db2ec2053cdc5271)
cve_desc: regcomp.c in Perl before 5.30.3 allows a buffer overflow via a crafted regular expression because of recursive S_study_chunk calls.

commit_message:
LZWDecodeCompat(): fix potential index-out-of-bounds write. Fixes http://bugzilla.maptools.org/show_bug.cgi?id=2780 / CVE-2018-8905

The fix consists in using the similar code LZWDecode() to validate we
don't write outside of the output buffer.
cve_desc: In LibTIFF 4.0.9, a heap-based buffer overflow occurs in the function LZWDecodeCompat in tif_lzw.c via a crafted TIFF file, as demonstrated by tiff2ps.

vulnerabilities.description: After receiving a code that is not CODE_EOI and not CODE_CLEAR, the code computes `codep = sp->dec_codetab + code` without validating that `code` is within the bounds of `dec_codetab` (size CSIZE). A malicious LZW stream can provide an arbitrary `code` value, leading to an out-of-bounds access on the `dec_codetab` array. While `free_entp` is bounds-checked, `codep` itself is not, allowing reads of `codep->length`, `codep->value`, `codep->firstchar`, and `codep->next` from out-of-bounds memory. Additionally, `free_entp->value = (codep < free_entp) ? codep->firstchar : free_entp->firstchar;` writes a value derived from this out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the main `while (occ > 0)` loop with valid output buffer space remaining.
NextCode macro reads the next LZW code from the bitstream. A crafted input provides a code value >= CSIZE (e.g., code = 5000 when CSIZE is 4096).
The code is checked against CODE_EOI and CODE_CLEAR, but it is neither, so execution falls through to the general case.
`codep = sp->dec_codetab + code` is computed. Since code (5000) >= CSIZE (4096), codep points past the end of the dec_codetab array.

The free_entp bounds check passes (free_entp is within bounds), so execution continues. `free_entp->next = oldcodep` is set, and `free_entp->value` is computed using `codep->firstchar`, which is an out-of-bounds read from the codep pointer.

state.value: read from out-of-bounds memory at codep->firstchar

step_by_step_execution.description: `oldcodep = codep` is set to the out-of-bounds pointer. Since code >= 256, the code enters the string copy branch and reads `codep->length` from out-of-bounds memory. If this value is non-zero and <= occ, the loop `do { ... *tp = (char)t; codep = codep->next; } while (codep && tp > op)` dereferences out-of-bounds memory for values and next pointers, potentially writing attacker-influenced data to the output buffer or causing further out-of-bounds reads.

commit_message:
study_chunk: avoid mutating regexp program within GOSUB

gh16947 and gh17743: studying GOSUB may restudy in an inner call
(via a mix of recursion and enframing) something that an outer call
is in the middle of looking at.  Let the outer frame deal with it.

(CVE-2020-12723)

(cherry picked from commit c4033e740bd18d9fbe3456a9db2ec2053cdc5271)
cve_desc: regcomp.c in Perl before 5.30.3 allows a buffer overflow via a crafted regular expression because of recursive S_study_chunk calls.

commit_message:
cadence_gem: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Alexander Bulekov <alxndr@bu.edu>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

commit_message:
Avoid an integer overflow in the xbm loader

At the same time, reject some silly input, such as negative
width or height.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=672811
cve_desc: Multiple integer overflows in the read_bitmap_file_data function in io-xbm.c in gdk-pixbuf before 2.26.1 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) via a negative (1) height or (2) width in an XBM file, which triggers a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `size = bytes_per_line * hh`. Both `ww` and `hh` are parsed from attacker-controlled file input as unsigned integers. A crafted bitmap file can specify large width and height values such that their product overflows a 32-bit int, resulting in a small `g_malloc` allocation. The subsequent loop then writes far more data than was allocated, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The file contains a `#define` line setting width to a large value, e.g. 0x40000. `ww` is assigned this value.
The file contains a `#define` line setting height to a large value, e.g. 0x40000. `hh` is assigned this value.
The file contains a `static unsigned char ... = {` line, matching the bits[] pattern. The check `!ww || !hh` passes since both are non-zero.
`bytes_per_line` is computed as `(ww+7)/8 + padding = (262144+7)/8 = 32768`.
`size = bytes_per_line * hh = 32768 * 262144 = 0x200000000`. On a 32-bit int, this overflows to 0 (or a small value). Since `size` is `int`, the result wraps around.
`g_malloc(size)` allocates a very small buffer (or minimal allocation for size 0).

The loop iterates `bytes < size` writing data. If size wrapped to 0, no write occurs (benign case). But with carefully chosen ww/hh values, size can wrap to a small positive value while the actual data written per iteration via ptr increment exceeds the allocation. For example, ww=0x20001, hh=0x10000 gives bytes_per_line=16385, size=16385*65536=1073807360 on 32-bit but this fits. Better example: ww=64, hh=0x10000001 gives bytes_per_line=8, size=8*0x10000001=0x80000008 which as signed int is negative — but size is int and the comparison `bytes < size` would fail. Actually the real danger is when size overflows to a small positive number. E.g. ww=8, hh=0x80000001: bytes_per_line=1, size=0x80000001 which is negative as int, loop won't execute. Let me reconsider: ww=16, hh=0x40000000: bytes_per_line=2, size=0x80000000 (negative, loop skipped). ww=24, hh=0x20000000: bytes_per_line=3, size=0x60000000 (no overflow). Correct overflow: ww=65536, hh=65537: bytes_per_line=8192, size=8192*65537=536879104 (no overflow). Let's try ww=2^17=131072, hh=131073: bpl=16384, size=16384*131073=2147500032 overflows signed int to negative. But since size is int and bytes is int, `bytes < size` with negative size means loop doesn't run. The real risk: if size overflows to a small positive value. E.g. ww=8, bytes_per_line=1, need hh such that hh mod 2^32 is small. hh=0x100000010 won't fit in guint on 32-bit. On 32-bit systems guint is 32 bits so hh max is ~4 billion. bytes_per_line=2 (ww=16), hh=0x80000001=2147483649: size = 2*2147483649 = 4294967298 mod 2^32 = 2 as int. g_malloc(2) allocates 2 bytes. Loop writes 2 bytes — matches allocation. Hmm but ptr is incremented independently... Actually in the non-version10p path, ptr increments with bytes, so it writes exactly `size` bytes. So if size wraps to small value, only small writes happen. But the DATA is wrong/truncated — more of a logic bug. In the version10p path though, ptr advances differently from bytes. bytes increments by 2 each iteration but ptr can advance by 1 or 2 depending on padding. So the ptr could exceed the allocated size.

state.value: small positive value due to overflow
writes up to `size` bytes via ptr, but ptr may advance beyond allocated size in version10p path

commit_message:
openvswitch: fix OOB access in reserve_sfa_size()

Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and
reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, if next_offset is
greater than MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE, the function reserve_sfa_size() does
not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, but it allocates MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE
bytes increasing actions_len by req_size. This can then lead to an OOB
write access, especially when further actions need to be copied.

Fix it by rearranging the flow action size check.

KASAN splat below:

==================================================================
BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in reserve_sfa_size+0x1ba/0x380 [openvswitch]
Write of size 65360 at addr ffff888147e4001c by task handler15/836

CPU: 1 PID: 836 Comm: handler15 Not tainted 5.18.0-rc1+ #27
...

 <TASK>

 ? __mod_node_page_state+0x99/0x120
 ? __mod_lruvec_page_state+0x2e3/0x470
 ? __kasan_kmalloc_large+0x90/0xe0

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: f28cd2af22a0 ("openvswitch: fix flow actions reallocation")
Signed-off-by: Paolo Valerio <pvalerio@redhat.com>
Acked-by: Eelco Chaudron <echaudro@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An integer coercion error was found in the openvswitch kernel module. Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, the reserve_sfa_size() function does not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, potentially leading to an out-of-bounds write access. This flaw allows a local user to crash or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow when computing new_acts_size. The expression `next_offset + req_size` produces a `size_t` result, but it is stored in `int new_acts_size`. If the sum exceeds INT_MAX, the value wraps to a negative integer. A negative `new_acts_size` will pass the `new_acts_size > MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE` check (since the comparison is signed and a negative value is less than MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE), leading to `nla_alloc_flow_actions` being called with a negative/small value. This results in an undersized allocation, and the subsequent `memcpy` of `(*sfa)->actions_len` bytes into the undersized buffer causes a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: req_size is computed as NLA_ALIGN(attr_len), a size_t value. next_offset is computed as the header offset plus the current actions_len.

state.value: NLA_ALIGN(attr_len), a large size_t if attr_len is large

step_by_step_execution.description: The fast-path check `req_size <= (ksize(*sfa) - next_offset)` fails because there is not enough room, so we proceed to reallocation.

state.value: large value exceeding remaining space in current buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: new_acts_size is computed as max(next_offset + req_size, ksize(*sfa) * 2). The sum `next_offset + req_size` is a size_t that can exceed INT_MAX. Storing this in `int new_acts_size` causes signed integer overflow/truncation, wrapping to a small or negative value.

state.value: negative or small int due to truncation from size_t to int

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `new_acts_size > MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE` is evaluated with signed comparison. Since new_acts_size is negative/small, the check is false, and the cap logic is skipped entirely.

state.value: negative or small int, less than MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE

step_by_step_execution.description: nla_alloc_flow_actions is called with the small/negative new_acts_size, allocating an undersized buffer (or if the function casts to unsigned, a huge allocation that may fail—but if it allocates a small buffer, the overflow proceeds).
memcpy copies (*sfa)->actions_len bytes from the old actions into the new undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
sctp: Fix kernel panic while process protocol violation parameter

Since call to function sctp_sf_abort_violation() need paramter 'arg' with
'struct sctp_chunk' type, it will read the chunk type and chunk length from
the chunk_hdr member of chunk. But call to sctp_sf_violation_paramlen()
always with 'struct sctp_paramhdr' type's parameter, it will be passed to
sctp_sf_abort_violation(). This may cause kernel panic.

   sctp_sf_violation_paramlen()
     |-- sctp_sf_abort_violation()
        |-- sctp_make_abort_violation()

This patch fixed this problem. This patch also fix two place which called
sctp_sf_violation_paramlen() with wrong paramter type.

Signed-off-by: Wei Yongjun <yjwei@cn.fujitsu.com>
Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: The Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel before 2.6.27 does not properly handle a protocol violation in which a parameter has an invalid length, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) via unspecified vectors, related to sctp_sf_violation_paramlen, sctp_sf_abort_violation, sctp_make_abort_violation, and incorrect data types in function calls.

commit_message:
hfsplus: fix Buffer overflow with a corrupted image

When an hfsplus image gets corrupted it might happen that the catalog
namelength field gets b0rked.  If we mount such an image the memcpy() in
hfsplus_cat_build_key_uni() writes more than the 255 that fit in the name
field.  Depending on the size of the overwritten data, we either only get
memory corruption or also trigger an oops like this:

commit_message:
hfsplus: fix Buffer overflow with a corrupted image

When an hfsplus image gets corrupted it might happen that the catalog
namelength field gets b0rked.  If we mount such an image the memcpy() in
hfsplus_cat_build_key_uni() writes more than the 255 that fit in the name
field.  Depending on the size of the overwritten data, we either only get
memory corruption or also trigger an oops like this:

[  221.628020] BUG: unable to handle kernel paging request at c82b0000
