cve_desc: Buffer overflow in the hfsplus_find_cat function in fs/hfsplus/catalog.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption or system crash) via an hfsplus filesystem image with an invalid catalog namelength field, related to the hfsplus_cat_build_key_uni function.

commit_message:
i2c: ismt: prevent memory corruption in ismt_access()

The "data->block[0]" variable comes from the user and is a number
between 0-255.  It needs to be capped to prevent writing beyond the end
of dma_buffer[].

Fixes: 5e9a97b1f449 ("i2c: ismt: Adding support for I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL")
Reported-and-tested-by: Zheyu Ma <zheyuma97@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Mika Westerberg <mika.westerberg@linux.intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel Intel’s iSMT SMBus host controller driver in the way it handled the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case (via the ioctl I2C_SMBUS) with malicious input data. This flaw could allow a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write case, `dma_size` is declared as `u8` and computed as `data->block[0] + 1`. If `data->block[0]` is 255, the addition overflows the u8 to 0. Later, `memcpy(&dma_buffer[1], &data->block[1], dma_size - 1)` is called. With `dma_size` being 0 (u8), the expression `dma_size - 1` is promoted to `size_t`, resulting in SIZE_MAX, causing a massive out-of-bounds memory copy. Even without the overflow, there is no validation that `data->block[0]` is within `I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX` bounds (unlike the `I2C_SMBUS_I2C_BLOCK_DATA` case which explicitly validates it), so values between 33 and 254 would also overflow the DMA buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with size = I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA, read_write = I2C_SMBUS_WRITE, and data->block[0] = 255.
dma_size is declared as u8 and initialized to 0.
Execution enters the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write branch. dma_size is computed as data->block[0] + 1 = 255 + 1 = 256, which overflows u8 to 0.
memcpy is called with size argument dma_size - 1. Since dma_size is 0 (u8), the expression (0 - 1) is promoted to size_t, yielding SIZE_MAX (~18 exabytes on 64-bit).
memcpy copies a massive amount of memory from data->block[1] into dma_buffer[1], causing a catastrophic out-of-bounds write that corrupts kernel memory and likely crashes the system.

vulnerabilities.description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case, data->block[0] is used directly as the size for memcpy without any bounds validation. If data->block[0] exceeds the size of the DMA buffer (which is derived from priv->buffer), this causes an out-of-bounds write into kernel memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with size = I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL, and data->block[0] set to a value larger than what the DMA buffer can hold (e.g., 255).
Execution enters the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL branch. There is no validation of data->block[0].
memcpy copies data->block[0] (255) bytes from data->block[1] into dma_buffer[1], writing far beyond the bounds of the DMA buffer.

commit_message:
grep -F: fix a heap buffer (read) overrun

grep's read buffer is often filled to its full size, except when
reading the final buffer of a file.  In that case, the number of
bytes read may be far less than the size of the buffer.  However, for
certain unusual pattern/text combinations, grep -F would mistakenly
examine bytes in that uninitialized region of memory when searching
for a match.  With carefully chosen inputs, one can cause grep -F to
read beyond the end of that buffer altogether.  This problem arose via
commit v2.18-90-g73893ff with the introduction of a more efficient
heuristic using what is now the memchr_kwset function. The use of
that function in bmexec_trans could leave TP much larger than EP,
and the subsequent call to bm_delta2_search would mistakenly access
beyond end of the main input read buffer.

* src/kwset.c (bmexec_trans): When TP reaches or exceeds EP,
do not call bm_delta2_search.
* tests/kwset-abuse: New file.
* tests/Makefile.am (TESTS): Add it.
* THANKS.in: Update.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.

Prior to this patch, this command would trigger a UMR:

  printf %0360db 0 | valgrind src/grep -F $(printf %019dXb 0)

  Use of uninitialised value of size 8
     at 0x4142BE: bmexec_trans (kwset.c:657)
     by 0x4143CA: bmexec (kwset.c:678)
     by 0x414973: kwsexec (kwset.c:848)
     by 0x414DC4: Fexecute (kwsearch.c:128)
     by 0x404E2E: grepbuf (grep.c:1238)
     by 0x4054BF: grep (grep.c:1417)
     by 0x405CEB: grepdesc (grep.c:1645)
     by 0x405EC1: grep_command_line_arg (grep.c:1692)
     by 0x4077D4: main (grep.c:2570)

See the accompanying test for how to trigger the heap buffer overrun.

Thanks to Nima Aghdaii for testing and finding numerous
ways to break early iterations of this patch.
cve_desc: The bmexec_trans function in kwset.c in grep 2.19 through 2.21 allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds heap read and crash) via crafted input when using the -F option.

commit_message: Fixed miscomputation bugs in secp-256r1 modulo functions.
cve_desc: The ecc_256_modp function in ecc-256.c in Nettle before 3.2 does not properly handle carry propagation and produces incorrect output in its implementation of the P-256 NIST elliptic curve, which allows attackers to have unspecified impact via unknown vectors, a different vulnerability than CVE-2015-8805.

commit_message: Fix bug #72606: heap-buffer-overflow (write) simplestring_addn simplestring.c
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the simplestring_addn function in simplestring.c in xmlrpc-epi through 0.54.2, as used in PHP before 5.5.38, 5.6.x before 5.6.24, and 7.x before 7.0.9, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a long first argument to the PHP xmlrpc_encode_request function.

vulnerabilities.description: The `add_len` parameter is a signed `int`. If a negative value is passed, the size check `target->len + add_len + 1 > target->size` will evaluate to false (no realloc occurs), but `memcpy` implicitly converts the negative `add_len` to a very large `size_t`, causing a massive heap buffer overflow. Similarly, if `target->len + add_len + 1` overflows the `int` range to a negative value, the reallocation check is bypassed, leading to a heap buffer overflow when memcpy writes past the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a valid target (str initialized, e.g., len=0, size=64) and a negative add_len, e.g., add_len = -1.
The null checks pass: target and source are non-null, target->str is non-null.
The reallocation check evaluates: target->len + add_len + 1 = 0 + (-1) + 1 = 0, which is NOT > 64, so the reallocation block is skipped.

target->str is non-null, and add_len is non-zero (-1), so memcpy is called. The int value -1 is implicitly converted to size_t, yielding SIZE_MAX (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF or 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF), causing a massive out-of-bounds heap read from source and write into target->str.

commit_message: libnsgif: fix oob in lzw_decode
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.12.0.

vulnerabilities.description: When code_new == current_entry and current_entry >= (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX), the code takes the 'code not in table' path but the dictionary write is skipped (guarded by the size check). However, the code still accesses table[code_new] (i.e., table[current_entry]) when setting ctx->previous_code_first, which is an out-of-bounds read since the table only has (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) entries (indices 0 to (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) - 1).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads current_entry from ctx. Over many prior calls, current_entry has been incremented to reach (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX).
A new code is read from the input stream via lzw__next_code. The attacker-controlled input provides code_new == current_entry.

The check code_new == clear_code fails, code_new == eoi_code fails, code_new > current_entry fails (they are equal). code_new < current_entry also fails (they are equal). So the else branch ('code not in table') is taken.
The dictionary add is guarded by current_entry < (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX). Since current_entry == (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX), the condition is false and the entry is NOT written to the table.

The code executes ctx->previous_code_first = table[code_new].first_value, which reads table[(1 << LZW_CODE_MAX)]. The table has valid indices 0 through (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) - 1, so this is an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX), which is one past the end of the table

commit_message:
Fix possible NULL dereference (savannah bug #55369)

* src/sparse.c (pax_decode_header): Check return from find_next_block.
cve_desc: pax_decode_header in sparse.c in GNU Tar before 1.32 had a NULL pointer dereference when parsing certain archives that have malformed extended headers.

commit_message: Fixed miscomputation bugs in secp-256r1 modulo functions.
cve_desc: The ecc_256_modp function in ecc-256.c in Nettle before 3.2 does not properly handle carry propagation and produces incorrect output in its implementation of the P-256 NIST elliptic curve, which allows attackers to have unspecified impact via unknown vectors, a different vulnerability than CVE-2015-8805.

commit_message:
Fix possible NULL dereference (savannah bug #55369)

* src/sparse.c (pax_decode_header): Check return from find_next_block.
cve_desc: pax_decode_header in sparse.c in GNU Tar before 1.32 had a NULL pointer dereference when parsing certain archives that have malformed extended headers.

commit_message:
Submitted by: Tomas Hoger <thoger@redhat.com>

Fix for CVE-2010-0433 where some kerberos enabled versions of OpenSSL
could be crashed if the relevant tables were not present (e.g. chrooted).
cve_desc: The kssl_keytab_is_available function in ssl/kssl.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8n, when Kerberos is enabled but Kerberos configuration files cannot be opened, does not check a certain return value, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and daemon crash) via SSL cipher negotiation, as demonstrated by a chroot installation of Dovecot or stunnel without Kerberos configuration files inside the chroot.

commit_message: Fix bug #72750: wddx_deserialize null dereference
cve_desc: The php_wddx_pop_element function in ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid base64 binary value, as demonstrated by a wddx_deserialize call that mishandles a binary element in a wddxPacket XML document.

vulnerabilities.description: php_base64_decode can return NULL on failure. The old string is freed via STR_FREE, then the NULL pointer is stored as the zval's string value without any validation. Subsequent operations that access this string value (e.g., when the element is inserted into a hash table and later used) will dereference a NULL pointer, causing a crash or potential information disclosure.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is entered via XML parser callback for a closing element tag named 'binary'. stack->top > 0.
ent1 is retrieved from the top of the stack. ent1->data is non-NULL and contains a zval of type IS_STRING with invalid base64 content.

state.value: pointer to string with invalid base64 data

step_by_step_execution.description: The name matches EL_BINARY, so php_base64_decode is called. Because the input is invalid base64, php_base64_decode returns NULL and new_len is set to 0.
STR_FREE frees the old string value of ent1->data. Then the NULL new_str is assigned as the new string value.

Processing continues. The zval with a NULL string pointer is inserted into a parent hash table via zend_hash_next_index_insert or zend_symtable_update. Any subsequent access to this string value will dereference NULL.

vulnerabilities.description: The NULL check on ent2->data only applies when ent2->type is ST_FIELD. If ent2->data is NULL for any other entry type (e.g., ST_ARRAY, ST_STRUCT), the code falls through to Z_TYPE_P(ent2->data) which dereferences the NULL pointer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is entered for a closing element (e.g., EL_STRING). stack->top > 1.
ent1 is retrieved from the top of the stack. ent1->data is non-NULL.
stack->top is decremented and ent2 is retrieved. ent2->type is not ST_FIELD (e.g., ST_STRUCT or ST_ARRAY) but ent2->data is NULL due to a prior failed initialization.
The NULL guard check fails because ent2->type != ST_FIELD, so execution continues to Z_TYPE_P(ent2->data) which dereferences the NULL pointer.

commit_message: libclamav/mspack.c: fix Quantum decompressor (bb#1771)
cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

vulnerabilities.description: In the match-offset-wrapping case (match_offset > window_posn), the copy length `i` (initialized to `match_length`) is never clamped against the remaining space in the window (`window_size - window_posn`). This allows writing past the end of the `window` buffer. The non-wrapping else branch has this clamp, but the wrapping branch does not.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry. `out_bytes` is positive and there are no stored-up bytes, so decompression loop is entered.
Local state is restored. Assume `window_posn` is close to `window_size`, e.g. `window_size = 4096`, `window_posn = 4090`.

Inner loop iterates. A selector >= 4 is decoded (e.g. selector 6, variable length match). `match_length` is decoded to a value like 20. `match_offset` is decoded to a value greater than `window_posn`, e.g. 4095.
`rundest` is set to `&window[4090]`. `i` is set to `match_length` = 20. Since `match_offset (4095) > window_posn (4090)`, the wrapping branch is taken.

`j = match_offset - window_posn = 5`. Check `j > window_size` fails (5 < 4096). `runsrc = &window[4096 - 5] = &window[4091]`. Since `j (5) < i (20)`, the split-copy path is taken: first copy 5 bytes (i becomes 15), then `runsrc = window`.

Second copy loop runs 15 times: `while (i-- > 0) *rundest++ = *runsrc++`. `rundest` starts at `&window[4095]` and advances 15 bytes, writing up to `&window[4109]`. Since `window` has size 4096, this writes 9 bytes beyond the buffer boundary.

commit_message: squash heap-buffer-overflow, PoC TIFF from Hardik
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow issue was found in ImageMagick. When an application processes a malformed TIFF file, it could lead to undefined behavior or a crash causing a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of 'extent' for strip pixel buffer allocation in ReadStripMethod. The multiplication of 4 * ((image->depth+7)/8) * (samples_per_pixel+1) * TIFFStripSize(tiff) can wrap around, resulting in a small allocation. Subsequent TIFFReadEncodedStrip reads TIFFStripSize(tiff) bytes into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: TIFF fields are read: bits_per_sample (e.g. 32), samples_per_pixel (e.g. 32, max is MaxPixelChannels=32). image->depth is set to bits_per_sample.
The method is determined to be ReadStripMethod based on rows_per_strip being present.

extent is calculated: 4 * ((32+7)/8) * (32+1) * TIFFStripSize(tiff) = 4 * 4 * 33 * TIFFStripSize(tiff) = 528 * TIFFStripSize(tiff). If TIFFStripSize returns a large value (e.g., on a 32-bit system or with size_t=32 bits, ~8MB strip), 528 * 8388608 = 4,429,185,024 which overflows a 32-bit size_t to a small value.

state.value: wraps around to a small value due to integer overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: AcquireQuantumMemory allocates extent * sizeof(*strip_pixels) bytes. Because extent wrapped around, this is a small allocation.

state.value: points to a small heap buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: TIFFReadEncodedStrip reads up to TIFFStripSize(tiff) bytes (the original large value) into the undersized strip_pixels buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of 'extent' for tile pixel buffer allocation in ReadTileMethod. The multiplication of 4 * (samples_per_pixel+1) * MagickMax(rows*TIFFTileRowSize, TIFFTileSize) can wrap around, resulting in a small allocation. Subsequent TIFFReadTile writes into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: TIFF fields are read. samples_per_pixel can be up to 32 (MaxPixelChannels). Tile dimensions are obtained via TIFFGetField.
Method is determined to be ReadTileMethod because TIFFIsTiled returns true.

extent is calculated: 4*(32+1)*MagickMax(rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff), TIFFTileSize(tiff)) = 132 * large_value. With large tile sizes and samples, this multiplication overflows size_t, resulting in a small value.
AcquireQuantumMemory(extent, sizeof(*tile_pixels)) allocates a buffer far too small for actual tile data.
TIFFReadTile writes the full tile data into tile_pixels, overflowing the undersized buffer.

commit_message:
Remove 'umask' calls from 'mkdir'.

Fixes <http://bugs.gnu.org/24659>.

* libguile/filesys.c (SCM_DEFINE): Remove calls to 'umask' when MODE is
unbound; instead, use 0777 as the mode.  Update docstring to clarify
this.
* doc/ref/posix.texi (File System): Adjust accordingly.
* NEWS: Mention it.
cve_desc: The mkdir procedure of GNU Guile temporarily changed the process' umask to zero. During that time window, in a multithreaded application, other threads could end up creating files with insecure permissions. For example, mkdir without the optional mode argument would create directories as 0777. This is fixed in Guile 2.0.13. Prior versions are affected.

commit_message:
grep -F: fix a heap buffer (read) overrun

grep's read buffer is often filled to its full size, except when
reading the final buffer of a file.  In that case, the number of
bytes read may be far less than the size of the buffer.  However, for
certain unusual pattern/text combinations, grep -F would mistakenly
examine bytes in that uninitialized region of memory when searching
for a match.  With carefully chosen inputs, one can cause grep -F to
read beyond the end of that buffer altogether.  This problem arose via
commit v2.18-90-g73893ff with the introduction of a more efficient
heuristic using what is now the memchr_kwset function. The use of
that function in bmexec_trans could leave TP much larger than EP,
and the subsequent call to bm_delta2_search would mistakenly access
beyond end of the main input read buffer.

* src/kwset.c (bmexec_trans): When TP reaches or exceeds EP,
do not call bm_delta2_search.
* tests/kwset-abuse: New file.
* tests/Makefile.am (TESTS): Add it.
* THANKS.in: Update.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.

Prior to this patch, this command would trigger a UMR:

  printf %0360db 0 | valgrind src/grep -F $(printf %019dXb 0)

  Use of uninitialised value of size 8
     at 0x4142BE: bmexec_trans (kwset.c:657)
     by 0x4143CA: bmexec (kwset.c:678)
     by 0x414973: kwsexec (kwset.c:848)
     by 0x414DC4: Fexecute (kwsearch.c:128)
     by 0x404E2E: grepbuf (grep.c:1238)
     by 0x4054BF: grep (grep.c:1417)
     by 0x405CEB: grepdesc (grep.c:1645)
     by 0x405EC1: grep_command_line_arg (grep.c:1692)
     by 0x4077D4: main (grep.c:2570)

See the accompanying test for how to trigger the heap buffer overrun.

Thanks to Nima Aghdaii for testing and finding numerous
ways to break early iterations of this patch.
cve_desc: The bmexec_trans function in kwset.c in grep 2.19 through 2.21 allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds heap read and crash) via crafted input when using the -F option.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable 'len' is declared as 'int' but kwset->mind may be a size_t or unsigned value. If kwset->mind is larger than INT_MAX, 'len' will overflow to a negative or unexpected value, bypassing the safety checks (len == 0, len > size) and leading to out-of-bounds memory accesses when used as an offset (e.g., tp = text + len).

step_by_step_execution.description: kwset->mind is assigned to 'int len'. If kwset->mind is a large value (e.g., greater than INT_MAX), this causes integer overflow.

state.value: negative value due to signed overflow, e.g. -2147483647

step_by_step_execution.description: The check 'if (len == 0)' is false since len is negative.

The check 'if (len > size)' compares signed int to size_t. A negative len is converted to a large unsigned value, but depending on compiler behavior with signed/unsigned comparison, if size is small this may or may not catch it. In many implementations negative int compared to size_t gets implicitly converted to a large unsigned, so this check may pass (return -1). However, if size is also very large, the check passes through.
tp = text + len uses a negative offset, causing tp to point before the text buffer, leading to out-of-bounds read when tp[-1] is accessed.

commit_message:
CPU time limit patch / setrlimit(RLIMIT_CPU, 0) cheat fix

As discovered here today, the change in Kernel 2.6.17 intended to inhibit
users from setting RLIMIT_CPU to 0 (as that is equivalent to unlimited) by
"cheating" and setting it to 1 in such a case, does not make a difference,
as the check is done in the wrong place (too late), and only applies to the
profiling code.

On all systems I checked running kernels above 2.6.17, no matter what the
hard and soft CPU time limits were before, a user could escape them by
issuing in the shell (sh/bash/zsh) "ulimit -t 0", and then the user's
process was not ever killed.

Attached is a trivial patch to fix that.  Simply moving the check to a
slightly earlier location (specifically, before the line that actually
assigns the limit - *old_rlim = new_rlim), does the trick.

Do note that at least the zsh (but not ash, dash, or bash) shell has the
problem of "caching" the limits set by the ulimit command, so when running
zsh the fix will not immediately be evident - after entering "ulimit -t 0",
"ulimit -a" will show "-t: cpu time (seconds) 0", even though the actual
limit as returned by getrlimit(...) will be 1.  It can be verified by
opening a subshell (which will not have the values of the parent shell in
cache) and checking in it, or just by running a CPU intensive command like
"echo '65536^1048576' | bc" and verifying that it dumps core after one
second.

Regardless of whether that is a misfeature in the shell, perhaps it would
be better to return -EINVAL from setrlimit in such a case instead of
cheating and setting to 1, as that does not really reflect the actual state
of the process anymore.  I do not however know what the ground for that
decision was in the original 2.6.17 change, and whether there would be any
"backward" compatibility issues, so I preferred not to touch that right
now.

Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: Linux kernel 2.6.17, and other versions before 2.6.22, does not check when a user attempts to set RLIMIT_CPU to 0 until after the change is made, which allows local users to bypass intended resource limits.

vulnerabilities.description: TOCTOU race condition: The permission check reading old_rlim->rlim_max is performed without holding task_lock, but the actual write to *old_rlim happens later under task_lock. A concurrent thread calling sys_setrlimit on the same resource can lower rlim_max after the first thread's permission check passes but before it writes the new value. This allows an unprivileged process to set rlim_max higher than the current limit without CAP_SYS_RESOURCE.

step_by_step_execution.description: Thread A (unprivileged) and Thread B (unprivileged) both call sys_setrlimit on the same resource. Assume current rlim_max is 100.

Thread A calls sys_setrlimit with new_rlim.rlim_max = 100 (permitted, equal to current). Thread A passes the permission check since new_rlim.rlim_max (100) is not greater than old_rlim->rlim_max (100).

Thread B calls sys_setrlimit with new_rlim.rlim_max = 50. Thread B passes the permission check since 50 <= 100. Thread B acquires task_lock and writes *old_rlim = new_rlim, lowering rlim_max to 50. Thread B releases task_lock.

Now Thread A (which already passed the permission check when rlim_max was 100) proceeds to acquire task_lock and writes *old_rlim = new_rlim with rlim_max = 100, effectively raising rlim_max from 50 back to 100 without CAP_SYS_RESOURCE.
By repeating this pattern, an unprivileged process can ratchet rlim_max upward arbitrarily, bypassing the intended privilege check.

state.value: can be raised beyond originally permitted value

commit_message:
auth: Don't crash expanding %variables when username isn't set.

This continues the auth-policy fix in
c3d3faa4f72a676e183f34be960cff13a5a725ae
cve_desc: The auth component in Dovecot before 2.2.27, when auth-policy is configured, allows a remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) by aborting authentication without setting a username.

commit_message: Fix bug #72606: heap-buffer-overflow (write) simplestring_addn simplestring.c
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the simplestring_addn function in simplestring.c in xmlrpc-epi through 0.54.2, as used in PHP before 5.5.38, 5.6.x before 5.6.24, and 7.x before 7.0.9, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a long first argument to the PHP xmlrpc_encode_request function.

vulnerabilities.description: The pointer `target` is dereferenced to access `target->size` on the very first line of the function, before the null check `if(target && source)` on line 3. If `target` is NULL, this is undefined behavior (null pointer dereference).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function `simplestring_addn` is called with `target` set to NULL.
The first statement dereferences `target` to read `target->size`, which is a null pointer dereference causing undefined behavior.
The null check `if(target && source)` on the next line would have prevented this, but it comes too late.

vulnerabilities.description: If `realloc` returns NULL (allocation failure), the original pointer `target->str` is overwritten with NULL, losing the only reference to the previously allocated memory. This causes a memory leak and loss of all previously stored data in the simplestring. Subsequent operations on this simplestring will behave as if it was never initialized.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a valid `target` that has an existing allocated `str` buffer, and `add_len` requires a larger buffer.

state.value: valid heap pointer to existing data
some value S where S < N + add_len + 1

step_by_step_execution.description: The code determines a reallocation is needed and computes `newsize`.
`realloc` is called but fails and returns NULL. The result is directly assigned to `target->str`, overwriting the original pointer.

commit_message:
auth: Don't crash expanding %variables when username isn't set.

This continues the auth-policy fix in
c3d3faa4f72a676e183f34be960cff13a5a725ae
cve_desc: The auth component in Dovecot before 2.2.27, when auth-policy is configured, allows a remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) by aborting authentication without setting a username.

commit_message: fix oob write bug inside libmobi
cve_desc: libmobi is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Write

vulnerabilities.description: When offset is negative, the code only checks that `buf->offset >= aoffset` (to prevent source pointer underflow), but it never validates that `buf->offset + len <= buf->maxlen` or that the source region `(buf->offset - aoffset + len)` stays within the buffer. This allows an out-of-bounds read from `source` and an out-of-bounds write to the destination via `memmove`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a negative offset (e.g., offset = -1) and a large len (e.g., len = buf->maxlen).
aoffset is computed as abs(-1) = 1. source is set to buf->data + buf->offset (512).
offset < 0, so the else branch is taken. The check `buf->offset < aoffset` evaluates to `512 < 1`, which is false, so execution continues.
source is decremented by aoffset: source = buf->data + 512 - 1 = buf->data + 511.

memmove is called: memmove(buf->data + 512, buf->data + 511, 1024). The source read region is [511, 1535) and the destination write region is [512, 1536), both exceeding buf->maxlen of 1024. This is an out-of-bounds read and write.

commit_message:
sctp: correct bounds check in sctp_setsockopt_auth_key

The bonds check to prevent buffer overlflow was not exactly
right.  It still allowed overflow of up to 8 bytes which is
sizeof(struct sctp_authkey).

Since optlen is already checked against the size of that struct,
we are guaranteed not to cause interger overflow either.

Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the sctp_setsockopt_auth_key function in net/sctp/socket.c in the Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel 2.6.24-rc1 through 2.6.26.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted sca_keylength field associated with the SCTP_AUTH_KEY option.

vulnerabilities.description: The validation of `sca_keylength` checks against `optlen` directly, but the key data starts after the `struct sctp_authkey` header. The correct check should be `sca_keylength > optlen - sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)`. An attacker can set `sca_keylength` to a value larger than the actual key bytes provided (but still <= optlen), causing `sctp_auth_set_key` to read beyond the valid key data into uninitialized heap memory within the `authkey` allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function checks that optlen > sizeof(struct sctp_authkey). Suppose sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) is 8 (containing assoc_id, key_id, key_length fields). Attacker passes optlen = 12.
kmalloc allocates 12 bytes for authkey.
copy_from_user copies 12 bytes from userspace into authkey. The actual key data occupies bytes after the header, i.e., 4 bytes of key data (optlen - sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) = 4).
Attacker sets authkey->sca_keylength = 12. The check `sca_keylength > optlen` evaluates to `12 > 12` which is false, so validation passes.

sctp_auth_set_key is called and will process 12 bytes of key data starting from the key field inside the struct, but only 4 bytes are valid user-provided key data. The remaining 8 bytes read beyond the key data into uninitialized heap memory within the allocated buffer (or the header fields themselves are misinterpreted as key material).

commit_message:
Prevent over long nonces in ChaCha20-Poly1305

ChaCha20-Poly1305 is an AEAD cipher, and requires a unique nonce input for
every encryption operation. RFC 7539 specifies that the nonce value (IV)
should be 96 bits (12 bytes). OpenSSL allows a variable nonce length and
front pads the nonce with 0 bytes if it is less than 12 bytes. However it
also incorrectly allows a nonce to be set of up to 16 bytes. In this case
only the last 12 bytes are significant and any additional leading bytes are
ignored.

It is a requirement of using this cipher that nonce values are unique.
Messages encrypted using a reused nonce value are susceptible to serious
confidentiality and integrity attacks. If an application changes the
default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes and then makes a change to
the leading bytes of the nonce expecting the new value to be a new unique
nonce then such an application could inadvertently encrypt messages with a
reused nonce.

Additionally the ignored bytes in a long nonce are not covered by the
integrity guarantee of this cipher. Any application that relies on the
integrity of these ignored leading bytes of a long nonce may be further
affected.

Any OpenSSL internal use of this cipher, including in SSL/TLS, is safe
because no such use sets such a long nonce value. However user
applications that use this cipher directly and set a non-default nonce
length to be longer than 12 bytes may be vulnerable.

CVE-2019-1543

Fixes #8345

Reviewed-by: Paul Dale <paul.dale@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Richard Levitte <levitte@openssl.org>
(Merged from https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/8406)

(cherry picked from commit 2a3d0ee9d59156c48973592331404471aca886d6)
cve_desc: ChaCha20-Poly1305 is an AEAD cipher, and requires a unique nonce input for every encryption operation. RFC 7539 specifies that the nonce value (IV) should be 96 bits (12 bytes). OpenSSL allows a variable nonce length and front pads the nonce with 0 bytes if it is less than 12 bytes. However it also incorrectly allows a nonce to be set of up to 16 bytes. In this case only the last 12 bytes are significant and any additional leading bytes are ignored. It is a requirement of using this cipher that nonce values are unique. Messages encrypted using a reused nonce value are susceptible to serious confidentiality and integrity attacks. If an application changes the default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes and then makes a change to the leading bytes of the nonce expecting the new value to be a new unique nonce then such an application could inadvertently encrypt messages with a reused nonce. Additionally the ignored bytes in a long nonce are not covered by the integrity guarantee of this cipher. Any application that relies on the integrity of these ignored leading bytes of a long nonce may be further affected. Any OpenSSL internal use of this cipher, including in SSL/TLS, is safe because no such use sets such a long nonce value. However user applications that use this cipher directly and set a non-default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes may be vulnerable. OpenSSL versions 1.1.1 and 1.1.0 are affected by this issue. Due to the limited scope of affected deployments this has been assessed as low severity and therefore we are not creating new releases at this time. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1c (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1b). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.0k (Affected 1.1.0-1.1.0j).

commit_message:
Prevent an invalid memory writes in compileRule

Thanks to Han Zheng for reporting it

Fixes #1214
cve_desc: Liblouis 3.21.0 has an out-of-bounds write in compileRule in compileTranslationTable.c, as demonstrated by lou_trace.

vulnerabilities.description: During macro expansion, when substituting arguments into `tmpFile.line`, there is no bounds check against `MAXSTRING` before writing argument characters. While individual definition characters are checked against `MAXSTRING`, the argument substitution loop copies the entire argument without verifying that `tmpFile.linelen + arg.length` stays within the bounds of `tmpFile.line`. This can cause a heap or stack buffer overflow depending on how `FileInfo.line` is allocated.

code_snippets:
for (int j = 0; j < arg.length; j++)
										tmpFile.line[tmpFile.linelen++] = arg.chars[j];

step_by_step_execution.description: The opcode is CTO_None or CTO_UpLow, and a matching macro `m` is found in `inScopeMacros`.

state.value: a Macro with argument_count >= 1 and a definition that places a substitution near end of a line

step_by_step_execution.description: Arguments are allocated and read. A macro argument `token` with a large length (close to MAXSTRING) is read.

state.value: CharsString with length close to MAXSTRING

step_by_step_execution.description: The inner loop processes definition characters one by one, checking MAXSTRING for each individual character. `tmpFile.linelen` is incremented up to some value less than MAXSTRING.

The code reaches a substitution point and copies the entire argument into tmpFile.line without any bounds check. Since arg.length can be large and tmpFile.linelen is already near MAXSTRING, the writes go past the end of tmpFile.line.

state.value: starts at MAXSTRING - 1, incremented by arg.length (e.g. up to 2*MAXSTRING - 1)
large value, e.g. close to MAXSTRING

commit_message: auth: mech-rpa - Fail on zero len buffer
cve_desc: In Dovecot before 2.3.11.3, sending a specially formatted RPA request will crash the auth service because a length of zero is mishandled.

commit_message:
CPU time limit patch / setrlimit(RLIMIT_CPU, 0) cheat fix

As discovered here today, the change in Kernel 2.6.17 intended to inhibit
users from setting RLIMIT_CPU to 0 (as that is equivalent to unlimited) by
"cheating" and setting it to 1 in such a case, does not make a difference,
as the check is done in the wrong place (too late), and only applies to the
profiling code.

On all systems I checked running kernels above 2.6.17, no matter what the
hard and soft CPU time limits were before, a user could escape them by
issuing in the shell (sh/bash/zsh) "ulimit -t 0", and then the user's
process was not ever killed.

Attached is a trivial patch to fix that.  Simply moving the check to a
slightly earlier location (specifically, before the line that actually
assigns the limit - *old_rlim = new_rlim), does the trick.

Do note that at least the zsh (but not ash, dash, or bash) shell has the
problem of "caching" the limits set by the ulimit command, so when running
zsh the fix will not immediately be evident - after entering "ulimit -t 0",
"ulimit -a" will show "-t: cpu time (seconds) 0", even though the actual
limit as returned by getrlimit(...) will be 1.  It can be verified by
opening a subshell (which will not have the values of the parent shell in
cache) and checking in it, or just by running a CPU intensive command like
"echo '65536^1048576' | bc" and verifying that it dumps core after one
second.

Regardless of whether that is a misfeature in the shell, perhaps it would
be better to return -EINVAL from setrlimit in such a case instead of
cheating and setting to 1, as that does not really reflect the actual state
of the process anymore.  I do not however know what the ground for that
decision was in the original 2.6.17 change, and whether there would be any
"backward" compatibility issues, so I preferred not to touch that right
now.

Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: Linux kernel 2.6.17, and other versions before 2.6.22, does not check when a user attempts to set RLIMIT_CPU to 0 until after the change is made, which allows local users to bypass intended resource limits.

vulnerabilities.description: There is a TOCTOU race condition between reading old_rlim->rlim_max for the permission check and writing the new rlimit value. The permission check (new_rlim.rlim_max > old_rlim->rlim_max) is performed without holding any lock, but the actual write is done under task_lock. Two threads sharing the same signal struct can race: Thread A lowers rlim_max while Thread B reads the old (higher) rlim_max, passes the capability check, and then writes a rlim_max higher than the value Thread A just set, effectively bypassing the privilege check.

step_by_step_execution.description: Thread B enters sys_setrlimit with a new_rlim where rlim_max equals the current old rlim_max (e.g., 1024). The permission check reads old_rlim->rlim_max without holding any lock.
Thread A concurrently calls sys_setrlimit and successfully lowers rlim_max by writing under task_lock. Thread A's write completes, reducing rlim_max to 512.
Thread B's permission check already passed (it saw 1024 >= 1024), so it proceeds to acquire task_lock and writes its new_rlim with rlim_max=1024, overwriting Thread A's lower limit.

state.value: 1024 (Thread B overwrites with the stale-checked value)

step_by_step_execution.description: The result is that an unprivileged process raised rlim_max from 512 back to 1024, bypassing the check that should prevent non-CAP_SYS_RESOURCE processes from increasing hard limits.

commit_message:
sftp: Fix bug in sftp_mkdir not returning on error.

resolves: #84
(cherry picked from commit a92c97b2e17715c1b3cdd693d14af6c3311d8e44)
cve_desc: Multiple double free vulnerabilities in the (1) agent_sign_data function in agent.c, (2) channel_request function in channels.c, (3) ssh_userauth_pubkey function in auth.c, (4) sftp_parse_attr_3 function in sftp.c, and (5) try_publickey_from_file function in keyfiles.c in libssh before 0.5.3 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via unspecified vectors.

commit_message: imptcp: fix Segmentation Fault when octet count is to high
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in octet count parsing can lead to a negative iOctetsRemain value. When this negative value is later used as the size for memcpy (cast to size_t, becoming a very large unsigned value), it causes a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a digit character while inputState is eAtStrtFram. The state transitions to eInOctetCnt and iOctetsRemain is initialized to 0.

Over many successive calls with digit characters, iOctetsRemain is accumulated via iOctetsRemain * 10 + c - '0'. With enough digits (e.g., 11+ nines for a 32-bit int), signed integer overflow occurs, wrapping iOctetsRemain to a negative value.

state.value: negative value due to signed integer overflow (e.g., after parsing '99999999999')

step_by_step_execution.description: A non-digit character (e.g., space ' ') is received. The code checks iOctetsRemain < 1, logs an error, but does NOT return or reset state. It unconditionally sets inputState to eInMsg and returns.
On the next call, inputState is eInMsg and eFraming is TCP_FRAMING_OCTET_COUNTING, so the octet-counting copy branch is entered. octatesToCopy is assigned the negative iOctetsRemain.
The check 'buffLen < octatesToCopy' compares int values. Since buffLen is positive and octatesToCopy is negative, this is false — the branch is NOT taken.
The check 'octatesToCopy + pThis->iMsg > iMaxLine' is likely false since adding a negative to iMsg produces a small or negative number. The branch is NOT taken, so octatesToCopy remains negative.

memcpy is called with octatesToCopy cast to size_t (the third parameter type). The negative int becomes a very large unsigned value (e.g., ~3 billion), causing a massive heap buffer over-read from *buff and over-write into pThis->pMsg.

commit_message:
Add a test for GENERAL_NAME_cmp

Based on a boringssl test contributed by David Benjamin

Reviewed-by: Tomas Mraz <tmraz@fedoraproject.org>
cve_desc: The X.509 GeneralName type is a generic type for representing different types of names. One of those name types is known as EDIPartyName. OpenSSL provides a function GENERAL_NAME_cmp which compares different instances of a GENERAL_NAME to see if they are equal or not. This function behaves incorrectly when both GENERAL_NAMEs contain an EDIPARTYNAME. A NULL pointer dereference and a crash may occur leading to a possible denial of service attack. OpenSSL itself uses the GENERAL_NAME_cmp function for two purposes: 1) Comparing CRL distribution point names between an available CRL and a CRL distribution point embedded in an X509 certificate 2) When verifying that a timestamp response token signer matches the timestamp authority name (exposed via the API functions TS_RESP_verify_response and TS_RESP_verify_token) If an attacker can control both items being compared then that attacker could trigger a crash. For example if the attacker can trick a client or server into checking a malicious certificate against a malicious CRL then this may occur. Note that some applications automatically download CRLs based on a URL embedded in a certificate. This checking happens prior to the signatures on the certificate and CRL being verified. OpenSSL's s_server, s_client and verify tools have support for the "-crl_download" option which implements automatic CRL downloading and this attack has been demonstrated to work against those tools. Note that an unrelated bug means that affected versions of OpenSSL cannot parse or construct correct encodings of EDIPARTYNAME. However it is possible to construct a malformed EDIPARTYNAME that OpenSSL's parser will accept and hence trigger this attack. All OpenSSL 1.1.1 and 1.0.2 versions are affected by this issue. Other OpenSSL releases are out of support and have not been checked. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1i (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1h). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2x (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2w).

commit_message:
Remove 'umask' calls from 'mkdir'.

Fixes <http://bugs.gnu.org/24659>.

* libguile/filesys.c (SCM_DEFINE): Remove calls to 'umask' when MODE is
unbound; instead, use 0777 as the mode.  Update docstring to clarify
this.
* doc/ref/posix.texi (File System): Adjust accordingly.
* NEWS: Mention it.
cve_desc: The mkdir procedure of GNU Guile temporarily changed the process' umask to zero. During that time window, in a multithreaded application, other threads could end up creating files with insecure permissions. For example, mkdir without the optional mode argument would create directories as 0777. This is fixed in Guile 2.0.13. Prior versions are affected.

vulnerabilities.description: There is a TOCTOU race condition on the process-wide umask. The code calls umask(0) to read the current umask, then immediately calls umask(mask) to restore it. Between these two calls, the umask is set to 0, meaning any other thread creating files in that window will do so with no permission mask applied, potentially creating world-readable/writable files.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with no mode argument, so SCM_UNBNDP(mode) is true.
umask(0) is called, which atomically sets the process-wide umask to 0 and returns the old value. At this point, the process umask is 0 (no masking).
A concurrent thread in the same process can now create files or directories. Because the process umask is 0, those files may be created with overly permissive modes (e.g., 0777).
umask(mask) is called to restore the original umask, closing the race window.
mkdir is called with (0777 ^ mask), using the value read earlier. The directory is created correctly, but damage from the race window may already have occurred for other threads.

commit_message:
tipc: fix size validations for the MSG_CRYPTO type

The function tipc_crypto_key_rcv is used to parse MSG_CRYPTO messages
to receive keys from other nodes in the cluster in order to decrypt any
further messages from them.
This patch verifies that any supplied sizes in the message body are
valid for the received message.

Fixes: 1ef6f7c9390f ("tipc: add automatic session key exchange")
Signed-off-by: Max VA <maxv@sentinelone.com>
Acked-by: Ying Xue <ying.xue@windriver.com>
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Acked-by: Jon Maloy <jmaloy@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/tipc/crypto.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.16. The Transparent Inter-Process Communication (TIPC) functionality allows remote attackers to exploit insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes for the MSG_CRYPTO message type.

vulnerabilities.description: The algorithm name is copied from untrusted network data into skey->alg_name without ensuring null-termination. If the attacker sends TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME bytes of non-null data, the alg_name field will lack a null terminator. When skey is later consumed (e.g., the algorithm name is passed as a C string to crypto_alloc_aead or similar), this results in an out-of-bounds read past the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a network message. size and data are extracted from the header.
The minimum size check passes because the attacker provides a sufficiently large message.
keylen is read from the network data at offset TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME.
The size/keylen consistency check passes: size == keylen + sizeof(struct tipc_aead_key) and keylen <= TIPC_AEAD_KEY_SIZE_MAX.
skey is allocated with kmalloc(size, GFP_ATOMIC), which succeeds.

state.value: valid heap pointer, size bytes allocated

step_by_step_execution.description: The algorithm name is copied from the network data into skey->alg_name via memcpy for TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME bytes. The attacker has filled all TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME bytes with non-null characters (e.g., 'A'). No null terminator is written.

state.value: TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME bytes of 'A' with no null terminator

step_by_step_execution.description: skey is stored in rx->skey and later processed by the delayed work handler, which will use skey->alg_name as a C string (e.g., passing it to crypto API). Because there is no null terminator, string functions will read past the alg_name buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read from the heap allocation.

state.value: not null-terminated, string operations will read beyond buffer bounds

commit_message: Fix bug #72750: wddx_deserialize null dereference
cve_desc: The php_wddx_pop_element function in ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid base64 binary value, as demonstrated by a wddx_deserialize call that mishandles a binary element in a wddxPacket XML document.

vulnerabilities.description: NULL pointer dereference when ent2->data is NULL but ent2->type is not ST_FIELD. The code only guards against a NULL ent2->data when the type is ST_FIELD. If a stack entry with a different type (e.g., ST_STRUCT, ST_ARRAY) has a NULL data pointer, the subsequent Z_TYPE_P(ent2->data) macro dereferences a NULL pointer, leading to a crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with an element name matching one of the handled types (e.g., EL_STRING). stack->top is > 1.
wddx_stack_top retrieves ent1 from the top of the stack. ent1->data is non-NULL so we pass the NULL check.

stack->top is decremented and wddx_stack_top retrieves ent2, the entry below ent1 on the stack. ent2->type is not ST_FIELD (e.g. ST_STRUCT or ST_ARRAY) but ent2->data is NULL (pushed with NULL data by a start handler for a malformed element).
The NULL-data guard only checks (ent2->type == ST_FIELD && ent2->data == NULL). Since ent2->type is not ST_FIELD, this condition is false and execution continues.
Z_TYPE_P(ent2->data) dereferences ent2->data which is NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference / segfault.

commit_message:
sftp: Fix bug in sftp_mkdir not returning on error.

resolves: #84
(cherry picked from commit a92c97b2e17715c1b3cdd693d14af6c3311d8e44)
cve_desc: Multiple double free vulnerabilities in the (1) agent_sign_data function in agent.c, (2) channel_request function in channels.c, (3) ssh_userauth_pubkey function in auth.c, (4) sftp_parse_attr_3 function in sftp.c, and (5) try_publickey_from_file function in keyfiles.c in libssh before 0.5.3 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.description: When any of the buffer_add or sftp_packet_write calls fail, `buffer` and `path` are freed inside the if-block, then freed again unconditionally after the if-block, causing a double-free. Additionally, the missing `return -1` in the error branch means execution continues into the while loop, attempting to read a response for a packet that was never successfully sent.

step_by_step_execution.description: A buffer and path string are successfully allocated.
The id is obtained and the compound condition is evaluated. Suppose buffer_add_u32 fails (returns < 0).
Execution enters the if-block error path. ssh_buffer_free(buffer) and ssh_string_free(path) are called, freeing both objects.
Execution falls through (no return statement in if-block) to the unconditional ssh_buffer_free(buffer) and ssh_string_free(path) calls, causing a double-free on both objects.

commit_message:
Add a test for GENERAL_NAME_cmp

Based on a boringssl test contributed by David Benjamin

Reviewed-by: Tomas Mraz <tmraz@fedoraproject.org>
cve_desc: The X.509 GeneralName type is a generic type for representing different types of names. One of those name types is known as EDIPartyName. OpenSSL provides a function GENERAL_NAME_cmp which compares different instances of a GENERAL_NAME to see if they are equal or not. This function behaves incorrectly when both GENERAL_NAMEs contain an EDIPARTYNAME. A NULL pointer dereference and a crash may occur leading to a possible denial of service attack. OpenSSL itself uses the GENERAL_NAME_cmp function for two purposes: 1) Comparing CRL distribution point names between an available CRL and a CRL distribution point embedded in an X509 certificate 2) When verifying that a timestamp response token signer matches the timestamp authority name (exposed via the API functions TS_RESP_verify_response and TS_RESP_verify_token) If an attacker can control both items being compared then that attacker could trigger a crash. For example if the attacker can trick a client or server into checking a malicious certificate against a malicious CRL then this may occur. Note that some applications automatically download CRLs based on a URL embedded in a certificate. This checking happens prior to the signatures on the certificate and CRL being verified. OpenSSL's s_server, s_client and verify tools have support for the "-crl_download" option which implements automatic CRL downloading and this attack has been demonstrated to work against those tools. Note that an unrelated bug means that affected versions of OpenSSL cannot parse or construct correct encodings of EDIPARTYNAME. However it is possible to construct a malformed EDIPARTYNAME that OpenSSL's parser will accept and hence trigger this attack. All OpenSSL 1.1.1 and 1.0.2 versions are affected by this issue. Other OpenSSL releases are out of support and have not been checked. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1i (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1h). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2x (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2w).

commit_message:
MDEV-25636: Bug report: abortion in sql/sql_parse.cc:6294

The asserion failure was caused by this query

  select /*id=1*/ from t1
  where
   col= ( select /*id=2*/ from ... where corr_cond1
          union
          select /*id=4*/ from ... where corr_cond2)

Here,
- select with id=2 was correlated due to corr_cond1.
- select with id=4 was initially correlated due to corr_cond2, but then
  the optimizer optimized away the correlation, making the select with id=4
  uncorrelated.

However, since select with id=2 remained correlated, the execution had to
re-compute the whole UNION. When it tried to execute select with id=4, it
hit an assertion  (join buffer already free'd).

This is because select with id=4 has freed its execution structures after
it has been executed once. The select is uncorrelated, so it did not expect
it would need to be executed for the second time.

Fixed this by adding this logic in
st_select_lex::optimize_unflattened_subqueries():

  If a member of a UNION is correlated, mark all its members as
  correlated, so that they are prepared to be executed multiple times.
cve_desc: MariaDB through 10.5.9 allows an application crash in sub_select_postjoin_aggr for a NULL value of aggr.

commit_message:
MDEV-25636: Bug report: abortion in sql/sql_parse.cc:6294

The asserion failure was caused by this query

  select /*id=1*/ from t1
  where
   col= ( select /*id=2*/ from ... where corr_cond1
          union
          select /*id=4*/ from ... where corr_cond2)

Here,
- select with id=2 was correlated due to corr_cond1.
- select with id=4 was initially correlated due to corr_cond2, but then
  the optimizer optimized away the correlation, making the select with id=4
  uncorrelated.

However, since select with id=2 remained correlated, the execution had to
re-compute the whole UNION. When it tried to execute select with id=4, it
hit an assertion  (join buffer already free'd).

This is because select with id=4 has freed its execution structures after
it has been executed once. The select is uncorrelated, so it did not expect
it would need to be executed for the second time.

Fixed this by adding this logic in
st_select_lex::optimize_unflattened_subqueries():

  correlated, so that they are prepared to be executed multiple times.
cve_desc: MariaDB through 10.5.9 allows an application crash in sub_select_postjoin_aggr for a NULL value of aggr.

commit_message:
MDEV-26402: A SEGV in Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order...

When doing condition pushdown from HAVING into WHERE,
Item_equal::create_pushable_equalities() calls
item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL) for constant items.
Then, Item::cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor() checks for this flag
to see if it should call item->cleanup() or leave the item as-is.

The failure happens when a constant item has a non-constant one inside it,
like:

   (tbl.col=0 AND impossible_cond)

item->walk(cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor) works in a bottom-up
way so it
1. will call Item_func_eq(tbl.col=0)->cleanup()
2. will not call Item_cond_and->cleanup (as the AND is constant)

This creates an item tree where a fixed Item has an un-fixed Item inside
it which eventually causes an assertion failure.

Fixed by introducing this rule: instead of just calling

  item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL);

we call Item::walk() to set the flag for all sub-items of the item.
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.7 and below was discovered to contain a segmentation fault via the component Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order.

commit_message:
MDEV-26402: A SEGV in Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order...

When doing condition pushdown from HAVING into WHERE,
Item_equal::create_pushable_equalities() calls
item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL) for constant items.
Then, Item::cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor() checks for this flag
to see if it should call item->cleanup() or leave the item as-is.

The failure happens when a constant item has a non-constant one inside it,
like:

   (tbl.col=0 AND impossible_cond)

item->walk(cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor) works in a bottom-up
way so it
1. will call Item_func_eq(tbl.col=0)->cleanup()
2. will not call Item_cond_and->cleanup (as the AND is constant)

This creates an item tree where a fixed Item has an un-fixed Item inside
it which eventually causes an assertion failure.

Fixed by introducing this rule: instead of just calling

  item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL);

we call Item::walk() to set the flag for all sub-items of the item.
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.7 and below was discovered to contain a segmentation fault via the component Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order.

commit_message:
Submitted by: Tomas Hoger <thoger@redhat.com>

Fix for CVE-2010-0433 where some kerberos enabled versions of OpenSSL
could be crashed if the relevant tables were not present (e.g. chrooted).
cve_desc: The kssl_keytab_is_available function in ssl/kssl.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8n, when Kerberos is enabled but Kerberos configuration files cannot be opened, does not check a certain return value, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and daemon crash) via SSL cipher negotiation, as demonstrated by a chroot installation of Dovecot or stunnel without Kerberos configuration files inside the chroot.

code_snippets:
krb5rc = krb5_sname_to_principal(krb5context, NULL, 
                                     kssl_ctx->service_name ? kssl_ctx->service_name: KRB5SVC,
                                     KRB5_NT_SRV_HST, &princ);

    krb5rc = krb5_kt_get_entry(krb5context, krb5keytab, 
                                princ,
                                0 /* IGNORE_VNO */,
                                0 /* IGNORE_ENCTYPE */,
                                &entry);
vulnerabilities.description: The return value of krb5_sname_to_principal is not checked before using 'princ' in the subsequent call to krb5_kt_get_entry. If krb5_sname_to_principal fails, 'princ' remains NULL, and passing a NULL principal to krb5_kt_get_entry can cause a NULL pointer dereference.

code_snippets:
krb5rc = krb5_kt_get_entry(krb5context, krb5keytab, 
                                princ,
                                0 /* IGNORE_VNO */,
                                0 /* IGNORE_ENCTYPE */,
                                &entry);

step_by_step_execution.description: krb5_init_context succeeds, krb5context is valid.
A keytab is resolved or defaulted successfully, so krb5keytab is valid.

krb5_sname_to_principal is called. If it fails (e.g., due to DNS resolution failure or misconfiguration), it returns a non-zero error code, and princ may remain NULL. The return code is stored in krb5rc but is never checked.
krb5_kt_get_entry is called with princ still NULL. This dereferences the NULL pointer internally, causing a crash.

commit_message: MDEV-24176 fixup: GCC -Wmaybe-uninitialized
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6.5 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component Item_args::walk_arg, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

commit_message:
sctp: correct bounds check in sctp_setsockopt_auth_key

The bonds check to prevent buffer overlflow was not exactly
right.  It still allowed overflow of up to 8 bytes which is
sizeof(struct sctp_authkey).

Since optlen is already checked against the size of that struct,
we are guaranteed not to cause interger overflow either.

Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the sctp_setsockopt_auth_key function in net/sctp/socket.c in the Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel 2.6.24-rc1 through 2.6.26.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted sca_keylength field associated with the SCTP_AUTH_KEY option.

commit_message:
Prevent an invalid memory writes in compileRule

Thanks to Han Zheng for reporting it

Fixes #1214
cve_desc: Liblouis 3.21.0 has an out-of-bounds write in compileRule in compileTranslationTable.c, as demonstrated by lou_trace.

vulnerabilities.description: When expanding macro argument substitutions into tmpFile.line, the code copies all characters of the argument without checking if tmpFile.linelen would exceed MAXSTRING. The MAXSTRING bounds check only applies to individual characters copied from the macro definition, not to argument substitutions. This can cause a buffer overflow on the stack-allocated tmpFile structure.

code_snippets:
for (int j = 0; j < arg.length; j++)
										tmpFile.line[tmpFile.linelen++] = arg.chars[j];

step_by_step_execution.description: The opcode is CTO_None or CTO_UpLow, and inScopeMacros is not NULL. A matching macro is found in the macro list.
A local FileInfo tmpFile is created on the stack. tmpFile.linelen is initialized to 0. tmpFile.line is a fixed-size array of size MAXSTRING.
Arguments are read from the file and stored in the malloc'd arguments array. A macro argument can have up to the max length of a CharsString.

state.value: CharsString tokens read from file, potentially long

step_by_step_execution.description: The macro definition expansion loop begins. Characters from m->definition are copied into tmpFile.line one by one, with a MAXSTRING check on each character.

state.value: close to but below MAXSTRING after copying definition chars

step_by_step_execution.description: A substitution point is reached (subst < m->substitution_count). The corresponding argument is fetched and its characters are copied into tmpFile.line WITHOUT any bounds check on tmpFile.linelen + arg.length vs MAXSTRING.

state.value: could be large enough that tmpFile.linelen + arg.length > MAXSTRING
incremented past MAXSTRING, overflowing tmpFile.line buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: The write to tmpFile.line[tmpFile.linelen++] writes beyond the bounds of the fixed-size line buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: The dynamically allocated 'arguments' array is never freed. On the success path (return 1) and on several error paths (e.g. when argument_count doesn't match, or when compileRule fails), the function returns without calling free(arguments), resulting in a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: A matching macro is found. Memory is allocated for the arguments array.
Arguments are successfully read and the macro is expanded. The function reaches 'return 1;' at the end of the macro expansion block.
The function returns without freeing the arguments pointer, leaking memory.

commit_message:
MDEV-28095 crash in multi-update and implicit grouping

disallow implicit grouping in multi-update.
explicit GROUP BY is not allowed by the grammar.
cve_desc: There is an Assertion failure in MariaDB Server v10.9 and below via 'node->pcur->rel_pos == BTR_PCUR_ON' at /row/row0mysql.cc.

commit_message:
flxdec: Don't unref() parent in the chain function

We don't own the reference here, it is owned by the caller and given to
us for the scope of this function. Leftover mistake from 0.10 porting.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=774897
cve_desc: The gst_decode_chain_free_internal function in the flxdex decoder in gst-plugins-good in GStreamer before 1.10.2 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (invalid memory read and crash) via an invalid file, which triggers an incorrect unref call.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in computing flxdec->size from attacker-controlled width and height header fields. If width * height overflows a guint, a small buffer is allocated. Subsequent decode operations write data assuming the full (non-overflowed) dimensions, causing a heap buffer overflow. Additionally, flxdec->size * 4 can overflow, leading to an undersized output buffer allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted FLX file is pushed as a buffer into the adapter. The state is GST_FLXDEC_READ_HEADER and enough bytes are available.
The header is copied and endianness-fixed. flxh->width and flxh->height are attacker-controlled values from the file. For example, width=65536, height=65537.
The type field is a valid magic value, so the type check passes.
flxdec->size is computed as (guint)width * (guint)height. 65536 * 65537 = 0x100010000 which overflows a 32-bit guint to 0x10000 (65536).
g_malloc(flxdec->size) allocates only 65536 bytes for frame_data and delta_data, but the logical frame dimensions require ~4GB.
During frame decoding (GST_FLXDEC_PLAYING state), flx_decode_chunks writes decoded pixel data for a 65536x65537 frame into the 65536-byte frame_data buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

state.value: 65536-byte buffer being written with up to 4GB of data

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow when computing chunk size. If the attacker-controlled flxfh.size is less than FlxFrameChunkSize, the subtraction wraps around to a very large value, causing gst_adapter_take to attempt reading a huge amount of data or returning garbage.

step_by_step_execution.description: The decoder is in GST_FLXDEC_PLAYING state and reads a frame chunk header from the adapter.
The frame chunk header is read and flxfh.id is FLX_FRAME_TYPE. flxfh.size is attacker-controlled and set to a small value, e.g. 2.
The check 'avail < flxfh.size' passes since avail >= FlxFrameChunkSize > 2. The FlxFrameChunkSize header is flushed.

gst_adapter_take is called with flxfh.size - FlxFrameChunkSize. Since flxfh.size (2) < FlxFrameChunkSize, this unsigned subtraction wraps to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFA), causing an out-of-bounds read or unexpected behavior.

commit_message: imptcp: fix Segmentation Fault when octet count is to high
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

vulnerabilities.description: In the eInOctetCnt state, digit characters of the octet count are written to pMsg at offset iMsg without any bounds check. After a previous message is submitted via the octet-counting path, iMsg is not reset to 0 before entering eInOctetCnt for the next frame. This means iMsg retains its value from the previous message (potentially up to iMaxLine), causing an out-of-bounds write into pMsg when digit characters of the next frame's octet count are stored.

step_by_step_execution.description: A first message is received using TCP_FRAMING_OCTET_COUNTING. After parsing the octet count and SP delimiter, pThis->iMsg is set to 0 and inputState becomes eInMsg.
During the eInMsg octet-counting branch, message bytes are copied. iMsg grows up to iMaxLine (the buffer size limit), as the truncation logic caps octatesToCopy to (iMaxLine - pThis->iMsg).
When iOctetsRemain reaches 0, doSubmitMsg is called and inputState is set to eAtStrtFram. Critically, iMsg is NOT reset to 0 here.

The next byte arrives. Since inputState is eAtStrtFram, bSuppOctetFram is true, and the character is a digit, the code transitions to eInOctetCnt. iOctetsRemain is set to 0, but iMsg is still not reset.

Execution falls into the eInOctetCnt block. The character is a digit, iOctetsRemain <= 200000000, so the code executes *(pThis->pMsg + pThis->iMsg++) = c. Since iMsg equals iMaxLine, this writes one byte past the end of the pMsg buffer (which is allocated with size iMaxLine), causing a heap buffer overflow. Each subsequent digit character writes further out of bounds.

commit_message:
gzip -d: do not clobber stack for valid input on x86_64

* unlzw.c (unlzw): Avoid integer overflow.
Aki Helin reported the segfault along with an input to trigger the bug.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.
cve_desc: Integer underflow in the unlzw function in unlzw.c in gzip before 1.4 on 64-bit platforms, as used in ncompress and probably others, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) or possibly execute arbitrary code via a crafted archive that uses LZW compression, leading to an array index error.

vulnerabilities.description: The while loop that traverses the LZW prefix chain has no bounds check on `stackp`. A crafted input can construct prefix table entries that form a very long chain or a cycle, causing `stackp` to be decremented past the beginning of the `de_stack` buffer, resulting in a stack-based buffer overflow (write before the start of the buffer).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads `maxbits` from the input stream and initializes LZW decompression tables. `free_ent` starts at FIRST (256 or 257).

As codes are decoded, new entries are added to the prefix table via `tab_prefixof(code) = (unsigned short)oldcode` and `tab_suffixof(code) = (char_type)finchar`. The attacker controls the input stream and thus controls which prefix/suffix entries are created.
When a code >= 256 is encountered, `stackp` is set to `de_stack` and the prefix chain is traversed. Each iteration decrements `stackp` and writes a byte.

If the attacker has crafted the prefix table entries to form a cycle (e.g., entry A points to entry B which points to entry A) or a very long chain, the while loop continues indefinitely or for far too many iterations, decrementing `stackp` each time without any bounds check.
Each iteration writes via `*--stackp = tab_suffixof(code)`, writing to memory before the `de_stack` buffer, corrupting adjacent memory.

commit_message:
flxdec: Don't unref() parent in the chain function

We don't own the reference here, it is owned by the caller and given to
us for the scope of this function. Leftover mistake from 0.10 porting.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=774897
cve_desc: The gst_decode_chain_free_internal function in the flxdex decoder in gst-plugins-good in GStreamer before 1.10.2 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (invalid memory read and crash) via an invalid file, which triggers an incorrect unref call.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of flxdec->size from attacker-controlled width and height fields from the FLX header. If width * height overflows a 32-bit guint, flxdec->size will be much smaller than expected, leading to undersized heap allocations. Subsequent operations (flx_decode_chunks, memcpy, flx_colorspace_convert) will write beyond the allocated buffers. Additionally, flxdec->size * 4 can also overflow, causing the output buffer to be too small.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted FLX file is fed to the decoder. The adapter accumulates enough data (>= FlxHeaderSize) and the header is read.
The header fields are parsed and endianness-fixed. The attacker sets width and height to values whose product overflows a 32-bit unsigned integer, e.g. width=65536, height=65537.
flxdec->size is computed as (guint)width * (guint)height. 65536 * 65537 = 0x100010000, which truncates to 0x10000 (65536) in a 32-bit guint.
g_malloc allocates only 65536 bytes for frame_data and delta_data, while the converter is created expecting 65536 * 65537 pixels.
During frame decoding, flx_decode_chunks writes decoded pixel data assuming width*height pixels into the 65536-byte frame_data buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow when computing chunk data size. If the attacker-controlled flxfh.size field is less than FlxFrameChunkSize, the subtraction underflows to a very large value, causing gst_adapter_take to attempt to read a huge amount of data or return invalid data. The only check is 'avail < flxfh.size' which passes if flxfh.size is small (e.g., 0 or 1).

step_by_step_execution.description: The decoder is in PLAYING state and reads a frame chunk header from the adapter.
The chunk header is parsed. The attacker sets flxfh.id to FLX_FRAME_TYPE and flxfh.size to a value smaller than FlxFrameChunkSize (e.g., 2).
The check 'avail < flxfh.size' is evaluated. Since avail >= FlxFrameChunkSize > 2, the check passes (not less than), so execution continues.

The adapter flushes FlxFrameChunkSize bytes, then gst_adapter_take is called with flxfh.size - FlxFrameChunkSize. Since flxfh.size (2) < FlxFrameChunkSize, this underflows to a very large unsigned value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFF8 if FlxFrameChunkSize is 10).
gst_adapter_take attempts to allocate and copy a huge amount of memory, leading to undefined behavior, crash, or heap corruption.

cve_desc: In Dovecot before 2.3.11.3, sending a specially formatted RPA request will crash the auth service because a length of zero is mishandled.

step_by_step_execution.description: The guard check `p > end` is evaluated. Since p == end, the condition is false, and execution continues.
`len = *p++` dereferences `p`, which points to `end` (one past the last valid byte). This is an out-of-bounds read of one byte.

patch 8.2.4436: crash with weird 'vartabstop' value

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters VREPLACE mode path; `orig_line` is successfully allocated via `vim_strsave(ml_get_curline())`
Various indent-changing operations are performed (set_indent or shift_line), replace stack adjustments, etc.

`new_line = vim_strsave(ml_get_curline())` is called but returns NULL due to memory allocation failure.
