commit_message: squash heap-buffer-overflow, PoC TIFF from Hardik
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow issue was found in ImageMagick. When an application processes a malformed TIFF file, it could lead to undefined behavior or a crash causing a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of 'extent' for the strip pixel buffer in ReadStripMethod. The multiplication `4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*TIFFStripSize(tiff)` can overflow size_t (especially on 32-bit systems), resulting in a small allocation. TIFFReadEncodedStrip then writes up to TIFFStripSize(tiff) bytes into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted TIFF file is opened and basic header fields are read. The image has strip-based storage (method=ReadStripMethod).
TIFFStripSize(tiff) returns a large value from the crafted TIFF metadata.

The extent is computed: 4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*TIFFStripSize(tiff). With samples_per_pixel=32 and TIFFStripSize=33554432, this is 4*33*33554432 = 4,429,185,024 which overflows a 32-bit size_t, wrapping to a small value.

state.value: Wrapped small value due to 32-bit overflow, e.g., 134217728 mod 2^32

step_by_step_execution.description: AcquireQuantumMemory allocates a buffer of size 'extent' bytes, which is much smaller than the actual strip data size.

state.value: Points to an undersized heap buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: TIFFReadEncodedStrip is called requesting TIFFStripSize(tiff) bytes to be read into strip_pixels, writing far more data than the allocated buffer can hold, causing a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of 'extent' for the tile pixel buffer in ReadTileMethod. Both `rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff)` and the outer multiplication `4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*...` can overflow, resulting in a small allocation. TIFFReadTile then writes tile data into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted tiled TIFF file is opened and header fields are read. TIFFIsTiled returns true, so method=ReadTileMethod.
Tile dimensions are read from the TIFF. rows and TIFFTileRowSize may be large values.

state.value: Large uint32 value from TIFF tag

step_by_step_execution.description: The inner multiplication `rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff)` overflows, or the outer multiplication `4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*MagickMax(...)` overflows size_t on 32-bit, producing a small extent value.

state.value: Small wrapped value due to integer overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: AcquireQuantumMemory allocates the undersized tile_pixels buffer based on the overflowed extent.
TIFFReadTile reads a full tile into tile_pixels, writing more data than allocated, causing heap buffer overflow.

state.value: Bytes written exceed allocated buffer size

commit_message:
lib-smtp: smtp-command-parser - Fix infinite loop occurring when command stream ends in UTF-8 character.

Broken by 8f08f1944be438a2422b604c08e5060b5c7bd72f.
cve_desc: lib-smtp in submission-login and lmtp in Dovecot 2.3.9 before 2.3.9.3 mishandles truncated UTF-8 data in command parameters, as demonstrated by the unauthenticated triggering of a submission-login infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.description: The size argument to uni_utf8_get_char_n is computed as `(size_t)(p - parser->end)`. Since the loop condition guarantees `p < parser->end`, the value `p - parser->end` is negative. Casting a negative value to `size_t` (an unsigned type) produces a very large value (close to SIZE_MAX). This tells uni_utf8_get_char_n that the available buffer is enormous, allowing it to read well beyond the actual end of the buffer (`parser->end`), resulting in an out-of-bounds read. The correct expression should be `(size_t)(parser->end - p)`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins. `p` is set to `parser->cur + parser->state.poff`. The while loop condition `p < parser->end` is checked and is true.
Since `parser->auth_response` is false, the else branch is taken to call `uni_utf8_get_char_n`.

The size argument `(size_t)(p - parser->end)` is computed. Since `p < parser->end`, `p - parser->end` is a negative number (e.g., -10). Casting to `size_t` wraps it to a very large unsigned value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFF6 on 64-bit).

`uni_utf8_get_char_n` is called with the pointer `p` and a size parameter that far exceeds the actual available buffer. The function may read bytes past `parser->end`, causing an out-of-bounds read. For multi-byte UTF-8 sequences where the leading byte indicates a multi-byte character, the function will read subsequent bytes beyond `parser->end`.

state.value: result from reading potentially out-of-bounds memory

commit_message:
Fix race condition in NewSessionTicket

If a NewSessionTicket is received by a multi-threaded client when
attempting to reuse a previous ticket then a race condition can occur
potentially leading to a double free of the ticket data.

CVE-2015-1791

This also fixes RT#3808 where a session ID is changed for a session already
in the client session cache. Since the session ID is the key to the cache
this breaks the cache access.

Parts of this patch were inspired by this Akamai change:
https://github.com/akamai/openssl/commit/c0bf69a791239ceec64509f9f19fcafb2461b0d3

Reviewed-by: Rich Salz <rsalz@openssl.org>
(cherry picked from commit 27c76b9b8010b536687318739c6f631ce4194688)

Conflicts:
	ssl/ssl.h
	ssl/ssl_err.c
cve_desc: Race condition in the ssl3_get_new_session_ticket function in ssl/s3_clnt.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8zg, 1.0.0 before 1.0.0s, 1.0.1 before 1.0.1n, and 1.0.2 before 1.0.2b, when used for a multi-threaded client, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (double free and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact by providing a NewSessionTicket during an attempt to reuse a ticket that had been obtained earlier.

commit_message: fix oob write bug inside libmobi
cve_desc: libmobi is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Write

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the bounds check when offset >= 0. The expression `buf->offset + aoffset + len` can wrap around if the sum exceeds SIZE_MAX, causing the comparison against buf->maxlen to pass. This allows memmove to read from and write to out-of-bounds memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a large positive offset and a large len value. For a 32-bit size_t example: offset=0x7FFFFFFF, len=0x80000002, buf->offset=0.
aoffset is computed as (size_t)abs(offset). Since offset is positive and INT_MAX, aoffset = 0x7FFFFFFF.
offset >= 0, so the positive branch is taken. The bounds check computes buf->offset + aoffset + len = 0 + 0x7FFFFFFF + 0x80000002 = 0x100000001, which on a 32-bit size_t wraps to 0x00000001.
The wrapped value 0x00000001 is NOT greater than buf->maxlen (e.g. 256), so the bounds check passes and execution continues.
source is set to buf->data + buf->offset + aoffset = buf->data + 0x7FFFFFFF, pointing far beyond the actual buffer.
memmove is called with destination buf->data + 0, source buf->data + 0x7FFFFFFF, and len 0x80000002. This reads from and writes to memory far outside the allocated buffer.

commit_message:
gzip -d: do not clobber stack for valid input on x86_64

* unlzw.c (unlzw): Avoid integer overflow.
Aki Helin reported the segfault along with an input to trigger the bug.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.
cve_desc: Integer underflow in the unlzw function in unlzw.c in gzip before 1.4 on 64-bit platforms, as used in ncompress and probably others, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) or possibly execute arbitrary code via a crafted archive that uses LZW compression, leading to an array index error.

vulnerabilities.description: The loop that follows prefix table chains to decode an LZW string has no bounds checking on `stackp` or validation that `code` values from `tab_prefixof(code)` remain within the valid table range. A crafted compressed input can create a cycle in the prefix table (since `tab_prefixof` entries are written based on previous codes, and an attacker controls the stream of codes). This causes the while loop to run indefinitely, decrementing `stackp` below the start of the `de_stack` buffer, resulting in an out-of-bounds stack buffer write. Additionally, if `tab_prefixof(code)` yields a value outside the allocated table, it results in out-of-bounds reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input is read and initial LZW decoding state is established. `free_ent` starts at FIRST (typically 257). The prefix and suffix tables are initialized for codes 0-255.

The decoder reads codes from the compressed input stream. For each valid code, it adds a new entry to the prefix table via `tab_prefixof(code) = (unsigned short)oldcode`. An attacker crafts codes such that the prefix table entries form a cycle (e.g., entry A's prefix points to entry B, and entry B's prefix points to entry A).
When a subsequent code triggers the chain-following while loop, `code` starts at a value >= 256 and enters the loop `while ((cmp_code_int)code >= (cmp_code_int)256)`.

The loop follows the cyclic chain: code = A -> B -> A -> B -> ... Each iteration decrements `stackp` and writes a byte via `*--stackp = tab_suffixof(code)`. There is no check that `stackp` remains within the bounds of the `de_stack` buffer.

state.value: decrements below de_stack on each iteration
alternates between A and B indefinitely

step_by_step_execution.description: After sufficient iterations, `stackp` points before the `de_stack` buffer and writes corrupt memory, causing an out-of-bounds write (heap or stack buffer overflow depending on where `de_stack` is allocated).

commit_message:
lib-smtp: smtp-command-parser - Fix infinite loop occurring when command stream ends in UTF-8 character.

Broken by 8f08f1944be438a2422b604c08e5060b5c7bd72f.
cve_desc: lib-smtp in submission-login and lmtp in Dovecot 2.3.9 before 2.3.9.3 mishandles truncated UTF-8 data in command parameters, as demonstrated by the unauthenticated triggering of a submission-login infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.description: The size argument to uni_utf8_get_char_n is computed as `(size_t)(p - parser->end)`. Because the loop condition guarantees `p < parser->end`, the expression `p - parser->end` is always negative. Casting a negative value to `size_t` wraps it to a very large positive number, effectively removing the bounds check. This allows uni_utf8_get_char_n to read beyond the valid buffer boundary (past `parser->end`) when decoding a multi-byte UTF-8 character, causing an out-of-bounds read. The intended expression was almost certainly `(size_t)(parser->end - p)`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the while loop with `p < parser->end`.

state.value: parser->cur + parser->state.poff, which is less than parser->end

step_by_step_execution.description: Since auth_response is false, the else branch is taken, calling uni_utf8_get_char_n with the size argument `(size_t)(p - parser->end)`.

state.value: negative (e.g., -5 if there are 5 bytes remaining)
Very large number due to unsigned wrap (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFB on 64-bit)

step_by_step_execution.description: uni_utf8_get_char_n receives an effectively unlimited size parameter. If `p` points to a leading byte of a multi-byte UTF-8 sequence (e.g., a 3- or 4-byte sequence) but the actual valid data ends at `parser->end` before the sequence completes, the function will read continuation bytes beyond `parser->end` because the passed size allows it.

state.value: bytes at and beyond parser->end, which are outside the valid buffer

commit_message:
kdc: validate sname in TGS-REQ

In tgs_build_reply(), validate the server name in the TGS-REQ is present before
dereferencing.
cve_desc: A null pointer de-reference was found in the way samba kerberos server handled missing sname in TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Server - Request). An authenticated user could use this flaw to crash the samba server.

code_snippets:
ret = check_PAC(context, config, cp, NULL,
		    client, server, krbtgt,
		    &tkey_check->key,
		    ekey, &tkey_sign->key,
		    tgt, &rspac, &signedpath);
vulnerabilities.description: When _kdc_db_fetch for the client principal fails and the client's realm differs from the krbtgt's realm, the code logs a message but does NOT 'goto out'. Execution falls through with 'client' being NULL (set by the failed fetch). The NULL 'client' pointer is then passed to check_PAC(), check_KRB5SignedPath(), kdc_check_flags(), and potentially tgs_make_reply(), which may dereference it, leading to a NULL pointer dereference and KDC crash (denial of service).

code_snippets:
ret = check_PAC(context, config, cp, NULL,
		    client, server, krbtgt,
		    &tkey_check->key,
		    ekey, &tkey_sign->key,
		    tgt, &rspac, &signedpath);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function fetches the client entry from the database using _kdc_db_fetch with the client principal 'cp'.

state.value: principal from tgt->cname/tgt->crealm, where crealm is a foreign realm (different from krbtgt_out realm)

step_by_step_execution.description: _kdc_db_fetch returns an error (e.g., HDB_ERR_NOENTRY) because the client is not found. The client pointer is not set to a valid entry.
The first condition (ret == HDB_ERR_NOT_FOUND_HERE) is false, so we enter the 'else if(ret)' branch.
Inside the 'else if(ret)' branch, the client's realm is compared with krbtgt_realm. Since the client is from a foreign realm, strcmp returns non-zero, so the 'goto out' is NOT taken.

state.value: non-zero, so we skip the 'goto out'

step_by_step_execution.description: The code logs 'Client not found in database' and falls through without goto out. Execution continues past the entire if/else if/else if block.
check_PAC is called with the NULL client pointer. If check_PAC dereferences client (e.g., to access client->entry), a NULL pointer dereference occurs, crashing the KDC.

state.value: overwritten by check_PAC's return, but crash may occur before return

commit_message: Fixed: HTML escape the log file content when viewed via Monit GUI.
cve_desc: Persistent cross-site scripting (XSS) in http/cervlet.c in Tildeslash Monit before 5.25.3 allows a remote unauthenticated attacker to introduce arbitrary JavaScript via manipulation of an unsanitized user field of the Authorization header for HTTP Basic Authentication, which is mishandled during an _viewlog operation.

vulnerabilities.description: Log file contents are inserted directly into an HTML <textarea> element without any HTML entity escaping. If the log file contains the string '</textarea>', an attacker who can influence log contents (e.g., by triggering log entries with crafted hostnames, usernames, or other logged fields) can break out of the textarea and inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript, leading to a stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function checks readonly access and proceeds to render the view log page.
The code checks that logging is enabled and not using syslog, then opens the log file.

state.value: Run_Log is set, Run_UseSyslog is not set
non-NULL FILE pointer to the log file

step_by_step_execution.description: The code outputs the opening HTML including a <textarea> tag.
The code reads up to 511 bytes from the log file into buf. If an attacker influenced a logged value, the log file may contain a payload such as '</textarea><script>alert(document.cookie)</script>'.

buf is null-terminated and appended directly to the HTML output buffer with %s, without any HTML entity encoding. The '</textarea>' in the content closes the textarea element, and the subsequent <script> tag is rendered as active HTML/JavaScript.

state.value: contains the textarea element prematurely closed, followed by injected script code

commit_message: MDEV-26323 use-after-poison issue of MariaDB server
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component my_strcasecmp_8bit, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

vulnerabilities.description: If dl->length is smaller than so_ext_len (sizeof(SO_EXT) - 1), the expression `dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len` computes a pointer that points before the start of the dl->str buffer, resulting in an out-of-bounds read (buffer under-read) when my_strcasecmp dereferences it.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a dl whose length is very short, e.g., dl->length = 0 (empty string) or 1.
so_ext_len is computed as sizeof(SO_EXT) - 1, e.g. 3 for ".so" extension.
The pointer arithmetic dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len is evaluated. With dl->length=0 and so_ext_len=3, this becomes dl->str - 3, pointing before the buffer.
my_strcasecmp reads from the out-of-bounds pointer, causing an out-of-bounds read of memory before the dl->str buffer.

state.value: points 3 bytes before dl->str, reading arbitrary memory

code_snippets:
char *s= (char*)alloc_root(root, dl->length + so_ext_len + 1);
    memcpy(s, dl->str, dl->length);
vulnerabilities.description: The return value of alloc_root is not checked for NULL. If alloc_root fails and returns NULL, the subsequent memcpy and strcpy will dereference a NULL pointer, causing a crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function determines the dl string does not end with SO_EXT, so it enters the if block.
alloc_root is called to allocate memory. If the memory pool is exhausted, it returns NULL.
memcpy is called with s=NULL as destination, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message: ZipFile:  Add path checks to uncompressEntry()
cve_desc: This affects the package juce-framework/JUCE before 6.1.5. This vulnerability is triggered when a malicious archive is crafted with an entry containing a symbolic link. When extracted, the symbolic link is followed outside of the target dir allowing writing arbitrary files on the target host. In some cases, this can allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code. The vulnerable code is in the ZipFile::uncompressEntry function in juce_ZipFile.cpp and is executed when the archive is extracted upon calling uncompressTo() on a ZipFile object.

vulnerabilities.description: The entry filename from the zip archive is used directly in getChildFile without sanitization. A malicious zip can contain entries with path traversal sequences such as '../../etc/cron.d/malicious', causing files to be written outside the intended target directory (Zip Slip attack).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives an index into zip entries and a target directory.

state.value: attacker-controlled index pointing to a crafted zip entry

step_by_step_execution.description: The entry filename is read from the zip metadata. A malicious zip can set this to a path-traversal string.
entryPath is not empty, so the empty check is passed.
targetFile is computed by joining the target directory with the attacker-controlled entryPath, resulting in a path outside the target directory.
The entry does not end with '/' or '\', so it is treated as a file. An InputStream is created successfully.
The parent directory is created and a FileOutputStream is opened to the traversed path, writing attacker-controlled content outside the target directory.

vulnerabilities.description: A malicious zip entry marked as a symbolic link can point to an arbitrary file path. Combined with path traversal in the entry name or by itself, this allows an attacker to create symlinks that point to sensitive files, enabling reading or overwriting of arbitrary files on the system.

step_by_step_execution.description: A zip entry is crafted with isSymbolicLink set to true and the entry content pointing to a sensitive path like /etc/shadow.
targetFile is resolved inside the target directory.
The stream content (symlink target) is read. It contains an arbitrary attacker-chosen path.
A symbolic link is created at targetFile pointing to the attacker-chosen path, with no validation.

commit_message:
Fix EC_GROUP_new_from_ecparameters to check the base length

Check that there's at least one byte in params->base before trying to
read it.

CVE-2021-3712

Reviewed-by: Viktor Dukhovni <viktor@openssl.org>
Reviewed-by: Paul Dale <pauli@openssl.org>
cve_desc: ASN.1 strings are represented internally within OpenSSL as an ASN1_STRING structure which contains a buffer holding the string data and a field holding the buffer length. This contrasts with normal C strings which are repesented as a buffer for the string data which is terminated with a NUL (0) byte. Although not a strict requirement, ASN.1 strings that are parsed using OpenSSL's own "d2i" functions (and other similar parsing functions) as well as any string whose value has been set with the ASN1_STRING_set() function will additionally NUL terminate the byte array in the ASN1_STRING structure. However, it is possible for applications to directly construct valid ASN1_STRING structures which do not NUL terminate the byte array by directly setting the "data" and "length" fields in the ASN1_STRING array. This can also happen by using the ASN1_STRING_set0() function. Numerous OpenSSL functions that print ASN.1 data have been found to assume that the ASN1_STRING byte array will be NUL terminated, even though this is not guaranteed for strings that have been directly constructed. Where an application requests an ASN.1 structure to be printed, and where that ASN.1 structure contains ASN1_STRINGs that have been directly constructed by the application without NUL terminating the "data" field, then a read buffer overrun can occur. The same thing can also occur during name constraints processing of certificates (for example if a certificate has been directly constructed by the application instead of loading it via the OpenSSL parsing functions, and the certificate contains non NUL terminated ASN1_STRING structures). It can also occur in the X509_get1_email(), X509_REQ_get1_email() and X509_get1_ocsp() functions. If a malicious actor can cause an application to directly construct an ASN1_STRING and then process it through one of the affected OpenSSL functions then this issue could be hit. This might result in a crash (causing a Denial of Service attack). It could also result in the disclosure of private memory contents (such as private keys, or sensitive plaintext). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1l (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1k). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2za (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2y).

vulnerabilities.description: The code checks that `params->base->data` is non-NULL but does not verify that `params->base->length` is greater than zero before accessing `params->base->data[0]`. An attacker can craft an ECPARAMETERS structure where `params->base` has a non-NULL `data` pointer but `length` of 0, leading to an out-of-bounds read when accessing `data[0]`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives `params` with `params->base->data` pointing to a valid (possibly zero-sized) allocation and `params->base->length == 0`.
The NULL checks on `params->order`, `params->base`, and `params->base->data` all pass because they are non-NULL.
The code dereferences `params->base->data[0]` to determine the point conversion form, but with length 0, there is no valid byte to read, resulting in an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: out-of-bounds read: reading beyond the allocated buffer

commit_message: Limit the number of elements in a vector (found by oss-fuzz)
cve_desc: cdf_read_property_info in cdf.c in file through 5.37 does not restrict the number of CDF_VECTOR elements, which allows a heap-based buffer overflow (4-byte out-of-bounds write).

vulnerabilities.description: On 32-bit platforms, the bounds check `o4 + l > left` can be bypassed via integer overflow. Both `o4` (size_t, 32-bit) and `l` (uint32_t) are 32-bit values, so their sum can wrap around to a small value, passing the check even when the actual offset is out of bounds. This allows setting `pi_str.s_len` to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF) with `pi_str.s_buf` pointing inside the buffer, resulting in an out-of-bounds read when the string is subsequently consumed. Furthermore, when `l` is UINT32_MAX (odd), `l++` wraps to 0, causing `slen` and `o4` to not advance, leading to repeated reads of the same malicious length in the inner loop.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a crafted CDF stream. The section header passes initial validation checks, and sh.sh_properties is set to a small valid value (e.g., 2).
Memory is allocated for sh.sh_properties entries and *count is updated. Pointers p and e are set up to bound the section data.

state.value: points to start of allocated info entries

step_by_step_execution.description: Outer loop begins at i=0. Property 0 is a normal type, processed without issue. Loop advances to i=1.
Property i=1 has type CDF_LENGTH32_STRING with CDF_VECTOR flag set. nelements is read as 2, slen is set to 2.
cdf_grow_info is called for the vector. Inner loop begins with j=0, i=1.
The check `o4 + sizeof(uint32_t) > left` passes (left is large enough). l is read from the crafted stream as 0xFFFFFFFF (UINT32_MAX). o4 becomes 12.
On a 32-bit platform, the bounds check `o4 + l > left` is evaluated. o4 + l = 12 + 0xFFFFFFFF = 0x0000000B (11) due to 32-bit unsigned integer wraparound. Since 11 < left, the check passes.
inp[1].pi_str.s_len is set to 0xFFFFFFFF and pi_str.s_buf points to &q[12]. Any subsequent code reading s_len bytes from s_buf will perform a massive out-of-bounds read.

state.value: &q[12], a pointer inside the stream buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: Since l is odd (0xFFFFFFFF), l++ wraps to 0. slen += 0 >> 1 = 0, so slen and o4 do not advance, potentially allowing repeated exploitation in subsequent loop iterations.

commit_message: Removed a potential buffer overflow crash in GmfOpenMesh and debugged the test_libmeshb_block_pipeline.f that crashed
cve_desc: A buffer overflow in the GmfOpenMesh() function of libMeshb v7.61 allows attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted MESH file.

vulnerabilities.description: The function reads strings from the file using fscanf with "%s" format specifier into a fixed-size stack buffer `str[GmfStrSiz]` without any length limit. A crafted mesh file containing a token longer than GmfStrSiz bytes (before any whitespace) will cause a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a filename ending in `.mesh` (ASCII format) and mod == GmfRead.
The file is opened for reading in ASCII mode.
The code enters the do-while loop to search for 'MeshVersionFormatted', calling fscanf(msh->hdl, "%s", str) on each iteration.
A malicious file contains a single token (no whitespace) longer than GmfStrSiz bytes. fscanf("%s") reads the entire token into str, writing past the end of the buffer.

state.value: overflowed beyond GmfStrSiz bytes, corrupting stack

vulnerabilities.description: When ScaKwdTab() fails, the function returns 0 without freeing `msh` or closing the file handle, causing a memory leak and file descriptor leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called in GmfRead mode and the file is successfully opened and parsed up to the dimension.
ScaKwdTab(msh) is called and returns 0 (failure).
The function returns 0 without calling free(msh) or closing the file, leaking memory and the file descriptor.

commit_message: Removed a potential buffer overflow crash in GmfOpenMesh and debugged the test_libmeshb_block_pipeline.f that crashed
cve_desc: A buffer overflow in the GmfOpenMesh() function of libMeshb v7.61 allows attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted MESH file.

vulnerabilities.description: When ScaKwdTab(msh) fails and returns 0, the function returns 0 without freeing the allocated `msh` structure or closing the open file handle (msh->hdl or msh->FilDes). This causes a memory leak and a file descriptor leak, which could lead to resource exhaustion (denial of service) if the function is called repeatedly with malformed files.

step_by_step_execution.description: Memory is allocated for the mesh structure via calloc.
MshIdx is set to the pointer value cast to int64_t.
setjmp returns 0, so the error-handling block is skipped on first pass.
The filename is validated and copied. The file type is determined from the extension. The file is opened for reading (mod == GmfRead). The version and dimension are successfully read.
ScaKwdTab(msh) is called and returns 0 (failure), e.g., due to a malformed keyword table in the file.
The function returns 0 immediately without calling free(msh) or closing the file handle. The allocated memory and open file descriptor are leaked.

commit_message:
hfsplus: check read_mapping_page() return value

While testing more corrupted images with hfsplus, i came across
one which triggered the following bug:

[15840.675016]  =======================
[15840.675016] Code: 00 00 75 10 83 3d 88 2f ec c0 02 75 07 89 d0 e8 12 56 05 00 5d c3 55 ba 06 00 00 00 89 e5 53 89 c3 b8 3d eb 7e c0 e8 16 74 00 00 <8b> 03 c1 e8 1e 69 c0 d8 02 00 00 05 b8 69 8e c0 2b 80 c4 02 00
[15840.675016] EIP: [<c0116a4f>] kmap+0x15/0x56 SS:ESP 0068:cab0bc94

This happens because the return value of read_mapping_page() is passed on
to kmap unchecked.  The bug is triggered after the first
read_mapping_page() in hfsplus_block_allocate(), this patch fixes all
three usages in this functions but leaves the ones further down in the
file unchanged.

Signed-off-by: Eric Sesterhenn <snakebyte@gmx.de>
Cc: Roman Zippel <zippel@linux-m68k.org>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The hfsplus_block_allocate function in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 does not check a certain return value from the read_mapping_page function before calling kmap, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (system crash) via a crafted hfsplus filesystem image.

code_snippets:
page = read_mapping_page(mapping, offset / PAGE_CACHE_BITS, NULL);
	pptr = kmap(page);
vulnerabilities.description: read_mapping_page() can return an ERR_PTR (error-encoded pointer) on I/O failure or memory pressure. The return value is never checked with IS_ERR() before being passed to kmap() and dereferenced. This leads to dereferencing an invalid kernel pointer, causing a kernel crash/oops (denial of service or worse).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with valid sb, size, offset, and max parameters. len = *max is non-zero, so execution continues.
The first call to read_mapping_page() is made. Under I/O error or memory allocation failure, this returns an ERR_PTR value (e.g., ERR_PTR(-EIO) = 0xFFFFFFF...BA).

state.value: ERR_PTR(-EIO), e.g., (void *)(-5) — an invalid pointer, not a valid page struct

step_by_step_execution.description: kmap(page) is called with the ERR_PTR value. kmap dereferences the page pointer to access page metadata, causing an invalid memory access.

state.value: result of dereferencing invalid pointer — kernel oops/crash

step_by_step_execution.description: Even if the first read_mapping_page succeeds, the same unchecked pattern repeats in the scanning while(1) loop and the allocation while(1) loop, where subsequent pages are read without error checking.

state.value: potentially ERR_PTR on subsequent page reads

commit_message:
aaef on arm/thumb switches causes uaf ##crash

* Reported by peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: poc_uaf_r_reg_get
cve_desc: Use After Free in r_reg_get_name_idx in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

vulnerabilities.description: After `buf` is allocated via `malloc`, several early return paths fail to free `buf`, causing a memory leak. Specifically, the returns when ESIL is NULL after initialization, when `spname` is NULL, and when `pcname` is NULL/empty all leak the `buf` allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function computes `iend = end - start` and validates it is non-negative and not too large.
`buf` is allocated with `malloc((size_t)iend + 2)`. The allocation succeeds.

state.value: non-NULL heap pointer to iend+2 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: `r_io_read_at` reads data into `buf`. Then `ESIL` is checked. If ESIL is NULL, `r_core_cmd0(core, "aei")` is called. If ESIL is still NULL afterwards, the function returns without freeing `buf`.
Alternatively, if ESIL is initialized but `spname` is NULL (no SP register defined), the function returns without freeing `buf`.
Alternatively, if `pcname` is NULL or empty, the function returns without freeing `buf`.

commit_message:
kdc: validate sname in TGS-REQ

In tgs_build_reply(), validate the server name in the TGS-REQ is present before
dereferencing.
cve_desc: A null pointer de-reference was found in the way samba kerberos server handled missing sname in TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Server - Request). An authenticated user could use this flaw to crash the samba server.

vulnerabilities.description: The return value of krb5_make_principal() is not checked in the capath referral loop. If the call fails (e.g., due to memory allocation failure), sp remains NULL (it was set to NULL just before the call). The subsequent call to krb5_unparse_name(context, sp, ...) will dereference the NULL pointer, causing a crash (denial of service).

step_by_step_execution.description: Server lookup (_kdc_db_fetch) fails with a generic error (not HDB_ERR_NOT_FOUND_HERE and not HDB_ERR_WRONG_REALM).
get_krbtgt_realm(&sp->name) returns non-NULL, indicating sp is a krbtgt principal, so we enter the capath referral logic.
_krb5_find_capath succeeds and returns a capath with num_capath > 0.
new_rlm is set to capath[--num_capath], which is non-NULL, so we enter the referral block.
sp is freed and set to NULL in preparation for creating a new principal.
krb5_make_principal is called but its return value is NOT checked. If it fails (e.g., OOM), sp remains NULL.
krb5_unparse_name is called with the NULL sp pointer, causing a NULL pointer dereference and crash.

vulnerabilities.description: In the need_referral path, the return value of krb5_make_principal() is also not checked. If it fails, sp remains NULL and the subsequent krb5_unparse_name dereferences a NULL pointer, causing a crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: Server lookup fails with a generic error and get_krbtgt_realm returns NULL, so we fall to the need_referral path.
need_referral returns true and realms[0] differs from sp->realm, entering the referral block.
sp is freed and set to NULL.
krb5_make_principal is called but its return value is not checked. If it fails, sp remains NULL.
krb5_unparse_name is called with NULL sp, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message: ZipFile:  Add path checks to uncompressEntry()
cve_desc: This affects the package juce-framework/JUCE before 6.1.5. This vulnerability is triggered when a malicious archive is crafted with an entry containing a symbolic link. When extracted, the symbolic link is followed outside of the target dir allowing writing arbitrary files on the target host. In some cases, this can allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code. The vulnerable code is in the ZipFile::uncompressEntry function in juce_ZipFile.cpp and is executed when the archive is extracted upon calling uncompressTo() on a ZipFile object.

vulnerabilities.description: The function uses `getUnchecked` to access the entries array with the caller-supplied `index` parameter. If `index` is negative or greater than or equal to the number of entries, this results in an out-of-bounds memory read with no bounds validation.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with an `index` value that is out of range (e.g., negative, or >= entries.size()).
`entries.getUnchecked(index)` is called, which by definition performs no bounds checking, resulting in an out-of-bounds read from the internal array.

vulnerabilities.description: When the zip entry is a symbolic link, its target path is read from the zip stream content and used directly without validating that the target stays within the target directory. An attacker can craft a zip archive where a symlink entry points to an arbitrary location (e.g., `/etc/passwd` or `../../sensitive_file`). A subsequent zip entry whose path traverses this symlink can then write to an arbitrary location outside the target directory, bypassing the `isAChildOf` check (which only validates the logical path, not resolved symlinks). Even when `followSymlinks` is `no`, the symlink itself is still created; only the *next* entry's parent is checked for symlinks, meaning the symlink artifact pointing outside the directory is left on disk.

step_by_step_execution.description: An attacker crafts a zip file with entry at index N that has `isSymbolicLink = true` and filename `link` (a valid child of the target directory).
The `isAChildOf` check passes because `targetDirectory/link` is logically a child of targetDirectory.
The entry content (the symlink target) is read from the stream. It is attacker-controlled and points outside the target directory, e.g., `/etc/cron.d`.
`File::createSymbolicLink` creates a symlink at `targetDirectory/link` pointing to `/etc/cron.d`. No validation is performed on the symlink target.

A subsequent zip entry with filename `link/malicious` is extracted. Its logical path `targetDirectory/link/malicious` passes `isAChildOf`, but the actual file written is `/etc/cron.d/malicious` because `link` is a symlink.

Check that there's at least one byte in params->base before trying to

This can also happen by using the ASN1_STRING_set0() function. The same thing can also occur during name constraints processing of certificates (for example if a certificate has been directly constructed by the application instead of loading it via the OpenSSL parsing functions, and the certificate contains non NUL terminated ASN1_STRING structures). It can also occur in the X509_get1_email(), X509_REQ_get1_email() and X509_get1_ocsp() functions. This happens because the return value of read_mapping_page() is passed on
to kmap unchecked.  The bug is triggered after the first
read_mapping_page() in hfsplus_block_allocate(), this patch fixes all
three usages in this functions but leaves the ones further down in the
file unchanged.

Signed-off-by: Eric Sesterhenn <snakebyte@gmx.de>
Cc: Roman Zippel <zippel@linux-m68k.org>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The hfsplus_block_allocate function in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 does not check a certain return value from the read_mapping_page function before calling kmap, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (system crash) via a crafted hfsplus filesystem image.

vulnerabilities.description: When a free bit is found but 'start' turns out to be >= 'size', the code jumps directly to 'out' without calling kunmap(page). The page was kmapped earlier via kmap(page), and the 'out' label only performs a mutex_unlock. This leaks a kmap mapping. Repeatedly triggering this path can exhaust the limited kmap slots, leading to a denial of service.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with 'offset' close to 'size', and *max > 0, so len > 0.

state.value: total number of blocks in bitmap

step_by_step_execution.description: The mutex is locked and the bitmap page is read and kmapped. 'curr' and 'end' are set up for scanning.

state.value: result of kmap(page), page is now mapped

step_by_step_execution.description: The scanning loop searches for a zero bit. It finds one (or iterates across page boundaries finding one) and jumps to 'found'.

state.value: bit index of the found zero bit within the current u32
pointer to u32 containing the zero bit

step_by_step_execution.description: At 'found:', start is computed. Due to the bit being in a portion of the bitmap page that extends logically past 'size' (padding bits in the last word), start >= size.
The condition 'start >= size' is true, so the code executes 'goto out', skipping any kunmap(page) call. The kmap mapping is leaked.

state.value: kmapped but never kunmapped — resource leak

commit_message: Fix #1 about out-of-bounds
cve_desc: elfspirit is an ELF static analysis and injection framework that parses, manipulates, and camouflages ELF files. When analyzing the ELF file format in versions prior to 1.1, there is an out-of-bounds read bug, which can lead to application crashes or information leakage. By constructing a special format ELF file, the information of any address can be leaked. elfspirit version 1.1 contains a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: Variables 'dynstr' and 'dynamic' are declared but never initialized. If the ELF file does not contain sections named '.dynstr' or '.dynamic', these variables remain uninitialized and are subsequently used as indices into the shdr[] array, causing out-of-bounds memory access. This occurs in both the 32-bit and 64-bit code paths.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted ELF file is opened and mmap'd. The ELF is ELFCLASS32 and has e_shnum > 0 but no section named '.dynstr' or '.dynamic'.
Variables 'dynstr' and 'dynamic' are declared as local int variables on the stack without initialization.
The loop iterates over all section headers looking for '.dynstr' and '.dynamic' by name. Since neither section exists in the crafted file, neither variable is ever assigned.

The uninitialized 'dynamic' is used as an index into shdr[]: shdr[dynamic].sh_offset is read, producing an out-of-bounds read from the shdr array and subsequently an out-of-bounds access into elf_map.

vulnerabilities.description: ELF header fields (e_shoff, e_phoff, e_shstrndx, sh_offset, sh_name) are read from the untrusted file and used directly as offsets into the mmap'd buffer without validation against the file size (st.st_size). A crafted ELF file can set these to arbitrary values, causing out-of-bounds reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted ELF file is opened. The ehdr is cast from the beginning of the mmap'd region.
ehdr->e_shoff is read from the crafted file. It can be set to a value larger than st.st_size, e.g. 0xFFFFFF.
shdr is computed as &elf_map[ehdr->e_shoff], pointing beyond the mmap'd region.
Accessing shdr[ehdr->e_shstrndx] or any shdr[i] dereferences memory outside the mmap'd region, causing an out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.description: When a section name is longer than 15 characters, the code truncates it by writing '[...]' (6 bytes) into the mmap'd buffer at name[9]. Since 'name' points into the mmap'd ELF file data (mapped with PROT_WRITE, MAP_PRIVATE), this modifies the in-memory copy. If sh_name offsets for different sections overlap or are close together, this write corrupts data used by subsequent iterations, and if the name region is near the end of the mapping, it could write past the mmap boundary.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted ELF file has a section name string table (shstrtab) with a section name placed near the very end of the file, such that the string extends close to or at the boundary of the mmap'd region.

name is computed as elf_map + shstrtab.sh_offset + shdr[i].sh_name, pointing near the end of the mapped region. strlen(name) reads until a null byte; if no null exists before the mapping boundary this is already an over-read, but if strlen returns >15...
strcpy(&name[9], "[...]") writes 6 bytes starting at elf_map + 509. If the mapping is only 512 bytes, the write at offset 509+5=514 goes past the mmap boundary.

state.value: 6 bytes ([...]\0), reaching elf_map + 515, past mapping end at 512

commit_message: Fix #1 about out-of-bounds
cve_desc: elfspirit is an ELF static analysis and injection framework that parses, manipulates, and camouflages ELF files. When analyzing the ELF file format in versions prior to 1.1, there is an out-of-bounds read bug, which can lead to application crashes or information leakage. By constructing a special format ELF file, the information of any address can be leaked. elfspirit version 1.1 contains a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The variables 'dynstr' and 'dynamic' are declared but never initialized. If the ELF file does not contain sections named '.dynstr' or '.dynamic', these variables retain indeterminate values and are subsequently used as array indices into shdr[], leading to out-of-bounds memory access. This applies to both the 32-bit and 64-bit code paths.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted ELF file is opened and mmap'd. The file is a valid 32-bit ELF (ELFCLASS32) but contains no section named '.dynstr' or '.dynamic'.
Local variables 'dynstr' and 'dynamic' are declared as int without initialization.
The loop iterates over all section headers comparing names to '.dynstr' and '.dynamic'. Since neither section exists, neither variable is ever assigned.
The code accesses shdr[dynamic].sh_offset using the uninitialized 'dynamic' as an index into the section header array, causing an out-of-bounds read.
The resulting garbage offset is used to index into elf_map, and subsequent reads through dyn pointer and shdr[dynstr] also use uninitialized indices, leading to further out-of-bounds memory access.

state.value: pointer to arbitrary location based on garbage offset

vulnerabilities.description: The ELF header fields e_shoff, e_phoff, and e_shstrndx are read directly from the (potentially crafted) input file and used as offsets/indices into the mmap'd buffer without any bounds validation. A crafted ELF with e_shoff or e_phoff larger than the file size causes out-of-bounds memory reads. This applies to both the 32-bit and 64-bit code paths.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted ELF file with ELFCLASS32 is opened and mmap'd. The file has e_shoff set to a value larger than the file size (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF).
The section header pointer is computed as &elf_map[ehdr->e_shoff], pointing well beyond the mapped region.
Accessing shdr[ehdr->e_shstrndx] dereferences the out-of-bounds pointer, reading arbitrary memory.

vulnerabilities.description: When a section name is longer than 15 characters, the code writes '[...]' (6 bytes including null terminator) into the mmap'd buffer at name+9. Since the mapping is PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE with MAP_PRIVATE, this modifies data in the mapped buffer. If a crafted ELF has sh_name pointing such that name+9 is near the end of the mapped region, or the string has no null terminator within the mapped region, strlen() could read out of bounds, and the strcpy could write out of bounds. Additionally, this corrupts the mmap'd data that is used in later iterations (e.g., 'Section to segment mapping' loop uses the same name pointers).

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted ELF file is parsed. A section header's sh_name is set such that the section name string is long (> 15 chars) and positioned near the end of the file.

state.value: elf_map + shstrtab.sh_offset + shdr[i].sh_name (pointing near end of mapped region)

step_by_step_execution.description: validated_offset checks that 'name' pointer itself is within [elf_map, elf_map+st.st_size), but doesn't check that name+14 or name+9+5 is within bounds.

state.value: within bounds at start, but string extends to or beyond mapped boundary

step_by_step_execution.description: strlen(name) reads bytes starting from name looking for a null terminator. If no null terminator exists within the mapped region, this reads out of bounds. If it finds one beyond offset 15, the condition is true.
strcpy(&name[9], "[...]") writes 6 bytes at name+9. If name+9+5 exceeds the mapped region boundary, this is an out-of-bounds write.

state.value: name+9 through name+14, potentially beyond mapped region boundary

commit_message: Limit the number of elements in a vector (found by oss-fuzz)
cve_desc: cdf_read_property_info in cdf.c in file through 5.37 does not restrict the number of CDF_VECTOR elements, which allows a heap-based buffer overflow (4-byte out-of-bounds write).

vulnerabilities.description: On 32-bit platforms, the bounds check `o4 + l > left` can be bypassed via integer overflow when `l` is a large attacker-controlled uint32_t value. This allows setting `inp[i].pi_str.s_len` to a very large value pointing past the actual buffer, and subsequent `o4 = slen * sizeof(uint32_t)` can also overflow, causing `o4` to wrap to a small value and allowing further out-of-bounds access in later iterations.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function starts, `offs` is validated and section header is read. `sh.sh_len` and `sh.sh_properties` pass their limit checks.
`cdf_grow_info` allocates space for `sh.sh_properties` entries, `inp` and `*count` are updated.

state.value: points to start of allocated property info array

step_by_step_execution.description: Outer loop iteration i=0: property type is CDF_LENGTH32_STRING with CDF_VECTOR flag set. `nelements` is read from attacker-controlled data.
Inner loop j=0: `o4 + sizeof(uint32_t) > left` check passes. `l` is read from attacker-controlled stream data as a large value, e.g. 0xFFFFFFF0.
On a 32-bit platform (size_t is 32 bits), the check `o4 + l > left` computes `12 + 0xFFFFFFF0 = 0x00000002` due to integer overflow. Since `2 > 240` is false, the bounds check is bypassed.

state.value: false (2 > 240 is false), check is bypassed

step_by_step_execution.description: `inp[i].pi_str.s_len` is set to the huge value `l` (0xFFFFFFF0), and `s_buf` points to `&q[o4]`. This stores a string descriptor with length far exceeding the actual available buffer, enabling out-of-bounds reads by any consumer of this data.

state.value: &q[12] — within bounds, but s_len extends far past buffer end

step_by_step_execution.description: The rounding `l & 1` is false (l is even), so `slen += l >> 1 = 0x7FFFFFF8`. On 32-bit, `o4 = slen * 4` overflows, wrapping `o4` to a small value, corrupting offset tracking for subsequent iterations.

state.value: small wrapped value due to multiplication overflow

commit_message: libclamav/mspack.c: fix Quantum decompressor (bb#1771)
cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

vulnerabilities.description: In the literal byte path (selector < 4), `window[window_posn++] = sym` writes to the window buffer without checking whether `window_posn` has reached `window_size`. The `frame_end` value is capped at `frame_start + QTM_FRAME_SIZE`, but not at `window_size`. If `frame_start + QTM_FRAME_SIZE > window_size` (which happens when `window_size < QTM_FRAME_SIZE`, or when `frame_start` is positioned such that the sum exceeds the allocation), the inner loop writes past the window buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a valid qtm stream where qtm->window_size is less than QTM_FRAME_SIZE (e.g., window_size = 1024, while QTM_FRAME_SIZE is typically 32768), and out_bytes is large enough to request more data than the window can hold.
Local state is restored: window_posn = 0, frame_start = 0.
frame_end is computed. First as window_posn + (out_bytes - buffered) which could be very large, then capped to frame_start + QTM_FRAME_SIZE.
frame_end (32768) is much larger than window_size (1024). The inner while loop runs with condition window_posn < frame_end (window_posn < 32768).

With selector < 4 (literal bytes), the code executes `window[window_posn++] = sym` repeatedly. After 1024 iterations, window_posn equals window_size (1024), but the loop continues since window_posn < frame_end. Subsequent writes go past the allocated window buffer.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable `j` is declared as `int` but receives the result of an unsigned subtraction `match_offset - window_posn`. If `match_offset` is crafted to produce a result exceeding INT_MAX, `j` becomes negative due to signed integer overflow. The subsequent guard `j > (int) qtm->window_size` evaluates to false (negative compared to positive), bypassing the bounds check. Then `qtm->window_size - j` with negative `j` (implicitly converted to a large unsigned value) computes a pointer far outside the window buffer, leading to out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: A match is decoded with selectors 4, 5, or 6. match_offset is computed as position_base[sym] + extra + 1. If the compressed data is crafted so that match_offset is very large (> 2^31 + window_posn), the unsigned value overflows when stored in int j.
The code enters the branch match_offset > window_posn (true since 0x80000010 > 5). j = match_offset - window_posn is computed as unsigned, giving ~0x8000000B, which overflows to a negative int.
The guard `j > (int) qtm->window_size` is evaluated. Since j is negative and window_size cast to int is positive, the comparison is false, bypassing the bounds check.

runsrc is set to &window[qtm->window_size - j]. With j negative, window_size - j (unsigned arithmetic with implicit conversion) yields an enormous index, pointing far outside the window buffer. Subsequent copy loop reads from this invalid location.

commit_message: Fix ultra rare but potential memory-leak
cve_desc: ImageMagick before 7.0.8-50 has a memory leak vulnerability in the function ReadBMPImage in coders/bmp.c.

vulnerabilities.description: On 32-bit platforms, the expression `image->columns*bmp_info.bits_per_pixel` in the `bytes_per_line` calculation can suffer an integer overflow, causing `bytes_per_line` and `length` to wrap to very small values. This leads to a small `ReadBlob` read, while the subsequent pixel processing loops iterate over the full `image->columns`, reading far past the initialized portion of the allocated buffer (heap buffer over-read). The size validation check `length/8 > GetBlobSize` is easily bypassed because the wrapped `length` is tiny.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted BMP file is read with bmp_info.width = 134217729 (0x08000001), rows = 1, bits_per_pixel = 32, compression = BI_RGB.

Validation checks pass: width > 0, height != 0, bits_per_pixel is 32 (valid), compression is BI_RGB (valid). SetImageExtent is called for a 134217729×1 image, which may succeed on a 32-bit system (~512MB-1GB for pixel cache).

bytes_per_line is computed. On a 32-bit platform, image->columns * bmp_info.bits_per_pixel = 0x08000001 * 32 = 0x100000020 which truncates to 0x20 (32-bit overflow). Thus bytes_per_line = 4*((0x20+31)/32) = 4*2 = 8.
length = bytes_per_line * image->rows = 8 * 1 = 8. The check (length/8 > GetBlobSize) evaluates to (1 > GetBlobSize) which is false for any valid file, so no exception is thrown.
pixel_info is allocated via AcquireVirtualMemory(1, MagickMax(8, 134217729+256)) = AcquireVirtualMemory(1, 134217985). This allocates ~134MB successfully.
ReadBlob reads `length` = 8 bytes into the ~134MB buffer. Only 8 bytes are initialized; the rest is uninitialized heap memory.

In the 32-bit pixel processing case, bytes_per_line is recalculated as 4*image->columns = 4*134217729 = 536870916. The inner loop iterates image->columns (134217729) times, reading 4 bytes per pixel via p++, consuming 536870916 bytes total from a buffer of only ~134217985 bytes. This reads ~402MB past the end of the allocated buffer.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0104: going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant

Problem:    Going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant.
Solution:   Properly skip over <Key> form.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0102.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer truncation when casting the buffer size calculation to int. If the input string is very large, `p - *arg + extra` (computed in ptrdiff_t/long arithmetic) can exceed INT_MAX. The cast to `(int)` truncates the value, potentially resulting in a small positive number. This causes a small heap buffer to be allocated, while the subsequent copy loop writes the full string contents, leading to a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a very large double-quoted string (>2GB), with `evaluate` set to true.

state.value: pointer to a >2GB string starting with '"'

step_by_step_execution.description: The first for-loop scans to the closing '"', advancing p through the entire string. After the loop, p points to the closing '"'.
The code passes the check that *p == '"' and proceeds because evaluate is true.
len is computed as `(int)(p - *arg + extra)`. The ptrdiff_t value exceeds INT_MAX, so the cast to int truncates to a small positive value.
alloc(len) allocates only 16 bytes on the heap, succeeding.
The second for-loop copies the entire >2GB string content into the 16-byte buffer via MB_COPY_CHAR, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
glob: fix heap buffer overflow

* lib/glob.c (glob): Fix off-by-one error introduced into
glibc in commit dd7d45e838a42b0ed470c44b55901ea98d0c2bab
dated 1997-10-29 20:33:40.  Problem reported by Tim Rühsen in:
https://sourceware.org/bugzilla/show_bug.cgi?id=22320
Fix suggested by Bruno Haible.
cve_desc: The GNU C Library (aka glibc or libc6) before 2.27 contains an off-by-one error leading to a heap-based buffer overflow in the glob function in glob.c, related to the processing of home directories using the ~ operator followed by a long string.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the tilde expansion path when dirname[1] != '\0' (e.g., pattern is "~/path..."). The expression `home_len + dirlen` can wrap around on 32-bit systems if the HOME environment variable and the pattern are both very large (summing to > SIZE_MAX). This causes a small buffer to be allocated, followed by large mempcpy operations that write far beyond the buffer boundary, resulting in a heap-based buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: glob() is called with a pattern starting with '~/' followed by a very long path (e.g., ~2GB on a 32-bit system), and flags include GLOB_TILDE.
The '/' is found by strrchr, so filename points past the last '/'. dirname is allocated as a copy of the directory portion of pattern.

state.value: points to the filename portion after last '/'

step_by_step_execution.description: The tilde check matches (dirname[0] == '~'), and dirname[1] == '/' so we enter the home directory expansion branch.
HOME environment variable is read, containing a very long string (~2GB).
The size calculation `home_len + dirlen` overflows size_t (32-bit), wrapping to a small value.
A small buffer (e.g., 32 bytes) is allocated via malloc with the overflowed size.
mempcpy writes home_len (~2GB) bytes from home_dir into the 32-byte buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1337
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-13 Q16, there is an infinite loop in the ReadBMPImage function of the coders/bmp.c file. Remote attackers could leverage this vulnerability to cause a denial of service via a crafted bmp file.

vulnerabilities.description: For 1-bit BMP images, the pixel index (0 or 1) is written directly without validating it against the colormap size (via IsValidColormapIndex, which IS used in the 4-bit and 8-bit cases). A crafted BMP with bits_per_pixel=1 and number_colors=1 will allocate a colormap with only 1 entry (index 0). When pixel data contains set bits, index 1 is stored, and the subsequent SyncImage call accesses colormap[1], which is an out-of-bounds read. The validation check only rejects number_colors > (1 << bits_per_pixel), so number_colors=1 passes the check (1 > 2 is false).

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted BMP file is read with Windows BMP header (bmp_info.size >= 40), bits_per_pixel=1, and number_colors=1.
The validation check for number_colors is evaluated: number_colors (1) > (1U << bits_per_pixel) which is (1 > 2), evaluating to false. The check passes.

state.value: false (1 is not > 2), so no exception thrown

step_by_step_execution.description: Since bits_per_pixel < 16, image->storage_class is set to PseudoClass. image->colors is set to bmp_info.number_colors (1). AcquireImageColormap allocates a colormap with exactly 1 entry (valid index: 0 only).

In the case 1 pixel processing loop, for each bit in the pixel data, the index is computed as 0 or 1 depending on whether the bit is set. No IsValidColormapIndex check is performed (unlike the 4-bit and 8-bit cases).

state.value: 0 or 1 depending on bit value in pixel data

step_by_step_execution.description: When a set bit is encountered, index=1 is stored via SetPixelIndex. This index exceeds the colormap bounds (only index 0 is valid).
SyncImage is called, which maps stored index values through the colormap. Accessing colormap[1] with a colormap of size 1 results in an out-of-bounds heap read.

commit_message:
LZWDecodeCompat(): fix potential index-out-of-bounds write. Fixes http://bugzilla.maptools.org/show_bug.cgi?id=2780 / CVE-2018-8905

The fix consists in using the similar code LZWDecode() to validate we
don't write outside of the output buffer.
cve_desc: In LibTIFF 4.0.9, a heap-based buffer overflow occurs in the function LZWDecodeCompat in tif_lzw.c via a crafted TIFF file, as demonstrated by tiff2ps.
The ReadTGAImage function in coders\tga.c in ImageMagick 7.0.5-6 has a memory leak vulnerability that can cause memory exhaustion via invalid colors data in the header of a TGA or VST file.

vulnerabilities.description: When a TGA file has colormap_type != 0 with colormap_index = 0 and colormap_length = 0, image->colors is set to 0. AcquireImageColormap is then called with 0 entries. During pixel reading in the 8-bit case, the code accesses image->colormap via ConstrainColormapIndex which may return index 0 on a zero-length colormap allocation, resulting in a heap out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted TGA file is read with image_type = TGAColormap, colormap_type = 1, colormap_index = 0, colormap_length = 0, bits_per_pixel = 8.
The header validation passes because image_type is TGAColormap and colormap_type is non-zero (1).
Since image_type is TGAColormap, storage_class is set to PseudoClass. Since colormap_type != 0, image->colors is set to colormap_index + colormap_length = 0 + 0 = 0.
In the colormap reading block (colormap_type != 0), the check 'image->colors < tga_info.colormap_index' evaluates to '0 < 0' which is false, so image->colors stays 0.
AcquireImageColormap(image, 0, exception) is called, allocating a colormap with 0 entries (or a minimal degenerate allocation).
Both colormap initialization loops don't execute since colormap_index and image->colors are both 0.

During pixel decoding, a byte is read as index (e.g., value 0-255). Since colormap_type != 0, the code accesses image->colormap[(ssize_t) ConstrainColormapIndex(image, index, exception)]. With image->colors = 0, any returned index accesses the colormap out of bounds.

vulnerabilities.description: When image_type is TGAMonochrome (PseudoClass) with colormap_type = 0 and bits_per_pixel = 32, the expression 'one << 32' where 'one' is a size_t causes undefined behavior on platforms where size_t is 32 bits (shifting by the full width of the type). This could result in image->colors being 0, leading to a zero-size colormap allocation followed by out-of-bounds access, or other unexpected behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted TGA file is read with image_type = TGAMonochrome, colormap_type = 0, bits_per_pixel = 32.
Header validation passes: bits_per_pixel = 32 is explicitly allowed (not != 24 and not != 32).
Since image_type is TGAMonochrome, storage_class is set to PseudoClass. Since colormap_type is 0, the else branch executes.

one is declared as size_t and set to 1. image->colors = one << 32. On 32-bit platforms where size_t is 32 bits, this is undefined behavior (shift amount equals type width). The result is implementation-defined, commonly yielding 0 or 1.
AcquireImageColormap is called with potentially 0 colors, leading to a degenerate or zero-size colormap allocation.

commit_message: Fix ultra rare but potential memory-leak
cve_desc: ImageMagick before 7.0.8-50 has a memory leak vulnerability in the function ReadBMPImage in coders/bmp.c.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1337
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-13 Q16, there is an infinite loop in the ReadBMPImage function of the coders/bmp.c file. Remote attackers could leverage this vulnerability to cause a denial of service via a crafted bmp file.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `length = bytes_per_line * image->rows`. If this multiplication wraps around `size_t`, `length` becomes a small value (potentially 0). The sanity check `length/8 > GetBlobSize` then passes trivially. Subsequently, `ReadBlob` reads the small truncated `length` bytes into the pixel buffer, but the pixel-processing loops access the buffer using the original (non-overflowed) `bytes_per_line` and `image->rows`, potentially reading far beyond the data actually read (heap buffer over-read of uninitialized memory). On systems where `AcquireVirtualMemory` also fails to detect a corresponding overflow in its own multiplication, this can additionally lead to an out-of-bounds heap read past the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Image columns and rows are set from BMP header fields. `image->columns` and `image->rows` can each be up to ~2^31 (from signed 32-bit width/height fields).

`bytes_per_line` is computed. For large columns and bits_per_pixel=32, the intermediate `image->columns * bmp_info.bits_per_pixel` can overflow on 32-bit platforms, making `bytes_per_line` smaller than expected.

state.value: Potentially small due to overflow in image->columns*bits_per_pixel

step_by_step_execution.description: `length = bytes_per_line * image->rows` is computed. Even without overflow in `bytes_per_line`, this multiplication can overflow `size_t`, wrapping to a small value (e.g., 0).

state.value: Small or 0 due to size_t overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: The sanity check `(length/8) > GetBlobSize(image)` is evaluated. With `length` overflowed to a small value, `length/8` is also small, so the check passes for any non-trivial file.

state.value: Small value, passes check against file size

step_by_step_execution.description: Memory is allocated via `AcquireVirtualMemory(image->rows, MagickMax(bytes_per_line, image->columns+256))`. If this also overflows internally, a small buffer is allocated.

state.value: Potentially small due to overflow in allocation size computation

step_by_step_execution.description: `ReadBlob(image, length, pixels)` reads the overflowed small `length` bytes. Since `count == (ssize_t)length`, the count check passes.

Pixel processing loops iterate over `image->rows` rows, each accessing `bytes_per_line` bytes from the pixel buffer at offset `(image->rows-y-1)*bytes_per_line`. These accesses can go beyond both the data actually read and potentially beyond the allocated buffer, causing heap buffer over-read.

state.value: (image->rows-1)*bytes_per_line + bytes_per_line - 1, much larger than allocated size

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in `image->columns * image->rows` passed as the byte-count limit to `DecodeImage`. On 32-bit systems, if both dimensions are large (but individually valid), the product wraps to a small value. This gives `DecodeImage` an incorrect (too small) limit for the output buffer, which could cause it to either write past the indicated limit or produce incomplete data that is later processed as if it were complete.

step_by_step_execution.description: For RLE-compressed BMPs, `image->columns` and `image->rows` are set from BMP header. On 32-bit platforms, both can be up to ~2^16+ while still passing SetImageExtent.
The product `image->columns * image->rows` is computed as a `size_t` multiplication. On 32-bit, this can overflow.
DecodeImage receives the overflowed small value as the buffer size limit, potentially allowing it to mismanage buffer writes or producing incomplete pixel data that is then processed as full rows.

state.value: Small overflowed value instead of true columns*rows

cve_desc: The ReadTGAImage function in coders\tga.c in ImageMagick 7.0.5-6 has a memory leak vulnerability that can cause memory exhaustion via invalid colors data in the header of a TGA or VST file.
